`C` denotes Chassis. This is typically a **Manufacturer Specific** code (digit `1` would be Mfg; `0` is generic). The reader must consult OEM service literature.

---

## 3.5 Master DTC Decoding Matrix (Reference for KTU Answers)

| DTC | System | Type | Subsystem | Specific Fault |
|-----|--------|------|-----------|----------------|
| `P0301` | Powertrain | Generic | Ignition | Cylinder 1 Misfire |
| `P0171` | Powertrain | Generic | Fuel/Air | Bank 1 System Lean |
| `P0172` | Powertrain | Generic | Fuel/Air | Bank 1 System Rich |
| `P0420` | Powertrain | Generic | Aux Emissions | Catalyst Efficiency Low (Bank 1) |
| `P0440` | Powertrain | Generic | Aux Emissions | EVAP System Malfunction |
| `P0500` | Powertrain | Generic | Vehicle Speed | VSS Malfunction |
| `B1342` | Body | Generic | — | ECU Defective (manufacturer-specific) |
| `C0561` | Chassis | Generic | ABS/Stability | Disabled by DTC |
| `U0100` | Network | Generic | Communication | Lost Comm with ECM/PCM |
| `U0121` | Network | Generic | Communication | Lost Comm With ABS Module |

---

## 3.6 MIL Illumination Logic — Closed-Form Decision Tree

The MIL trip decision is a **state machine** with four states.

Define:

- $T$ = current drive cycle
- $T_{\text{prev}}$ = previous drive cycle result
- $F$ = fault-present boolean (set when sensor reading exceeds threshold)
- $C$ = warm-up cycle completed boolean (cold-start → operating temperature)

The state transitions are:

$$
\text{State}_{n+1} =
\begin{cases}
\text{No DTC}, & \text{if } F = \text{false} \\[4pt]
\text{Pending DTC}, & \text{if } (F = \text{true}) \land (C = \text{true}) \land (T_{\text{prev}} = \text{No DTC}) \\[4pt]
\text{Confirmed DTC + MIL ON}, & \text{if } (F = \text{true}) \land (C = \text{true}) \land (T_{\text{prev}} = \text{Pending}) \\[4pt]
\text{MIL FLASH}, & \text{if } (\% \text{misfire} \geq 1.0) \land (T_{\text{prev}} = \text{Confirmed}) \\[4pt]
\text{Historical DTC}, & \text{if } F = \text{false in subsequent cycles, MIL OFF but DTC retained}
\end{cases}
$$

**Line-by-line interpretation** (use this exact wording in your 14-mark answer):

1. **No DTC state** — All sensor values are within normal operating range. No fault code is stored.
2. **Pending DTC** — A fault was detected in the *current* drive cycle after a proper warm-up. The DTC is written to KAM, but the MIL is **NOT** yet illuminated. Pending codes appear under **Mode 07**.
3. **Confirmed DTC + MIL ON** — The fault recurred in the *next* drive cycle. Only now is the MIL lit and the DTC moved to **Mode 03** (stored) and **Mode 0A** (permanent).
4. **MIL FLASH** — Reserved for severe engine misfires where continued operation can destroy the catalytic converter in minutes due to unburnt fuel igniting in the catalyst bed.
5. **Historical DTC** — If the fault does not recur over **40 consecutive warm-up cycles**, the MIL turns off automatically. The DTC remains as a historical record.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OBD-II System Block Diagram (Mermaid — Flow of Diagnostic Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[O2 Sensor Bank1 S1] --> ECU1
    A2[O2 Sensor Bank1 S2] --> ECU1
    A3[MAF Mass Air Flow Sensor] --> ECU1
    A4[MAP Manifold Absolute Pressure] --> ECU1
    A5[CKP Crankshaft Position] --> ECU1
    A6[CMP Camshaft Position] --> ECU1
    A7[ECT Engine Coolant Temp] --> ECU1
    A8[TPS Throttle Position] --> ECU1
    A9[Knock Sensor] --> ECU1
    A10[VSS Vehicle Speed] --> ECU1

    ECU1[Engine Control Unit ECU] --> ROM1[ROM Calibration Maps]
    ECU1 --> KAM1[KAM Keep Alive Memory]
    ECU1 --> DIAG1[Diagnostic Executive Software]
    DIAG1 --> DTC1[Set DTC P xxxx]
    DIAG1 --> FF1[Capture Freeze Frame]
    DIAG1 --> MIL1[Illuminate MIL Check Engine Lamp]
    DIAG1 --> LIMP1[Activate Limp Home Mode]
    DTC1 --> KAM1
    FF1 --> KAM1

    ECU1 --> ACT1[Fuel Injector Driver]
    ECU1 --> ACT2[Ignition Coil Driver]
    ECU1 --> ACT3[EGR Valve Control]
    ECU1 --> ACT4[Canister Purge Valve]

    DLC1[DLC 16 pin J1962 Connector] --> SCAN1[OBD II Scan Tool]
    SCAN1 --> ECU1

    MIL1 --> DRV[Driver Awareness]
    SCAN1 --> TECH[Service Technician]
```

## 4.2 OBD-II Communication Protocol Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[OBD II Scan Tool Connected to DLC] --> P1{Which pins are active}
    P1 -->|Pins 2 and 10| P2[SAE J1850 VPW or PWM]
    P1 -->|Pin 7 only K Line| P3[ISO 9141 2 or ISO 14230 KWP2000]
    P1 -->|Pins 6 and 14| P4[ISO 15765 4 CAN mandatory post 2008]
    P2 --> P2a{VPW single ended 10.4 kbps}
    P2 --> P2b{PWM differential 41.6 kbps}
    P3 --> P3a{10.4 kbps async serial}
    P4 --> P4a{500 kbps high speed CAN modern}
    P4 --> P4b{250 kbps low speed CAN}
```

## 4.3 MIL Trip State Machine (Two-Trip Detection Logic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoDTC
    NoDTC --> PendingDTC: Drive cycle 1 fault detected warm up done
    PendingDTC --> NoDTC: Fault not repeated in drive cycle 2
    PendingDTC --> ConfirmedDTC: Fault repeated in drive cycle 2 MIL ON
    ConfirmedDTC --> HistoricalDTC: No fault for 40 warm up cycles MIL OFF
    ConfirmedDTC --> FlashingMIL: Severe misfire 1 percent catalyst damage risk
    FlashingMIL --> ConfirmedDTC: Driver reduces load fault still present
    HistoricalDTC --> [*]: Technician clears with scan tool Mode 04
```

## 4.4 DLC Connector Pinout Reference (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DLC[DLC J1962 16 pin female]
    P1[Pin 1 OEM] --- DLC
    P2[Pin 2 J1850 Bus +] --- DLC
    P4[Pin 4 Chassis GND] --- DLC
    P5[Pin 5 Signal GND] --- DLC
    P6[Pin 6 CAN High] --- DLC
    P7[Pin 7 ISO 9141 K Line] --- DLC
    P10[Pin 10 J1850 Bus -] --- DLC
    P14[Pin 14 CAN Low] --- DLC
    P15[Pin 15 ISO 9141 L Line] --- DLC
    P16[Pin 16 Battery +12 V] --- DLC
    DLC --> VEH[Vehicle Harness]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set B, Q1]**

**Q1. Define OBD-II. List any four standardized OBD-II service modes.**

*Model Answer (write this verbatim in exam):*

**OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, Generation II)** is a vehicle's built-in self-diagnostic system that continuously monitors engine, transmission, and emission-related components for malfunctions. It is standardized under SAE J1962 (DLC connector), SAE J1979 (service modes), and SAE J2012 (DTC format). Four standardized service modes are:

1. **Mode 01** — Show current live parameter data.
2. **Mode 03** — Show stored (confirmed) diagnostic trouble codes.
3. **Mode 04** — Clear stored DTCs and freeze frame.
4. **Mode 09** — Request vehicle information (VIN, calibration ID).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between OBD-I and OBD-II in four points.**

*Model Answer:*

| Parameter | OBD-I | OBD-II |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| Year mandated | 1988 (California) | 1996 (US) / 2001 (EU as EOBD) |
| Connector | Proprietary per OEM | Standardized 16-pin J1962 |
| DTC format | OEM-specific | Universal 5-character alphanumeric |
| Protocols | OEM-specific | SAE J1850, ISO 9141, ISO 14230, ISO 15765 (CAN) |
| Coverage | Basic engine | Engine + transmission + emissions + body |
| MIL logic | One-trip | Two-trip (more reliable) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q11(b)]**

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and working of the OBD-II system with a neat block diagram. List the major OBD-II communication protocols.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Decode the following DTCs and explain the most likely cause of each:

1. `P0301`
2. `P0420`
3. `U0100`

---

#### SOLUTION A (a) — Architecture & Working (7 Marks)

**Architecture (3 marks):**

The OBD-II system has a **closed-loop diagnostic architecture** comprising:

- **Sensor layer** — O2 (pre/post catalyst), MAF, MAP, CKP, CMP, ECT, TPS, knock, VSS.
- **ECU / Diagnostic executive** — The on-board software running on the Engine Control Module that runs **continuous and periodic monitor tests**.
- **Memory** — KAM (Keep-Alive Memory) for DTCs, ROM for calibration thresholds.
- **Actuator feedback** — Fuel injectors, ignition coils, EGR valve, purge valve.
- **Reporting layer** — Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) and Data Link Connector (DLC, 16-pin J1962).

**Working (3 marks):**

1. At **ignition-on**, the ECU performs a **bulb-check** of the MIL.
2. The diagnostic executive runs **continuous monitors** (misfire, fuel system) every crank revolution.
3. **Periodic monitors** (catalyst, EGR, evap, O2 sensor) run only when the engine is in closed loop at normal operating temperature.
4. When a fault is detected, the DTC is **written to KAM** and a **freeze frame** captured.
5. If the fault repeats in the **next drive cycle** (two-trip logic), the MIL is illuminated.
6. A scan tool connected to the DLC can issue **Service Mode 03** to read DTCs, **Mode 02** for freeze frame, **Mode 04** to clear.

**Protocols (1 mark):** SAE J1850 VPW (GM), SAE J1850 PWM (Ford), ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000), ISO 15765-4 (CAN — modern standard).

---

#### SOLUTION A (b) — DTC Decoding (7 Marks)

**DTC 1 — `P0301` (2.3 marks):**

| Position | Decode |
|----------|--------|
| P | Powertrain |
| 0 | Generic (SAE) |
| 3 | Ignition system |
| 01 | Cylinder 1 |

**Meaning:** Misfire detected in Cylinder 1. ECU counts misfires per 1000 crank revolutions; if the misfire rate exceeds **1%**, the MIL flashes (catalyst damage threshold). Probable causes: bad spark plug, failing ignition coil, low compression, bad fuel injector, vacuum leak at intake runner 1.

**DTC 2 — `P0420` (2.3 marks):**

| Position | Decode |
|----------|--------|
| P | Powertrain |
| 0 | Generic |
| 4 | Auxiliary emissions |
| 20 | Catalyst efficiency below threshold (Bank 1) |

**Meaning:** The catalyst monitor compares pre- and post-catalyst O2 sensor amplitudes. When the post-cat O2 sensor starts oscillating in sync with the pre-cat, catalyst efficiency is below the **regulatory threshold (typically 50%)**. Causes: aged catalytic converter, exhaust leak between the two O2 sensors, faulty downstream O2 sensor, engine oil contamination (phosphorus/sulphur poisoning of the washcoat).

**DTC 3 — `U0100` (2.4 marks):**

| Position | Decode |
|----------|--------|
| U | Network (Communication) |
| 0 | Generic |
| 1 | Communication subsystem |
| 00 | Lost communication with ECM/PCM |

**Meaning:** The gateway module has not received a valid CAN heartbeat from the ECM within the timeout window (typically 200 ms). Causes: open or shorted CAN-H / CAN-L wires, missing 120 Ω terminating resistor, faulty ECM, bad connector at DLC pins 6/14, or low battery voltage. The vehicle may go into limp-home mode.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **DLC (Data Link Connector)** J1962 pinout and the **standardized OBD-II service modes**. Mention the legal two-trip MIL logic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **five OBD-II communication protocols** in a table. Why is **ISO 15765 CAN** the dominant modern standard?

#### SOLUTION B (a) — DLC + Service Modes (7 Marks)

**DLC J1962 (3 marks):**

The DLC is a **16-pin female trapezoidal connector** located within reach of the driver (usually under the dashboard, left of the steering column). Key pins are:

- **Pin 4** — Chassis ground.
- **Pin 5** — Signal ground.
- **Pin 6** — CAN High.
- **Pin 14** — CAN Low.
- **Pin 7** — K-Line (ISO 9141 / ISO 14230).
- **Pin 16** — Battery +12 V unswitched (allows scan tool to operate with ignition OFF).

**Service Modes (3 marks):** Refer to the table in §2.4. Highlight Mode 01 (live data), 02 (freeze frame), 03 (stored DTCs), 04 (clear), 06 (test results), 0A (permanent).

**Two-trip MIL logic (1 mark):** As derived in §3.6, the MIL illuminates only after the **same fault is detected in two consecutive drive cycles** to prevent false alarms and nuisance warnings.

---

#### SOLUTION B (b) — Protocol Comparison (7 Marks)

| Protocol | Pins Used | Speed | Differential? | Modern? |
|----------|-----------|-------|---------------|---------|
| SAE J1850 VPW | 2 + 10 | 10.4 kbps | No (single-ended) | Legacy (GM) |
| SAE J1850 PWM | 2 + 10 | 41.6 kbps | Yes (2-wire) | Legacy (Ford) |
| ISO 9141-2 | 7 (K) | 10.4 kbps | No (asynchronous) | Legacy EU/Asia |
| ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000) | 7 (K) | 10.4 kbps | No | Legacy EU diesel |
| **ISO 15765-4 (CAN)** | **6 (H) + 14 (L)** | **250/500 kbps** | **Yes (differential)** | **Universal (2008+)** |

**Why CAN dominates (3 marks):**

1. **Speed** — 500 kbps vs 10.4 kbps → 50× faster diagnostic data exchange, enabling real-time bidirectional actuator tests.
2. **Noise immunity** — Differential signaling on CAN-H / CAN-L cancels common-mode noise, critical in the harsh automotive EMI environment (ignition coils, alternators).
3. **Multi-master arbitration** — Multiple ECUs (ECM, TCM, ABS, BCM, Gateway) can arbitrate on the bus without collisions using a non-destructive bitwise arbitration scheme based on message ID priority.
4. **Industry-wide adoption** — OEMs standardize on CAN, reducing ECU development cost and tooling cost.
5. **Legal mandate** — Post-2008 ISO 15765-4 is required by regulation worldwide.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in OBD questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `P` codes with `U` codes** — `P` = powertrain, `U` = network. Writing "engine problem" for a `U0100` is worth **0 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the second character** — `P0xxx` = generic (worth full marks). `P1xxx` = manufacturer-specific (must be qualified with the OEM). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you skip this.
> 3. **Skipping the two-trip logic** — If a 14-mark question says "explain MIL activation," write the **pending → confirmed** sequence. Simply saying "MIL turns on" loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the DLC pin numbers** — A sketch without **Pin 4 (chassis GND), Pin 5 (signal GND), Pin 16 (+12 V), and Pins 6/14 (CAN H/L)** is incomplete.
> 5. **Forgetting freeze frame** — A common 3-mark Q is *"What is freeze frame data?"* — answer: "A snapshot of all critical sensor values captured at the exact moment a DTC was set, used by technicians for forensic diagnosis." Do not write "a frozen picture."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read this section 5 times the night before the exam:**

- [ ] OBD-II = 1996 (US) / 2001 (EU as EOBD). OBD-III = remote/wireless emissions reporting (emerging).
- [ ] **DLC** = Data Link Connector, 16-pin, SAE **J1962** standard, female trapezoidal, under dashboard driver side.
- [ ] **Mandatory DLC pins:** 4 (chassis GND), 5 (signal GND), 16 (+12 V), 6 (CAN-H), 14 (CAN-L).
- [ ] **DTC format:** 5 characters — System (P/B/C/U), Code type (0/1), Subsystem (1–8), Specific fault (00–99).
- [ ] **MIL = Malfunction Indicator Lamp**, amber, "Check Engine" light. Two-trip logic to illuminate, **flashes** for severe misfire ($\geq 1\%$ rate).
- [ ] **Freeze frame** = snapshot of sensor data at the moment the DTC was set. Stored in KAM. Read via Mode 02.
- [ ] **KAM** = Keep-Alive Memory — RAM with battery backup; retains DTCs and freeze frame even when ignition is OFF.
- [ ] **OBD-II Service Modes** = 10 standardized modes (01–0A). Mode 03 = stored DTCs; Mode 04 = clear; Mode 06 = test results; Mode 0A = permanent.
- [ ] **Five OBD-II protocols:** SAE J1850 VPW/PWM, ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000), **ISO 15765-4 (CAN)**.
- [ ] **CAN bus** = 2-wire differential, 120 Ω terminating resistors at each end, 500 kbps high-speed, multi-master with non-destructive arbitration.
- [ ] **Decoding must-know DTCs:** P0301 (Cyl 1 misfire), P0171 (Bank 1 lean), P0420 (Catalyst low eff), P0440 (EVAP), U0100 (lost comm with ECM).
- [ ] **Catalyst efficiency equation:** $\eta_{\text{cat}} = 1 - A_{\text{post}}/A_{\text{pre}}$; threshold typically $0.5$.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric AFR** = 14.7:1 (gasoline); $\lambda = 1$.
- [ ] **Permanent DTCs (Mode 0A)** cannot be cleared by a scan tool — only by the ECU itself after 40+ warm-up cycles without the fault.
- [ ] **OBD-II does NOT monitor tires, brakes for wear, airbags, or battery health** — it focuses on **emissions-related** components per CARB/EPA regulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems Components for Electronic Engine Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems Components for Electronic Engine Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Open Loop System — Formal Definition

An **Open Loop System (OLS)** is a control architecture in which the control action is entirely independent of the desired output. The system receives a pre-programmed reference input and executes the control action without measuring, comparing, or correcting the resulting output. There is **no feedback path** from the output back to the controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *An open loop system is one in which the control action has no relationship with the output. The accuracy of the system depends solely on the precision of the calibration of the controller, and the system cannot self-correct for disturbances.*

In the context of an **Electronic Engine Management System (EEMS)**, an open loop fuel injection strategy delivers a pre-computed quantity of fuel based purely on:
- **Throttle Position (TPS)**
- **Engine Speed (RPM)**
- **Coolant Temperature (CTS)**
- **Manifold Air Pressure (MAP)**

…**without** using the signal from the **Exhaust Gas Oxygen (EGO/HO2S) sensor** to trim the fuel delivery.

## 1.2 Closed Loop System — Formal Definition

A **Closed Loop System (CLS)** is a control architecture in which the control action is continuously modulated by **negative feedback** derived from a measurement of the actual output. The system uses the difference (error) between the **desired reference** and the **measured output** to drive a corrective action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed Loop Fuel Control:** The ECU reads the signal from the upstream O2 sensor (narrowband λ = 1.0 ± 0.02), calculates the deviation from stoichiometry, and modulates the injector pulse-width via an integral (or PID) controller to maintain the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** at exactly **14.7:1** (for gasoline engines) under all operating conditions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 🧠 The Cruise Control Analogy
Imagine you are driving a Maruti Suzuki Swift on NH-66:

- **Open Loop Mode:** You press the accelerator pedal and **lock your foot position** at a fixed angle. If the road inclines uphill, the car slows down — but you do **not** adjust the pedal. The car is "blind" to the speed drop.
- **Closed Loop Mode (Cruise Control):** You engage cruise control at **80 km/h**. The system continuously measures actual vehicle speed via the wheel-speed sensor. If a hill causes the car to slow to 75 km/h, the ECU commands the throttle body to open further until 80 km/h is restored. The system "sees" the error and **auto-corrects** it.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** *Open Loop = "Fire and Forget" | Closed Loop = "Measure, Compare, Correct"*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Relevance |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline) | **14.7 : 1** | Closed loop target |
| Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel) | **14.5 : 1** | Theoretical reference |
| Lambda (λ) at Stoichiometry | **1.000** | Narrowband O2 sensor switch point |
| Lambda — Rich | **λ < 1.000** | Excess fuel, HC + CO emissions |
| Lambda — Lean | **λ > 1.000** | Excess air, NOx emissions up |
| O2 Sensor Output (Rich) | **~ 0.8 – 0.9 V** | Below 0.45 V threshold |
| O2 Sensor Output (Lean) | **~ 0.1 – 0.2 V** | Below 0.45 V threshold |
| Engine Operating Speed Range | **700 – 7000 RPM** | Idle to redline |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Open Loop EEMS

**Signal Flow Chain:**

$$
\text{Driver Input} \rightarrow \text{ECU (Look-up Table)} \rightarrow \text{Injector Driver} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Injector} \rightarrow \text{Engine Output}
$$

**Operational Steps:**
1. Driver presses accelerator → TPS sends a **0–5 V analog signal** to the ECU.
2. ECU also reads **engine coolant temperature (NTC thermistor)**, **intake air temperature**, **RPM (crank position sensor)**, and **MAP** (or **MAF** in speed-density variants).
3. The ECU executes a **3-D look-up table** stored in EPROM/Flash memory — indexing fuel pulse width (in milliseconds) based on (RPM × Load).
4. The injector driver circuit energizes the solenoid for the **precomputed pulse width**.
5. **No correction is applied** for component aging, altitude, barometric pressure changes, fuel quality, or exhaust gas composition.

**Disadvantages of Open Loop:**
- Cannot compensate for **injector drift** (clogging, leakage)
- No compensation for **altitude / barometric pressure**
- **Cold start** and **warm-up enrichment** must be statically mapped
- **Higher exhaust emissions** — fails Euro 6 / BS-VI norms

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Closed Loop EEMS

**Signal Flow Chain with Negative Feedback:**

$$
\text{Ref} \rightarrow \oplus \rightarrow \text{ECU Controller} \rightarrow \text{Injector} \rightarrow \text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{O2 Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Ref}
$$

The summing junction computes the error:

$$
e(t) = \lambda_{\text{ref}}(t) - \lambda_{\text{meas}}(t)
$$

**Operational Steps:**
1. After **cold start** and **warm-up** (coolant > 70 °C), the ECU enters **closed loop mode**.
2. The **narrowband O2 sensor** generates a voltage oscillating between **0.1 V (lean)** and **0.9 V (rich)** around stoichiometry.
3. The ECU implements an **integral-only controller** (standard in production ECUs) using the equation:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{inj}}(n) = K_I \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) \cdot \Delta t_{\text{samp}}
$$

4. The **long-term fuel trim (LTFT)** and **short-term fuel trim (STFT)** are continuously updated in **non-volatile RAM (KAM)**.

**LTFT Range:** **−25 % to +25 %** (clamps at limit)
**STFT Range:** **−25 % to +25 %** (rapidly fluctuating)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every KTU-relevant formula. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error signal: reference minus measured output | dimensionless / V |
| 2 | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Open-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback $H(s)=1$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | Simplified unity-feedback closed-loop TF | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Definition of equivalence ratio (Lambda) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich,gasoline}} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (gasoline) | kg air / kg fuel |
| 7 | $t_{\text{inj}} = t_{\text{base}} \cdot (1 + STFT + LTFT)$ | Effective injector pulse-width command | ms |
| 8 | $S_{\text{steady}} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Steady-state error | — |
| 9 | $S_{\text{steady,unity}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | Steady-state error for step input (Type-0 system) | — |
| 10 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Natural frequency of a rotational actuator | rad/s |
| 11 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | Damping ratio of a 2nd-order throttle-body plant | dimensionless |
| 12 | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Maximum overshoot (2nd-order) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Settling time (2 % criterion) | s |
| 14 | $f_{\text{switch}} = \dfrac{v \cdot N_c}{120}$ | O2 sensor switching frequency at speed $v$ | Hz |
| 15 | $P_{\text{out}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{HV}$ | Indicated power from fuel mass flow | kW |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Emissions Compliance (BS-VI / Euro 6):** Three-way catalytic converters (TWC) require the AFR to remain within **±1 % of stoichiometry** to simultaneously reduce HC, CO, and NOx. This precision is **impossible** with open loop.
- **OBD-II / OBD-Bharat:** The diagnostic protocol mandates **monitoring** the O2 sensor response time. A slow O2 sensor (> 100 ms) trips **P0133** and forces a check-engine light.
- **Drive-by-Wire Throttle:** Even the electronic throttle body is itself a **closed-loop servo** controlled by dual redundant TPS sensors feeding a PID controller running at **1 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Open-Loop Plant to Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Given:** A first-order engine dynamics model with fuel-pulse-width $u(t)$ as input and AFR $y(t)$ as output.

**Plant (Engine) Equation:**

$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot u(t)
$$

where $\tau$ is the engine time constant (combustion + transport delay) and $K$ is the static gain from injector pulse-width to AFR deviation.

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform:**

$$
\tau s \cdot Y(s) + Y(s) = K \cdot U(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the Transfer Function $G(s)$:**

$$
Y(s) (\tau s + 1) = K \cdot U(s)
$$

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

This is the **open-loop transfer function** of the engine plant.

**Step 3 — Introduce Negative Feedback.** Let $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback through the O2 sensor calibrated to stoichiometry). The closed-loop TF is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $G(s)$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau s + 1)$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1 + K}
$$

**Step 6 — Normalize to monic denominator form:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{K}{K + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\tau}{K+1}\right) s + 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Closing the loop reduces the **effective time constant** from $\tau$ to $\tau / (K+1)$. The system becomes **faster** by a factor of $(K+1)$, and the steady-state gain becomes $K / (K+1)$ — slightly attenuated but tightly regulated.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error for a Step Disturbance

A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"What is the steady-state error of an open-loop vs closed-loop fuel controller when barometric pressure changes?"*

**Disturbance model:** Let the disturbance $D(s)$ (e.g., barometric change) act on the output.

**Open-loop response to $D(s)$:**

$$
Y(s) = G(s) U(s) + G_d(s) D(s)
$$

There is no compensation for $D(s)$, so steady-state error:

$$
e_{\text{ss,OL}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot [-G_d(s) D(s)]
$$

For a step disturbance $D(s) = D_0 / s$:

$$
e_{\text{ss,OL}} = G_d(0) \cdot D_0 \quad (\text{NON-ZERO, depends on } G_d)
$$

**Closed-loop response to $D(s)$:**

$$
Y(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} \left[ R(s) - H(s) D(s) \cdot G_d(s)/G(s) \right]
$$

For unity $H(s)$ and $G_d(s) = G(s)$:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} D(s) \cdot G(s)
$$

**Steady-state error for step $D(s) = D_0 / s$:**

$$
e_{\text{ss,CL}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} \cdot \frac{D_0}{s} = \frac{G(0)}{1+G(0)} D_0
$$

As controller gain $K_p \to \infty$, $e_{\text{ss,CL}} \to D_0$ — the disturbance is **fully rejected** when using **integral action** (Type-1 system).

> [!TIP]
> This is precisely why production ECUs use **I-control** rather than pure P-control for fuel trim — integral action drives the steady-state error to **zero**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Open vs Closed)

| Criterion | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | **Low** — depends on calibration | **High** — self-correcting |
| Stability | **Inherently stable** | Requires careful design (Nyquist / Bode) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by map density | Limited by O2 sensor dynamics |
| Cost | **Lower** (no O2 sensor) | **Higher** (O2 + wideband sensor) |
| Emissions | High HC, CO, NOx | Meets BS-VI / Euro 6 |
| Sensitivity to disturbance | **High** | **Low** |
| Transient response | Fast (no feedback lag) | Slower (~ 100–300 ms O2 delay) |
| Diagnostic capability | Poor | Excellent (OBD-II ready) |
| Typical application | Carburettors, old TBI | All modern MPFI / GDI systems |
| Compensation for altitude | Manual (altitude map) | Automatic via O2 feedback |
| Cold-start operation | Suitable | NOT used (open loop during warm-up) |
| Full-load enrichment | Static map | Static map + WOT fueling (open loop overrides) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open-Loop Electronic Engine Management — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    driver[Driver Pedal] --> tps[TPS Sensor]
    cts[Coolant Temp NTC] --> ecuA[ECU Lookup Table]
    map[MAP Sensor] --> ecuA
    rpm[RPM Crank Sensor] --> ecuA
    tps --> ecuA
    ecuA --> driverA[Injector Driver]
    driverA --> inj[Fuel Injector]
    inj --> engine[Engine Combustion]
    engine --> exhaustA[Exhaust Gas]
    exhaustA -.->|NO FEEDBACK| ecuA
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Electronic Engine Management — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    ref[Stoich Reference lambda=1] --> sumJ[Sum Junction]
    sumJ --> ecuC[ECU Integral Controller]
    ecuC --> injDrv[Injector Driver]
    injDrv --> injector[Fuel Injector]
    injector --> engineC[Engine Combustion]
    engineC --> exh[Exhaust Manifold]
    exh --> o2[Narrowband O2 Sensor]
    o2 -->|Voltage 0.1V to 0.9V| inv[Signal Conditioner]
    inv --> sumJ
    engineC --> cat[Three Way Catalyst]
    cat --> tailpipe[Tailpipe]
    o2 -->|Fault P0133| obd[OBD II Diagnostics]
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction — Electronic Engine Management System (EEMS)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        tpsA[TPS]
        ctsA[CTS NTC]
        mapaA[MAP / MAF]

    subgraph CTRL["ECU CONTROLLER"]

    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]

    end

    end

    ckpA --> adc

    cpusig --> egrA

```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to draw the "EEMS block diagram," always show **both** the input sensors and the **feedback path** explicitly. Marks are awarded for the feedback loop arrow.

## 4.4 O2 Sensor Switching Waveform (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    time[Time axis] --> rich[Rich region Voltage approx 0.8V]
    time --> lean[Lean region Voltage approx 0.1V]
    rich --> switch[Switching at 0.45V threshold]
    lean --> switch
    switch --> osc[Oscillates around 0.45V]
    osc --> stoich[Stoich lambda equals 1.0 maintained]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]
**Differentiate between an open-loop and closed-loop electronic engine management system. State two situations where open-loop operation is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (O2 sensor) |
| Accuracy | Calibration-dependent | Self-correcting |
| Emissions | High | Low (BS-VI compliant) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Two situations where open-loop operation is preferred:**
1. **Cold start / warm-up phase** — O2 sensor is below 350 °C and produces no useful signal.
2. **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) / full-load enrichment** — A rich mixture is intentionally commanded for maximum power; lambda targeting is suspended.
3. **Deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO)** — During trailing-throttle over-run, injectors are simply switched off (open-loop command).

*(Any two — 1.5 marks each)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]
**List the major input sensors of an Electronic Engine Management System and state the physical quantity each one measures.**

**Model Answer (any 6 expected):**

| Sensor | Physical Quantity |
|---|---|
| TPS (Throttle Position Sensor) | Throttle plate angle (°) |
| MAF (Mass Air Flow) | Air mass per second (kg/s) |
| MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) | Manifold pressure (kPa) |
| CTS (Coolant Temperature Sensor) | Coolant temperature (°C) |
| IAT (Intake Air Temperature) | Intake air temperature (°C) |
| CKP (Crankshaft Position) | Engine RPM and piston TDC |
| CMP (Camshaft Position) | Camshaft angle / phase |
| O2 / HO2S | Lambda / exhaust O2 partial pressure |
| Knock Sensor | Engine block vibration (kHz) |
| Vehicle Speed Sensor | Wheel speed (km/h) |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU mandates that students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks). Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop electronic fuel control system** using a narrowband oxygen sensor. Derive the expression for the closed-loop transfer function assuming a first-order engine plant.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the terms **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** and **Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)**. State their typical ranges, update rates, and storage location in the ECU memory.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Derivation:**

Refer to the closed-loop block diagram in **Section 4.2**.

**Engine plant (first-order):**
$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K u(t)
$$

**Laplace transform:**
$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

**Closed-loop with unity feedback $H(s)=1$:**
$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1 + K}
$$

**Normalizing:**
$$
T(s) = \frac{K}{K+1} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\tau}{K+1}\right) s + 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** The closed-loop system has:
- New effective gain: $K / (K+1)$
- New effective time constant: $\tau / (K+1)$ — **faster response**
- Steady-state error to step: $1 / (1 + K)$ — **smaller error**

The ECU acts as an **integral controller**:
$$
\Delta t_{\text{inj}}(t) = K_I \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau
$$
where $e(t) = \lambda_{\text{ref}} - \lambda_{\text{meas}}$.

The O2 sensor produces a **switching waveform** between 0.1 V (lean) and 0.9 V (rich), crossing 0.45 V at λ = 1.000. The ECU counts zero-crossings and adjusts injector pulse width every **10–100 ms**.

**Part (b) — STFT vs LTFT:**

| Parameter | STFT | LTFT |
|---|---|---|
| Range | **−25 % to +25 %** | **−25 % to +25 %** |
| Update rate | **Every 10–100 ms** (real-time) | **Slow** (~ 1 Hz or per-trip) |
| Storage | Volatile RAM (cleared at key-off) | **KAM (Keep-Alive Memory)** — non-volatile |
| Purpose | Immediate correction for transient errors | Learn permanent offset (e.g., injector aging) |
| Diagnostic flag | If |STFT| > 25 % for > 10 s | If |LTFT| > 25 % persistent |

**STFT** handles **transient** disturbances (e.g., sudden throttle change). **LTFT** compensates for **permanent drifts** (e.g., clogged injector, vacuum leak). When STFT saturates, its value is **transferred to LTFT** to recenter STFT at 0 %.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **components of an Electronic Engine Management System (EEMS)** with a block diagram, classifying them into Input, Control, and Output subsystems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare open-loop and closed-loop fuel control strategies with respect to: (i) emissions performance, (ii) transient response, (iii) cold-start behavior, and (iv) diagnostic capability.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — EEMS Components:**

Refer to the comprehensive block diagram in **Section 4.3**.

**1. Input Subsystem (Sensors):**
- **TPS** — Hall-effect or potentiometric
- **MAP / MAF** — piezoresistive / hot-film
- **CKP / CMP** — variable reluctance or Hall
- **CTS / IAT** — NTC thermistor (resistance decreases with temperature)
- **O2 / HO2S** — Nernst cell (narrowband) or pumped-cell (wideband)
- **Knock sensor** — piezoelectric accelerometer (5–15 kHz band)

**2. Control Subsystem (ECU):**
- 32-bit microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix, NXP MPC5xxx)
- ADC front-end (10–12 bit, multiplexed)
- EEPROM / Flash for calibration maps
- **KAM (Keep-Alive RAM)** for adaptive learns
- CAN / LIN bus for inter-ECU communication

**3. Output Subsystem (Actuators):**
- **Fuel injector drivers** — peak-and-hold current (peak ~ 8 A, hold ~ 2 A)
- **Ignition coil drivers** — IGBT-based, dwell-time controlled
- **EGR valve** — stepper motor or PWM solenoid
- **Variable valve timing (VVT) solenoids** — PWM at 100–300 Hz
- **Fuel pump relay / control module** — PWM for variable pressure
- **MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp)** — DTC indicator

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Emissions** | Poor — HC, CO, NOx exceed limits | Excellent — λ held at 1.000 ± 0.02; TWC efficient |
| **(ii) Transient response** | Fast — no feedback delay; precomputed map is instantaneous | Slower — limited by O2 sensor response (~ 100 ms) and transport delay |
| **(iii) Cold-start** | Suitable — O2 sensor cold/inactive; uses cranking & warm-up maps | NOT used below ~ 70 °C coolant |
| **(iv) Diagnostics** | Poor — no self-check on combustion quality | Excellent — STFT/LTFT monitoring, O2 sensor response check, catalyst efficiency test |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing MAP vs MAF** — MAP systems are "speed-density" (use RPM × MAP); MAF systems measure actual air mass. Mixing them in derivations costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of feedback** — Closed loop uses **negative** feedback. Drawing a positive feedback arrow will be penalized.
> 3. **Omitting the O2 sensor cold-start caveat** — Closed loop does NOT work below 350 °C sensor temperature. Stating "the ECU always runs in closed loop" is **factually wrong** and loses marks.
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value in tables** — Causes markdown parsing errors; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 5. **Drawing the O2 sensor as a "control input" instead of feedback** — It is on the **output side** of the loop. Marks specifically allocated for the **feedback arrow** will be lost.
> 6. **STFT / LTFT confusion** — STFT is **fast & volatile**; LTFT is **slow & persistent** in KAM. Swapping them is a classic error.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Open Loop** = No feedback; output is **blind** to actual result. Used in **carburettors**, **cold start**, **WOT**, and **DFCO**.
- 🔹 **Closed Loop** = O2 sensor feedback maintains **λ = 1.000** (AFR = **14.7:1** for gasoline).
- 🔹 **Open-loop transfer function** of a 1st-order plant: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$.
- 🔹 **Closed-loop transfer function** with unity feedback: $T(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1 + K}$.
- 🔹 Closing the loop **reduces the time constant by a factor of (1 + K)** → faster response.
- 🔹 Closing the loop **reduces steady-state error** to $1/(1+K_p)$ for a Type-0 step response.
- 🔹 **Integral control** drives the steady-state error to **zero** for constant disturbances.
- 🔹 **Narrowband O2 sensor** switches between **0.1 V (lean)** and **0.9 V (rich)** at 0.45 V.
- 🔹 **STFT range: ±25 %**, volatile; **LTFT range: ±25 %**, stored in **KAM**.
- 🔹 **OBD-II / OBD-Bharat** monitors O2 sensor response time; slow O2 trips **P0133**.
- 🔹 **Closed loop is disabled** during: cold start, warm-up, WOT, DFCO, and during explicit catalyst purge strategies.
- 🔹 **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** requires λ within **±1 %** of stoich to reduce HC, CO, and NOx simultaneously.
- 🔹 **EEMS** consists of three subsystems: **Input (sensors)**, **Control (ECU)**, **Output (actuators)**, with an **O2-sensor feedback path** wrapping the loop.
- 🔹 **Drive-by-Wire (DBW)** throttle is itself a **closed-loop servo** with **dual TPS** and a **1 kHz PID** controller.
- 🔹 **Wideband O2 sensors** (Bosch LSU 4.9) extend closed loop across the **full operating envelope** (0.5 < λ < ∞), unlike narrowband (0.97 < λ < 1.03).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Motion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Motion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

**Vehicle Motion Control (VMC)** refers to the integrated suite of electronic, electro-mechanical, and mechatronic sub-systems that actively regulate the longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics of an automobile in order to enhance **safety**, **stability**, **comfort**, and **driver convenience**. According to the KTU 2024 scheme framing of automobile instrumentation, VMC is positioned at the convergence of sensor networks, signal conditioning, embedded controllers, and high-bandwidth actuators.

In engineering taxonomy, VMC is a **Closed-Loop Feedback Control Problem** where the Controlled Variables are vehicle velocity, wheel slip, yaw rate, and pitch, while the Manipulated Variables are engine torque, brake torque, and steering angle. The standard ISO 26262 framework classifies VMC functions as ASIL-C or ASIL-D safety systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Vehicle Motion Control in Module 1 specifically focuses on **Cruise Control, Electronic Throttle Control, Antilock Braking Systems (ABS), Traction Control System (TCS), and Electronic Stability Program (ESP/ESC)** and the instrumentation that enables them.

## Conceptual Analogy — The Charioteer and the Reins

Imagine a horse-drawn chariot with a skilled charioteer. The charioteer's hands hold the reins (actuators), his eyes track the road and surroundings (sensors), and his brain (ECU) continuously decides how hard to pull. If the horse stumbles on a wet stone, he instantly loosens the rein; if the road is straight and free, he lets the horse run at a steady pace. **Vehicle Motion Control is exactly this — except the horse is the powertrain, the reins are the brake/throttle actuators, the eyes are wheel-speed, IMU, and steering-angle sensors, and the brain is the Engine Control Unit (ECU) or Vehicle Domain Controller.**

## The Four Pillars of VMC

| Pillar | Primary Controlled Variable | Key Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Cruise / Adaptive Cruise | Longitudinal velocity | Electronic Throttle |
| Antilock Braking (ABS) | Wheel slip ratio (0.1–0.3) | Hydraulic Brake Modulator |
| Traction Control (TCS) | Driven-wheel slip on accel. | Throttle + Brake Intervention |
| Stability Control (ESC) | Yaw rate vs. steer demand | Selective Braking + Throttle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - Slip ratio optimum for maximum friction coefficient ($\lambda$): **0.10 – 0.30** (peak at ~0.15)
> - Wheel deceleration threshold for ABS intervention: **≈ 1.0 g** (≈ 9.81 m/s²)
> - Yaw-rate sensor bandwidth: **≥ 30 Hz**
> - Standard braking deceleration: **0.8 g** on dry asphalt
> - Stopping distance at 100 km/h (dry): **~40 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy — The Closed-Loop Control Paradigm

Every VMC sub-system follows the canonical **Sense → Compute → Actuate** triad:

1. **Sense Layer:** Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS — inductive or Hall-effect), Steering Angle Sensor (SAS), Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU with accelerometer + gyroscope), Yaw-Rate Sensor, Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Brake Pressure Sensor.
2. **Compute Layer:** A real-time microcontroller (e.g., Infineon AURIX TC3xx) running a deterministic control law at a fixed sampling period $T_s$ (typically **5–10 ms** for ABS, **10–20 ms** for ESC).
3. **Actuate Layer:** Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) with DC motor, Hydraulic Modulator with solenoid valves, Electric Parking Brake (EPB) motor.

## 2.2 The Slip-Ratio Equation — The Heart of ABS/TCS

When a wheel rotates with angular velocity $\omega_w$ under a vehicle of forward speed $V$, the **slip ratio** $\lambda$ is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{V - \omega_w r}{\max(V,\ \omega_w r)}$$

where $r$ is the effective rolling radius of the tire. The convention is:
- **Braking slip** (ABS regime): $V > \omega_w r$ → $\lambda > 0$
- **Traction slip** (TCS regime): $\omega_w r > V$ → $\lambda < 0$ (or signed negative)
- **Free rolling**: $\lambda = 0$

The friction coefficient between tire and road $\mu(\lambda)$ is **non-linear** and exhibits a peak near $\lambda \approx 0.15$. ABS operates by regulating brake pressure to keep $\lambda$ on the **rising flank**, just before the peak.

## 2.3 PID Control in Cruise Control

Classical cruise control uses a **Proportional–Integral (PI)** controller (derivative is usually avoided to prevent amplification of sensor noise):

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

$$e(t) = V_{set} - V_{actual}$$

The integral term eliminates steady-state error on flat roads; on grades, the controller must compensate for the gravitational disturbance $m g \sin\theta$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = (V - \omega r)/\max(V,\omega r)$ | dimensionless | Wheel slip ratio | ABS, TCS |
| 2 | $F_x = \mu(\lambda)\cdot F_z$ | N | Longitudinal tire force | Tire-road interaction |
| 3 | $F_z = mg/4$ (static, per wheel) | N | Vertical load per wheel | Braking balance |
| 4 | $a_{max} = \mu g$ | m/s² | Max. achievable decel. | Stopping distance |
| 5 | $s_{stop} = V_0^2/(2\mu g)$ | m | Stopping distance | ABS verification |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt$ | %throttle | PI control law | Cruise control |
| 7 | $\omega_{ref} = V_{set}/r$ | rad/s | Target wheel speed | Speed governor |
| 8 | $\dot{\psi}_{des} = V\cdot \delta/L$ | rad/s | Reference yaw rate (bicycle model) | ESC target |
| 9 | $K_{understeer} = (W_f/C_{af}) - (W_r/C_{ar})$ | rad/g | Understeer gradient | Vehicle dynamics |
| 10 | $\tau_{brake} = J_w \dot{\omega}_w + F_b r$ | N·m | Wheel rotational dynamics | ABS modulator |
| 11 | $J\dot{\omega} = T_{eng} - T_{brake} - T_{load}$ | N·m | Driveline dynamics | TCS torque control |
| 12 | $T_{sample} \le 10$ ms | s | Real-time deadline | Embedded scheduling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations collectively form the foundation of model-based controller design. In modern VMC, **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** uses a discretized version of the bicycle model (eq. 8) to predict yaw behaviour 0.5–1.0 s ahead and pre-emptively brake individual wheels.

## 2.5 The Bicycle Model (Lateral Dynamics)

For yaw-rate control in ESC, the vehicle is reduced to a two-wheel "bicycle" with mass $m$, yaw inertia $I_z$, front-to-CG distance $a$, and CG-to-rear distance $b$:

$$m V (\dot{\beta} + r) = C_{af}\cdot \alpha_f + C_{ar}\cdot \alpha_r$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = a\cdot C_{af}\cdot \alpha_f - b\cdot C_{ar}\cdot \alpha_r$$

where $\beta$ is sideslip angle, $r$ is yaw rate, $C_{af}$, $C_{ar}$ are cornering stiffnesses, and $\alpha_f$, $\alpha_r$ are slip angles of front and rear tires. This linear model is valid for $ \vert \alpha \vert < 4°$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Distance with ABS-Optimal Braking

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg travels at $V_0 = 100$ km/h on dry asphalt ($\mu_{peak} = 0.9$). Compute the theoretical minimum stopping distance under optimal ABS regulation, and compare with a non-ABS locked-wheel braking ($\mu_{slide} = 0.7$).

### Step 1 — Unit Conversion

The vehicle speed is given in km/h, which must be converted to SI units (m/s) for dimensional consistency:

$$V_0 = 100\ \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000\ \text{m}}{1\ \text{km}} \times \frac{1\ \text{h}}{3600\ \text{s}}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{100\,000}{3600}\ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$$

$$V_0 = 27.778\ \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Optimal Deceleration under ABS

ABS modulates brake pressure to maintain slip ratio $\lambda^*$ at which $\mu$ is maximum. By Newton's Second Law on a flat road:

$$F_{brake,\ max} = \mu_{peak}\cdot m g$$

$$m a_{opt} = \mu_{peak}\cdot m g$$

Solving for the optimal deceleration by cancelling $m$ from both sides:

$$a_{opt} = \mu_{peak}\cdot g = 0.9 \times 9.81\ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}$$

$$a_{opt} = 8.829\ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}$$

### Step 3 — Kinematic Integration

Using the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 a s$ with $v_f = 0$ (vehicle stops):

$$0 = V_0^2 + 2(-a_{opt})\cdot s_{ABS}$$

Rearranging by isolating $s_{ABS}$:

$$s_{ABS} = \frac{V_0^2}{2 a_{opt}}$$

$$s_{ABS} = \frac{(27.778)^2}{2 \times 8.829}$$

$$s_{ABS} = \frac{771.62}{17.658}$$

$$s_{ABS} = 43.70\ \text{m}$$

### Step 4 — Locked-Wheel Comparison

For non-ABS locked-wheel braking, the friction coefficient drops to the sliding value:

$$a_{slide} = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867\ \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}$$

$$s_{slide} = \frac{V_0^2}{2 a_{slide}} = \frac{771.62}{13.734}$$

$$s_{slide} = 56.18\ \text{m}$$

### Step 5 — Net Benefit of ABS

$$\Delta s = s_{slide} - s_{ABS} = 56.18 - 43.70 = 12.48\ \text{m}$$

ABS thus saves approximately **12.5 m** at 100 km/h — a 22.2 % reduction. This justifies the regulatory mandate of ABS under ECE R13-H and Indian AIS-104.

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip-Ratio Control Law for ABS

Let $\lambda_{target} = 0.15$ (peak friction point). The ABS controller uses a **bang-bang with hysteresis** strategy around this reference:

$$\Delta P_{brake} = \begin{cases} +P_{inc}, & \lambda < \lambda_{target} - \delta \\ 0, & \vert \lambda - \lambda_{target}\vert \le \delta \\ -P_{dec}, & \lambda > \lambda_{target} + \delta \end{cases}$$

where $\delta \approx 0.05$ is the hysteresis band to prevent solenoid chattering. The modulator cycles at **5–15 Hz**, producing the characteristic "pulsed" pedal feel.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — VMC Sub-System Comparison

| Parameter | Cruise Control | ABS | TCS | ESC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling $T_s$ | 50 ms | 5–10 ms | 10–20 ms | 10–20 ms |
| Primary sensor | VSS | WSS (4×) | WSS + TPS | WSS, SAS, IMU |
| Actuator | ETB | Hyd. modulator | ETB + Brake | Selective brake (4×) |
| Control law | PI | Bang-bang/hyst. | Slip-clamp | Yaw MPC |
| Failure class (ISO 26262) | QM/B | ASIL-D | ASIL-C | ASIL-D |
| Net benefit | Comfort | $\Delta s \approx$12 m/100 km/h | Traction on $\mu$-split | Roll-over prevention |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level VMC Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        s1["WSS1 Front Left"]
        s2["WSS2 Front Right"]
        s3["WSS3 Rear Left"]
        s4["WSS4 Rear Right"]
        s5["SAS Steering Angle"]
        s6["IMU Yaw and Lateral G"]

    end

    subgraph ECU["ECU COMPUTE LAYER"]
        c1["Signal Conditioning"]
        c2["State Estimator Vehicle Speed Yaw Slip"]

    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        a1["Electronic Throttle Body"]

    subgraph VEH["VEHICLE DYNAMICS PLANT"]
        v1["Tire Road Interaction"]

    end

    s1 --> c1
    s2 --> c1
    s3 --> c1

    s6 --> c1
```
