<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three sensors used in the basic sensor arrangement of a modern Engine Management System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** — Detects rotational speed (RPM) and piston position of the crankshaft. It uses either inductive (VR) or Hall-effect principle and provides a frequency signal proportional to engine speed. *(1 mark)*
2. **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF)** — Measures the mass of air entering the engine using a hot-wire or hot-film element. Its output is an analog voltage (0–5 V) directly proportional to air mass. *(1 mark)*
3. **Lambda / Oxygen Sensor (HO2S / UEGO)** — Senses residual oxygen in the exhaust to compute the air–fuel ratio (λ). Provides feedback to the ECU for closed-loop fuel control. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Self-generating; does **not** need external excitation. | Needs external excitation (voltage/current) from ECU. |
| Output | Voltage or charge directly | Resistance / reactance change |
| Example | Piezoelectric knock sensor | NTC thermistor (ECT, IAT) |

*(1 mark for definition of active + example, 1 mark for passive + example, 1 mark for the tabulated comparison.)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the basic sensor arrangement of an Engine Management System. Classify the sensors into three functional groups and give two examples for each group. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block diagram:** Refer to *Section 4.1* of these notes for the standard KTU architecture. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Three functional groups:**
1. **Powertrain Position Sensors** — CKP, CMP *(1 mark)*
2. **Air-Intake and Charge Sensors** — MAF, MAP, IAT, TPS *(1 mark)*
3. **Feedback and Condition Sensors** — UEGO, ECT, Knock sensor *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Examples with one-line functions** *(2 marks)*:
- CKP → engine speed + crank angle
- MAF → mass of intake air
- UEGO → closed-loop fuel trim

**(b)** An NTC thermistor used as an Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor has $R_0 = 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298$ K and $\beta = 3435$ K. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_{ref}=2.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{ref}=5$ V. Compute the output voltage when the coolant temperature is $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin. *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 80 + 273 = 353\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the β-equation. *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_T &= R_0\,e^{\,\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)} \\
    &= 2500 \times e^{\,3435 \times \left(\frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{298}\right)} \\
    &= 2500 \times e^{\,3435 \times (-5.222 \times 10^{-4})} \\
    &= 2500 \times e^{-1.794} \\
    &= 2500 \times 0.1664 \\
    &= 416.0\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the voltage divider. *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{ref}\,\frac{R_T}{R_{ref}+R_T} \\
        &= 5 \times \frac{416}{2500 + 416} \\
        &= 5 \times \frac{416}{2916} \\
        &= 5 \times 0.1427 \\
        &= \mathbf{0.714\ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Concluding sentence. *(2 marks)*
> At $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ the ECT sensor delivers **0.714 V** to the ECU. This falls within the expected hot-engine voltage band (0.3 – 1.0 V), confirming a healthy sensor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Explain with a functional diagram how a Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) of the **inductive (variable reluctance)** type works. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction:** A permanent magnet, soft-iron pole piece, and a pickup coil of N turns are mounted opposite a ferromagnetic trigger wheel with $n_{teeth}$ teeth (e.g., 60–2). *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Working:** As each tooth approaches the pole piece, magnetic flux through the coil increases; as it moves away, flux collapses. By Faraday's law, a sinusoidal EMF is induced: *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ind}(t) &= -N\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency relation:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{CKP} &= n_{teeth}\cdot\frac{N_{RPM}}{60}\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Concluding remark:** *(1 mark)*
> The ECU's zero-crossing detector converts this AC signal into a digital square wave used to compute engine RPM and crank-angle for ignition/injection timing.

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine runs at 2500 RPM with a 60–2 tooth CKP trigger wheel. Calculate the CKP frequency, the time interval between two successive teeth, and the peak induced EMF if $N=1500$ turns, $\Phi_{max}=1.5\times 10^{-6}$ Wb. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Effective teeth $n_{teeth} = 60 - 2 = 58$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** CKP frequency: *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{CKP} &= 58 \times \frac{2500}{60} \\
        &= 2416.67\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Time between teeth: *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= \frac{1}{f_{CKP}} = \frac{1}{2416.67} \\
         &= 413.8\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Peak EMF: *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f_{CKP} = 2\pi \times 2416.67 = 15{,}185.5\ \text{rad/s} \\
V_{peak} &= N\cdot\Phi_{max}\cdot\omega \\
         &= 1500 \times 1.5\times 10^{-6} \times 15{,}185.5 \\
         &= \mathbf{34.17\ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion from °C to K** in the thermistor β-equation — this alone can cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using 60 teeth instead of 58** for a 60–2 trigger wheel — the missing two teeth are the *reference gap*, not active teeth.
> 3. **Skipping the concluding engineering interpretation** — KTU examiners expect a sentence such as *"This voltage falls in the normal operating band of 0.3 – 4.7 V, confirming a healthy sensor."* Without it, up to **1 mark** is lost.
> 4. **Mixing up λ and AFR** — λ = 1 at AFR 14.7; students often write "λ = 14.7" which is wrong.
> 5. **Drawing hand-sketched blocks instead of labelled functional diagrams** in 7-mark questions — diagrams carry 2 marks; unlabelled ones earn zero.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Basic Sensor Arrangement**

- ☐ **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7 : 1**, λ at stoich = **1.000**.
- ☐ Three functional groups: **Position → Charge → Feedback**.
- ☐ **Active sensors** (piezo, thermocouple, inductive CKP) need **no** excitation; **passive sensors** (thermistor, potentiometric TPS) need **5 V reference**.
- ☐ **ECT and IAT** are **NTC thermistors** in a **voltage divider** with the ECU's 5 V reference.
- ☐ **Inductive CKP** follows Faraday's law; frequency $f = n_{teeth}\cdot N_{RPM}/60$.
- ☐ **60–2 trigger wheel** has **58 active teeth**; the missing 2-tooth gap is the TDC reference.
- ☐ **Knock sensor** is **piezoelectric**, outputs charge (pC) — needs a charge amplifier.
- ☐ **UEGO (wide-band λ)** gives both **rich and lean** measurement; **HO2S (narrow-band)** only switches around stoich.
- ☐ MAP sensor uses a **Wheatstone bridge** with piezoresistive elements.
- ☐ MAF uses **hot-wire heat-loss principle** — output voltage ↑ with mass flow.
- ☐ All analog sensors are conditioned through **EMI filter → amplifier → ESD protection → 12-bit ADC** inside the ECU.
- ☐ Closed-loop fuel control is **impossible** without the lambda sensor.
- ☐ Always convert **°C → K** before substituting into the β-equation.
- ☐ Always convert **RPM → rev/s** by dividing by **60** before frequency calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Sensors Such as Oxygen Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Sensors Such as Oxygen Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen Sensor (Lambda Sensor, λ-Sensor):** An electrochemical transducer installed in the exhaust manifold or downpipe of an internal combustion engine that measures the proportion of oxygen (O₂) remaining in the exhaust gas after combustion. It generates a voltage signal proportional to the air–fuel ratio deviation from the chemically ideal (stoichiometric) mixture, enabling the Engine Control Unit (ECU) to perform closed-loop fuel injection control.

The output is reported as the **lambda (λ) value**, defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{\text{Actual Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR}_{\text{actual}}\text{)}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR}_{\text{stoich}}\text{)}}$$

| Fuel Type | Stoichiometric AFR |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Gasoline (Petrol) | **14.7 : 1** |
| Diesel | **14.5 : 1** |
| LPG | **15.5 : 1** |
| CNG | **17.2 : 1** |
| Methanol | **6.4 : 1** |
| Ethanol | **9.0 : 1** |

- **λ = 1** → Stoichiometric mixture (chemically balanced).
- **λ < 1** → Rich mixture (excess fuel, low residual O₂).
- **λ > 1** → Lean mixture (excess air, high residual O₂).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Nose of the Engine":** Think of the oxygen sensor as the *nose* of a chef tasting a simmering pot. After the food cooks, the chef dips a spoon, tastes it, and decides whether to add salt (fuel) or water (air). The oxygen sensor does the same thing: it "smells" the exhaust gases, decides whether the mixture is too rich (too much fuel) or too lean (too much air), and tells the ECU — the chef's brain — to adjust the next injector pulse width. Without it, the engine would either suffocate in unburnt fuel or starve of fuel, polluting heavily and wasting energy.

### 1.3 Sensor Location in a Typical Exhaust System

> [!NOTE]
> Most modern petrol engines carry **two oxygen sensors**:
> 1. **Upstream Sensor (Sensor 1, before catalytic converter)** — *Closed-loop control*.
> 2. **Downstream Sensor (Sensor 2, after catalytic converter)** — *Catalyst efficiency monitoring*.

Forced-induction and multi-bank engines (V6, V8) may carry **four** sensors — one per bank before and after each catalyst.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Operation (Zirconia Nernst Cell)

The heart of a switching-type oxygen sensor is a **Zirconium Dioxide (ZrO₂)** ceramic element stabilized with **Yttrium Oxide (Y₂O₃)**. The porous platinum electrodes on either face act as catalysts and electrical contacts. At high exhaust temperatures (≥ 350 °C), ZrO₂ becomes an oxygen-ion conductor.

The half-cell reactions are:

- **Rich exhaust (low O₂) — Cathode:**
$$\text{O}_2 + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{O}^{2-}$$
- **Lean exhaust (high O₂) — Anode:**
$$2\text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4e^-$$

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium EMF:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{reference}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exhaust}}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = generated EMF in volts
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96 485 C/mol**
- $P_{\text{O}_2}$ = partial pressure of oxygen

The reference side is open to ambient air (≈ 20.9 % O₂).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Walk-Through:**
> 1. Hot exhaust gas (≥ 350 °C) flows past the sensor tip.
> 2. The platinum electrodes catalyze the O₂ molecules, ionizing them at the surfaces.
> 3. Oxygen ions migrate through the ZrO₂ electrolyte from the high-concentration side (exhaust or reference) to the low-concentration side.
> 4. The resulting charge imbalance produces a measurable DC voltage.
> 5. The ECU reads the voltage; if it deviates from ~0.45 V, the ECU trims the injector pulse width by ±25 % (typical long-term fuel trim range).

### 2.3 Switching Behaviour of a Narrow-Band Sensor

A narrow-band zirconia sensor is *binary* in nature — it acts like a switch:

| Exhaust Condition | Sensor Output | ECU Action |
|-------------------|---------------|------------|
| Rich (λ < 1) | ~0.8 – 0.9 V (High) | Reduce fuel |
| Lean (λ > 1) | ~0.1 – 0.2 V (Low) | Add fuel |
| Stoichiometric (λ = 1) | ~0.45 V (Toggle point) | No change |

The voltage switches sharply near λ = 1 because the equilibrium O₂ partial pressure changes by **four orders of magnitude** at the stoichiometric point.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|----------------------|
| Lambda | $\lambda$ | $\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} \div \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}$ | dimensionless |
| Nernst EMF | $E$ | $\frac{RT}{4F}\ln(P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}}/P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}})$ | 0 – 1 V |
| Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) | — | mass basis | **14.7 : 1** |
| Heater power | $P$ | $V^2 \div R$ | **7 – 12 W** typical |
| Operating temp | $T$ | — | **350 – 850 °C** |
| Response time (90 %) | $t_{90}$ | — | **< 100 ms** (heated) |
| Light-off time (cold start) | $t$ | — | **10 – 30 s** with heater |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem mentions *"stoichiometric air–fuel ratio of 14.7:1"*, immediately recall that λ = 1 corresponds to AFR = 14.7 for petrol. The ECU uses the lambda sensor to maintain this value within **±1 %** for best three-way catalytic converter (TWC) efficiency.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Closed-loop fuel control** → optimum combustion, ~15 % fuel economy improvement.
- **Catalyst protection** → prevents rich burnings that overheat the three-way catalyst.
- **Emission compliance** → keeps HC, CO, and NOₓ within **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** / Euro 6 limits.
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** → P0130–P0167 diagnostic trouble codes flag O₂ sensor failure.
- **Cold-start and idling stability** → modern sensors include internal heaters to reach light-off temperature within seconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Nernst Voltage for an Oxygen Sensor

Starting from the Gibbs free-energy change of the half-cell reaction at the cathode:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

The free energy of dilution of oxygen is given by the chemical potential:

$$\mu = \mu^\circ + RT \ln(P_{\text{O}_2})$$

For $n = 4$ electrons transferred per mole of O₂:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{reference}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exhaust}}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute numerical constants at $T = 800\ \text{°C} = 1073\ \text{K}$:**

$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 1073}{4 \times 96485} = \frac{8920.92}{385940} \approx 0.0231\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — For a rich exhaust with $P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}} = 10^{-20}\ \text{atm}$ and reference air $P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}} = 0.209\ \text{atm}$:**

$$E = 0.0231 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{10^{-20}}\right) = 0.0231 \times \ln(2.09 \times 10^{19})$$

$$= 0.0231 \times ( \ln(2.09) + 19 \ln(10) ) = 0.0231 \times (0.737 + 43.749)$$

$$= 0.0231 \times 44.486 \approx 1.028\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — The result (≈ 1 V) matches the rich-condition output (0.8 – 0.9 V) once internal losses and electrode polarization are considered.**

### 3.2 Derivation — Conversion Between AFR and Lambda

Let $\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = 13.2$ for a petrol engine. Compute λ:

$$\lambda = \frac{13.2}{14.7} = 0.898$$

Interpretation: mixture is **rich** (10.2 % excess fuel). The O₂ sensor will output ≈ 0.8 V, and the ECU will reduce injector pulse width.

Reverse calculation — if λ = 1.05, find AFR:

$$\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \times \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 1.05 \times 14.7 = 15.435$$

Interpretation: **lean** mixture; ECU will enrich by ~5 %.

### 3.3 Derivation — Heater Power Dissipation

A typical heated sensor uses $R_{\text{heater}} = 6\ \Omega$ and is fed by the ECU at $V_{\text{batt}} = 12\ \text{V}$.

$$P_{\text{heater}} = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{12^2}{6} = \frac{144}{6} = 24\ \text{W}$$

But because the ECU uses **PWM duty-cycle control** (typically 30 % at steady state):

$$P_{\text{effective}} = 0.30 \times 24 = 7.2\ \text{W}$$

This 7.2 W keeps the sensor at ≈ 750 °C during normal operation — enough to maintain the 350 °C activation threshold with safety margin.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for KTU Problems

| Application | Sensor Type | Output Range | Accuracy | Typical Cost |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------|--------------|
| Petrol closed-loop (pre-cat) | Narrow-band ZrO₂ | 0 – 1 V (switching) | ±0.02 λ | ₹ 1500 – 3000 |
| Petrol post-cat monitoring | Narrow-band ZrO₂ | 0 – 1 V | ±0.05 λ | ₹ 1200 – 2500 |
| Premium SI engine / diesel | Wide-band ZrO₂ (5-wire) | 0 – 5 V (linear) | ±0.007 λ | ₹ 8000 – 15000 |
| Lean-burn CNG / LPG | Titania (TiO₂) | 0 – 5 V (resistive) | ±0.03 λ | ₹ 1800 – 3500 |
| Motorsport / racing | Bosch LSU 4.9 wide-band | IPump current, 0 – 5 V | ±0.008 λ | ₹ 20000 – 35000 |
| Motorcycle / 2-wheeler | Planar ZrO₂ (small form) | 0 – 1 V | ±0.03 λ | ₹ 800 – 1500 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A *Titania* sensor is **resistive** (changes resistance with O₂), not a voltage source like a *Zirconia* sensor. Examiners often test this distinction. Always remember: **Zirconia = Voltage generator**, **Titania = Resistance transducer**.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Lambda to Voltage Mapping

A four-wire heated ZrO₂ sensor outputs 0.45 V at λ = 1.00. If the heater fails and the sensor cools to 200 °C, the electrolyte ceases ionic conduction, and the output becomes essentially *open-circuit* (≈ 0 V regardless of AFR). The ECU interprets this as a "sensor cold" condition (P0135) and runs the engine in **open-loop** mode with a pre-programmed map, raising emissions and fuel consumption by 10 – 25 %.

Quantitative impact — for a vehicle running 20 km/L in closed loop, in open loop it drops to:

$$\text{New FE} = 20 \times 0.82 \approx 16.4\ \text{km/L}$$

A 3.6 km/L mileage penalty — this is a frequently-asked KTU numerical.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Oxygen Sensor in Closed-Loop Fuel Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intake Manifold] --> B[Fuel Injector]
    B --> C[Engine Cylinder]
    C --> D[Exhaust Manifold]
    D --> E[Oxygen Sensor 1 Upstream]
    E --> F[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    F -->|Pulse Width Modulation PWM| B
    D --> G[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    G --> H[Oxygen Sensor 2 Downstream]
    H --> F
    F --> I[OBD II Diagnostic Port]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Four-Wire Heated Zirconia O₂ Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensorAssembly [ZrO2 Sensor Cross Section]
        S1[Exhaust Gas In] --> S2[Porous Platinum Electrode Outer]
        S2 --> S3[Zirconia ZrO2 Y2O3 Electrolyte]
        S3 --> S4[Porous Platinum Electrode Inner]
        S4 --> S5[Reference Air Vent Ambient]
    end
    subgraph Wiring [4 Wire Harness]
        W1[Signal High +] --- S2
        W2[Signal Low -] --- S4
        W3[Heater 12V] --- S6[Heating Coil Nichrome]
        W4[Heater Ground] --- S6
    end
    S2 --> ECU[ECU Analog Input Pin]
    S4 --> ECUG[ECU Ground]
    S6 --> ECUHTR[ECU Heater Driver]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Wide-Band vs Narrow-Band Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NB [Narrow Band ZrO2 Switching]
        NB1[Rich Lambda less than 1] --> NB2[Output 0.8 to 0.9 V]
        NB3[Stoichiometric Lambda 1] --> NB4[Output 0.45 V]
        NB5[Lean Lambda greater than 1] --> NB6[Output 0.1 to 0.2 V]
    end
    subgraph WB [Wide Band LSU Type]
        WB1[Diffusion Gap Reference Air] --> WB2[Nernst Pump Cell]
        WB2 --> WB3[IPump Controller ECU]
        WB3 --> WB4[Lambda 0.7 to 1.3 Linear]
        WB4 --> WB5[Output 0 to 5 V]
    end
    NB --> ECUCompare[ECU Compares Both]
    WB --> ECUCompare
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Sensor Fault Diagnostics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Check Engine Light ON Code P0130 to P0167] --> Q1{Sensor Voltage Stuck}
    Q1 -->|Stuck at 0.45V| A1[Open Circuit Check Wiring]
    Q1 -->|Stuck at 0.0V| A2[Cold Sensor or Open Heater]
    Q1 -->|Stuck at 0.9V| A3[Rich Exhaust Leak in Intake]
    Q1 -->|Slow Switching more than 100ms| A4[Aged Contaminated Sensor]
    A1 --> Fix1[Repair or Replace Harness]
    A2 --> Fix2[Replace Heater Element]
    A3 --> Fix3[Vacuum Leak Repair]
    A4 --> Fix4[Replace O2 Sensor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define an oxygen sensor. Why is it called a "lambda sensor"?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An oxygen sensor is an electrochemical device fitted in the exhaust system of a petrol engine that measures the residual oxygen content in the exhaust gases. It generates a voltage proportional to the difference between the oxygen partial pressure in the exhaust and that of ambient air (reference).
It is called a "lambda sensor" because its primary output is the **air–fuel equivalence ratio λ** (lambda), where λ = (actual AFR) / (stoichiometric AFR). The ECU uses this λ value for closed-loop fuel metering.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. List any two differences between Zirconia and Titania oxygen sensors.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Zirconia (ZrO₂) | Titania (TiO₂) |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Output type | Voltage generator (Nernst EMF) | Resistance change (resistive) |
| Reference gas | Needs ambient air reference | No reference gas required |
| Operating temp | 350 – 850 °C | 300 – 900 °C |
| Wiring | 1, 3, or 4 wires | Usually 2 or 3 wires |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Zirconia-dioxide (ZrO₂) oxygen sensor used in a three-way catalytic converter system. Derive the Nernst equation governing its EMF. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of the upstream and downstream oxygen sensors in meeting BS-VI emission norms. What happens when one of them fails? *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a):**

**Construction** — A ZrO₂ sensor is a thimble-shaped (or planar) ceramic element made of zirconium dioxide stabilized with yttrium oxide. The inner and outer surfaces are coated with porous platinum. The inner side is exposed to ambient air (reference, $P_{\text{O}_2} \approx 0.209\ \text{atm}$); the outer side faces the exhaust gas. A heating element (nichrome coil) is embedded to bring the sensor to its operating temperature quickly.

**Working Principle** — When the sensor temperature exceeds 350 °C, the ZrO₂ lattice becomes conductive to O²⁻ ions. Oxygen molecules at the platinum electrodes are catalytically reduced/oxidized, and ions migrate across the electrolyte. A Nernst EMF is established between the two electrodes.

**Nernst Derivation:**

For the half-cell reaction $\text{O}_2 + 4e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O}^{2-}$, the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}}}\right)$$

At equilibrium $\Delta G = -nFE$, therefore:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}}}\right)$$

This EMF varies between 0 V (very lean) and ≈ 1 V (very rich), with a sharp transition at λ = 1.

**Part (b):**

- **Upstream sensor (Sensor 1):** Mounted before the catalytic converter. Provides fast feedback (response < 100 ms) to the ECU for closed-loop fuel control. Maintains AFR at 14.7 ± 0.1.
- **Downstream sensor (Sensor 2):** Mounted after the TWC. Monitors the catalyst's oxygen storage capacity. If Sensor 2 mirrors Sensor 1, the catalyst is aged and must be replaced (this is the OBD-II "catalyst efficiency" test, code P0420).
- **BS-VI relevance:** BS-VI limits NOₓ to 60 mg/km (petrol) and 80 mg/km (diesel). Without accurate upstream O₂ feedback, the TWC cannot reduce NOₓ to mandated levels.
- **Failure mode — upstream sensor stuck at 0.45 V:** ECU runs in open loop, fuel trim deviates, emissions increase by 50 – 200 %, fuel economy drops 15 %.
- **Failure mode — downstream sensor failure:** No catalyst monitoring, MIL illuminates, vehicle may fail PUC (Pollution Under Control) certification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **wide-band oxygen sensor** (LSU type). How does it differ from a narrow-band sensor in terms of measurement range and accuracy? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A four-wire heated ZrO₂ sensor in a BS-VI petrol car shows a heater resistance of 4 Ω. If the ECU drives the heater with 14 V using 25 % PWM duty, calculate (i) the peak power, (ii) the average power, and (iii) the time taken to reach 350 °C if the thermal mass is 30 J/°C starting from 30 °C. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a):**

A wide-band sensor (Bosch LSU 4.9 / NTK LZAxx) has **two electrochemical cells**:

1. **Nernst cell** — measures EMF like a narrow-band sensor.
2. **Pump cell** — pumps oxygen ions *into or out of* a small diffusion gap using an external current $I_{\text{p}}$. The ECU adjusts $I_{\text{p}}$ to hold the Nernst cell at 0.45 V (i.e., λ = 1 reference point). The magnitude and direction of $I_{\text{p}}$ directly correspond to the actual λ.

This gives a **linear** output from λ ≈ 0.7 to ∞ (truly lean operation) with an accuracy of ±0.007 λ — roughly **ten times better** than a narrow-band sensor.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Narrow-Band | Wide-Band |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| Range | λ ≈ 0.8 to 1.2 | λ ≈ 0.7 to ∞ |
| Output | Switching 0/1 V | Linear 0 – 5 V |
| Accuracy | ±0.02 λ | ±0.007 λ |
| Wires | 1, 3, 4 | 5 or 6 |
| Application | Petrol closed-loop | Diesel, GDI, lean-burn,HEV |

**Part (b):**

**(i) Peak power:**

$$P_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{14^2}{4} = \frac{196}{4} = 49\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Average power (PWM duty = 25 %):**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \times 49 = 12.25\ \text{W}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 350 °C from 30 °C:**

Required temperature rise $\Delta T = 350 - 30 = 320\ ^\circ\text{C}$. With thermal mass $C = 30\ \text{J/°C}$:

$$E_{\text{required}} = C \times \Delta T = 30 \times 320 = 9600\ \text{J}$$

Assuming 100 % of electrical energy converts to heat:

$$t = \frac{E}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{9600}{12.25} \approx 783.7\ \text{s} \approx 13.1\ \text{min}$$

In practice the duty rises to 60 – 70 % during cold start, so the actual light-off time is **15 – 30 seconds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Zirconia (voltage) with Titania (resistance).** Examiners frequently award zero marks to students who write "ZrO₂ is a resistive sensor" — a classic one-mark-loss error.
> 2. **Always state the operating temperature** (≥ 350 °C) when describing the Nernst effect; without it, the derivation is incomplete.
> 3. **In lambda–AFR conversions, mention fuel type.** A student who assumes diesel stoichiometry (14.5) for a petrol question loses 1 – 2 marks.
> 4. **Don't omit the reference gas** when drawing the ZrO₂ cell; examiners specifically look for "ambient air reference" labelling.
> 5. **For numericals, include units** in every line of the working — kW vs W, s vs min, V vs mV.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - Oxygen sensor = Lambda sensor; measures residual O₂ in exhaust.
> - Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) = **14.7 : 1**; λ = 1 is the toggle point.
> - **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** voltage generator, Nernst EMF = $(RT/4F)\ln(P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}}/P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}})$.
> - **Titania (TiO₂):** resistive transducer; resistance falls as exhaust becomes lean.
> - **Narrow-band:** switching between 0.1 V and 0.9 V around λ = 1.
> - **Wide-band (LSU):** linear from 0.7 ≤ λ ≤ ∞ using pump cell principle.
> - Operating temperature: **350 – 850 °C**; cold sensors fail to give valid output.
> - Sensors: **Sensor 1 (upstream)** for closed-loop control; **Sensor 2 (downstream)** for catalyst monitoring.
> - Key emissions: HC, CO, NOₓ; TWC needs λ = 1 ± 0.01 for best conversion.
> - Diagnostic codes: P0130 – P0167; P0420 = catalyst efficiency below threshold.
> - Modern sensors are **heated (3- or 4-wire)** for fast light-off (10 – 30 s).
> - **BS-VI / Euro 6** mandate accurate lambda control for NOₓ reduction to 60 mg/km.
> - **BS-VI = Bharat Stage VI emission norms** in India.
> - **TWC = Three-Way Catalytic Converter** (reduces HC, CO, NOₓ simultaneously).
> - **ECU = Engine Control Unit**, the central computer of the vehicle.
> - **OBD-II = On-Board Diagnostics, 2nd generation** standardised diagnostic port.
> - **GDI = Gasoline Direct Injection** — requires wide-band sensors due to stratified charge operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crank Angle Position Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Crank Angle Position Sensors ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Crank Angle Position (CKP) Sensor** is a non-contact electromagnetic, Hall-effect, or optical transducer mounted in the engine block or bell housing that monitors the precise angular position and rotational speed of the engine's crankshaft. It generates an electrical pulse train whose **frequency is proportional to engine speed (RPM)** and whose **phase is locked to a known reference datum** (typically the piston position of cylinder #1 relative to Top Dead Center, TDC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT744 – Module 2):** The CKP sensor is treated as a foundational input device for the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**. It is the master timing reference that synchronizes fuel injection events and ignition spark timing, making it indispensable to sequential fuel injection (SFI) and distributor-less ignition systems (DIS). The signal produced by the CKP sensor is generally called the **Profile Ignition Pickup (PIP)** signal, and it is used by the ECU to: 1. Determine **engine RPM** (crankshaft speed). 2. Identify **TDC / BDC reference positions** for fuel injection. 3. Compute **ignition dwell** and spark advance. 4. Detect **misfires** and **combustion irregularities**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Clock Imagine a **bicycle wheel with a small reflector** rotating in front of a flashlight aimed at a light sensor. Every time the reflector passes, the sensor "sees" a flash. By counting the flashes per minute, you know how fast the wheel spins (RPM). Now, paint **one reflector in red** to mark a special position (TDC). The instant you sense the red flash, you know *exactly* which angle the wheel is at. The CKP sensor works on this exact principle, but instead of one reflector, it uses a **toothed reluctor wheel (reluctor = reluctance)** with 60 teeth (one for every 6° of crank rotation) and one or more **missing teeth** that act as the red reflector — the reference marker. > [!NOTE] > A **60-minus-2** reluctor wheel (58 teeth + 2 missing) is the most common OEM pattern. The gap of 2 missing teeth creates a uniquely long pulse that the ECU interprets as the **TDC reference for cylinder #1**. ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | Output frequency range | **0 Hz to 10 kHz** | Covers idle (~120 RPM ≈ 6 Hz) to redline (~7000 RPM ≈ 350 Hz) | | Air gap (sensor-to-tooth) | **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** | Critical for signal amplitude | | Output voltage amplitude | **0.5 V to 200 V (magnetic type)** | Magnetic types are speed-dependent | | Hall-effect output | **0 V / 5 V square wave** | Speed-independent, ECU-friendly | | Tooth count | **60 (with 1–2 missing)** | 360° / 60 = 6° resolution | | Temperature range | **−40 °C to +150 °C** | Under-hood thermal environment | | Response time | **< 10 μs** | Required for high-RPM accuracy | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principles of CKP Sensors There are three dominant transduction technologies used in modern CKP sensors. KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare them. #### 2.1.1 Variable Reluctance (VR / Magnetic) Type A permanent magnet wrapped with a coil of fine wire sits adjacent to a ferromagnetic toothed wheel (the reluctor). As each tooth approaches the magnet, the **magnetic reluctance** of the flux path decreases, increasing flux. As the tooth moves away, reluctance rises and flux collapses. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, this time-varying flux induces an EMF in the coil: $$\varepsilon(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ The resulting waveform is an **analog sinusoid** whose **amplitude is proportional to engine speed**. At low RPM (cranking, ~120 RPM), the signal may be only a few hundred millivolts — too small for some ECUs without an integrated amplifier. #### 2.1.2 Hall-Effect Type A Hall-effect IC is biased by a small permanent magnet. The reluctor tooth acts as a **magnetic shunt** — when the tooth is between the magnet and the Hall element, flux is diverted, switching the Hall output to LOW. When the slot is opposite, flux reaches the Hall element, output goes HIGH. The output is a clean **0 V / 5 V (or 12 V) square wave**, **amplitude-independent of RPM**, which is the major advantage. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** The Hall-effect CKP sensor has a constant-amplitude digital output, making it ideal for the modern ECU. The VR sensor, though cheaper, produces a *speed-dependent* analog output that requires a **zero-crossing detector** inside the ECU to convert to a digital square wave. #### 2.1.3 Optical Type An LED and a photodiode face each other across slots in a code disc attached to the crankshaft. Each slot produces a light pulse → voltage pulse. Optical sensors offer **excellent resolution** (sub-degree accuracy) and are immune to electromagnetic interference, but are **sensitive to oil contamination and heat**, limiting their use to research and racing applications. ### 2.2 Working Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Mechanical coupling:** The reluctor wheel is rigidly mounted on the crankshaft (or on the crankshaft pulley / harmonic balancer). Rotation of the crank is therefore 1:1 with the wheel. 2. **Flux modulation:** As the teeth sweep past the sensor tip, they modulate the magnetic field. 3. **EMF generation:** A time-varying flux induces an EMF in the pickup coil (VR type) or the Hall element switches state (Hall type). 4. **Signal conditioning:** The ECU's input circuit amplifies, filters, and **zero-cross detects** the waveform to extract the digital pulse train. 5. **Reference recognition:** The ECU's firmware continuously watches for the long-pulse "missing tooth" pattern to reset its event counter and re-synchronize timing. 6. **Event dispatch:** For every tooth transition, the ECU decrements timers for injection pulse-width (τ) and ignition dwell, firing the appropriate actuator at the correct crank angle (e.g., 10° BTDC for spark). ### 2.3 Relationship to Camshaft Position (CMP) Sensor The CKP sensor alone cannot tell whether cylinder #1 is on the **compression stroke** or the **exhaust stroke** — both produce the same tooth pattern. A **Camshaft Position (CMP) sensor** provides one pulse per cam revolution, and together CKP + CMP form the **synchronizing pair** required for **Sequential Fuel Injection (SFI)**. The combined signal is called **SYNC**. ### 2.4 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Necessity - **Why is the CKP so important?** Modern engines run with **closed-loop control** in milliseconds. Without an exact crank-angle reference, the ECU cannot time spark or fuel within ±1°, leading to misfires, poor emissions, and damaged catalysts. - **How does missing-tooth detection work?** The ECU measures the **period between consecutive tooth edges** (T_tooth). When a period **≈ 2 × T_tooth** is detected, the ECU recognizes the gap as the missing-tooth reference and resets its internal angle counter to a calibrated value (typically 0° TDC for cylinder #1). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law for VR sensor EMF | Volts (V) | | 2 | $V_{peak} \approx 2\pi f N \Phi_{max} \times 10^{-8}$ | Peak output of a magnetic pickup (empirical) | Volts (V) | | 3 | $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60}$ | Output pulse frequency vs. RPM | Hz | | 4 | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N_{teeth}}$ | Engine speed (RPM) from frequency | rev/min (RPM) | | 5 | $\theta_{tooth} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{teeth}}$ | Angular resolution per tooth | degrees (°) | | 6 | $\theta_{tooth} = \dfrac{6\pi}{N_{teeth}}$ | Same, in radians | rad | | 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60}$ | Crankshaft angular velocity | rad/s | | 8 | $V_{Hall} = R_H \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot B}{t}$ | Hall voltage (R_H = Hall coefficient) | Volts (V) | | 9 | $T_{tooth} = \dfrac{60}{n \cdot N_{teeth}}$ | Period between two consecutive teeth | seconds (s) | | 10 | $TDC_{detect} \rightarrow \dfrac{T_{gap}}{T_{tooth}} \approx 2$ | Missing-tooth detection criterion | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick (KTU favorite):** For a 60-tooth wheel, $f(\text{Hz}) = n(\text{RPM})$. Example: at 3000 RPM, output frequency = 3000 Hz = **3 kHz**. This 1:1 mapping is because $f = n \cdot 60 / 60 = n$. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility CKP sensors are used in: - **Gasoline port injection (PFI)** and **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** engines. - **Common-rail diesel** engines for injector timing. - **Hybrid vehicles** for ICE speed monitoring and crank-start synchronization. - **Motorcycles, marine engines, and gensets** with electronic governors. - **Motorsport data acquisition** for crank-angle-resolved combustion analysis. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Frequency vs. Engine RPM A reluctor wheel has $N_{teeth}$ teeth. For every complete revolution of the crankshaft, the sensor produces exactly $N_{teeth}$ pulses. If the engine rotates at $n$ revolutions per minute, the pulses per minute are $n \cdot N_{teeth}$. Converting to pulses per second (Hz): $$ \begin{aligned} f \; [\text{Hz}] &= \frac{\text{pulses per minute}}{60} \\ &= \frac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60} \\ \therefore \; f &= \frac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** - $n$ = engine speed in rev/min. - One revolution → $N_{teeth}$ pulses, so pulses per minute = $n \cdot N_{teeth}$. - Divide by 60 to convert minutes to seconds → frequency in Hz. - Result: $f = n \cdot N_{teeth} / 60$. **Inverting the relation** to compute RPM from a measured frequency: $$ \begin{aligned} n \cdot N_{teeth} &= 60 f \\ n &= \frac{60 f}{N_{teeth}} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Peak EMF in a Variable Reluctance Sensor Assume the magnetic flux through the pickup coil varies sinusoidally as the tooth sweeps past: $$ \Phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \sin(2\pi f t) $$ Applying Faraday's law with $N$ turns: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon(t) &= -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} \\ &= -N \frac{d}{dt}\left[\Phi_{max}\sin(2\pi f t)\right] \\ &= -N \cdot \Phi_{max} \cdot 2\pi f \cos(2\pi f t) \\ \varepsilon_{peak} &= 2\pi f N \Phi_{max} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic of each line:** - Line 1: Faraday's law. - Line 2: Substitute the assumed sinusoidal flux. - Line 3: Differentiate — derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$, with $\omega = 2\pi f$. - Line 4: Take the peak (magnitude) of the cosine term = 1. This shows that **the peak EMF is directly proportional to engine speed $f$**, confirming the speed-dependent nature of VR sensors. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style > **Problem:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine uses a 60-tooth reluctor wheel with 2 missing teeth for the CKP sensor. Calculate (a) the tooth angular spacing, (b) the output frequency at 3000 RPM, and (c) the time gap corresponding to the missing teeth. **(a) Angular spacing per tooth:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{tooth} &= \frac{360°}{N_{teeth}} \\ &= \frac{360°}{60} \\ &= 6° \; \text{per tooth} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Output frequency at 3000 RPM:** $$ \begin{aligned} f &= \frac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60} \\ &= \frac{3000 \times 60}{60} \\ &= 3000 \; \text{Hz} = 3 \; \text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Time for the missing-tooth gap (2 missing teeth = 3 × normal spacing):** The 2 missing teeth create a gap equivalent to **3 tooth periods** (because the missing teeth plus the surrounding teeth = 3 × 6° = 18° gap). $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tooth} &= \frac{60}{n \cdot N_{teeth}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{3000} = 0.333 \; \text{ms} \\ T_{gap} &= 3 \times T_{tooth} = 3 \times 0.333 \; \text{ms} = 1.0 \; \text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Validation:** The ratio $T_{gap}/T_{tooth} = 3$, which clearly satisfies the missing-tooth detection criterion ($T_{gap}/T_{tooth} \geq 1.5$). The ECU's "missing tooth detector" therefore reliably identifies the reference position. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CKP Sensor Technologies | Parameter | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Hall-Effect | Optical | |---|---|---|---| | Output type | Analog sinusoid | Digital square wave | Digital pulse | | Amplitude vs. RPM | **Proportional** (low at idle) | **Constant** | Constant | | Sensitivity to air gap | High | Moderate | Moderate | | Minimum speed (RPM) | ~30 RPM (cranking limit) | **0 RPM** (true zero-speed) | 0 RPM | | EMI immunity | Low (analog) | High (digital) | Very high | | Temperature tolerance | **Excellent** (>150 °C) | Good (<150 °C) | Poor (LED degradation) | | Oil/dirt contamination | Immune | Immune | **Highly sensitive** | | Cost | Low | Moderate | High | | ECU interface needs | Zero-cross detector | Direct GPIO / comparator | Schmitt trigger | | Typical OEM use | Older / heavy-duty | **Modern (most cars)** | Motorsport | ### 3.5 Derivation: Angular Resolution and ECU Event Scheduling If the engine rotates at $\omega$ rad/s and the tooth spacing is $\theta_{tooth}$ rad, then the time between teeth is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tooth} &= \frac{\theta_{tooth}}{\omega} \\ &= \frac{360°/N_{teeth}}{2\pi n / 60} \\ &= \frac{60}{n \cdot N_{teeth}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **fundamental time unit** used by the ECU to schedule fuel injection and ignition events. The ECU maintains a **free-running timer** that is initialized (reset to 0) at the missing-tooth reference, and then counts tooth edges to determine *exactly* which crank angle the engine is currently at. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — CKP Sensor Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Engine Crankshaft] --> B[Reluctor Wheel
60 teeth minus 2] B --> C{Transduction
Type} C -->|VR| D[Magnetic Pickup Coil
Analog Sine EMF] C -->|Hall| E[Hall-Effect IC
0 to 5 V Square] C -->|Optical| F[LED + Photodiode
Light Pulse] D --> G[ECU Input Stage
Amplifier + ZCD] E --> H[ECU Input Stage
Schmitt Trigger] F --> I[ECU Input Stage
Comparator] G --> J[Missing Tooth
Detector] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Engine Speed
RPM Calculator] J --> L[Crank Angle
Counter Reset] K --> M[ECU Scheduler] L --> M M --> N[Fuel Injector
Pulse Width] M --> O[Ignition Coil
Dwell + Spark] M --> P[Misfire
Detection] ``` ### 4.2 Operational State Diagram — ECU CKP Logic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> WaitingForSync WaitingForSync --> ToothCounting: First Edge Detected ToothCounting --> ToothCounting: Normal Tooth T_tooth ToothCounting --> SyncDetected: T_gap >= 1.5 T_tooth SyncDetected --> ToothCounting: Resume Count from Reset ToothCounting --> MisfireDetected: Skipped Tooth or Excess Period MisfireDetected --> MILOn: Set Diagnostic Trouble Code P0300 MILOn --> ToothCounting: After 3 Consecutive Normal Cycles ToothCounting --> [*]: Engine Off ``` ### 4.3 Mounting and Physical Layout Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EngBlock[Engine Block / Bell Housing] Crankshaft[Crankshaft] Pulley[Timing Pulley / Harmonic Balancer] Reluctor[Reluctor Wheel
60 minus 2] Crankshaft --> Pulley --> Reluctor end Sensor[CKP Sensor
Magnetic / Hall / Optical] Airgap[Air Gap 0.5 to 1.5 mm] Reluctor -.Passes Teeth.-> Airgap Airgap -.Modulates Flux.-> Sensor Sensor --> Wiring[Harness + Shielded Twisted Pair] Wiring --> ECU[Engine Control Unit] ECU --> Inj[Fuel Injector Driver] ECU --> Ign[Ignition Coil Driver] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students draw the sensor inside the timing cover touching the wheel. In reality, the CKP sensor is mounted on a stationary bracket with a precisely controlled **air gap**. Contact between sensor tip and reluctor will physically destroy both components at high RPM. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the Crank Angle Position (CKP) sensor and list its two primary outputs used by the ECU. **Model Answer:** The CKP sensor is a non-contact transducer that monitors the angular position and rotational speed of the crankshaft. Its two primary outputs are: 1. **Engine RPM (speed)** — derived from the pulse frequency. 2. **Crank angle reference** — derived from the missing-tooth pattern (the long-pulse signal) that resets the ECU's internal event counter, typically at TDC of cylinder #1. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Compare the output of a **variable reluctance** CKP sensor and a **Hall-effect** CKP sensor with respect to amplitude and engine speed. **Model Answer:** | Property | VR (Variable Reluctance) | Hall-Effect | |---|---|---| | Output waveform | Analog sinusoid | Digital 0 V / 5 V square wave | | Amplitude vs. RPM | Proportional to RPM (very low at cranking) | Constant at all RPMs (true zero-speed capability) | | ECU interface | Requires zero-cross detector and amplifier | Direct digital input (Schmitt trigger) | --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **variable reluctance (magnetic) CKP sensor** used in a 4-cylinder gasoline engine. Discuss how the missing-tooth pattern is used to generate the TDC reference. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine uses a **60-minus-2** tooth reluctor wheel. At an engine speed of 4500 RPM, compute: - (i) the output frequency of the CKP sensor, - (ii) the time between consecutive tooth edges, - (iii) the time width of the missing-tooth gap, - (iv) the angular resolution per tooth. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Working of a VR CKP sensor (7 marks):** A variable reluctance CKP sensor consists of a **permanent magnet** with a **pickup coil** wound around a soft-iron pole piece, mounted on a stationary bracket near the **toothed reluctor wheel** fixed to the crankshaft. 1. As each tooth approaches the pole piece, the **magnetic reluctance** of the flux path decreases, increasing flux through the coil. 2. As the tooth moves away, reluctance increases and flux decreases. 3. By Faraday's law, this time-varying flux induces an **EMF** in the coil: $\varepsilon = -N \, d\Phi / dt$. 4. The induced voltage is a **sinusoidal waveform** whose **amplitude is proportional to engine RPM** and **frequency** equals the tooth-passing rate. 5. The **ECU** amplifies this analog signal, applies a zero-crossing detector, and converts it to a digital square wave. 6. The **60-minus-2 reluctor** has 58 teeth + one 18° gap (3-tooth period) acting as a **reference marker**. The ECU continuously measures the period between consecutive tooth edges; when it detects a period **≈ 3 × T_tooth**, it identifies the missing-tooth event and **resets the crank-angle counter to TDC of cylinder #1**. 7. After reset, every subsequent tooth edge increments the counter by 6°, providing a **6° crank-angle resolution** for fuel and spark scheduling. **Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** Crankshaft → Reluctor → Sensor (magnet + coil) → Amplifier → Zero-Cross Detector → ECU Counter → Injector/Ignition Drivers. --- **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** Given: $N_{teeth} = 60$, missing teeth = 2, $n = 4500$ RPM. **(i) Output frequency:** $$ \begin{aligned} f &= \frac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60} \\ &= \frac{4500 \times 60}{60} \\ &= 4500 \; \text{Hz} = 4.5 \; \text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Time between consecutive teeth:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tooth} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{4500} \\ &= 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{s} \\ &\approx 222.2 \; \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Time width of the missing-tooth gap (3 × T_tooth):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{gap} &= 3 \times T_{tooth} = 3 \times 222.2 \; \mu\text{s} \\ &\approx 666.7 \; \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Angular resolution per tooth:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{tooth} &= \frac{360°}{60} = 6° \; \text{of crank rotation} \\ \theta_{tooth} &= 6 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1047 \; \text{rad} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted] **(a)** Explain the working of a **Hall-effect CKP sensor** with a neat sketch. Why is it preferred over a variable reluctance sensor in modern ECUs? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke engine uses a **36-minus-1** reluctor wheel. Determine: - (i) the angular resolution, - (ii) the output frequency at 2400 RPM, - (iii) the time gap corresponding to the missing tooth, - (iv) the peak EMF if the sensor coil has 5000 turns and a peak flux of $4 \times 10^{-5}$ Wb. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Hall-effect CKP sensor (7 marks):** A Hall-effect CKP sensor integrates a **Hall-effect IC** with a **permanent magnet** behind it and a **signal-conditioning ASIC** (Schmitt trigger, open-collector output stage) in a single package. **Working principle:** 1. The permanent magnet produces a magnetic field perpendicular to the Hall element. 2. The reluctor tooth acts as a **magnetic shunt**: when a tooth is in front of the sensor, it diverts the flux away from the Hall element, so the output is LOW (0 V). 3. When a slot is in front of the sensor, flux passes through the Hall element, producing a Hall voltage $V_H = R_H \cdot (I \cdot B / t)$, and the output switches HIGH (5 V or 12 V). 4. As the wheel rotates, the output toggles between 0 V and supply voltage, producing a **clean square wave** with a 50% duty cycle. **Why preferred over VR sensors:** - **Speed-independent amplitude** — works perfectly at cranking speeds (~0 RPM) where VR sensors give a tiny signal. - **Digital output** — direct interface to ECU logic, no zero-crossing detector needed. - **Better noise immunity** due to the large voltage swing. - **Integrated electronics** allow built-in diagnostics and protection. **Sketch (described):** A small IC in a plastic package with three leads — supply (+5 V), ground, and signal output — mounted opposite the reluctor wheel with a 0.5–1.5 mm air gap. --- **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** Given: $N_{teeth} = 36$, missing teeth = 1, $n = 2400$ RPM, $N = 5000$ turns, $\Phi_{max} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ Wb. **(i) Angular resolution per tooth:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{tooth} &= \frac{360°}{N_{teeth}} = \frac{360°}{36} = 10° \;\text{per tooth} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Output frequency at 2400 RPM:** $$ \begin{aligned} f &= \frac{n \cdot N_{teeth}}{60} = \frac{2400 \times 36}{60} \\ &= \frac{86400}{60} = 1440 \; \text{Hz} = 1.44 \; \text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Time gap for the missing tooth:** The 1 missing tooth creates a gap equivalent to **2 tooth periods** (1 missing + 1 normal): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tooth} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1440} \approx 694.4 \; \mu\text{s} \\ T_{gap} &= 2 \times T_{tooth} \approx 1388.9 \; \mu\text{s} \approx 1.389 \; \text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Peak EMF using Faraday's law:** $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{peak} &= 2\pi f N \Phi_{max} \\ &= 2 \pi \times 1440 \times 5000 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \\ &= 2 \pi \times 1440 \times 0.2 \\ &= 2 \pi \times 288 \\ &\approx 1809.6 \; \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ (Realistic peak values in production VR sensors are limited by saturation; this is the **idealized** theoretical value.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by 60** in the frequency formula $f = n N_{teeth}/60$. Many students write $f = n \cdot N_{teeth}$ and end up with absurdly large values — instant loss of 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing the missing-tooth count**: A 60-minus-2 wheel has a gap of **3 tooth periods** (not 2). Always add 1 to the number of missing teeth when computing $T_{gap}$. > 3. **Mixing up Hall-effect and VR outputs**: Hall-effect = digital square wave; VR = analog sinusoid. Examiners deduct a full mark for this swap. > 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — KTU mandates SI units (Hz, s, V, RPM). Missing units → −½ mark per quantity. > 5. **Not explaining *why* Hall-effect is preferred** — merely stating "it is digital" is incomplete. Always mention **zero-speed capability** and **constant amplitude**. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — Rapid Revision Checklist** - **CKP sensor = crankshaft speed + position sensor** (RPM + TDC reference). - Two outputs: **pulse train (frequency → RPM)** and **missing-tooth gap (→ TDC reference)**. - **Variable Reluctance (VR):** analog sine wave, amplitude ∝ RPM, cheap, no electronics inside. - **Hall-effect:** digital 0/5 V square wave, amplitude independent of RPM, **true zero-speed** capability. - **Optical:** highest resolution but oil/heat sensitive — used mainly in motorsport. - **Reluctor wheel patterns:** 60-minus-2 (most common, 6° resolution) and 36-minus-1. - **Key formulas:** $f = n N_{teeth}/60$, $n = 60 f / N_{teeth}$, $\theta_{tooth} = 360°/N_{teeth}$. - **Faraday's law:** $\varepsilon = -N d\Phi / dt$ → $\varepsilon_{peak} = 2\pi f N \Phi_{max}$ for sinusoidal flux. - **Missing-tooth detection:** $T_{gap}/T_{tooth} \geq 1.5$ triggers ECU resync. - **CKP + CMP (camshaft) = SYNC** — required for **Sequential Fuel Injection**. - **Air gap** between sensor and toothed wheel: 0.5–1.5 mm — critical for signal integrity. - **Failure mode:** faulty CKP sensor → no start, no spark, no injection, MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) ON, DTC P0335 stored. - **1 RPM = 1 Hz** for a 60-tooth wheel (mnemonic for quick mental calculation). - **Firing order for 4-cyl:** 1-3-4-2 — ECU fires injectors at the **right tooth edge** after missing-tooth reset. - **EMI mitigation:** always use **shielded twisted-pair** cables for the CKP signal; never route near ignition HT leads.

topics.title: Fuel Metering/Vehicle Speed Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Metering/Vehicle Speed Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Fuel Metering & Vehicle Speed Sensors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuel Metering — Formal KTU Definition

**Fuel Metering** in automobile instrumentation refers to the precise measurement, monitoring, and indication of the quantity of fuel present in the vehicle's fuel tank, the instantaneous rate of fuel flow to the engine, and the cumulative fuel consumed over a journey. The instrumentation subsystem performing this function is called the **Fuel Level / Fuel Quantity Indication System**, while the dynamic flow measurement is carried out by **Fuel Flow Meters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The fuel metering subsystem is part of the *Driver Information System (DIS)*. It interfaces with the Engine Control Unit (ECU) via the CAN/LIN bus in modern vehicles and provides the driver a real-time estimate of **Range-to-Empty (RTE)** and **Instantaneous Fuel Economy (IFE)**.

### 1.2 Vehicle Speed Sensors — Formal KTU Definition

A **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** is a transducer that converts the rotational or translational velocity of the vehicle into a proportional electrical signal (analog voltage, frequency, or digital pulse train). This signal is consumed by the speedometer, the ECU, the ABS, the transmission control module (TCM), and the odometer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference (ISO/Wheel-Slip Convention):** Vehicle speed is reported in **km/h** (cluster display) and **m/s** (internal ECU calculations), with cruise-control and ABS systems requiring a **signal refresh rate ≥ 10 Hz**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of your motorcycle/bike dashboard as a "doctor's clinic":
- The **fuel level sensor** is like the **measuring jar** in a water tank — it tells you *how much* fuel remains (a static quantity).
- The **fuel flow meter** is like a **water tap with a flow indicator** — it tells you *how fast* fuel is being consumed (a dynamic rate).
- The **vehicle speed sensor** is like a **treadmill's display** — it counts wheel revolutions per second and converts that into a km/h reading.

Just as a doctor needs three readings (level, flow, motion) to assess health, the ECU needs all three to compute **driving range, mileage, and gear-shift logic**.

| Sensor Type | Physical Quantity Measured | Output Nature | Driver-Visible Information |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Level Sensor** | Remaining fuel volume (L) | Resistive / Capacitive | Fuel gauge needle / bar-graph |
| **Fuel Flow Meter** | Volume flow rate (L/h) | Pulse frequency | Trip fuel, Average MPG |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor** | Wheel/ground speed (km/h) | Pulse train (Hz) | Speedometer, odometer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fuel Level Sensor — Operating Principle

### A. Float-Type Variable Resistor (Most Common in Indian/Asian Cars)

A buoyant **foam/polypropylene float** rides on the fuel surface. The float is mechanically coupled to the wiper of a **rheostat (variable resistor)** through a pivoted arm.

- When the tank is **FULL** → float is **UP** → wiper at one extreme → resistance = **R_min** (typically 10–40 Ω).
- When the tank is **EMPTY** → float is **DOWN** → wiper at other extreme → resistance = **R_max** (typically 240–300 Ω).
- The rheostat forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge / voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor.

The gauge (or ADC input) measures the voltage across the divider:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{float}}{R_{float} + R_{fixed}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The non-linear shape of the fuel tank means the float-arm **angle** vs. **fuel volume** is non-linear. Manufacturers pre-shape the rheostat's resistance track (using a **non-linear card/wiper profile**) so that the output voltage is approximately **linear with fuel volume**.

### B. Capacitive Fuel Level Sensor

A **coaxial cylindrical capacitor** is immersed in fuel. The dielectric constant of the medium between the plates changes with fuel level:

- **Air** (εᵣ ≈ 1.0006) above the fuel surface.
- **Petrol/Diesel** (εᵣ ≈ 2.1 for petrol, ≈ 2.3 for diesel) below the surface.

Total capacitance is a linear combination of the air-filled and fuel-filled sections:

$$C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(r_{out}/r_{in})} \left[ \varepsilon_{air} \cdot L_{air} + \varepsilon_{fuel} \cdot L_{fuel} \right]$$

where $L = L_{air} + L_{fuel}$ is the total probe length. This capacitance is measured by an LC oscillator whose frequency changes with level — a much more **corrosion-resistant and accurate** alternative, used in modern luxury/electric vehicles.

## 2.2 Fuel Flow Meters

### A. Positive-Displacement Flow Meter (Oval-Gear / Piston)

A known volume of fuel is trapped between meshing gears and physically transferred from inlet to outlet per revolution. Each rotation ejects a **discrete volume V_d (mL/rev)**.

- Output: **Pulse train** generated by a Hall-effect pickup sensing a gear tooth per revolution.
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = V_d \cdot f$ where $f$ is the pulse frequency in Hz.

### B. Turbine Flow Meter

Fuel impinges on a small lightweight turbine, causing rotation at angular velocity ω proportional to flow velocity v. A magnetic pickup senses blade passage:

$$f = \frac{N \cdot v}{2\pi r} = \frac{N \cdot Q}{2\pi r \cdot A}$$

where $N$ is the number of turbine blades, $r$ is the mean blade radius, and $A$ is the flow cross-section.

### C. K-Type / Hot-Film Air-Mass Sensor (Indirect Fuel Metering)

Used in **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** to *indirectly* measure fuel consumed. The ECU computes injector pulse width $T_i$ from the air-mass $\dot{m}_{air}$ and the target air-fuel ratio $\lambda$:

$$T_i = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj,max}}$$

where $AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.7:1$ for petrol and $\lambda$ is the equivalence ratio (λ=1 stoichiometric, λ>1 lean, λ<1 rich).

## 2.3 Vehicle Speed Sensors — Theory of Operation

### A. Variable Reluctance (Magnetic Pickup / MPU) Sensor

A **permanent magnet** wrapped with a coil is placed near a **reluctor wheel (tone wheel)** with N teeth rotating at wheel angular velocity ω.

- As each tooth passes, magnetic flux through the coil rises and falls → an **EMF** is induced (Faraday's Law):

$$e(t) = -N_c \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

- The output is a **sinusoidal AC voltage** whose **frequency** is proportional to wheel speed:

$$f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{N \cdot v}{2\pi r_{wheel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The amplitude of this AC signal depends on speed (not just frequency). At very low speeds (creeping traffic), the amplitude becomes too small for the ECU to detect — this is the **Achilles heel of VR sensors**. They are also called **Passive Speed Sensors**.

### B. Hall-Effect Speed Sensor

A semiconductor **Hall element** is exposed to a magnetic field whose strength is modulated by a rotating toothed wheel (or a magnet ring). The output is a **clean 0–5 V digital square wave** with **50% duty cycle** — independent of speed amplitude.

- Output frequency: same relation as VR sensor, $f = N \cdot \omega / (2\pi)$.
- Active sensor — requires **supply voltage (5 V or 12 V)** and draws low current.
- Used in **ABS wheel-speed sensors** of all modern cars (Bosch, Continental, ZF systems).

### C. Optical Speed Sensor

An **LED** shines light through slots on a rotating disc onto a **phototransistor**. Each slot generates one pulse. Very high resolution achievable (hundreds of slots), but **susceptible to dust/oil contamination** — not preferred in harsh underbody environments.

### D. Reed-Switch Sensor

A **magnet on the rotating shaft** closes a reed switch once per revolution. Used in **older two-wheelers and bicycles** (e.g., Hero Honda, Bajaj pre-2010 models). Crude but cheap.

### E. GPS-Based Vehicle Speed

Modern telematics (OBD-II dongles, fleet trackers) compute speed by **numerical differentiation of position**:

$$v_{GPS} = \frac{d}{dt}\sqrt{(x_{GPS}(t))^2 + (y_{GPS}(t))^2}$$

- Advantages: independent of wheel slip / tyre pressure / gear ratio.
- Disadvantages: low update rate (1–10 Hz), no signal in tunnels, latency issues.

### F. Doppler Radar Speed Sensor

Used in **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** to measure the relative velocity of the vehicle ahead:

$$f_{Doppler} = \frac{2 \cdot v_{rel} \cdot f_{transmit}}{c}$$

where $f_{transmit}$ is typically **76.5 GHz** (mm-wave automotive radar band).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — at least one of these formulas is guaranteed to appear in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks) of every KTU ESE cycle.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot R_{float} / (R_{float} + R_{fix})$ | Voltage divider for float sensor | Volts | 2–3 |
| 2 | $C = (2\pi\varepsilon_0 / \ln(r_{out}/r_{in})) \cdot (\varepsilon_{air} L_{air} + \varepsilon_{fuel} L_{fuel})$ | Capacitive level sensor | Farads | 3 |
| 3 | $Q = V_d \cdot f$ | Positive-displacement flow | L/s | 2 |
| 4 | $f = N \cdot v / (2\pi r_{wheel})$ | Speed → frequency conversion | Hz | 5–7 |
| 5 | $e(t) = -N_c \cdot d\Phi / dt$ | Faraday's law for VR sensor | Volts | 3 |
| 6 | $T_i = \dot{m}_{air} / (\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj,max})$ | EFI injector pulse width | seconds | 7 |
| 7 | $f_{Doppler} = 2 v_{rel} f_{tx} / c$ | ACC radar frequency shift | Hz | 3 |
| 8 | $v_{GPS} = d/dt \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | Differentiated GPS speed | m/s | 2 |
| 9 | $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio (O2 sensor) | dimensionless | 3 |
| 10 | $RTE = V_{tank} / IFE$ | Range to empty (km) | km | 3 |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin:
> - **OBD-II diagnostics** (P0171, P0174 — fuel trim codes)
> - **ABS/ESC** (wheel-speed based slip control)
> - **Fleet management** (telematics-based insurance, UBI)
> - **EV BMS** (range estimation, regenerative-braking metering)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Wheel Angular Velocity to Dashboard km/h

**Given:**
- Wheel rolling radius $r = 0.30$ m
- Tone-wheel teeth $N = 48$ pulses/revolution
- Output frequency measured $f = 800$ Hz

**Find:** Dashboard speed in km/h.

### Step 1 — Relate pulse frequency to wheel angular velocity

Each tooth passing the sensor generates one pulse. Therefore, the **number of pulses per second** equals the **number of teeth times the revolutions per second**:

$$f = N \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = N \cdot n$$

where $n$ is revolutions per second. Solving for $n$:

$$n = \frac{f}{N} = \frac{800}{48} = 16.667 \text{ rev/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute wheel angular velocity ω

$$\omega = 2\pi n = 2\pi \cdot 16.667 = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Linear velocity at the wheel circumference

The vehicle's translation speed equals the tangential speed at the tyre contact patch (assuming **no wheel slip**):

$$v = \omega \cdot r = 104.72 \cdot 0.30 = 31.416 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert m/s → km/h

Multiplying by 3.6:

$$v_{km/h} = v_{m/s} \cdot 3.6 = 31.416 \cdot 3.6 = 113.10 \text{ km/h}$$

### Step 5 — Final consolidated expression

$$\boxed{\,v_{km/h} = \frac{2\pi \cdot r \cdot f \cdot 3.6}{N}\,}$$

Plugging the numbers back:

$$v_{km/h} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.30 \cdot 800 \cdot 3.6}{48} = \frac{5428.67}{48} = 113.10 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion factor memory trick:** 1 m/s = 18/5 km/h = 3.6 km/h. Always multiply m/s by 3.6 to get km/h. This is the **most-skipped step** in KTU answer scripts.

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacitive Fuel Sensor — Sensitivity Analysis

**Given:**
- Probe radii: $r_{in} = 2$ mm, $r_{out} = 5$ mm
- Probe length $L = 300$ mm
- $\varepsilon_{air} = 1.0006$, $\varepsilon_{fuel} = 2.10$

**Find:** Change in capacitance between empty (all air) and full (all fuel) conditions.

### Step 1 — Geometric factor

$$\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(r_{out}/r_{in})} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{\ln(5/2)} = \frac{5.562 \times 10^{-11}}{0.9163} = 6.07 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$$

### Step 2 — Capacitance when empty (only air fills probe)

$$C_{empty} = 6.07 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 1.0006 \cdot 0.300 = 1.822 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 18.22 \text{ pF}$$

### Step 3 — Capacitance when full (only fuel fills probe)

$$C_{full} = 6.07 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 2.10 \cdot 0.300 = 3.824 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 38.24 \text{ pF}$$

### Step 4 — Sensitivity (capacitance swing)

$$\Delta C = C_{full} - C_{empty} = 38.24 - 18.22 = 20.02 \text{ pF}$$

This **20 pF swing** drives a Colpitts oscillator whose frequency shifts from, say, 500 kHz to ~480 kHz, easily countable by the ECU.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Range-to-Empty (RTE) Computation

**Given:**
- Tank capacity = 45 L
- Instantaneous fuel economy = 18 km/L
- Float sensor indicates 60% of tank volume

**Step 1 — Determine remaining fuel**

$$V_{rem} = 0.60 \cdot 45 = 27 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute RTE**

$$RTE = V_{rem} \cdot IFE = 27 \cdot 18 = 486 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with on-board trip computer**

If the trip computer reports 480 km, the discrepancy (6 km) is the calibration error and adaptive-learning offset that the ECU must store as a long-term fuel-trim coefficient.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Guide

| Application Environment | Best Speed-Sensor Type | Reason | KTU-Mention Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS / ESC wheel speed | **Hall-effect** | Near-zero-speed detection, digital output | Very High |
| Cruise control (input) | **Hall-effect / VR** | Reliable mid-speed operation | High |
| Heavy commercial truck | **VR (magnetic pickup)** | Robust, no electronics at the wheel | High |
| 2-wheeler speedometer | **Reed switch / Hall** | Cheap, low resolution OK | Medium |
| EV telematics | **GPS** | Independent of wheel slip | Growing |
| Luxury ACC | **mm-Wave radar** (77 GHz) | Range + relative velocity | Specialized |
| Older carburetted cars | **Cable-driven mechanical** | Pre-electronic era | Historical |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
