The resistive element acts as a classic two-resistor voltage divider. As the wiper moves, the proportion of resistance on either side changes continuously, producing an output voltage that is a linear (or piecewise-linear) function of the throttle angle.

#### B. Hall-Effect (Non-Contact) TPS

**Construction:**
- A **diametrically magnetized ring magnet** is fixed to the throttle shaft.
- A **Hall-effect IC** (e.g., Melexis MLX90316, Allegro A1330) is mounted on a stationary PCB facing the magnet.
- The integrated circuit contains the Hall element, an **analog front-end**, a **16-bit ΔΣ ADC**, and an **SPI/analog output stage**.

**Working Principle:**
- When the magnet rotates, the **magnetic flux density B** crossing the Hall element changes sinusoidally with angle.
- The Hall voltage is amplified, digitised, and either output as an analog ratiometric voltage or as a digital PWM/SENT/SPI frame.
- Because there is **no physical contact**, wear, wiper bounce, and oxidation are eliminated.

#### C. Magneto-Resistive / Inductive TPS

Used in some premium applications. Relies on the **anisotropic magneto-resistance (AMR)** or **tunnel magneto-resistance (TMR)** effect. Operates without a magnet on the rotating part by sensing a soft-magnetic target wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Almost every university question asks you to *compare* potentiometric vs. Hall-effect TPS. Memorize the table in §2.4 — it has appeared verbatim in Dec 2022, May 2023, and Dec 2023 KTU question papers.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below use **SI units**. The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value, written here as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = \left(\dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}}\right) V_{ref}$ | Voltage divider for potentiometric TPS | Output voltage at any angle |
| 2 | $V_{out} = k_1 \, \theta + V_{CTV}$ | Linear calibration: angle $\theta$ (degrees) to voltage | Closed-throttle offset $V_{CTV}$ |
| 3 | $k_1 = \dfrac{V_{WOTV} - V_{CTV}}{\theta_{max}}$ | Slope (V per degree), typically $\approx 40 \,\text{mV}/^\circ$ | Sensitivity |
| 4 | $V_{out1}(t) + V_{out2}(t) = V_{ref} \pm \Delta$ | Dual-track **sum-check** (rationality test) | Fault detection |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$ | Angular velocity from voltage rate | Acceleration enrichment |
| 6 | $V_{H} = R_H \dfrac{I \, B}{t}$ | **Hall voltage** ($R_H$ Hall coeff., $I$ bias current, $B$ flux density, $t$ Hall-plate thickness) | Hall-effect TPS |
| 7 | $B(\theta) = B_0 \cos(\theta - \theta_0)$ | Flux density vs. magnet rotation | Hall TPS sinusoidal model |
| 8 | $ADC_{code} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{N} - 1) \right\rfloor$ | Quantisation: $N$-bit ADC ($N = 12$ typical) | ECU digitisation |
| 9 | $R_{wiper} \le 0.5 \,\text{k}\Omega$ | Maximum permissible contact resistance (potentiometric) | Aging diagnostic limit |
| 10 | $\Delta V_{sum} = \vert V_{out1} + V_{out2} - V_{ref} \vert \le 0.10 \,\text{V}$ | Sum-check tolerance window | OEM DTC threshold |

---

### 2.4 Comparative Analysis — Three TPS Technologies

| Parameter | Potentiometric | Hall-Effect | Magneto-Resistive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Resistive wiper | Hall voltage on semiconductor | Resistance change in ferromagnet |
| Contact Type | Sliding contact | Non-contact | Non-contact |
| Output | Analog 0–5 V | Analog or SENT/SPI | Analog or digital |
| Resolution | ~10 bits (limited by wiper granularity) | 12–16 bits | 12–16 bits |
| Wear-Out Mechanism | Track erosion, wiper fatigue | None mechanical | None mechanical |
| Temperature Drift | Moderate (carbon track) | Low (compensated IC) | Very low |
| Cost | **Low** | **Medium** | **High** |
| Lifetime | ~10 M cycles | >100 M cycles | >100 M cycles |
| EMI Susceptibility | Low | Medium (requires shielding) | Medium |
| Typical Application | Older EFI, motorcycles, two-wheelers | Modern DBW systems, BS-VI/VI+ | Premium / EV throttle pedals |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **Very High** | Moderate |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond the Automobile

The TPS measurement chain (rotary position → ratiometric voltage → ADC → control loop) is a **canonical pattern** in modern mechatronics. The same architecture appears in:

- **Industrial servo valves** — position feedback to hydraulic proportional valves.
- **Robotic joint encoders** — but with much higher precision (optical).
- **Electronic Throttle Bodies (ETB)** for hybrid vehicles — *two* TPS sensors for ASIL-D redundancy.
- **Flight Control Surfaces** — dual-redundant Hall sensors for aerospace actuators.
- **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** — torque + angle sensors using Hall-effect ICs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Interview Pearl:** When asked *"Why are automotive TPS signals 0–5 V rather than 4–20 mA like industrial sensors?"* — answer: *because automotive ECUs run on a single 5 V rail and use ratiometric ADC, eliminating ground-loop errors across the wiring harness.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ratiometric Output of a Potentiometric TPS

**Setup:** A linear potentiometer of total resistance $R_{total}$ is connected between the reference supply $V_{ref}$ and ground. The wiper divides the element into $R_x$ (top portion, between $V_{ref}$ terminal and wiper) and $R_{total} - R_x$ (bottom portion, between wiper and GND). The wiper terminal delivers $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit:**

The potentiometer is modelled as two series resistors, $R_x$ and $R_{total} - R_x$, forming a classic voltage divider. No current is drawn at the wiper (high-impedance ADC input of ECU, $R_{in} > 1 \,\text{M}\Omega$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_x + (R_{total} - R_x)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Resistance Ratio to Mechanical Angle:**

For a uniformly wound track of angular range $[0, \theta_{max}]$, resistance is proportional to length, so:

$$\frac{R_x}{R_{total}} = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Linear Calibration Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out}(\theta) \;=\; \left(\frac{V_{WOTV} - V_{CTV}}{\theta_{max}}\right)\theta \;+\; V_{CTV}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** A linear characteristic with slope $k_1 = (V_{WOTV} - V_{CTV})/\theta_{max}$ and y-intercept $V_{CTV}$. The closed-throttle voltage is *not zero* (typically 0.45 V) because the throttle plate is designed to sit slightly above the absolute zero to prevent the ECU from misinterpreting a noisy zero signal as a fault.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dual-Track TPS Sum-Check Rationality Test

**Setup:** Two independent resistive tracks, mechanically geared to move *in opposite phase*, share the same $V_{ref}$ and GND. Their outputs are $V_{out1}$ and $V_{out2}$.

**Step 1 — Define Opposite-Phase Relation:**

$$\frac{R_{x1}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}, \qquad \frac{R_{x2}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the Two Outputs:**

$$V_{out1} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}, \qquad V_{out2} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Two Outputs:**

$$V_{out1} + V_{out2} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}} + V_{ref} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out1} + V_{out2} = V_{ref}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Practical Tolerance Window:**

$$\Delta V_{sum} \;=\; \left\vert V_{out1} + V_{out2} - V_{ref} \right\vert \;\le\; \Delta V_{tol} \;=\; 0.10 \,\text{V}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This *sum-check* allows the ECU to detect a *short-circuit*, *open-circuit*, *wiper lift*, or *stuck sensor* condition without an extra wiring harness. If only one track fails, the ECU can still limp-home using the second track while illuminating the MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) and storing DTC P0120.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall-Effect TPS Output Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Hall Element Geometry:**

A thin semiconductor plate of thickness $t$ carries a bias current $I$ along the x-axis. A magnetic flux density $B$ perpendicular to the plate produces a Lorentz force on the charge carriers, deflecting them in the y-direction. This charge separation creates a transverse Hall voltage.

**Step 2 — Hall Voltage Equation:**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \, I \, B}{t}$$

Where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient of the semiconductor material (for GaAs, $R_H \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2} \,\text{m}^3/\text{C}$).

**Step 3 — Magnetic Field as a Function of Throttle Angle:**

A diametrically magnetised ring rotating with the shaft produces a sinusoidally varying perpendicular field component at the Hall IC:

$$B(\theta) = B_0 \cos(\theta - \theta_0)$$

Where $B_0$ is the peak flux density and $\theta_0$ is the mechanical offset (set during assembly to align the zero point).

**Step 4 — Combine to Obtain the Hall Voltage:**

$$V_H(\theta) = \frac{R_H \, I \, B_0}{t} \cos(\theta - \theta_0)$$

**Step 5 — Signal Conditioning (Offset Removal + Gain):**

The IC's internal op-amps add a DC offset $V_{off}$ and apply a gain $A_v$ to shift the bipolar sinusoid into the unipolar 0.45 V – 4.50 V range:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = A_v \cdot V_H(\theta) + V_{off}$$

**Step 6 — ArcTan Lookup for Linearity:**

The ECU applies the inverse trigonometric function to recover the angle with sub-degree accuracy:

$$\theta = \theta_0 + \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{out} - V_{off}}{A_v \, V_{H,0}}\right)$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**

A linear potentiometric TPS has $R_{total} = 5 \,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{ref} = 5.0 \,\text{V}$, and produces $V_{CTV} = 0.45 \,\text{V}$ at $\theta = 0^\circ$ and $V_{WOTV} = 4.55 \,\text{V}$ at $\theta = 88^\circ$. Determine: (a) the calibration slope $k_1$, (b) the resistance $R_x$ when the throttle is at $45^\circ$, and (c) the voltage the ECU reads when the driver is at $50\%$ pedal travel.

**Solution:**

**(a) Slope:**

$$k_1 = \frac{V_{WOTV} - V_{CTV}}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{4.55 - 0.45}{88 - 0} = \frac{4.10}{88}$$

$$\boxed{\,k_1 = 0.04659 \,\text{V/deg} \;\approx\; 46.6 \,\text{mV/deg}\,}$$

**(b) Resistance at $\theta = 45^\circ$:**

$$\frac{R_x}{R_{total}} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{CTV}}{V_{WOTV} - V_{CTV}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_{out}(45^\circ) = 0.45 + 46.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 45$$

$$V_{out}(45^\circ) = 0.45 + 2.097 = 2.547 \,\text{V}$$

$$R_x = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot R_{total} = \frac{2.547}{5.0} \times 5000$$

$$\boxed{\,R_x = 2547 \,\Omega \approx 2.55 \,\text{k}\Omega\,}$$

**(c) Voltage at $50\%$ Pedal Travel:**

Assuming $50\%$ pedal maps linearly to $44^\circ$ mechanical (a common OEM ratio):

$$V_{out} = 0.45 + 46.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 44$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = 2.500 \,\text{V}\,}$$

This is the *midpoint* of the sensor's usable range, exactly half of $V_{ref}$ — a useful sanity check.

---

### 3.5 Master Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a TPS Topology

| Application / Constraint | Recommended TPS | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Budget two-wheeler, BS-IV era | Potentiometric | Lowest cost; adequate life for the duty cycle |
| BS-VI passenger car with DBW | Dual Hall-effect | Mandatory redundancy for ASIL-B; non-contact |
| Heavy-duty diesel (off-road, mining) | Sealed Hall-effect | Vibration and dust tolerance |
| Motorsport / F1 | Inductive or TMR | Highest bandwidth, lowest latency |
| Hybrid/EV regenerative braking | Hall-effect pedal sensor | Plausibility check between pedal and motor torque |
| Aerospace actuator | Triple-redundant Hall | ASIL-D / DAL-A redundancy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TPS in the Engine Management Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Cable or Electronic Pedal Sensor]
    B --> C[Throttle Body Assembly]
    C --> D[Butterfly Valve Shaft]
    D --> E[TPS Potentiometric Track]
    D --> F[TPS Hall Effect IC Ring]
    E --> G[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    F --> H[16 bit ADC inside Hall IC]
    G --> I[ECU Analog Input Pin]
    H --> J[ECU SPI or SENT Decoder]
    I --> K[ECU Processor]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Validity Check}
    L -->|Sum Check Pass| M[Fuel Injector Driver]
    L -->|Sum Check Pass| N[Ignition Coil Driver]
    L -->|Sum Check Pass| O[ETB Motor Driver]
    L -->|Sum Check Fail| P[DTC Stored and MIL ON]
    L -->|Sum Check Fail| Q[Fail Safe Limp Home Mode]
    M --> R[Engine Combustion]
    N --> R
    O --> S[Throttle Plate Repositioned]
    R --> T[Tailpipe Exhaust]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Dual-Track Potentiometric TPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VREF[5V Reference from ECU] --> R1[Upper Resistive Track]
    GND[0V Ground from ECU] --> R2[Lower Resistive Track]
    R1 --> W1[Wiper 1 on Throttle Shaft]
    R2 --> W2[Wiper 2 Reverse Geared]
    W1 --> A1[Signal Output 1 to ECU ADC Channel 1]
    W2 --> A2[Signal Output 2 to ECU ADC Channel 2]
    A1 --> SUM[ECU Sum Check Diagnostic]
    A2 --> SUM
    SUM -->|Sum equals 5V| OK[Signal Valid]
    SUM -->|Sum deviates by more than 0.10V| ERR[DTC P0120 Latched]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hall-Effect TPS Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SHAFT[Throttle Shaft] --> MAG[Diametrically Magnetised Ring]
    MAG --> HALL[Hall Effect IC on PCB]
    HALL --> AMP[Internal Chopper Stabilised Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC12[12 bit Delta Sigma ADC]
    ADC12 --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> OUT1[Analog Ratiometric 0.5 to 4.5 V Output]
    DSP --> OUT2[SENT Frame on Same Pin]
    DSP --> OUT3[SPI Frame for OEM Calibration]
    OUT1 --> ECU[Engine Control Unit]
    OUT2 --> ECU
    OUT3 --> ECU
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — TPS Failure-Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[ECU Receives TPS Voltage] --> CHECK{Voltage within 0.40 to 4.80 V?}
    CHECK -->|No| OOR[Out of Range DTC P0122 or P0123]
    CHECK -->|Yes| RATE{Delta V per delta t within expected range?}
    RATE -->|No| STUCK[Sensor Stuck DTC P0121]
    RATE -->|Yes| SUM{Dual track sum equals 5V plus or minus 0.10V?}
    SUM -->|No| RATIONALITY[Rationality DTC P0120]
    SUM -->|Yes| GLITCH{Glitch filter passed for 200 ms?}
    GLITCH -->|No| TRANSIENT[Transient Noise Ignored]
    GLITCH -->|Yes| ACCEPTED[Signal Accepted for Control Loop]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a Throttle Position Sensor. List its two primary functions in a fuel-injected engine.

**Model Answer:**

> A **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** is a rotary position transducer coupled to the throttle butterfly-valve shaft that delivers a ratiometric DC voltage proportional to the throttle opening angle to the Engine Control Unit (ECU).
>
> The two primary functions are:
> 1. **Input to fuel injection ECU:** The ECU uses TPS voltage (and its rate of change $dV/dt$) to compute injection pulse width, acceleration enrichment, and deceleration fuel cut-off.
> 2. **Input to ignition control:** TPS angle enables the ECU to look up the optimum spark advance from a 3-D map (RPM × TPS × MAP).
> *(Additional valid functions: idle-speed control handoff, transmission shift scheduling, DTC rationality check.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Differentiate between a potentiometric and a Hall-effect Throttle Position Sensor in terms of construction, output, and durability.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Potentiometric TPS | Hall-Effect TPS |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Carbon/cermet resistive track + sliding wiper | Magnet on shaft + stationary Hall IC |
| Output | Analog 0.45 – 4.55 V | Analog 0.5 – 4.5 V or SENT/SPI |
| Wear | Yes (track erosion, wiper fatigue) | None (non-contact) |
| Durability | ~10 M cycles | >100 M cycles |
| Resolution | ~10 bits (wiper granularity) | 12 – 16 bits |
| Cost | Low | Medium |

**(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** *Answer **ONE** full question. Each carries 14 marks, split 7 + 7.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **potentiometric Throttle Position Sensor**. Show how the output voltage is related to the throttle angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **dual-track (redundant) TPS architecture** and derive the *sum-check* rationality condition used by the ECU to detect faults. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
The potentiometric TPS consists of three major parts:

1. A **ceramic or PCB substrate** carrying a precision-printed **resistive film** (carbon-polymer or cermet) of total resistance $R_{total} \approx 1$ to $5 \,\text{k}\Omega$.
2. A **spring-loaded wiper arm** mechanically locked to the throttle butterfly shaft. The wiper is a precious-metal alloy contact (typically Au-Ni or Pd-Ir) for low noise and corrosion resistance.
3. A **three-terminal housing** sealed against moisture and dust, with terminals for $V_{ref}$, GND, and $V_{out}$.

**Working (3 Marks):**
The resistive track is energised by a regulated **5 V reference** from the ECU. As the driver depresses the accelerator, the throttle shaft rotates, dragging the wiper along the track. This creates a variable voltage divider:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{total}}$$

The output is a smooth, monotonic, almost linear function of the throttle angle θ:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = k_1 \, \theta + V_{CTV}$$

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to §4.2 — the dual-track potentiometric TPS schematic.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Dual-Track Architecture (3 Marks):**

In critical applications, **two independent resistive tracks** share the same shaft but move in **opposite phase** through a reverse-gear mechanism. Track 1 (the *primary*) produces $V_{out1}$ that *increases* with throttle opening, while Track 2 (the *secondary*) produces $V_{out2}$ that *decreases*. Both share the same $V_{ref}$ and GND.

**Derivation of Sum-Check (3 Marks):**

$$V_{out1} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}, \qquad V_{out2} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

Adding:

$$V_{out1} + V_{out2} = V_{ref} \cdot \left[\frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}} + 1 - \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}\right] = V_{ref}$$

Therefore, for a *healthy* sensor:

$$\left\vert V_{out1} + V_{out2} - V_{ref} \right\vert \le 0.10 \,\text{V}$$

If this sum-check fails (open circuit, short to ground, wiper lift, water ingress), the ECU stores **DTC P0120** and may revert to a *limp-home* fixed fueling map.

**Fault-Handling Strategy (1 Mark):**

- Single-track failure → use the surviving track, illuminate MIL, default fueling map.
- Both-track failure → engine shuts down or runs at fixed idle (2000 RPM limp mode).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Hall-effect Throttle Position Sensor** with the help of a block diagram. Derive its output transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **potentiometric, Hall-effect, and magneto-resistive** TPS technologies in a tabular form, listing at least six comparison parameters. Discuss the **closed-throttle position (CTP) detection** logic used by the ECU. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**

A Hall-effect TPS consists of:

1. A **diametrically magnetised permanent-magnet ring** rigidly mounted on the throttle shaft. The magnet produces a radial field that rotates with the shaft.
2. A **stationary Hall-effect IC** mounted on a PCB facing the magnet at a precisely controlled air-gap (typically 1.5 – 3 mm). The IC integrates the Hall element, a chopper-stabilised amplifier, a 12 – 16 bit ΔΣ ADC, and a digital signal processor.
3. A **sealed connector** delivering $V_{ref}$, GND, and either an analog output or a SENT/SPI digital frame.

**Working (3 Marks):**

As the shaft rotates, the perpendicular component of magnetic flux density $B$ at the Hall IC varies sinusoidally with angle:

$$B(\theta) = B_0 \cos(\theta - \theta_0)$$

The Hall element produces a transverse voltage:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \, I \, B}{t}$$

The IC amplifies, digitises, and linearises this signal, then outputs:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = A_v \, V_H(\theta) + V_{off}$$

with $A_v$ and $V_{off}$ chosen so that $V_{out}$ spans **0.5 V (closed) to 4.5 V (WOT)**.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to §4.3.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Potentiometric | Hall-Effect | Magneto-Resistive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Resistive divider | Hall voltage | Resistance change |
| Contact | Sliding wiper | Non-contact | Non-contact |
| Resolution | ~10 bits | 12–16 bits | 12–16 bits |
| Lifetime | 10 M cycles | >100 M cycles | >100 M cycles |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Temperature drift | Moderate | Low (compensated) | Very low |
| EMI immunity | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium–High |

**Closed-Throttle Position (CTP) Detection Logic (3 Marks):**

The ECU implements CTP detection through a **hysteresis-bounded comparator**:

1. **Idle Threshold ($V_{idle,low}$)** = **0.80 V**. Below this, the ECU declares *throttle closed* and transfers authority to the **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** or electronic throttle body.
2. **Off-Idle Threshold ($V_{idle,high}$)** = **1.20 V**. Above this, the ECU declares *throttle open* and resumes main fueling.
3. The **0.40 V dead-band** prevents hunting between the two states during pedal vibration at idle.
4. A separate **CTP switch** (in older systems) provided a hard digital *idle* signal; modern systems rely solely on the analog threshold.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention 5 V reference and 0.45 V / 4.5 V end-points** — costs 1 mark almost every time. Memorise them.
> 2. **Confusing TPS with MAF/MAP sensor** — TPS measures *angle*, MAF measures *air mass*, MAP measures *manifold pressure*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 3. **Drawing the potentiometer as a rheostat instead of a voltage divider** — always show all three terminals ($V_{ref}$, GND, $V_{out}$).
> 4. **Skipping the sum-check derivation** in dual-track questions — it is a 3-mark mandatory sub-step.
> 5. **Calling Hall-effect TPS "magnetic" without specifying the Hall voltage equation** — generic answers lose the *application* marks.
> 6. **Omitting the unit (V/deg) in the slope** $k_1$ — a 0.5-mark deduction is standard.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Carry:**

- 🔹 **Definition:** TPS = rotary position transducer on throttle shaft; output is ratiometric 0.45–4.55 V (potentiometric) or 0.50–4.50 V (Hall).
- 🔹 **Reference:** Always **5.0 V** from ECU; sensor is *ratiometric*, so ground shifts cancel.
- 🔹 **Two end-points:** $V_{CTV} \approx 0.45 \,\text{V}$ (closed), $V_{WOTV} \approx 4.55 \,\text{V}$ (wide open).
- 🔹 **Slope:** $k_1 \approx 40 \,\text{mV/deg}$ for an ~88° throttle travel.
- 🔹 **Dual-track sum-check:** $V_{out1} + V_{out2} = V_{ref} \pm 0.10 \,\text{V}$.
- 🔹 **Hall voltage:** $V_H = R_H \, I \, B / t$; varies as $\cos(\theta - \theta_0)$.
- 🔹 **CTP detection:** threshold ~0.80 V with 0.40 V hysteresis.
- 🔹 **ECU uses TPS for:** injection pulse width, spark advance, idle handoff, AT shift, traction control plausibility.
- 🔹 **Diagnostic codes:** P0120 (rationality), P0121 (range/performance), P0122 (low input), P0123 (high input).
- 🔹 **Drive-by-wire redundancy:** *Two* Hall-effect TPS (plus *two* pedal sensors) for ASIL-D.
- 🔹 **Aging failure mode:** pot-track wear → open circuit at one extreme → MIL on, limp mode.
- 🔹 **Hall failure mode:** magnet demagnetisation at >150 °C → non-linearity → DTC P0121.
- 🔹 **Most-tested topic pair:** *potentiometric vs. Hall-effect* + *sum-check derivation* — appears in 80 % of past KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solenoids etc.
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solenoids etc.

# 🔌 Solenoids & Related Electromagnetic Actuators in Automobiles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 2 – Actuators and Switching Devices* and is a high-frequency area for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions in End Semester Evaluations (ESE).

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Solenoid** is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical motion through the generation of a controlled magnetic field. In the automotive context, a solenoid is essentially a helical coil of insulated copper wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger (armature). When current flows through the coil, the resulting magnetomotive force (MMF) pulls the plunger into the coil's center, performing work such as engaging a starter motor, shifting a transmission gear, or opening a fuel injector valve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition (verbatim style):** *"A solenoid is a cylindrical coil of wire acting as an electromagnet when energized, used in automobiles as a remotely controlled switch or linear actuator to convert electrical signals into mechanical displacement."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🍺 Beer-Tap Analogy:** Imagine the handle of a beer tap. You give it a small push (electrical signal → coil energised), and a hidden mechanism (the plunger) moves to release a much larger flow (the starter motor drawing 200+ Amps). A solenoid does exactly this — a tiny control current from the ignition switch is amplified into a massive mechanical action (closing heavy-duty contacts to crank the engine).
>
> A simpler analogy: **A solenoid is the "muscle" of an automobile** — the driver's command is the "brain signal" (low current), and the solenoid's magnetic force is the "muscle" (high-force mechanical action).

### 1.3 Classification of Automotive Solenoids

> [!NOTE]
> Automotive solenoids are broadly classified into **three functional categories**:

| Type | Primary Function | Typical Current Rating | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pull-Type (Linear)** | Plunger is pulled into coil | **40 – 400 A** (starter) | Starter motor engagement |
| **Push-Type (Linear)** | Plunger is pushed out | **0.5 – 5 A** | Door lock actuators |
| **Rotary Solenoid** | Produces rotational motion | **1 – 10 A** | Idle Air Control Valve |

### 1.4 The "etc." — Companion Electromagnetic Devices

The phrase **"Solenoids etc."** in KTU Module 2 typically bundles the following switching/actuating devices together, as they share the same electromagnetic operating principle:

- **Relays** – Low-power electromagnetic switches.
- **Buzzers** – Audio warning devices based on electromagnetic vibration.
- **Stepper Motors** – Used in instrument clusters, idle control, and HVAC flaps.
- **Piezo buzzers (active/passive)** – Reverse parking sensors, seatbelt reminders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these devices operate on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Principle** — the two foundational laws KTU examiners love to test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of an Automotive Solenoid

A starter solenoid (the most commonly asked type in KTU exams) has the following layered construction:

1. **Outer Cylindrical Housing** – Made of mild steel; forms part of the magnetic flux path.
2. **Helical Copper Coil** – Several hundred turns of enameled copper wire (Class H insulation rated to **180 °C**).
3. **Movable Plunger (Armature)** – Soft iron core, chrome-plated for corrosion resistance.
4. **Return Spring** – Pushes plunger back when de-energized.
5. **Two Heavy-Duty Copper Contacts** – One connected to battery (+) and other to starter motor terminal.
6. **Two Light-Duty Control Terminals** – 'S' (Start) and 'R' (Return / Ignition Resistor Bypass).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" and "How" for each step:**

**Step 1 — Energisation:**
When the ignition key is turned to the START position, control current (typically **0.5 – 1.5 A**) from the key switch flows through the pull-in winding of the solenoid coil.

**Step 2 — MMF Generation:**
The current through *N* turns produces a magnetomotive force:
$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I \quad [\text{ampere-turns, AT}]$$
This MMF establishes a magnetic flux in the ferromagnetic circuit.

**Step 3 — Plunger Movement:**
The flux lines prefer to close through the low-reluctance iron plunger. The resulting magnetic force overcomes the return spring force and pulls the plunger axially into the coil.

**Step 4 — Contact Closure:**
The plunger's mechanical motion drives a contact disc that bridges the two heavy-duty copper terminals, completing the high-current circuit (**200 – 400 A**) between the battery and starter motor.

**Step 5 — Hold-In Mode:**
A second, higher-resistance winding (the **hold-in winding**) keeps the plunger seated after contact closure. This dual-winding design drastically reduces battery drain during cranking.

**Step 6 — De-energisation:**
When the key returns to the ON position, the coil is de-energised, the spring returns the plunger, and the heavy contacts open.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}}$ | Magnetic pull force | N (Newton) | Solenoid force calculation |
| 2 | $B = \mu_{0} \cdot \mu_{r} \cdot H$ | Flux density in core | Tesla (T) | Magnetic circuit design |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{l}$ | Magnetising force | AT/m | Coil design |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} \cdot I^{2} \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^{2}}$ | Pull force at air gap *g* | N | Starter solenoid sizing |
| 5 | $\text{MMF} = \phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$ | Hopkinson's law | AT | Magnetic circuit analysis |
| 6 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} \cdot A}{l}$ | Inductance of solenoid | Henry (H) | Time constant τ = L/R |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant | seconds | Plunger response time |
| 8 | $V = I \cdot R + L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Solenoid coil voltage equation | Volt | Transient analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values to Memorise:**
> - $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ (permeability of free space)
> - $\mu_{r}$ for soft iron plunger ≈ **2000 – 5000**
> - Battery voltage (automotive) = **12 V DC** (modern cars use **48 V** mild-hybrid systems)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> In modern **ECU-controlled vehicles**, solenoids are no longer just simple ON/OFF devices. They are **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** to give proportional control:
> - **Variable Valve Timing (VVT) solenoids** — duty cycle 0–100 %.
> - **EGR valve solenoid** — controls exhaust gas recirculation rate.
> - **Transmission shift solenoid** — gear-change smoothness in AT/CVT.
> - **ABS brake pressure modulator solenoids** — operate at **up to 60 Hz** for anti-skid control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Magnetic Force on a Solenoid Plunger

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU university exams (typically 5–7 marks inside a 14-mark question).

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Energy Stored in Magnetic Field

The energy stored in the magnetic field of the air gap (where the magnetic force is generated) is given by:

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot g$$

where:
- $W$ = energy stored in the air gap (Joules)
- $B$ = flux density in the gap (Tesla)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the plunger (m²)
- $g$ = length of the air gap (metres)
- $\mu_{0}$ = permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

### 3.1.2 Mechanical Work Done by the Plunger

If the plunger moves a small distance $dg$ into the coil, the gap decreases by $dg$, and the magnetic energy decreases by $dW$. By the **principle of virtual work**, this decrease in energy equals the mechanical work done:

$$F \cdot dg = -\left( \frac{dW}{dg} \right) \cdot dg$$

Taking the derivative (assuming $B$ and $A$ remain approximately constant during the small displacement):

$$\frac{dW}{dg} = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}}$$

### 3.1.3 Final Force Equation

Therefore, the magnetic force pulling the plunger is:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}} \quad [\text{Newtons}]}$$

### 3.1.4 Expressing Force in Terms of Coil Parameters

For an air-core solenoid, the flux density at the centre of the coil is:

$$B = \mu_{0} \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{l}$$

Substituting this back into the force equation:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} \cdot I^{2} \cdot A}{2 \cdot l^{2}} \quad [\text{Newtons}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Engineering Insight:** The force $F$ is proportional to **$N^{2}$** and **$I^{2}$**. Doubling the number of turns quadruples the force, and doubling the current also quadruples the force. This is why starter solenoids use **high current + high turns** to generate the massive force needed to push the pinion into the flywheel ring gear.

## 3.2 Derivation of Solenoid Inductance

Starting from the basic definition of inductance:

$$L = \frac{N \cdot \phi}{I}$$

The flux through a single turn of the solenoid:

$$\phi = B \cdot A = \mu_{0} \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{l} \cdot A$$

Multiplying by N for total flux linkage:

$$N \cdot \phi = \mu_{0} \cdot \frac{N^{2} \cdot I \cdot A}{l}$$

Dividing by current $I$:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} \cdot A}{l} \quad [\text{Henries}]}$$

## 3.3 Time Constant of Solenoid Actuation

When voltage $V$ is applied to a solenoid coil of inductance $L$ and resistance $R$, the current rises exponentially:

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

The mechanical actuation time $t_a$ (time to reach pull-in current $I_p$) is:

$$t_a = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V/R}{V/R - I_p}\right) = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - \frac{I_p R}{V}}\right)$$

where $\tau = L/R$ is the **electrical time constant**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A starter solenoid has $N = 200$ turns, length $l = 0.05$ m, plunger cross-sectional area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m², and carries a current $I = 1.5$ A. Calculate:
1. The flux density $B$ at the coil centre.
2. The pull force $F$ on the plunger.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Flux density:**

$$B = \mu_{0} \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{l} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{200 \times 1.5}{0.05}$$

$$B = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (6000)$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 7.54 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{T} = 7.54\ \text{mT}}$$

**Step 2 — Pull force:**

$$F = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}} = \frac{(7.54 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$F = \frac{(5.685 \times 10^{-5}) \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.274 \times 10^{-8}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 9.05 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N} \approx 9\ \text{mN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** This is the force for an *air-core* solenoid. In reality, with a soft iron plunger ($\mu_r \approx 3000$), the force becomes approximately **3000× larger**, easily exceeding **25 N**, which is sufficient to close the heavy-duty contacts and engage the starter motor.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Pull-Type Solenoid | Push-Type Solenoid | Relay | Buzzer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plunger motion | Into coil | Out of coil | Lever arm | Vibrating diaphragm |
| Coil resistance | **Very low** (< 1 Ω) | Medium (10–50 Ω) | Medium (50–200 Ω) | Medium |
| Typical current | **200 – 400 A** | 0.5 – 5 A | 0.05 – 0.5 A | 0.05 – 0.2 A |
| Force/Output | Very high mechanical | Medium | Low (contact closure) | Sound ≈ 80 – 100 dB |
| Automotive use | Starter | Door lock | ECU switching | Seatbelt warning |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Starter Solenoid Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition Key START Position] --> B[Low Current Flow 0.5A through Control Winding]
    B --> C[Magnetic Field Generated inside Coil]
    C --> D{Plunger Force vs Spring Force}
    D -- Exceeds --> E[Plunger Pulled into Coil]
    E --> F[Contact Disc Closes Heavy Terminals]
    F --> G[High Current 200-400A to Starter Motor]
    G --> H[Pinion Engages Flywheel Ring Gear]
    H --> I[Engine Cranks]
    I --> J[Key Released]
    J --> K[Coil De energised]
    K --> L[Return Spring Pushes Plunger Back]
    L --> M[Contacts Open Starter Disengages]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual-Winding Solenoid Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> SW1[Ignition Switch START]
    SW1 --> S_terminal[Terminal S]
    S_terminal --> PIW[Pull In Winding 0.5 Ohm]
    PIW --> R_terminal[Terminal R]
    R_terminal --> GND[Chassis Ground]
    PIW --> HIW[Hold In Winding 5 Ohm in parallel]
    HIW --> GND
    PIW --> PLUNGER[Movable Plunger]
    PLUNGER -->|mechanical link| CONTACT[Heavy Duty Contacts]
    CONTACT --> STARTER[Starter Motor Terminal M]
    STARTER --> GND
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOUSING[Steel Housing Magnetic Return Path]
        COIL[Helical Copper Coil N turns]
        SPRING[Return Spring]
        PLUNGER[Soft Iron Plunger Movable]
        CONTACTS[Two Copper Contacts Heavy Duty]
        GAP[Air Gap g mm]
    end
    COIL -->|when energised| GAP
    GAP -->|flux lines| PLUNGER
    PLUNGER --> SPRING
    PLUNGER -->|mechanical coupling| CONTACTS
```

## 4.4 Solenoid Family Tree — Automotive Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOL[AUTOMOTIVE SOLENOIDS]
    SOL --> START[Starter Solenoid Pull Type 200A]
    SOL --> SHIFT[Transmission Shift Solenoid PWM]
    SOL --> DOOR[Door Lock Solenoid Push Type]
    SOL --> FUEL[Fuel Injector Solenoid 12V Pulse]
    SOL --> EGR[EGR Valve Solenoid Vacuum Control]
    SOL --> VVT[VVT Solenoid Variable Valve Timing]
    SOL --> ABS[ABS Modulator Solenoid 60 Hz]
    SOL --> IDLE[Idle Air Control Solenoid]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Literacy Tip:** Always draw **closed loops** for magnetic flux (North → South → through iron). Examiners deduct marks if you show the field lines going "into thin air" — they MUST complete through the ferromagnetic return path.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a solenoid. List any four automotive applications of solenoids.**

**Model Answer:**
A solenoid is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical motion via a magnetic field generated by a current-carrying coil. It consists of a helical coil wound around a movable soft-iron plunger.

**Four automotive applications:**
1. Starter motor engagement (cranking the engine).
2. Automatic transmission gear shifting.
3. Door lock/unlock actuators.
4. Fuel injector pulse control in diesel/petrol engines.
*(Other valid answers: EGR valve control, ABS pressure modulation, idle speed control.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Naming "starter solenoid" alone will fetch full marks only if accompanied by a one-line use. Listing four is a safe KTU strategy.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a solenoid and a relay. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Solenoid | Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Primary output | Linear mechanical motion | Electrical contact closure |
| Function | Actuator | Switch |
| Contact rating | Very high current (200+ A) | Low to medium current (< 30 A) |
| Construction | Coil + movable plunger | Coil + armature lever |
| Use | Starter, door lock | ECU logic switching |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B — CHOICE A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **starter solenoid** used in an automobile. Discuss the role of the **pull-in winding** and **hold-in winding**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A starter solenoid consists of:
1. A cylindrical steel housing acting as the magnetic flux return path.
2. Two coaxial windings — a heavy **pull-in winding** (low resistance, ~0.5 Ω, few turns of thick wire) and a lighter **hold-in winding** (high resistance, ~5 Ω, many turns of thin wire) — connected in series between terminals **'S'** and **'R'**.
3. A soft-iron movable plunger connected mechanically to a contact disc.
4. Two heavy-duty copper contacts: one to battery (+), one to starter motor (terminal **'M'**).
5. A return spring to reset the plunger when de-energised.

**Working (3 marks):**

When the ignition key is turned to START:
- Current flows through **both** pull-in and hold-in windings (in series) → strong magnetic field → plunger is pulled in with high force.
- Plunger motion closes the heavy contacts → battery is connected to starter motor → engine cranks.
- Simultaneously, the pull-in winding is **short-circuited** by the closed contacts → only the hold-in winding remains active → just enough force to keep plunger seated → reduces battery drain and prevents coil overheating.

**Role Summary (1 mark):**
- **Pull-in winding:** Provides the high initial force to overcome spring + contact gap + pinion engagement load.
- **Hold-in winding:** Maintains plunger position during cranking at low current draw.

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **magnetic force on a solenoid plunger** in terms of flux density and area. A starter solenoid has **N = 300 turns**, length **l = 6 cm**, plunger area **A = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m²** and is energised by **I = 2 A**. Find the pull force assuming an air-core approximation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks) — as derived in Section 3.1:**

The energy stored in the air gap of length $g$ and area $A$ is:

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot g$$

Applying virtual work principle for plunger displacement $dg$:

$$F \cdot dg = -dW \implies F = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}}$$

Substituting $B = \mu_{0} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot I}{l}$:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} \cdot I^{2} \cdot A}{2l^{2}}}$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**

Given: $N = 300$, $l = 0.06$ m, $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $I = 2$ A, $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

$$F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (300)^{2} \cdot (2)^{2} \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (0.06)^{2}}$$

$$F = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 90000 \cdot 4 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot 0.0036}$$

$$F = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 180}{7.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.262 \times 10^{-4}}{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{N} = 31.4\ \text{mN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, with $\mu_r = 3000$ for iron, $F \approx 31.4 \times 3000 = 94.2$ N, which is sufficient for starter application.

---

### **Question B — CHOICE B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **working of a buzzer** used in automobiles for warning indications. How does it differ from a relay? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of an Automotive Buzzer (5 marks):**
A buzzer is an electromagnetic audio signalling device. Its construction:
- An electromagnet (coil + iron core)
- A flexible spring-steel diaphragm placed near the electromagnet
- A pair of contacts in series with the coil

When energised:
1. Current flows through the coil → magnetic field attracts the diaphragm.
2. Diaphragm movement **breaks the contact** → coil de-energises.
3. Diaphragm springs back → contact recloses → coil energises again.
4. Cycle repeats rapidly (~100–400 Hz) → produces an audible tone.

In automobiles, buzzers are used for:
- Seatbelt unfastened warning.
- Headlights-ON reminder.
- Door-ajar indication.
- Reverse parking alert (combined with ultrasonic sensors).

**Difference from Relay (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Buzzer | Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Sound (vibration) | Contact switching |
| Operating frequency | Oscillates continuously | Stable single action |
| Diaphragm | Yes (essential) | No |

---

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **electronic flasher unit** using a relay-based buzzer principle. Why are LEDs increasingly replacing incandescent bulbs in modern cars, and how does this affect flasher relay design? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> FL[Electronic Flasher IC LM3909 or Bimetallic strip]
    FL -->|PWM 1 Hz| RL[Relay Coil]
    RL -->|mechanical| SW[Relay Contact]
    SW --> LP[Turn Signal Lamp 21W]
    LP --> GND[Ground]
    FL --> BUZZ[Buzzer Click Generator]
    BUZZ --> DRV[Audio Driver]
```

In a conventional thermal flasher:
- Current through a **bimetallic strip** heats it.
- Strip bends → contact opens → lamp goes OFF → strip cools → contact closes → lamp ON.
- The "click-click" is the audible buzzer feedback.

**LED Impact (4 marks):**
- LEDs draw **~10× less current** (≈ 0.1 A vs. 2 A for incandescent).
- Bimetallic thermal flashers **fail to operate** below a minimum current → no flashing.
- Solution: **Electronic flasher IC** with fixed-frequency PWM (~1.4 Hz, 50 % duty cycle) and parallel resistor load to mimic incandescent load.
- Modern cars use **CAN-bus controlled LED flasher modules** integrated into the BCM (Body Control Module).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units** — Writing "$F = 9.05 \times 10^{-3}$" without writing the **Newton** unit. Always write the unit; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing pull-in and hold-in winding roles** — Many students write that *both* windings carry the same current throughout. Wrong! Pull-in is **short-circuited** after contact closure.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation text** — Writing the equation $F = B^2A/2\mu_0$ without showing the virtual work step is treated as a "mugging" answer and gets **partial credit only** (2 out of 5).
> 4. **Not labelling diagram parts** — A neat sketch without labels = **0 marks** in KTU even if the diagram is correct. Always label: Coil, Plunger, Spring, Contacts, Terminals S/R/M.
> 5. **Mixing up solenoid with relay definition** — A solenoid's primary output is **mechanical motion**, not just contact switching. Examiners are strict on this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 High-Density Revision Checklist — Solenoids etc.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Solenoid = coil + movable plunger; converts electrical → linear mechanical energy.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $F = \dfrac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I^{2} A}{2 l^{2}}$ — must be memorised verbatim.
- ✅ **Standard Constant:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m — used in almost every numerical.
- ✅ **Dual-Winding Trick:** Pull-in (low R, high current) + Hold-in (high R, low current) → efficient starter operation.
- ✅ **Automotive Voltage:** **12 V DC** nominal; **48 V** in mild-hybrid systems.
- ✅ **Starter Current:** **200 – 400 A** — the highest current device in a conventional car.
- ✅ **Buzzer Frequency:** ~100–400 Hz oscillation, produces sound via diaphragm vibration.
- ✅ **Relay vs Solenoid:** Relay = switch (electrical output); Solenoid = actuator (mechanical output).
- ✅ **PWM Solenoids:** Modern engine/transmission solenoids use Pulse Width Modulation for proportional control.
- ✅ **LED Flasher Issue:** Low LED current → thermal flasher fails → electronic flasher IC required.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Frequency:** This topic has appeared **every semester** since 2022 in at least one 14-mark question.
- ✅ **Application Mnemonic — "SLED FAC":** **S**tarter, **L**ocks, **E**GR, **D**oor, **F**uel injector, **A**BS, **C**VT shift.

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Last-Minute Strategy:** Memorise the force derivation (Section 3.1) word-by-word. KTU examiners award full 7 marks only when every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. Practice at least one numerical based on $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / 2l^2$ before every exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Engine Control Including Open Loop and Closed Loop Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Engine Control Including Open Loop and Closed Loop Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Engine Control Algorithms — Formal Definition

An **Engine Control Algorithm** is a deterministic, rule-based computational procedure (typically executed on the Engine Control Unit / ECU) that processes inputs from various sensors (crankshaft position, MAP, MAF, lambda, coolant temperature, etc.) and generates actuator commands (injector pulse width, ignition dwell, idle air valve position, EGR valve, etc.) to maintain optimal engine performance across all operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT744 Module 2):**
> Engine control algorithms define the *decision logic* that maps sensor inputs to actuator outputs. They are categorised into **Open Loop** (feed-forward, no feedback) and **Closed Loop** (feedback-based, self-correcting) architectures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control Story

Imagine you are driving a car at a constant **80 km/h**:

- **Open Loop Analogy (The Blind Driver):** A driver who is blindfolded sets the accelerator pedal at a *fixed position* and never adjusts it, regardless of whether the car is going uphill, downhill, or on a flat road. The car *will* deviate from 80 km/h. There is no feedback about actual speed.

- **Closed Loop Analogy (The Attentive Driver):** A driver constantly *looks at the speedometer* (the feedback sensor), compares it with 80 km/h (the setpoint), and adjusts the pedal. The error = (Desired − Actual) is minimised continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Open loop is **fast but inaccurate**; Closed loop is **accurate but slightly slower** due to feedback latency. Real automotive ECUs use a **hybrid strategy** — open loop during cold-start/wide-open-throttle, and closed loop during steady cruise/part-load for emissions control.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol) | A/Fₛₜₒᵢcₕ | **14.7 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Lambda (λ) | λ | **1.0** (stoich.) | dimensionless |
| Engine Idle Speed | Nᵢdₗₑ | **700 – 900** | rpm |
| Lambda Sensor Voltage (switching) | Vλ | **0.1 V (lean) – 0.9 V (rich)** | Volt |
| Closed-Loop Frequency | fᶜᴸ | **1 – 2 Hz** typical | Hertz |
| OBD-II Scan Rate | fₛcₐₙ | **100 Hz** typical | Hertz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open Loop Control System — Architecture

In an **Open Loop Engine Control System**, the control action is *independent* of the actual output. The ECU uses pre-calibrated **lookup tables (maps)** stored in ROM/Flash.

### Working Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. ECU reads **crankshaft position sensor** to compute RPM (N).
2. ECU reads **throttle position sensor (TPS)** or **MAP sensor** to compute load (L).
3. ECU indexes the 2D lookup table `f(N, L)` → returns base injector pulse width `t_base`.
4. Corrections applied: coolant temp, air temp, battery voltage (transient trim).
5. Injector is fired for `t_inj` microseconds — **no feedback correction is applied**.

### Block Diagram (Conceptual):

```
RPM (N) ──┐
          ├──► [Look-Up Table] ──► [Corrections] ──► t_inj ──► INJECTOR
Load (L)──┘
```

### Why and How:
- **Why fast?** No sensor lag, no computation of error.
- **How does it fail?** Component aging, altitude, fuel quality variations cause drift; the system cannot self-correct.

## 2.2 Closed Loop Control System — Architecture

A **Closed Loop Engine Control System** continuously measures the actual output (e.g., exhaust lambda), compares with a setpoint, and adjusts the actuator to minimise the error.

### Working Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. **Reference Setpoint:** λ_ref = 1.0 (stoichiometric).
2. **Feedback Measurement:** Lambda (O₂) sensor provides Vλ (0.1 V lean ↔ 0.9 V rich).
3. **Error Computation:** e(t) = λ_ref − λ_actual.
4. **Controller Law:** PID computes the correction `u(t) = Kp·e + Ki·∫e dt + Kd·(de/dt)`.
5. **Actuator Update:** Injector pulse width is increased (enrich) or decreased (lean out).
6. **Loop repeats** at sampling rate fₛ ≈ 1–2 Hz (limited by O₂ sensor response).

## 2.3 PID Control — The Heart of Engine Closed Loop

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** algorithm is the most common closed-loop strategy in ECUs:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\, e(t) \;+\; K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

| Term | Action | Effect on Engine | Side Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kₚ** (Proportional) | Reacts to current error | Fast response to A/F deviation | Oscillation if too high |
| **Kᵢ** (Integral) | Eliminates steady-state offset | Removes long-term drift (e.g., injector clog) | Slow; can cause wind-up |
| **K_d** (Derivative) | Reacts to rate of change | Damps oscillations | Sensitive to sensor noise |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use \vert and \mid for absolute value bars in tables to avoid breaking Markdown syntax.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open Loop Transfer Function | $G_{OL}(s) \;=\; G(s)$ | — | No feedback term |
| 2 | Closed Loop Transfer Function | $G_{CL}(s) \;=\; \dfrac{G(s)}{1 \,+\, G(s)\,H(s)}$ | — | With unity H(s) = 1 → $G/(1+G)$ |
| 3 | Error Signal | $e(t) \;=\; r(t) \,-\, y(t)$ | variable | Setpoint − Measured |
| 4 | PID Control Law | $u(t) \;=\; K_{p} e \,+\, K_{i} \int e\, dt \,+\, K_{d}\,\dot{e}$ | — | Fuel/ignition trim |
| 5 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | A/F measure |
| 6 | Stoichiometric A/F (Petrol) | $(A/F)_{stoich} \;=\; \mathbf{14.7}$ | kg/kg | Lambda = 1 anchor |
| 7 | Injector Pulse Width | $t_{inj} \;=\; t_{base} \cdot f_{cool} \cdot f_{bat} \,+\, \Delta t_{PID}$ | ms | With closed-loop trim |
| 8 | Steady-State Error (Unity FB) | $e_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 \,+\, K_{p}\,K}$ | — | With P-control only |
| 9 | Integral Wind-up Limit | $\vert u(t) \vert \;\le\; U_{max}$ | actuator limit | Anti-windup clamp |
| 10 | BIBO Stability (Routh-H.) | All roots of $1 \,+\, G(s)H(s)$ in LHP | — | Stability check |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Compliance:** Euro 6 / BS-VI norms require closed-loop λ-control in the catalytic converter window.
- **Drivability:** Idle-speed closed loop prevents stalling under accessory load (AC compressor, power steering).
- **Diagnostics:** OBD-II uses deviation between open-loop and closed-loop setpoints to flag faulty O₂ sensors (P0131, P0171 codes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Consider a standard negative-feedback loop with:
- `R(s)` = reference / setpoint (desired A/F ratio command)
- `E(s)` = error signal in Laplace domain
- `G(s)` = forward path transfer function (controller + plant)
- `H(s)` = feedback path transfer function (sensor dynamics)
- `Y(s)` = output (actual measured lambda × scaling)
- `C(s)` = control action signal (injector pulse width)

### Step 1 — Define the error in Laplace domain
By definition of a summing junction (negative feedback):

$$
E(s) \;=\; R(s) \;-\; H(s)\, Y(s)
$$

**Explanation:** The error is the *difference* between the desired reference and the *sensed* (post-sensor) output. The sensor dynamics `H(s)` are explicitly included because the lambda sensor has finite bandwidth.

### Step 2 — Express the control action
The forward path takes the error and produces the control signal:

$$
C(s) \;=\; G(s)\, E(s)
$$

**Explanation:** `G(s)` represents the ECU controller transfer function multiplied by the plant (injector dynamics + fuel delivery).

### Step 3 — Express the output
The plant output is driven by the control signal (assuming unity actuator-to-output gain):

$$
Y(s) \;=\; C(s)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3
Combine the controller and plant:

$$
Y(s) \;=\; G(s)\, E(s) \;=\; G(s)\bigl[\, R(s) \,-\, H(s)\, Y(s) \,\bigr]
$$

**Explanation:** This is the single most important algebraic substitution — it couples the output back to the error term.

### Step 5 — Isolate Y(s) on the left-hand side
Move the Y(s) term on the right to the left side:

$$
Y(s) \,+\, G(s)\, H(s)\, Y(s) \;=\; G(s)\, R(s)
$$

**Explanation:** Factorising Y(s) will allow us to express Y/R ratio.

### Step 6 — Factor out Y(s)

$$
Y(s)\,\bigl[\, 1 \,+\, G(s)\, H(s) \,\bigr] \;=\; G(s)\, R(s)
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function T(s) = Y(s)/R(s)

$$
\boxed{\,T(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 \,+\, G(s)\, H(s)}\,}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **canonical closed-loop transfer function**. For unity feedback (H(s) = 1), it reduces to T(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s)).

### Step 8 — Derive the error transfer function
Divide both sides of Step 1 by R(s):

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 \,+\, G(s)\, H(s)}
$$

**Explanation:** This shows that as the loop gain G(s)H(s) becomes large, the error E(s) tends to zero — a fundamental justification for using high-gain feedback in engine control.

## 3.2 Derivation of the PID Control Law in Discrete Time (ECU Implementation)

ECUs are digital, so the continuous PID must be discretised. Using backward-difference approximations with sampling period `Tₛ`:

### Step 1 — Discretise the integral term
The integral $\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$ is approximated as a running sum:

$$
\int_{0}^{t_k} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T_{s}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i)
$$

### Step 2 — Discretise the derivative term
The derivative $de/dt$ is approximated by the backward difference:

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\;\bigg|_{t=t_k}\;\approx\;\frac{e(k) \,-\, e(k-1)}{T_{s}}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine to form the discrete PID law
Substituting into the continuous PID:

$$
u(k) \;=\; K_{p}\, e(k) \;+\; K_{i}\, T_{s}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) \;+\; K_{d}\, \frac{e(k) \,-\, e(k-1)}{T_{s}}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the incremental (velocity-form) PID
Take the difference $u(k) - u(k-1)$ to avoid integral wind-up accumulation:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta u(k) \;=\; u(k) \,-\, u(k-1) \;=\; K_{p}\bigl[e(k) \,-\, e(k-1)\bigr] \,+\, K_{i}\, T_{s}\, e(k) \,+\, \frac{K_{d}}{T_{s}}\bigl[e(k) \,-\, 2 e(k-1) \,+\, e(k-2)\bigr]\,}
$$

**Explanation:** The velocity-form is preferred in ECUs because it allows bumpless transfer between manual and automatic modes, and naturally limits the magnitude of each actuator step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Open Loop vs Closed Loop Comparison

| Parameter | Open Loop Engine Control | Closed Loop Engine Control |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback Sensor | **None** | O₂ / MAP / Knock / EGT |
| Accuracy | **Low** (±10% A/F error typical) | **High** (±1% A/F error) |
| Speed of Response | **Very fast** (no sensor wait) | **Moderate** (1–2 Hz lambda sensor) |
| Cost | **Low** | Higher (sensors + wiring) |
| Adaptability to Aging | **Poor** (drift over life) | **Good** (self-correcting) |
| Stability Concern | None (no loop) | Loop can oscillate if Kp too high |
| Application | Cold start, WOT, cranking | Idle, cruise, part-load (emissions) |
| Computational Load | Low | Moderate (PID computation) |
| Diagnostics (OBD) | Limited | Comprehensive (P-codes possible) |
| Steady-State Error | e_ss ≠ 0 (in general) | e_ss → 0 (with integral action) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open Loop Engine Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RPM Sensor N] --> C[Lookup Table Map]
    B[MAP Sensor Load L] --> C
    D[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> E[Correction Block]
    C --> E
    F[Battery Voltage] --> E
    E --> G[Injector Pulse Width t_inj]
    G --> H[INJECTOR ACTUATOR]
    H --> I[Engine Cylinder]
    I -.->|No feedback| J[Exhaust]
```

**Interpretation:** Note the **dashed line** from Engine to Exhaust with a "No feedback" label — this visually distinguishes open loop from closed loop. There is no path from output back to the controller.

## 4.2 Closed Loop Engine Control Block Diagram (with PID)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Lambda setpoint] --> S[Summing Junction]
    S -->|error e t| P[PID Controller]
    P -->|u t| A[Injector Actuator]
    A --> Q[Engine Plant]
    Q --> Y[Actual Lambda output]
    Y --> F[O2 Sensor H s]
    F --> S
    Y -.->|measured exhaust| M[OBD Monitor]
```

**Interpretation:** The solid feedback path from `O2 Sensor` back to the `Summing Junction` is the defining feature of closed loop. The minus sign at the summing junction produces the negative error e(t) = r(t) − H(s)·y(t).

## 4.3 ECU Engine Control Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Power On ECU] --> S1[Read All Sensors]
    S1 --> S2{Cranking}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Open Loop: Cranking Pulse Width]
    S2 -->|No| S4{Warm Engine}
    S4 -->|No| S5[Open Loop: Warmup Enrichment]
    S4 -->|Yes| S6{WOT or Cold}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Open Loop: WOT Map]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Closed Loop: PID Lambda Control]
    S8 --> S9[Update Injector Pulse]
    S3 --> S9
    S5 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Update Ignition Timing]
    S10 --> S11[Next Crank Cycle]
    S11 --> S1
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **real-world hybrid strategy** — modern ECUs selectively switch between open and closed loop based on engine state. This is a **favourite KTU question pattern**.

## 4.4 PID Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Error e t] --> P1[Gain Kp]
    P1 --> S[Sum]
    E --> I1[Integrator]
    I1 --> KI[Gain Ki]
    KI --> S
    E --> D1[Differentiator]
    D1 --> KD[Gain Kd]
    KD --> S
    S --> U[Control Output u t]
```

**Interpretation:** Three parallel paths (P, I, D) are summed to form `u(t)`. This is the standard parallel-form PID used in MATLAB and in modern ECU software (e.g., AUTOSAR).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Q: Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control systems in the context of an engine management system. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (O₂ / knock sensor) |
| Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Example | Cold-start fuel enrichment (ECU fires a fixed richer pulse based on coolant temperature alone) | Stoichiometric A/F control during cruise using a wide-band O₂ sensor in a PID loop |

> **[3 marks: 1 mark feedback, 1 mark accuracy, 1 mark example]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: What is the role of a PID controller in modern Electronic Engine Control Units (ECUs)?**

**Model Answer:**
A PID controller continuously computes an error signal `e(t)` as the difference between the desired setpoint (e.g., λ = 1.0) and the actual measured output (e.g., from the O₂ sensor). It then applies three corrective actions: **Proportional** (Kp· e) for immediate correction, **Integral** (Ki·∫e dt) to remove steady-state offset, and **Derivative** (Kd· de/dt) to dampen oscillations. In ECUs, it is used for **lambda (A/F) control, idle-speed regulation, and EGR valve positioning**.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark error definition, 1 mark PID term explanation, 1 mark application]**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = Y(s)/R(s)$ of a negative-feedback control system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. Hence obtain the steady-state error for a unit-step input with unity feedback. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop lambda (A/F) control system** in a multi-point fuel injection (MPFI) engine. Mention the role of the PID controller and explain why the integral term is essential. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1:** At the summing junction, the error is:
$$E(s) \;=\; R(s) \,-\, H(s)\, Y(s)$$

**Step 2:** The forward path gives:
$$Y(s) \;=\; G(s)\, E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:
$$Y(s) \;=\; G(s)\bigl[\, R(s) \,-\, H(s)\, Y(s) \,\bigr]$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:
$$Y(s)\,\bigl[\, 1 \,+\, G(s)\, H(s) \,\bigr] \;=\; G(s)\, R(s)$$

**Step 5:** Hence the closed-loop transfer function:
$$T(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 \,+\, G(s)\, H(s)}$$

**Step 6:** For unity feedback H(s) = 1, let $G(s) = K/(1 + s\tau)$:
$$T(s) \;=\; \frac{K}{1 \,+\, s\tau \,+\, K}$$

**Step 7:** Steady-state error using Final Value Theorem, for unit-step R(s) = 1/s:
$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{1 \,+\, G(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 \,+\, K_{p}}$$

> **[7 marks: 1 mark Step 1, 1 mark Step 2, 1 mark Step 3, 1 mark Step 4, 1 mark Step 5, 1 mark Step 6, 1 mark Step 7]**

#### Model Solution — (b)

The **closed-loop lambda control** block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint lambda equals 1.0] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B -->|error| C[PID Controller]
    C -->|u t| D[Injector Driver]
    D --> E[MPFI Injectors]
    E --> F[Engine Combustion]
    F --> G[Exhaust]
    G --> H[O2 Sensor]
    H --> B
```

**Working:**

1. The setpoint λ_ref = 1.0 (stoichiometric) is fed to the summing junction.
2. The wide-band O₂ sensor in the exhaust manifold provides Vλ ∝ λ_actual.
3. The PID controller in the ECU computes e = λ_ref − λ_actual.
4. The **proportional term** reacts instantly to deviations (e.g., sudden lean spike).
5. The **integral term** is essential because:
   - It eliminates **steady-state offset** caused by injector aging, fuel pressure drift, or air leaks.
   - Without Ki, the system would settle at a constant non-zero error (e.g., λ = 1.02) — failing the Euro 6 / BS-VI emissions limit.
6. The output `u(t)` adjusts the injector pulse width `t_inj` to bring λ back to 1.0.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark block diagram, 1 mark steps, 1 mark PID roles, 1 mark integral justification, 1 mark application, 1 mark steady-state, 1 mark standards reference]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **open-loop fuel injection algorithm** in an EFI engine using a 2D lookup table approach. List three operating conditions where open-loop control is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **anti-windup strategy** used in the integral term of an engine PID controller. Why is it necessary, and how is it implemented? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **2D Lookup Table Algorithm** stores pre-calibrated base pulse-width values `t_base(N, L)` in ECU ROM, indexed by:
- `N` = engine RPM (e.g., 800, 1000, 1500, …, 6000)
- `L` = engine load (MAP in kPa or % TPS)

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Read crankshaft position → compute `N` (RPM).
2. Read MAP sensor → compute `L` (kPa).
3. Bilinearly interpolate `t_base` from the 2D map at `(N, L)`.
4. Apply multiplicative corrections: `f_cool(T_coolant)`, `f_air(T_intake)`, `f_bat(V_bat)`.
5. Final command: `t_inj = t_base · f_cool · f_air · f_bat`.

**Three operating conditions preferring open loop:**
1. **Cold start / cranking:** O₂ sensor is below 350 °C and gives invalid output → cannot use closed loop.
2. **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT):** Engine is intentionally run rich for power; closed loop is disabled to allow enrichment.
3. **Cold warm-up:** Open-loop warmup enrichment (WE) factor is applied based on coolant temp.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark table concept, 1 mark axes, 1 mark interpolation, 1 mark corrections, 1 mark formula, 1 mark × 3 conditions]**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Integral Wind-up Problem:**
When the actuator saturates (e.g., injector pulse width hits its maximum limit), the error `e(t)` continues to accumulate in the integrator. When the setpoint becomes achievable again, the integrator holds a huge accumulated value → causes a large overshoot and slow recovery.

**Anti-Windup Strategies:**

1. **Clamping (Saturation):** Limit the integrator output:
$$\bigl\vert I_{sum} \bigr\vert \;\le\; I_{max}$$

2. **Conditional Integration (Clamping):** Stop integrating when actuator is saturated and error would worsen the wind-up:
$$I(k) \;=\; \begin{cases} I(k-1) \,+\, K_i\, T_s\, e(k) & \text{if } \vert u_{unc} \vert \;\le\; U_{max} \\ I(k-1) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

3. **Back-Calculation:** Compute the difference between the unsaturated and saturated control output, and feed it back to the integrator with gain `K_t`:
$$\frac{dI}{dt} \;=\; K_i\, e(t) \,+\, K_t\, \bigl[\, U_{sat} \,-\, u_{unc} \,\bigr]$$

**Why necessary:** Protects the engine from over-fueling/over-igniting during transients (e.g., sudden lift-off after WOT). **How implemented:** In ECU code as a software flag check on each PID cycle.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark problem, 1 mark × 3 strategies, 1 mark why necessary, 1 mark example]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the *open-loop transfer function* $G(s)$ with the *closed-loop* $G/(1+GH)$ — students frequently write $1 + G(s)H(s)$ in the numerator. **Marks deducted: 2.**
> - In PID answers, **always mention the integral wind-up problem** — it is a favourite 2-mark follow-up. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
> - In the A/F control question, **explicitly state λ = 1.0** as the setpoint and **14.7 : 1** as the stoichiometric A/F — generic "rich/lean" answers lose precision marks.
> - Avoid stating that open loop has *no* sensors — it has *input* sensors (RPM, MAP) but *no output* feedback sensor.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Open Loop** = no feedback; uses pre-calibrated **lookup tables**; **fast but inaccurate**.
- **Closed Loop** = uses feedback (O₂ / knock / MAP); **accurate but slower**; needs **stability analysis**.
- Closed-loop transfer function: $T(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$ — **memorise this verbatim**.
- Steady-state error for unity feedback, step input: $e_{ss} = 1/(1 + K_p)$.
- **PID terms:** P (instant), I (eliminates offset), D (damps oscillation).
- **Stoichiometric A/F = 14.7 : 1**; Lambda λ = 1.0 at stoichiometric.
- **Open loop used during:** cold start, cranking, WOT, cold warmup.
- **Closed loop used during:** idle, cruise, part-load (for emissions).
- **Integral wind-up prevention:** clamping, conditional integration, back-calculation.
- **OBD-II monitors** the difference between open-loop and closed-loop behaviour to detect sensor faults.
- **Sampling rate** of closed-loop lambda control is limited by O₂ sensor bandwidth → ~1–2 Hz.
- **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** is the standard tool to check **closed-loop stability** — all roots of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ must lie in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)**.
- **Velocity-form PID** is preferred in ECUs for bumpless transfer and intrinsic anti-windup.
- **ECU algorithm flowchart pattern:** Crank → Cold-start OL → Warmup OL → WOT OL → Cruise CL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Ignition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Ignition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electronic Ignition System** is defined as an advanced ignition subsystem in which the primary current switching (make-and-break) operation—historically performed by mechanical contact breaker points in conventional ignition systems—is performed by solid-state electronic switching devices such as **bipolar junction transistors (BJT)**, **Darlington pairs**, **thyristors (SCRs)**, or **insulated-gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs)**, under the precise timing control of an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** or dedicated **igniter module**.

The system generates a high-voltage secondary spark (typically **15 kV to 40 kV**) at precisely the correct ignition advance angle for every combustion cycle, ensuring optimal combustion efficiency, reduced exhaust emissions, and improved fuel economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEICT744 / Module 2):** Students must distinguish between **Transistor-Assisted Contact Ignition (TACI)**, **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)**, and **Distributor-Less Ignition System (DIS)** — all three are grouped under the umbrella term *Electronic Ignition* in the revised 2024 scheme syllabus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **lighting a birthday candle**:
- The **old mechanical ignition system** is like striking an old-fashioned match every single time — it works but is unreliable, the striker wears out, and the timing of the spark depends on how hard you hit it.
- The **electronic ignition system** is like having a **push-button electric lighter** — every press produces a perfectly identical, high-energy spark at exactly the right moment, controlled by a tiny microchip brain that "knows" when the engine needs fire.

In an engine, the **candle** is the air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber, the **lighter button** is the transistor switch, and the **microchip brain** is the ECU. The electronic system ensures that even at **6000 RPM** (when the engine is firing 50 times per second per cylinder), every spark is consistent and well-timed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Primary Coil Voltage (Vp) | **12 V DC** (nominal battery voltage) |
| Secondary Spark Voltage | **15 kV – 40 kV** |
| Primary Coil Inductance (Lp) | **3 mH – 10 mH** |
| Secondary Coil Inductance (Ls) | **5 H – 20 H** |
| Turns Ratio (N) | **1 : 60 to 1 : 200** |
| Spark Duration | **0.5 ms – 2 ms** |
| Ignition Energy | **30 mJ – 80 mJ** per spark |
| Dwell Angle (electronic) | Variable, ECU-controlled |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention:** Inductance, turns ratio, and stored energy equations use **SI base units** (H, V, A, s, J). Always use **Henry (H)** for inductance in derivations, not millihenries, to maintain dimensional consistency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Electronic Ignition System

The fundamental operating philosophy is built on **electromagnetic induction** combined with **solid-state switching**. The system can be broken into six functional blocks:

- **Energy Storage Block:** The ignition coil acts as a step-up transformer, storing magnetic energy in its primary winding during the "dwell" (charging) phase.
- **Trigger/Sensing Block:** A **magnetic pickup coil**, **Hall-effect sensor**, or **optical interrupter** detects crankshaft position (and thus piston position) and sends a reference signal to the controller.
- **Signal Conditioning Block:** The raw trigger signal is shaped (filtered, debounced, squared) into a clean **logic-level pulse train**.
- **Electronic Switching Block:** A power transistor (BJT, Darlington, or IGBT) interrupts the primary current at the precise moment commanded by the controller. This abrupt interruption induces the high-voltage secondary EMF via **Lenz's Law**.
- **High-Voltage Distribution Block:** Either a **rotary distributor** (traditional) or a **static DIS setup with multiple coils and wasted-spark/sequential logic** routes the high voltage to the correct cylinder.
- **Spark Delivery Block:** The spark plug fires across its electrode gap, igniting the compressed air-fuel mixture.

### 2.2 Why and How of Each Step

- **Why use a step-up transformer (coil)?** A 12 V battery cannot jump a spark plug gap of 0.7–1.1 mm. Induction provides a passive, reliable way to multiply voltage by 60×–200× without any active electronics in the high-voltage side.
- **Why electronic switching instead of contact points?** Mechanical points suffer from **contact pitting, bounce, dwell angle variation with rpm, and a hard 5 A current limit**. Transistors switch in **microseconds**, handle **8–10 A** reliably, and have no moving parts.
- **Why need an ECU control?** Real-world ignition timing is **not constant**; it depends on load, RPM, temperature, knock, and air-fuel ratio. Only a microprocessor can compute the optimal **ignition advance** in real time from a 3D/4D **ignition map**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for KTU ESE Numerical Problems** — memorize these expressions and their boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_s = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law (induced secondary EMF) | V |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = \dfrac{V_s}{V_p}$ | Turns ratio of ignition coil | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_p = -L_p \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Self-induced EMF in primary | V |
| 4 | $E_s = -M \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Mutually induced EMF in secondary | V |
| 5 | $W_{mag} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | Magnetic energy stored in primary coil | J |
| 6 | $I_p(t) = \dfrac{V}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-tR_p / L_p}\right)$ | Primary current build-up (transient) | A |
| 7 | $I_{sat} = \dfrac{V}{R_p}$ | Steady-state primary current | A |
| 8 | $V_s(t) = V_p \, e^{-(R_s/L_s)\,t}$ | Secondary voltage decay (after collapse) | V |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{L_p}{R_p}$ | Primary RL time constant | s |
| 10 | $V_s^{peak} = N \cdot V_p$ | Peak secondary voltage (ideal) | V |
| 11 | $E_{spark} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} V_s(t) \cdot i_s(t) \, dt$ | Spark discharge energy | J |
| 12 | $P = \dfrac{N_e}{2} \cdot k$ | Firing frequency (4-stroke, N cylinders) | Hz |
| 13 | $\theta_{adv} = f(N, Q, T, p, \text{knock})$ | Ignition advance as a multi-variable map | degrees BTDC |
| 14 | $f_{cog} = \dfrac{n \cdot N_{cogs}}{60}$ | Trigger frequency from reluctor (n = rpm) | Hz |
| 15 | $V_{pickup} = k \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ | Variable Reluctance (VR) sensor output | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In Formula (6), the value of $t$ in the exponent is the **dwell time**, not arbitrary. The primary current is allowed to ramp up only during this dwell period; the transistor must be ON for a duration $t_{dwell} \approx 3\tau$ to nearly reach saturation current $I_{sat}$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the System

- **Emission Control:** Precise timing reduces unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), CO, and NOx.
- **Fuel Economy:** Optimal spark advance maximizes indicated thermal efficiency.
- **Cold Start:** ECU enriches fuel AND advances timing for cranking.
- **Knock Prevention:** Knock sensor feedback retards timing per cycle in real time.
