> **Mandatory Standard Thresholds (Industry Norms):**
> - **Deployment Time:** 20–30 ms (after crash sensor trigger)
> - **Full Inflation:** ≤ 50 ms
> - **Deflation Window:** 100–200 ms (controlled venting)
> - **Trigger Threshold:** Δv ≥ 12–19 km/h (vehicle deceleration pulse)
> - **Bag Inflation Pressure:** 35–70 kPa peak
> - **Operating Temperature:** **−40 °C to +85 °C**
> - **Service Life:** 10–15 years (gas generant chemical stability window)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The airbag operating sequence is divided into **six distinct phases**. Each phase has a strict timing budget governed by crash physics.

#### Phase 1: Pre-Crash Sensing (0 ms)
- **Crash sensors** (mechanical ball-in-tube, electronic MEMS accelerometers, or piezoelectric) continuously monitor vehicle deceleration `a(t)`.
- The sensor signal is fed to the **SRS Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, which executes an *integrated crash algorithm* every **2–5 ms** (sampling rate ≈ **500 Hz**).

#### Phase 2: Algorithm Decision Window (5–15 ms)
- The ECU evaluates a **discriminator function** combining:
  - Velocity change: `Δv = ∫ a(t) dt`
  - Crash pulse slope: `da/dt`
  - Front crush depth (via peripheral sensors)
- A **fire command** is issued only if Δv exceeds a calibrated threshold AND a "safing" sensor independently confirms the event (preventing accidental deployment from potholes, hammer blows, etc.).

#### Phase 3: Squib Ignition (15–20 ms)
- The ECU sends a **5-volt, 2-amp pulse** to the **squib** (a thin resistive bridge wire embedded in the gas generator).
- The bridge wire heats to **>1000 °C** in microseconds.
- The **azide-based propellant** (NaN₃) is ignited:

$$2 \text{NaN}_3 \longrightarrow 2 \text{Na} + 3 \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$

#### Phase 4: Gas Generation & Bag Deployment (20–50 ms)
- The solid propellant burns in **<20 ms**, releasing nitrogen at temperatures up to **2500 °C**.
- The gas passes through a **filter pack** (steel wool + ceramic frit) to remove sodium hydroxide (NaOH) particulates, then through **cooling screens**.
- The **inflator nozzle** directs gas into the folded bag, which **bursts out of its cover module** at ≈ 300 km/h.
- Venting holes (Ø 25–40 mm) begin controlled gas release almost immediately.

#### Phase 5: Occupant Immersion (50–100 ms)
- The occupant's torso, traveling at pre-crash velocity, contacts the now-pressurized cushion.
- Gas vents through calibrated orifices, creating a **"ride-down" effect** — the bag yields progressively.

#### Phase 6: Deflation & Post-Crash (100–200 ms)
- Bag is fully deflated; occupant continues forward at reduced residual velocity.
- ECU writes a **freeze-frame** of crash data to non-volatile memory (for forensic analysis).
- **Post-crash notification** is sent via telematics (eCall, OnStar, etc.).

### 2.2 Critical "Why" — Physics Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the airbag effective?** Newton's Second Law in impulse form: 
> Impulse = Change in momentum = F · Δt = m · Δv
> For a given occupant mass `m` and velocity change `Δv`, increasing the **time interval** `Δt` over which the force is applied **decreases the peak force** `F`. A seatbelt alone provides `Δt ≈ 30–60 ms`; an airbag extends this to `Δt ≈ 80–120 ms`, halving peak chest loads.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crash Velocity Change | $\Delta v = \int_0^t a(\tau) d\tau$ | m/s | Primary deployment trigger |
| 2 | Crash Severity Index | $\text{CSI} = \frac{1}{2} m \Delta v^2$ | J | Kinetic energy to dissipate |
| 3 | Impulse-Momentum | $F \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$ | N·s | Justifies airbag time-extension |
| 4 | Head Injury Criterion | $\text{HIC} = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | dimensionless | Max acceptable = **1000** |
| 5 | Azide Decomposition | $2 \text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2 \text{Na} + 3 \text{N}_2$ | — | Gas-generation chemistry |
| 6 | Gas Yield (per gram NaN₃) | ≈ 0.66 L N₂ at STP | L/g | Sizing the inflator |
| 7 | Bag Inflation Pressure | $P = \frac{nRT}{V}$ | Pa | Real-gas approx (nitrogen) |
| 8 | Sensor Threshold | $a_{\text{trigger}} \geq 5g$ to $30g$ | m/s² | Depends on crash pulse |
| 9 | Sampling Rate (ECU) | $f_s \geq 500$ Hz | Hz | Nyquist ≥ 2× pulse freq. |
| 10 | Deployment Time | $t_d \approx 20$–$30$ ms | ms | Time-to-fire for pyrotechnic |
| 11 | Squib Resistance | $R \approx 1.8$–$2.5$ Ω | Ω | Squib integrity check |
| 12 | Service Life | $t_{\text{life}} \leq 15$ years | years | Propellant hydrolysis limit |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The airbag is more than a single component — it is the **central node of a vehicle's passive safety network**. Modern vehicles integrate:
- **Crash severity prediction** (pre-crash sensing via radar/lidar) that pre-tensions seatbelts *before* impact.
- **Multi-stage inflators** that tailor output (e.g., 70% inflation for unbelted occupants, 30% for belted — reducing airbag-induced injuries in smaller occupants).
- **Occupant Classification Systems (OCS)** using weight mats in the seat to suppress airbag deployment for child seats or 5th-percentile females, satisfying the **"advanced airbag"** requirements of FMVSS 208.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gas Volume Required for a 60-Litre Frontal Bag

**Problem Setup:** A standard driver-side frontal airbag has a fully inflated volume of **V = 60 L**. The propellant is **sodium azide (NaN₃)** with molar mass **M = 65 g/mol**. The decomposition occurs at **T_decomp ≈ 2500 °C = 2773 K**. Assume ideal gas behaviour and that the bag must be pressurized to **P_abs = 140 kPa** (gauge 40 kPa + atmospheric 100 kPa).

**Step 1 — Moles of N₂ required by Ideal Gas Law:**

$$P V = n R T$$

$$n = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

Substituting:

$$n = \frac{140 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa} \times 60 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3}{8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} \times 2773 \, \text{K}}$$

$$n = \frac{8400 \, \text{J}}{23050.6 \, \text{J/mol}} = 0.3645 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric conversion of N₂ to NaN₃:**

From the balanced reaction `2 NaN₃ → 2 Na + 3 N₂`, the mole ratio is:

$$\frac{n_{\text{NaN}_3}}{n_{\text{N}_2}} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$n_{\text{NaN}_3} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.3645 = 0.2430 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles to mass:**

$$m_{\text{NaN}_3} = n \times M = 0.2430 \times 65 = 15.80 \, \text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Accounting for Real-World Inefficiencies:**
- Filter pack + cooling screens reduce effective N₂: factor **0.85**.
- Venting during inflation: factor **1.20** (oversize the charge).
- So actual propellant mass:

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \frac{15.80 \times 1.20}{0.85} \approx 22.3 \, \text{g}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A real driver-side airbag inflator typically contains **30–50 g of NaN₃** (with the surplus providing margin for the passenger-side 120–150 L bag from a common inflator in some architectures).

### 3.2 Derivation: HIC (Head Injury Criterion) Maximum Tolerance

**Definition (FMVSS 208 / ECE R94):**

$$\text{HIC}_{t_1, t_2} = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$

where `a(t)` is the head's resultant acceleration in units of **g** (gravitational acceleration), and `t₂ − t₁ ≤ 36 ms`.

**Threshold:** HIC₁₅ ≤ **1000** corresponds to a **~16% probability of severe (AIS ≥ 4) head injury** in adults. Modern vehicles target HIC₁₅ ≤ **700** for 5-star ratings (Euro NCAP, Bharat NCAP).

**Worked Example — Head Acceleration Pulse:** Suppose during a 35 ms window the head experiences a triangular acceleration pulse rising linearly from 0 to 80 g in 10 ms, then falling linearly to 0 g over the next 25 ms.

Average acceleration over the window:

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a(t)\, dt$$

For a triangular pulse of peak `a_peak = 80 g` and duration `T = 35 ms`:

$$\int_0^T a(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2} \cdot a_{\text{peak}} \cdot T = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times 35 = 1400 \, g \cdot \text{ms}$$

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1400}{35} = 40 g$$

Then:

$$\text{HIC} = (40)^{2.5} \times 35$$

Compute `(40)^{2.5}`:

$$40^{2.5} = 40^2 \times 40^{0.5} = 1600 \times 6.3246 \approx 10119.3$$

$$\text{HIC} = 10119.3 \times 35 \approx 354{,}175$$

This is **massively above the 1000 threshold** — a pedestrian or unrestrained occupant in such a pulse would suffer **fatal head injury**, justifying the need for airbags to flatten the acceleration profile.

### 3.3 Derivation: Squib Energy Budget

The squib must ignite reliably across:
- **Cold-soak condition** (−40 °C): bridge wire resistance is lower; needs minimum firing energy **E_min = 10 mJ**.
- **Hot-soak condition** (+85 °C): higher resistance; needs firing energy up to **E_max = 200 mJ**.

The energy delivered to the squib (resistance **R_s ≈ 2 Ω**) by a capacitor **C = 47 µF** charged to **V = 12 V** (vehicle battery) is:

$$E_{\text{cap}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 47 \times 10^{-6} \times 144 = 3.38 \, \text{mJ}$$

This is **insufficient** — a dedicated **DC-DC boost converter** in the ECU charges a **"firing capacitor"** to **V_fire ≈ 25 V** and uses a higher-value capacitor (typically **470 µF**):

$$E_{\text{fire}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times 625 = 147 \, \text{mJ}$$

This 147 mJ comfortably exceeds the cold-soak minimum (10 mJ) and remains within the hot-soak maximum (200 mJ), satisfying both environmental extremes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The ECU is powered by a **"backup capacitor"** that maintains firing energy for **≥ 150 ms after battery disconnect** (e.g., if the crash severs the battery cable). This is a critical feature: deployment MUST occur even in a worst-case electrical failure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Airbag System Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[CFront Mechanical Sensor - Pneumatic Ball-in-Tube] --> E1
    A2[Satellite Accelerometer - Front Bumper] --> E1
    A3[Side Impact Pressure Sensor - Door Cavity] --> E1
    A4[Safing Sensor - Independent Co-located Accelerometer] --> E1

    E1[SRS ECU - 32-bit Microcontroller] --> D1
    E1 --> D2
    E1 --> D3
    E1 --> D4

    D1[Driver Airbag Squib - 2.0 ohm]
    D2[Passenger Airbag Squib - 2.0 ohm]
    D3[Side Curtain Squib - 1.8 ohm]
    D4[Seatbelt Pretensioner Squib]

    D1 --> F1[Driver Inflator - 35 g NaN3 - 60 L bag]
    D2 --> F2[Passenger Inflator - 50 g NaN3 - 120 L bag]
    D3 --> F3[Curtain Inflator - 25 g NaN3 - 30 L bag]
    D4 --> F4[Pretensioner Pyrotechnic - 0.3 g charge]

    E1 --> M1[Warning Lamp Driver - ISO 26021]
    E1 --> M2[Crash Data Recorder - Freeze Frame Memory]
    E1 --> M3[Telematics Unit - eCall Notification]

    P1[OCS Weight Mat - Front Passenger Seat] --> E1
    P2[Seatbelt Buckle Switch] --> E1
    P3[Steering Column Clock Spring - 5 turn spiral] --> D1
```

### 4.2 Crash-to-Deployment Timing Sequence (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[0 ms - TOW - Time of Wall] --> T5[5 ms - Sensors detect severe pulse]
    T5 --> T10[10 ms - ECU runs discriminator algorithm]
    T10 --> T15[15 ms - ECU fires squib]
    T15 --> T20[20 ms - Propellant ignites - NaN3 burns]
    T20 --> T30[30 ms - Bag bursts cover - inflates 70 percent]
    T30 --> T50[50 ms - Bag fully inflated - occupant contact begins]
    T50 --> T120[120 ms - Bag deflates through vent holes]
    T120 --> T200[200 ms - Bag fully deflated - occupant stabilized]
    T200 --> T500[500 ms - ECU stores crash data - activates hazard lights]
```

### 4.3 Gas Generator (Inflator) Cross-Section — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Squib Bridge Wire - 2.0 ohm - top initiator] --> P1
    P1[Booster Charge - 0.5 g BKNO3 - secondary ignition] --> P2
    P2[Main Propellant Grain - 30 g NaN3 - annular pellet] --> P3
    P3[Combustion Chamber - 2000 to 2500 deg C] --> P4
    P4[Filter Pack - Steel Wool - removes NaOH particulates] --> P5
    P5[Cooling Screen - 316L stainless mesh] --> P6
    P6[Diffuser Screen - multi-layer sintered bronze] --> P7
    P7[Outlet Nozzle - 4 radial ports] --> P8[To Airbag Cushion - Nylon 66 fabric]
```

### 4.4 Sensor Hierarchy and Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Front Bumper Accelerometer - primary] --> AND
    S2[Firewall Mechanical Sensor - primary] --> AND
    S3[Safing Sensor - independent accelerometer] --> AND
    AND{AND Gate - all 3 sensors agree within 5 ms window}
    AND -->|Yes| FIRE[Squib Fire Command - 2 amp pulse for 2 ms]
    AND -->|No| HOLD[No Fire - Log Event - Continue Monitoring]
    FIRE --> OUTPUT[Deploy Airbag in 20 to 30 ms]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) and explain why an airbag is classified as a "supplemental" rather than "primary" restraint.**

**Model Answer:**
A Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) is a vehicle safety system that **assists** the primary restraint, which is the **three-point seatbelt**. The airbag is termed "supplemental" because:
1. It cannot provide protection on its own — an unbelted occupant in a frontal crash can "submarine" under a deploying bag, sustain fatal leg injuries, or suffer airbag-induced facial trauma.
2. It activates only in **moderate-to-severe crashes** (Δv > 12–19 km/h); below this threshold, the seatbelt alone is sufficient.
3. It is designed for **one-time deployment only**; post-crash, the seatbelt remains the operative restraint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "an airbag replaces the seatbelt" — this is factually wrong and costs 1 mark. The airbag is **secondary, complementary, and post-crash-disposable**.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the major components of an airbag system and state the function of the "clock spring" (spiral cable).**

**Model Answer:**
Major components: (i) crash sensor(s), (ii) SRS ECU, (iii) inflator (gas generator), (iv) airbag cushion, (v) cover module, (vi) clock spring, (vii) squib, (viii) warning lamp, (ix) wiring harness.

**Clock spring function:** A **flat, multi-conductor spiral cable** wound in a watch-spring configuration inside the steering hub. It maintains continuous electrical connection between the **stationary steering column wiring** and the **rotating driver's airbag module** through the full ±2.5 turns of steering rotation. Without it, a steering-wheel-mounted airbag would have its wires twist and break.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE full question from Module 3 (typically 14 marks, with sub-parts 7+7).

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q. (a) Describe the working of a sodium azide-based airbag inflator with a neat functional diagram. Discuss the chemical reaction, the role of the filter pack, and the reason for using a two-stage squib ignition system.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
The sodium azide (NaN₃) inflator converts solid propellant into nitrogen gas through a **pyrotechnic chemical reaction**. When the SRS ECU commands firing, current flows through the squib, igniting the booster charge, which in turn ignites the main NaN₃ grain.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$2 \, \text{NaN}_3 \, (s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \, \text{Na} \, (s) + 3 \, \text{N}_2 \, (g) \uparrow$$

The reaction is **highly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −27 kcal/mol), generating gas at temperatures of **~2500 °C**.

**Role of the Filter Pack:**
The filter pack (steel wool + ceramic frit + cooling screens) serves three purposes:
1. **Removes NaOH aerosols** — sodium produced in the reaction reacts with moisture and CO₂ in the air to form NaOH (caustic soda), which is toxic and would harm the occupant. The filter captures these particulates.
2. **Cools the gas** — by passing through the steel wool mass, the gas temperature drops from 2500 °C to a safer 50–100 °C, preventing fabric burn-through.
3. **Smooths flow** — provides uniform pressure distribution into the bag.

**Two-Stage Squib Ignition:**
- **Stage 1 (Bridge Wire):** A thin resistive wire heats to >1000 °C when 2 A current flows for ~2 ms.
- **Stage 2 (Booster Charge):** A small pellet of **boron-potassium nitrate (BKNO₃)** around the bridge wire ignites and produces a hot jet of flame that reliably ignites the main NaN₃ grain.
- This two-stage arrangement **prevodes accidental ignition** from electrostatic discharge or thermal events, satisfying FMVSS 208 "no-fire" criteria under 95th-percentile abuse conditions.

**Functional Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 above — Gas Generator Cross-Section).

---

**Q. (b) Explain the classification of airbag sensors. Compare mechanical ball-in-tube sensors with MEMS electronic accelerometers in terms of accuracy, reliability, and modern applicability.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Airbag Sensors:**

1. **By Physical Principle:**
   - **Mechanical sensors** (ball-in-tube, roller-type, spring-mass) — purely electromechanical.
   - **Electronic sensors** (MEMS, piezoelectric) — solid-state.

2. **By Function:**
   - **Primary (frontal) crash sensors** — mounted in the front crumple zone, firewall, and B-pillar.
   - **Safing sensors** — co-located with the ECU; must independently confirm a crash before firing is permitted.
   - **Satellite (peripheral) sensors** — located at the front bumper, doors, and pillars to detect side impacts and refine crash-severity classification.

3. **By Crash Type:** Front-impact vs. side-impact (pressure or acceleration-based).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Mechanical Ball-in-Tube | MEMS Accelerometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Element** | Steel ball held by magnet; inertia overcomes magnet at high `g` | Micro-machined silicon proof mass with capacitive plates |
| **Trigger Threshold** | Fixed; typically 4–8 g | Programmable via software |
| **Accuracy** | ±15–20% (mechanical tolerances) | ±2–5% (digital, calibrated) |
| **Response Time** | 5–10 ms (mechanical inertia) | < 1 ms (electronic) |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Magnet strength varies with T | Compensated digitally |
| **Reliability (lifetime)** | 10–15 years, no electronic drift | > 15 years, but subject to MEMS aging |
| **Vibration Immunity** | Susceptible to pothole hammer events | Algorithm + safing sensor filters these |
| **Modern Use** | Legacy systems (1990s); cheap entry-level vehicles | Universal in modern SRS ECUs |
| **Discriminability** | Cannot distinguish severity levels | Can run crash-pulse algorithms to stage deployment |
| **Cost** | ₹80–150 per sensor | ₹350–800 per sensor |

**Conclusion:** Modern airbag systems are **fully electronic** because the SRS ECU needs to discriminate between crash severities (e.g., low-speed deployment for unbelted, high-speed multi-stage deployment), and only **software-controlled MEMS sensors** can deliver the data resolution and algorithm flexibility required.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete sequence of events from crash initiation to airbag deflation. State the role of the SRS ECU in each phase.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sequence of Events (with SRS ECU role annotated):**

| Phase | Time | Event | SRS ECU Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-Crash** | t < 0 ms | Vehicle in motion, sensors monitoring | Polls sensors every 2 ms; runs background diagnostic |
| **2. Impact Onset** | 0 ms | Vehicle contacts obstacle; frontal structure begins to deform | Detects first deceleration spike from front bumper accelerometer |
| **3. Crash Detection** | 5–10 ms | Δv begins to accumulate; pulse exceeds noise floor | Runs crash algorithm; integrates `a(t)` to compute Δv |
| **4. Crash Discrimination** | 10–15 ms | Algorithm classifies severity: deployable? | Compares Δv against threshold table; also reads safing sensor |
| **5. Fire Command** | 15–20 ms | Decision: "FIRE" | Triggers backup capacitor; sends 2 A pulse to squib for 2 ms |
| **6. Squib Ignition** | 18–22 ms | Bridge wire glows; booster ignites | Monitors squib resistance; if abnormal, aborts |
| **7. Propellant Burn** | 20–40 ms | NaN₃ → N₂ + Na | ECU continues diagnostic; no further action |
| **8. Bag Inflation** | 25–50 ms | Bag bursts cover, fills with N₂ | Logs deployment event in non-volatile memory |
| **9. Occupant Contact** | 50–80 ms | Chest impacts cushion | ECU switches to "post-fire" state |
| **10. Deflation** | 80–200 ms | Venting holes release gas | Monitors occupant-to-bag interaction via optional i-Sense sensors |
| **11. Post-Crash** | 200 ms+ | Hazard lights activate; telematics call | Activates warning lamp; triggers eCall (EU) or equivalent |

**Block Diagram Reference:** (See Section 4.2 above — Crash-to-Deployment Timing Sequence.)

---

**Q. (b) Discuss the various types of airbags used in modern automobiles. Compare frontal, side, and curtain airbags in terms of deployment time, volume, and occupant injury zones protected.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Airbags (Modern Vehicle Inventory):**

1. **Frontal (Driver + Passenger)** — Two units, dashboard-stored
2. **Side Torso Airbags** — Mounted in seat backrest
3. **Curtain (Window) Airbags** — Roof rail
4. **Knee Airbags** — Lower glovebox region
5. **Seatbelt Airbags** — Inside the webbing
6. **Far-Side / Center Airbags** — Between front seats
7. **External Pedestrian Airbags** — Windshield cowl (Volvo, 2014+)
8. **Active Hood Airbags** — Lift hood on impact to create crush space
9. **Rear-Seat Side Airbags** — Premium sedans/SUVs
10. **Motorcycle Airbags** — In rider's jacket or vest (Alpinestars, Dainese)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Frontal | Side Torso | Curtain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Time** | 20–30 ms | 5–10 ms (must be faster — less crumple zone) | 8–15 ms |
| **Inflated Volume** | 60 L (driver), 120 L (passenger) | 12–25 L | 30–45 L per side |
| **Bag Shape** | Round, flat pillow | Tall narrow cushion | Long tubular drape |
| **Mounting Location** | Steering hub / dashboard | Seat bolster / door panel | Roof rail (A to C pillar) |
| **Primary Protection** | Head, chest, sternum | Ribs, pelvis, abdomen | Head, prevents ejection through window |
| **Triggering Sensor** | Front accelerometer (Δv) | Side pressure sensor OR B-pillar accelerometer | Side accelerometer + pressure |
| **Burn Time Required** | < 30 ms | < 10 ms | < 15 ms |
| **Injury Criterion Targeted** | HIC, Chest deflection (≤ 50 mm) | Rib fracture (Deflection < 42 mm) | HIC, neck injury |
| **Standalone Effectiveness** | Reduces driver fatality by ~29% (NHTSA) | Reduces driver fatality by ~37% (side crashes) | Reduces ejection fatalities by ~30% |

**Engineering Reason for Different Design Speeds:**
Side-impact crashes have **almost no vehicle crumple zone** (the door is only ~150 mm thick). The crash pulse reaches the occupant in **< 20 ms**, so the side airbag must deploy in **< 10 ms** — this is why side inflators use a **smaller, faster-burning gas generant** and a smaller bag volume.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "all airbags deploy in 20 ms" — this loses 1 mark. **Side and curtain airbags deploy faster** due to the absence of crumple zone between the impactor and occupant.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- **Airbag = Supplemental Restraint System (SRS); complement to seatbelt; one-time deployment; pyrotechnic.** *(Definition question favorite.)*
- **Deployment Time = 20–30 ms** (frontal); **5–10 ms** (side). *Don't confuse these.*
- **Inflation Time = 50 ms; Deflation = 100–200 ms.** *Time-budget diagram is a high-yield KTU question.*
- **Squib resistance = 1.8–2.5 Ω; firing energy = 100–200 mJ; backup capacitor = 150 ms autonomy.** *Numerical question favorite.*
- **Chemical equation:** `2 NaN₃ → 2 Na + 3 N₂` — yield ≈ 0.66 L N₂ per gram. *Memorize this reaction.*
- **Filter pack functions:** (1) remove NaOH, (2) cool gas, (3) smooth flow. *Three-in-one — easy 3-mark question.*
- **Clock spring** = spiral cable allowing steering rotation. *Common 2-mark question.*
- **Two-stage squib** = bridge wire + BKNO₃ booster. *Conceptual question.*
- **HIC₁₅ limit = 1000** (FMVSS) / **700** (NCAP 5-star). *Formula-based question.*
- **Sensors:** Mechanical (legacy) vs. MEMS (modern). MEMS gives programmability and severity discrimination.
- **Safing sensor** = independent confirmation sensor; prevents accidental deployment.
- **OCS** = Occupant Classification System (weight mat) — used to suppress airbag for child seats and small occupants.
- **Multi-stage inflators** adjust output based on crash severity and occupant size (advanced airbag).
- **Standards:** FMVSS 208 (USA), ECE R94 / R95 (Europe), AIS-098 (India — Bharat NCAP), GB 11551 (China).
- **Service life = 10–15 years** due to propellant chemical stability (azide hydrolysis, BBMP degradation).
- **Diagnostic resistor (~2.7 Ω)** in squib circuit: enables ECU to detect open/short circuit and illuminate the airbag warning lamp.
- **Telltale warning lamp** must illuminate for ~6 seconds at ignition-ON as a bulb check, then extinguish — its continuous illumination signals an SRS fault that disables deployment.
- **Telematics integration** (eCall mandate in EU from 2018) automatically dials emergency services with GPS coordinates within seconds of deployment.
- **Future trends:** External pedestrian airbags (Volvo V40), motorcycle rider airbags, multi-collision braking, and pre-crash active hood lifters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exam:**
> 1. **Writing "airbag replaces seatbelt"** — cost: 1 mark. Always say "supplemental."
> 2. **Forgetting the filter pack** in inflator diagrams — cost: 2 marks. Always include it between combustion chamber and outlet.
> 3. **Confusing deployment time (20–30 ms) with full inflation time (≤ 50 ms) with deflation time (100–200 ms)** — these are three distinct numbers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Hybrid Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Hybrid Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Hybrid Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Vehicle Technology (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Hybrid technology refers to the integrated propulsion system in an automobile that combines **two or more distinct energy sources** — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (Motors/Generators)** — along with an appropriate **Energy Storage System (ESS)** such as a battery or supercapacitor, controlled by an intelligent **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** to achieve superior fuel economy, reduced emissions, and enhanced drivetrain efficiency.

A hybrid vehicle therefore does **not** rely solely on a single power source. Instead, it intelligently blends mechanical (thermal) and electrical energy to optimize the overall energy conversion chain — addressing the inherently poor part-load efficiency of conventional gasoline/diesel engines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Muscle Athlete"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a cyclist climbing a steep hill. On flat roads, the cyclist uses leg muscles (efficient for cruising). On steep inclines, the cyclist calls upon arm muscles or gets a push from a friend (extra boost for peak load). Hybrid technology works exactly the same way:
> - The **ICE** is like the leg muscles — efficient for steady cruising.
> - The **Electric Motor** is like the extra helper — instantly available for acceleration, hill climbing, and regenerative braking.
> - The **Battery** is the friend's stored energy, ready to be deployed when needed.

This dual-source architecture allows the vehicle to *always operate each energy source near its peak efficiency point*, drastically cutting fuel wastage that occurs in conventional vehicles.

---

## 1.3 Why Hybrid Technology? — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Drivers for Hybridization:**
> 1. **Urban Stop-and-Go Traffic:** ICEs consume fuel even when idling → electric motors can shut off the engine (Idle Stop).
> 2. **Braking Energy Recovery:** Conventional cars waste ~**60–70%** of fuel energy as heat via friction brakes. Hybrid systems recover this as electricity (**Regenerative Braking**).
> 3. **Peak Power Boosting:** A small efficient ICE + electric boost gives performance of a large engine with economy of a small engine.
> 4. **Emission Compliance:** Meets stringent **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and global Euro 7 norms.
> 5. **Range Anxiety Bridge:** A stepping stone between pure ICE and pure Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs).

---

## 1.4 Core Components of a Hybrid Powertrain

| Component | Function | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **ICE** | Primary mechanical power source | Petrol / Diesel engine |
| **Traction Motor (M)** | Converts electrical → mechanical energy | AC Induction / PMSM |
| **Generator (G)** | Converts mechanical → electrical energy | Synchronous machine |
| **Traction Battery** | Stores electrical energy | Li-ion / NiMH |
| **Power Electronics Inverter** | DC ↔ AC conversion (Motor/Generator drive) | IGBT-based VSI |
| **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** | Supervisory energy management strategy | ECU + software algorithm |
| **Transmission** | Routes torque to wheels | Planetary gearset / e-CVT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:**
> The typical **electrical energy recovered per braking event** in urban driving ranges from **0.1 – 0.3 kWh**, sufficient to power an electric-only creep of **200 – 600 metres**.

---

## 1.5 Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> The world's first mass-produced hybrid, the **Toyota Prius (Japan, 1997)**, introduced the **Hybrid Synergy Drive (HSD)** using a planetary gear power-split device. This established the modern **PSCV (Power Split CVT)** architecture that dominates hybrid-electric vehicles (HEVs) today.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hybrid Vehicles (Degrees of Hybridization)

The level of hybridization determines how much electrical assistance replaces mechanical power. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following four canonical classes:

### 2.1.1 Micro-Hybrid (12V / 48V System)
- Only implements **Idle Stop-Start (ISS)** function.
- No traction motor — engine shuts off at traffic signals and restarts automatically.
- Uses a **48V Belt-Driven Starter Generator (BSG)** in mild forms.
- Fuel savings: **5 – 10%**.

### 2.1.2 Mild Hybrid
- Equipped with an **Integrated Starter Generator (ISG)** capable of torque assist.
- Cannot drive the vehicle purely on electric power.
- Fuel savings: **10 – 20%**.

### 2.1.3 Full Hybrid
- Features a **full-power traction motor** that can propel the vehicle **EV-only** for short distances.
- Combines series + parallel operation (Toyota HSD, Honda i-MMD).
- Fuel savings: **30 – 50%**.

### 2.1.4 Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
- Battery can be charged externally via the grid.
- EV-only range: **20 – 80 km**.
- Fuel savings: **50 – 80%**.

---

## 2.2 Architectural Topologies

### 2.2.1 Series Hybrid
- ICE drives **only** the generator; wheels are driven **only** by the electric motor.
- ICE operates at fixed optimal point on the **Willans Line**.
- Simple mechanical layout — no direct mechanical path.

### 2.2.2 Parallel Hybrid
- ICE and Electric Motor are **mechanically coupled** (via clutch) to the driveshaft.
- Both can deliver torque to wheels **simultaneously or independently**.
- More efficient on highways; complex clutch control.

### 2.2.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid
- Uses a **planetary gearset** (Toyota THS) to continuously split power between ICE, generator, motor, and wheels.
- Most commercially successful architecture.
- Combines benefits of both series and parallel topologies.

---

## 2.3 The Planetary Gear Power-Split Device

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Principle:**
> A planetary gearset has three nodes: **Sun gear (S)**, **Planet carrier (C)**, and **Ring gear (R)**. The fundamental kinematic constraint is:
>
> $$N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R = (N_S + N_R) \cdot \omega_C$$
>
> where $N_S$ and $N_R$ are the number of teeth on sun and ring gears, and $\omega$ represents angular velocity. By controlling motor/generator speeds, the HCU can force the ICE to operate at any desired speed independent of vehicle speed.

This is the **mechanical heart** of modern full hybrids.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All high-priority formulas for numerical problems in Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy recoverable during braking | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $P_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v$ | Regenerative braking power | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{batt}$ | Cumulative drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor}$ | Torque summation in parallel hybrid | N·m |
| 5 | $\omega_S N_S = \omega_R N_R - \omega_C (N_S + N_R)$ | Planetary gear constraint (Willis eqn) | rad/s |
| 6 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{C_{batt}} \int_0^t (I_{charge} - I_{discharge}) \, d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | % |
| 7 | $P_{ICE,opt} = f(\omega_{ICE,opt})$ | Optimal ICE operating point (BSFC map) | kW |
| 8 | $FC_{saved} = \frac{FC_{conv} - FC_{hybrid}}{FC_{conv}} \times 100$ | Percentage fuel consumption savings | % |
| 9 | $\omega_{sun} = (1+\rho)\omega_{carrier} - \rho \cdot \omega_{ring}$ | Willis equation (form) where $\rho = N_R/N_S$ | rad/s |
| 10 | $E_{batt} = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{Ah} \cdot 3600$ | Battery stored energy | Joules (J) |

---

## 2.5 Energy Management Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule-Based vs Optimization-Based Strategies:**

### 2.5.1 Rule-Based (Deterministic)
- Uses **if-then** logic from engineering heuristics.
- Example: *"If SOC < 30% AND engine power needed → start ICE."*
- Simple, real-time, robust — used in production vehicles.

### 2.5.2 Optimization-Based
- **Dynamic Programming (DP)** — finds globally optimal trajectory off-line.
- **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)** — real-time cost function:
$$\dot{m}_{eq} = \dot{m}_{fuel} + s \cdot \frac{P_{batt}}{Q_{lhv}}$$
where $s$ is the equivalence factor and $Q_{lhv}$ is the lower heating value of fuel.

### 2.5.3 Intelligent (AI-Based)
- Neural networks, fuzzy logic, reinforcement learning — emerging research area.

---

## 2.6 Regenerative Braking — Physics

When the driver releases the accelerator, the traction motor is commanded to operate as a generator. The back-EMF produces a counter-torque that decelerates the vehicle while charging the battery.

$$V_{back-emf} = K_e \cdot \omega_{motor}$$

$$T_{gen} = K_t \cdot I_{a}$$

where $K_e$ and $K_t$ are motor constants. The harvested power is:

$$P_{regen} = T_{gen} \cdot \omega_{motor}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Limitation:**
> Regenerative braking alone cannot provide emergency stopping — friction brakes must supplement above ~**0.3 g deceleration** to ensure safety.

---

## 2.7 Battery State of Charge (SOC) Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HCU must keep battery SOC within a **safe operating window**, typically:
> - **SOC_min ≈ 30 – 40%** (prevents deep discharge damage)
> - **SOC_max ≈ 70 – 80%** (prevents overcharge)
> - **Operating range ≈ 40%** of full capacity (compared to 80% usable in BEVs)
>
> This "shallow cycling" dramatically extends battery cycle life to **> 1,500,000 km** for Toyota NiMH packs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-typical numerical):**
> A hybrid car of mass **m = 1500 kg** decelerates from **v₁ = 90 km/h** to **v₂ = 30 km/h** on a level road. The regenerative braking efficiency is **η_regen = 0.65**. Calculate the electrical energy recovered and the equivalent fuel mass saved (assume fuel calorific value Q_lhv = 44 MJ/kg).

### Step 1: Convert velocities to SI units
$$v_1 = 90 \text{ km/h} = \frac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 30 \text{ km/h} = \frac{30 \times 1000}{3600} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2: Compute change in kinetic energy
$$\Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$\Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (25^2 - 8.333^2)$$

$$\Delta E_{kin} = 750 \times (625 - 69.44)$$

$$\Delta E_{kin} = 750 \times 555.56$$

$$\Delta E_{kin} = 416{,}670 \text{ J} = 416.67 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 3: Apply regenerative efficiency
$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \times \Delta E_{kin}$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.65 \times 416.67 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 270.83 \text{ kJ}}$$

### Step 4: Compute equivalent fuel mass
$$m_{fuel,equiv} = \frac{E_{recovered}}{Q_{lhv}}$$

$$m_{fuel,equiv} = \frac{270{,}830}{44 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{m_{fuel,equiv} = 6.155 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 6.16 \text{ grams}}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
> [!NOTE]
> A single deceleration event recovers enough energy equivalent to **~6 grams of petrol**. Over **1000 such events** in urban driving, this aggregates to **~6 kg of fuel saved**, demonstrating the compounding value of regenerative braking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Planetary Gear Velocity Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a Toyota THS planetary gearset, sun gear has **N_S = 30 teeth**, ring gear has **N_R = 78 teeth**. If the ring gear (connected to wheels) rotates at **2000 rpm** and the carrier (connected to ICE) at **1500 rpm**, find the generator (sun) speed.

### Step 1: Recall the Willis equation
$$\omega_S \cdot N_S + \omega_R \cdot N_R = (N_S + N_R) \cdot \omega_C$$

### Step 2: Substitute known values
$$\omega_S \cdot 30 + 2000 \cdot 78 = (30 + 78) \cdot 1500$$

$$30 \cdot \omega_S + 156{,}000 = 108 \cdot 1500$$

$$30 \cdot \omega_S + 156{,}000 = 162{,}000$$

### Step 3: Solve for sun (generator) speed
$$30 \cdot \omega_S = 162{,}000 - 156{,}000 = 6{,}000$$

$$\omega_S = \frac{6{,}000}{30} = 200 \text{ rpm}$$

### Step 4: Sign convention check
$$\boxed{\omega_S = +200 \text{ rpm (positive direction, generator mode)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A positive sun gear speed indicates the generator is being driven by the ICE → generating electricity. A negative value would indicate motoring (engine cranking or boost mode).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Parallel Hybrid Torque Blending

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A parallel hybrid SUV requires total tractive force of **F_t = 6000 N** at wheel radius **r = 0.35 m** and speed **v = 60 km/h**. The ICE alone operates at **η_ICE = 30%** BSFC, while electric path has **η_elec = 85%**. Compare fuel consumption if all torque is from ICE vs 40% electric assist.

### Step 1: Required total torque at wheels
$$T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r = 6000 \times 0.35 = 2100 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 2: Case A — 100% ICE
$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 6000 \times 16.667 = 100{,}000 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{fuel} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \frac{100}{0.30} = 333.33 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Case B — 40% electric assist
$$P_{electric} = 0.40 \times 100 = 40 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{ICE,required} = 60 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{fuel,new} = \frac{60}{0.30} = 200 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4: Battery must supply (with electrical inefficiency)
$$P_{batt,draw} = \frac{40}{\eta_{elec}} = \frac{40}{0.85} = 47.06 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Fuel energy saved per hour of operation
$$\Delta P_{fuel} = 333.33 - 200 = 133.33 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{saved/hour} = 133.33 \times 3600 = 480{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 480 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 6: Equivalent fuel saved
$$m_{saved} = \frac{480 \times 10^6}{44 \times 10^6} = 10.91 \text{ kg/hour}$$

$$\boxed{m_{saved} \approx 10.9 \text{ kg/hour} \quad (\sim39.4\% \text{ reduction})}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Comparison

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel (PSCV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical paths** | 1 (electric only) | 2 (ICE + Motor) | 3 (split) |
| **ICE efficiency** | Always optimal | Variable | Mostly optimal |
| **Transmission** | Simple | Complex clutch | Planetary gear |
| **Best use case** | City buses, trucks | Highway sedans | Mixed urban + highway |
| **Examples** | Chevrolet Volt (gen 1), Nissan e-Power | Honda Insight (gen 1) | Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid |
| **Control complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Full Hybrid Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Input: Accelerator Pedal] --> B[HCU Hybrid Control Unit]
    B --> C{Operating Mode Selector}
    C -->|EV Mode| D[Traction Motor Inverter]
    C -->|ICE Mode| E[ICE Throttle Actuator]
    C -->|Hybrid Mode| F[Torque Blending Controller]
    D --> G[AC Traction Motor]
    E --> H[Internal Combustion Engine]
    F --> I[Planetary Gear Power Split]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Drive and Differential]
    J --> K[Wheels]
    L[Battery Pack Li-ion] --> D
    M[Regenerative Brake Controller] --> L
    G --> M
    B -.SOC Feedback.-> L
    B -.Wheel Speed Feedback.-> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = power/energy flow. Dotted arrows = sensor feedback signals. The HCU is the central decision-maker, receiving SOC, speed, torque demand, and brake pressure data.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Series-Parallel Power-Split (Toyota HSD)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Powertrain["Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive THS"]
        ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> P1[Planet Carrier C]
        P1 --> P2[Sun Gear S]
        P2 --> GEN[Generator Motor G]
        GEN --> INV1[Inverter 1]
        P1 --> P3[Ring Gear R]
        P3 --> TR[Motor M2 Drive]
        TR --> INV2[Inverter 2]
        INV1 --> BAT[HV Battery]
        INV2 --> BAT
        BAT --> INV1
        BAT --> INV2
    end
    P3 --> WD[Final Drive Wheels]
    HCU1[Hybrid Control Unit] -.controls.-> GEN
    HCU1 -.controls.-> TR
    HCU1 -.controls.-> ICE
```

---

## 4.3 State Machine: Hybrid Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> VehicleOff
    VehicleOff --> ReadyMode: Ignition On
    ReadyMode --> EV_Mode: SOC high and low torque demand
    EV_Mode --> ICE_Mode: High torque demand
    EV_Mode --> Regen_Brake: Brake pedal pressed
    ICE_Mode --> Hybrid_Mode: Cruise moderate load
    ICE_Mode --> Regen_Brake: Brake pedal pressed
    Hybrid_Mode --> ICE_Mode: SOC low threshold reached
    Hybrid_Mode --> Regen_Brake: Brake pedal pressed
    Regen_Brake --> EV_Mode: Brake released SOC restored
    Regen_Brake --> ICE_Mode: High demand during braking
    ICE_Mode --> ReadyMode: Vehicle stopped idle stop
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Idle Stop state** is implicit within ReadyMode — modern hybrids shut the ICE off entirely when the vehicle is stationary with foot on brake, restarting seamlessly when the accelerator is pressed.

---

## 4.4 Energy Flow Comparison: Conventional vs Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV["Conventional Vehicle"]
        F1[Fuel Tank] -->|100%| I1[ICE 30 percent eff]
        I1 -->|70 percent loss| H1[Heat Exhaust]
        I1 -->|30 percent| W1[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph HYB["Hybrid Vehicle"]
        F2[Fuel Tank] --> I2[ICE 38 percent eff optimal]
        I2 --> P2[Planetary Split]
        P2 --> W2[Wheels]
        P2 --> G2[Generator]
        G2 --> B2[Battery]
        B2 --> M2[Motor]
        M2 --> W2
        W2 -->|Braking| R2[Regen Recovery]
        R2 --> B2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The hybrid pathway includes a **closed-loop energy recovery channel** (Wheels → Regen → Battery → Motor → Wheels) that simply does not exist in conventional vehicles. This is the single largest source of efficiency gains in urban duty cycles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define a hybrid electric vehicle. List the three main classifications based on the degree of hybridization.**

**Model Answer:**

> A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) is an automobile that uses **two or more energy sources** — typically an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and one or more electric motors — combined with an energy storage system to propel the vehicle.

The three main classifications based on degree of hybridization are:
1. **Micro-Hybrid** — Idle Stop-Start only, no traction motor.
2. **Mild Hybrid** — Integrated Starter Generator (ISG) provides torque assist but cannot drive vehicle alone.
3. **Full Hybrid** — Powerful traction motor enables **EV-only driving** for short distances.

*(Word limit ~70; aim for crisp definitions with all three terms bolded.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is regenerative braking? State its primary limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

> Regenerative braking is the process of using the traction motor as a generator during deceleration, converting the vehicle's **kinetic energy into electrical energy** stored in the battery, thereby extending range and reducing brake wear.

**Primary Limitation:**
- Cannot provide emergency braking force — typically limited to **0.2–0.3 g deceleration**; friction brakes must supplement above this threshold.
- Effectiveness drops at low vehicle speeds (below ~10 km/h) when motor back-EMF becomes insufficient to overcome battery voltage.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Electric Vehicle** using a planetary gearset. Discuss the roles of the ICE, Motor (M1), and Generator (M2) in different driving modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid vehicle of mass **1800 kg** decelerates from **80 km/h to 20 km/h**. The regenerative braking system recovers energy with an efficiency of **60%**. Compute the **electrical energy recovered** and the **equivalent fuel mass saved** (calorific value = **44 MJ/kg**). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** architecture uses a **planetary gearset** as the central power-splitting device. The three nodes of the gearset are:

- **Ring gear (R)** → connected to the wheels (final drive).
- **Sun gear (S)** → connected to Generator (M2).
- **Planet Carrier (C)** → connected to the ICE.

**Operating Modes:**

| Mode | ICE | M1 (Motor) | M2 (Generator) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EV-only (low speed)** | OFF | Drives wheels via ring gear | Spins passively |
| **Cruising (highway)** | ON, drives wheels through carrier | Off or minor assist | Generates to charge battery |
| **Acceleration (full throttle)** | ON | Provides boost torque to ring gear | Battery feeds M1 |
| **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | Acts as generator, charges battery | Off |

> **Key Insight:** The planetary gearset satisfies the **Willis constraint**: $N_S \omega_S + N_R \omega_R = (N_S + N_R) \omega_C$. By independently controlling M1 and M2 speeds, the HCU can place the ICE at any operating point on its BSFC map, independent of wheel speed — this is the essence of hybridization efficiency.

*(Refer to Block Diagram 4.2 in Section 4 for schematic.)*

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 1800$ kg
- Initial velocity $v_1 = 80$ km/h $= \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22$ m/s
- Final velocity $v_2 = 20$ km/h $= \frac{20 \times 1000}{3600} = 5.556$ m/s
- Efficiency $\eta_{regen} = 0.60$
- Calorific value $Q_{lhv} = 44$ MJ/kg

**Step 1:** Kinetic energy change
$$\Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$
$$\Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1800 \times (22.22^2 - 5.556^2)$$
$$\Delta E_{kin} = 900 \times (493.83 - 30.86)$$
$$\Delta E_{kin} = 900 \times 462.97$$
$$\Delta E_{kin} = 416{,}673 \text{ J} = 416.67 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2:** Electrical energy recovered
$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \times \Delta E_{kin} = 0.60 \times 416.67$$
$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 250.0 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent fuel mass saved
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{E_{recovered}}{Q_{lhv}} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{44 \times 10^6}$$
$$\boxed{m_{fuel} = 5.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 5.68 \text{ grams}}$$

**Interpretation:** A typical urban deceleration event recovers ~5.68 g of petrol equivalent — over a year of city commuting this aggregates to **10–15 litres saved**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of hybrid vehicles** based on (i) degree of hybridization and (ii) powertrain architecture. Compare series vs parallel hybrids with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **fuel savings percentage** in a parallel hybrid where 30% of tractive power is supplied by the electric motor. Assume ICE efficiency at part load is **25%** and the overall electrical path efficiency (motor + inverter + battery) is **80%**. Compare with a conventional vehicle having **20% drivetrain efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification by Degree of Hybridization:**

1. **Micro-Hybrid (Start-Stop only):** No traction motor; engine shuts off when stationary. Fuel saving: 5–10%.
2. **Mild Hybrid:** ISG (Integrated Starter Generator) assists ICE but cannot propel vehicle alone. Fuel saving: 10–20%.
3. **Full Hybrid:** Traction motor capable of EV-only operation for short distances. Fuel saving: 30–50%.
4. **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery with grid charging; 20–80 km EV range. Fuel saving: 50–80%.

**Classification by Architecture:**

- **Series Hybrid:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. Wheels driven **only** by motor. Simple but two energy conversions.
- **Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and Motor are **mechanically coupled** via clutch to the driveshaft. Both deliver torque to wheels.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split):** Combines both via planetary gearset. Most efficient and flexible.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Series | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Energy conversions | 2 (mech → elec → mech) | 1 (mech → mech via motor) |
| ICE operating point | Fixed optimum (efficient) | Variable (less efficient) |
| Transmission | Simple single-speed | Complex clutch logic |
| City efficiency | Excellent | Good |
| Highway efficiency | Lower (conversion losses) | Excellent |
| Component count | High (large motor + generator) | Lower (single motor) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

*(Refer to Block Diagram 4.1 in Section 4 for architecture comparison.)*

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Tractive power demand $P_{wheel} = P$ (arbitrary reference)
- Conventional drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{conv} = 20\% = 0.20$
- ICE part-load efficiency $\eta_{ICE} = 25\% = 0.25$
- Electrical path efficiency $\eta_{elec} = 80\% = 0.80$
- Electric power fraction $f_{elec} = 30\% = 0.30$
- ICE power fraction $f_{ICE} = 70\% = 0.70$

**Step 1: Conventional vehicle fuel power**
$$P_{fuel,conv} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{conv}} = \frac{P}{0.20} = 5P$$

**Step 2: Hybrid vehicle — ICE portion**
$$P_{ICE,mech} = f_{ICE} \cdot P_{wheel} = 0.70 P$$
$$P_{fuel,ICE} = \frac{P_{ICE,mech}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \frac{0.70 P}{0.25} = 2.8 P$$

**Step 3: Hybrid vehicle — Electric portion**
$$P_{elec,mech} = f_{elec} \cdot P_{wheel} = 0.30 P$$
$$P_{batt,draw} = \frac{P_{elec,mech}}{\eta_{elec}} = \frac{0.30 P}{0.80} = 0.375 P$$

The battery draws this from previously stored energy; we count its fuel-equivalent cost. Assuming the energy originally came from the ICE charging the battery at the same ICE efficiency, the equivalent fuel cost of the electrical path is:
$$P_{fuel,elec,equiv} = \frac{P_{batt,draw}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \frac{0.375 P}{0.25} = 1.5 P$$

But this over-counts — for fair comparison, we evaluate total equivalent fuel:

**Step 4: Total hybrid equivalent fuel power**
$$P_{fuel,hybrid} = P_{fuel,ICE} + P_{fuel,elec,equiv} = 2.8 P + 1.5 P = 4.3 P$$

**Step 5: Fuel savings percentage**
$$FC_{saved} = \frac{P_{fuel,conv} - P_{fuel,hybrid}}{P_{fuel,conv}} \times 100$$
$$FC_{saved} = \frac{5P - 4.3P}{5P} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{FC_{saved} = 14\%}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**
> A 14% fuel saving with only 30% electrical assist demonstrates that hybridization is highly effective even at modest electric power fractions. Increasing $f_{elec}$ to 50% (full hybrid territory) typically pushes savings beyond **30%**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s in kinetic energy problems. Always multiply by **1000/3600** explicitly.
> 2. **Sign Convention in Planetary Gears:** Confusing motoring vs generating directions — always state whether the sun gear spins **positive** (generator mode) or **negative** (motoring/boost mode).
> 3. **Battery SOC Limits:** Writing that the battery uses "100% capacity" — incorrect! Production HEVs only use ~**40%** of nameplate capacity to extend cycle life.
> 4. **Confusing Series with Parallel:** In **series**, the ICE has **no mechanical connection to wheels**. In **parallel**, both sources are mechanically coupled. Drawing a clutch between ICE and motor in a series diagram = −2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting Electrical Path Losses:** When computing equivalent fuel, you must include $\eta_{elec}$ losses; students often write $P_{fuel,elec} = 0.30P$ without dividing by $0.80$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Hybrid Technology**

- **Definition:** Hybrid = **ICE + Electric Motor + Battery + HCU**, with two distinct energy sources working in coordination.
- **Four Degrees of Hybridization:** Micro, Mild, Full, Plug-in (PHEV) — ordered by increasing electrical content.
- **Three Architectures:** Series (ICE→Gen→Batt→Motor→Wheels), Parallel (ICE+Motor mechanically coupled), Series-Parallel (planetary gear split).
- **Planetary Gear Equation:** $N_S \omega_S + N_R \omega_R = (N_S + N_R) \omega_C$ — the kinematic constraint that enables ICE speed decoupling from wheel speed.
- **Regenerative Braking Energy:** $\Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$; electrical recovery = $\eta_{regen} \times \Delta E_{kin}$.
- **Battery SOC Window:** Typically maintained between **30%–80%** (shallow cycling for longevity).
- **Willans Line Principle:** ICE is forced to operate near its BSFC minimum by the HCU — this is the core efficiency gain.
- **Torque Summation (Parallel):** $T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor}$ at the coupling point.
- **Equivalence Factor (ECMS):** $s$ balances fuel cost vs battery cost in real-time energy management.
- **Standard Conversions:** 1 km/h = **0.2778 m/s**; 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ**; petrol $Q_{lhv}$ = **44 MJ/kg**.
- **Plug-in vs Hybrid:** PHEV battery is charged externally from the grid; HEV battery is charged **only** by ICE and regen.
- **Industry Reference:** Toyota Prius introduced the HSD in **1997** — the blueprint for all modern full hybrids.

**Exam Tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** for architecture questions — examiners award 1–2 marks for visual clarity. Pair the diagram with a **mode table** showing which component is active in each driving state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to ADAS (Automatic Driver Assist Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to ADAS (Automatic Driver Assist Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are a class of electronic and electromechanical systems embedded within a road vehicle that utilise a fusion of onboard sensors, actuators, and intelligent processing algorithms to **assist the human driver** in the dynamic driving task. The objective is to enhance situational awareness, reduce driver workload, mitigate collision risk, and progressively enable higher levels of vehicle automation as classified by the **SAE J3016 standard**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *"ADAS is an umbrella term for a coordinated set of sensor-based, computationally intelligent subsystems within a vehicle that perceive the driving environment, reason about hazards, and either warn the driver (passive ADAS) or autonomously actuate vehicle controls (active ADAS) to improve safety, comfort, and efficiency."*

The architecture follows a canonical **Sense → Perceive → Plan → Act** computational pipeline, broadly conforming to the **3-tier functional stack** defined by ISO 26262 and the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Co-Pilot" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a foggy highway. Sitting next to you is an alert, well-trained, and tireless **co-pilot** who has:
- **Eyes in every direction** (cameras, radar, lidar, ultrasonic sensors)
- **Lightning-fast reflexes** (real-time micro-controllers and FPGAs)
- **An encyclopaedic memory of traffic rules** (rule-based and learning-based decision algorithms)
- **Hands on the brake, steering, and throttle** (drive-by-wire actuators)

This co-pilot is *always* scanning the road, *always* alert (never texts or dozes), and either **whispers a warning** ("Watch out!") or **gently grabs the wheel** to prevent an accident. That co-pilot is ADAS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** ADAS does **not** replace the driver (Levels 0–2). It **augments** the driver. Full autonomy (Level 5) is the broader goal of AVs, but the KTU Module 4 focus is strictly on the **driver-assist** paradigm.

## 1.3 SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires explicit knowledge of the six automation levels. Below is the canonical reference matrix:

| SAE Level | Name | Steering/Acceleration | Monitoring of Environment | Fallback Responsibility | ADAS Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | No Automation | Driver | Driver | Driver | Blind Spot Warning (BSW) |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | System (one) | Driver | Driver | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) |
| **2** | Partial Automation | System (both) | Driver | Driver | Lane Centering + ACC (Tesla Autopilot base) |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | System | System | Driver (must be ready) | Traffic Jam Pilot |
| **4** | High Automation | System | System | System (no driver in ODD) | Robotaxi (geofenced) |
| **5** | Full Automation | System | System | System | Theoretically any condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boundary between **ADAS** and **AV (Autonomous Vehicle)** lies between **Level 2 and Level 3**. KTU Module 4 explicitly covers **Levels 0, 1, and 2** systems — the "driver assist" regime.

## 1.4 Why ADAS? — The Engineering Motivation

| Metric | Pre-ADAS (1970s) | Modern ADAS-Equipped Vehicle | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fatality rate (per 1B vehicle-km) | ~25 (OECD avg.) | ~3–5 | **~80% reduction** |
| Rear-end collision rate | Baseline | Reduced by **~38%** with AEB | Euro NCAP 2022 |
| Lane-change crashes | Baseline | Reduced by **~11%** with BSD | IIHS data |

Standard reference metrics highlighted in the KTU syllabus are:
- **Euro NCAP 5-Star Rating Protocol**
- **GTR (Global Technical Regulation) No. 16** on motorcycle AEB
- **ISO 26262** functional safety standard

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic ADAS Functional Architecture

The KTU syllabus prescribes a **four-layer architecture** for every ADAS subsystem:

### Layer 1 — Perception (Sensing)
Raw physical signals from the environment are converted to digital form.
- **Radar** (24 GHz / 77 GHz FMCW) → range, relative velocity, bearing
- **Lidar** (905 nm / 1550 nm pulsed laser) → 3D point cloud
- **Camera** (Mono / Stereo / RGB-IR) → semantic segmentation, object detection
- **Ultrasonic** (40–58 kHz) → short-range proximity
- **GNSS + IMU** → ego-localisation

### Layer 2 — Fusion & Tracking
Multiple sensor streams are combined using **Kalman Filters**, **Extended Kalman Filters (EKF)**, **Particle Filters**, or modern **Bayesian Occupancy Grids** to produce a coherent world model.

### Layer 3 — Decision & Planning
Rule-based, model-predictive (MPC), or learning-based planners output a **trajectory** or **control intent**.

### Layer 4 — Actuation
Drive-by-wire subsystems execute the decision:
- **Brake-by-wire** (ESC/iBooster)
- **Steer-by-wire** (EPS)
- **Throttle-by-wire** (Electronic throttle body)
- **HMI** (HUD, haptic seat, audio chime)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) and internal assessments. Master the **units** in the right-most column.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TTC = \dfrac{d}{v_{rel}}$ | Time-to-Collision | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $d_{safe} = v \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | Stopping / safe-follow distance | metres (m) |
| 3 | $R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}}}$ | Radar max-range equation | metres (m) |
| 4 | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_{sensor}}{2 f}\right)$ | Camera field of view | radians (rad) |
| 5 | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{2 R_{max}}{c}$ | Lidar time-of-flight | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $P_{collision} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Poisson collision-risk model | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S = v_{ego} - v_{target}$ | Closing speed | m/s |
| 8 | $f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_{rel}}{\lambda}$ | Radar Doppler shift | Hz |
| 9 | $E_{lidar} = \dfrac{P_t A_r \eta_{atm}^{2R} \rho}{R^{2} \Omega}$ | Lidar received energy (simplified) | Joules (J) |
| 10 | $\theta_{res} = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | Rayleigh angular resolution | radians (rad) |

**Key Greek symbols used in the KTU Module:**
- $\mu$ — coefficient of friction (typical dry asphalt: **0.7**)
- $g$ — gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $c$ — speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\lambda$ — wavelength (77 GHz radar → **λ ≈ 3.9 mm**)
- $\sigma$ — radar cross-section (RCS), typical car: **10 m²**
- $k$ — Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

ADAS is the **commercial gateway to full autonomy**. The same Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act stack that powers a $500 Mobileye EyeQ chip (Level 2) is the seed from which Level 4 robotaxis (Waymo, Cruise) are grown. Mastery of ADAS therefore directly enables careers in:
- **OEM R&D** (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti-Suzuki)
- **Tier-1 suppliers** (Bosch, Continental, ZF, Magna, WABCO)
- **Silicon vendors** (NXP, Renesas, Texas Instruments, Qualcomm)
- **Sensor houses** (Velodyne, Luminar, Hesai, Continental Radar)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most 14-mark ESE questions follow the *system-level integration* pattern — "Explain the working of XYZ ADAS subsystem with a block diagram and derive the key safety parameter." Always pair every formula with a **numerical worked example** to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-to-Collision (TTC) and Safe Following Distance

The TTC is the **single most important safety metric** in any Forward Collision Warning (FCW) or Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) subsystem. The KTU ESE typically asks for a 7-mark derivation of this quantity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $v_{ego}$ = velocity of the ego (host) vehicle, in m/s
- $v_{target}$ = velocity of the leading target vehicle, in m/s
- $d$ = instantaneous bumper-to-bumper gap between the two vehicles, in m

**Step 1 — Define the relative velocity**

The closing speed is the rate at which the gap is shrinking:

$$v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{target}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the kinematic definition of time**

Time-to-collision is the gap divided by the rate of gap closure. Since both vehicles move in the same direction:

$$TTC = \dfrac{d}{v_{rel}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce driver reaction time**

A human driver does not respond instantaneously. The standard ISO 15622 ACC model assumes a **driver reaction time** $t_{react}$ = **1.2 s** (the KTU prescribed default).

During this interval, the ego vehicle continues to travel:

$$d_{react} = v_{ego} \cdot t_{react}$$

**Step 4 — Add the dynamic braking distance**

After reaction, the brakes are applied with maximum deceleration $a_{max}$. The distance required to come to a halt from velocity $v$:

$$v_{f}^{2} = v_{i}^{2} - 2 a_{max} \cdot d_{brake}$$

Setting $v_f = 0$:

$$d_{brake} = \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 a_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine for the minimum safe following distance**

$$d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 a_{max}}$$

If the coefficient of friction $\mu$ is used instead of the engineered deceleration $a_{max}$:

$$d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example (typical KTU problem)**

Given: $v_{ego} = 25 \, \text{m/s}$ (90 km/h), $v_{target} = 15 \, \text{m/s}$ (54 km/h), $d = 40 \, \text{m}$, $\mu = 0.7$, $t_{react} = 1.2 \, \text{s}$.

$$v_{rel} = 25 - 15 = 10 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$TTC = \dfrac{40}{10} = 4.0 \, \text{s}$$

$$d_{safe} = (25)(1.2) + \dfrac{(25)^{2}}{2 \cdot (0.7)(9.81)}$$

$$d_{safe} = 30 + \dfrac{625}{13.734} = 30 + 45.5 = 75.5 \, \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $d = 40 \, \text{m} < d_{safe} = 75.5 \, \text{m}$, the system must trigger **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** or **AEB** within 0.5 s.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — FMCW Radar Maximum Range Equation

The KTU Module 4 syllabus includes the **automotive radar equation** as a high-yield derivation. The radar transmits a chirp of power $P_t$, the wave travels to a target of radar cross-section $\sigma$, and a fraction returns to the antenna.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target**

At distance $R$, the isotropic power density is:

$$S_1 = \dfrac{P_t G}{4\pi R^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Echo power intercepted by the target**

$$P_{echo} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \dfrac{P_t G \sigma}{4\pi R^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Power density of the echo at the receiver**

The receiving antenna aperture is $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$, so:

$$S_2 = \dfrac{P_{echo}}{4\pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Received power**

$$P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Receiver thermal noise floor**

For a matched-filter receiver of bandwidth $B$ and noise figure $F$ at temperature $T$:

$$P_{noise} = k T B F$$

**Step 6 — Minimum detectable signal**

For a required output SNR:

$$P_{r,min} = k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}$$

**Step 7 — Equate and solve for $R_{max}$**

$$\dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R_{max}^{4}} = k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}}}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical sanity check (77 GHz long-range radar)**

$P_t = 10 \, \text{W}$, $G = 30 \, \text{dBi} = 1000$, $\lambda = 3.9 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $\sigma = 10 \, \text{m}^{2}$, $T = 290 \, \text{K}$, $B = 200 \, \text{MHz}$, $F = 4$, $SNR_{min} = 12 \, \text{dB} \approx 15.85$.

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{(10)(10^{6})(1.52 \times 10^{-5})(10)}{(198.4)(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(290)(2 \times 10^{8})(4)(15.85)}}$$

$$R_{max} \approx 252 \, \text{m}$$

This matches the **typical 250 m detection range** of a 77 GHz automotive ACC radar — a useful cross-check answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lidar Time-of-Flight (ToF)

A lidar fires a laser pulse at $t = 0$ and waits for the return photon. The round-trip distance is:

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{round}}{2}$$

For a maximum range $R_{max}$:

$$t_{scan} = \dfrac{2 R_{max}}{c}$$

For $R_{max} = 200 \, \text{m}$:

$$t_{scan} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 200}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1.33 \, \mu\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This micro-second scale explains why automotive lidars require **sub-microsecond timing circuits** and motivates the use of **SPAD (Single-Photon Avalanche Diode)** detectors.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Question Mapping

The following matrix maps the derivations above to typical KTU question patterns:

| KTU Question Theme | Relevant Derivation | Section Reference |
|---|---|---|
| "Derive the safe-following distance for an ACC system." | Derivation 1 (Steps 1–5) | §3.1 |
| "A vehicle travelling at X m/s detects a stationary obstacle. Compute AEB trigger time." | Derivation 1, Step 6 substitution | §3.1 |
| "Explain the radar range equation and compute maximum range." | Derivation 2 (Steps 1–7) | §3.2 |
| "Justify the use of 77 GHz over 24 GHz for ACC." | Substitute $\lambda = 3.9 \, \text{mm}$ vs 12.5 mm in radar equation | §3.2 |
| "How does a lidar detect a pedestrian at 80 m?" | Derivation 3 (ToF) | §3.3 |
| "Compare camera, radar, and lidar for an AEB use-case." | Cross-cuts all three | §3.1–§3.3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Generic ADAS Functional Block Architecture

This is the **canonical block diagram** every KTU answer on ADAS must contain. It is rendered using a Mermaid `flowchart` since the conceptual flow is best captured as a directed pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[DRIVING ENVIRONMENT] --> sens1[RADAR 77 GHZ]
    env --> sens2[LIDAR 905 NM]
    env --> sens3[FRONT CAMERA RGB]
    env --> sens4[ULTRASONIC ARRAY]
    env --> sens5[GNSS IMU MODULE]

    sens1 --> fus[SENSOR FUSION ECU]
    sens2 --> fus
    sens3 --> fus
    sens4 --> fus
    sens5 --> fus

    fus --> obj[OBJECT TRACKER KALMAN FILTER]
    obj --> plan[DECISION PLANNER MPC]
    plan --> act1[BRAKE BY WIRE]
    plan --> act2[STEER BY WIRE]
    plan --> act3[THROTTLE BY WIRE]
    plan --> hmi[HMI HUD HAPTIC]

    act1 --> wheel[VEHICLE DYNAMICS]
    act2 --> wheel
    act3 --> wheel
    wheel --> env

    fus -.-> safe[FUNCTIONAL SAFETY ISO 26262 ASIL]
    obj -.-> safe
    plan -.-> safe
```

**How to read this diagram in your KTU answer:**
- **Left column** = sensing (the vehicle's "eyes and ears").
- **Centre column** = processing (the "brain" — fusion, tracking, planning).
- **Right column** = actuation (the "hands and feet" — brake, steer, throttle).
- **Dashed red arrow** = the safety overlay (ISO 26262 ASIL monitoring of every block).

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act Pipeline with Sensor Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE[SENSE LAYER]
        s1[RADAR LONG RANGE 250 M]
        s2[RADAR MID RANGE 80 M]
        s3[CAMERA FORWARD 150 M]
        s4[ULTRASONIC CORNER 5 M]
    end

    subgraph PERCEIVE[PERCEIVE LAYER]
        p1[OBJECT DETECTION YOLO CNN]
        p2[LANE DETECTION OPENCV]
        p3[TRACKING SORT DEEPSORT]
        p4[SENSOR FUSION EKF]
    end

    subgraph PLAN[PLAN LAYER]
        pl1[BEHAVIOUR FSM]
        pl2[TRAJECTORY MPC]
        pl3[RISK ASSESSMENT TTC]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACT LAYER]
        a1[BRAKE ACTUATOR]
        a2[STEER ACTUATOR]
        a3[HUD WARNING]
        a4[BUZZER HAPTIC]
    end

    SENSE --> PERCEIVE
    PERCEIVE --> PLAN
    PLAN --> ACT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In 7-mark and 14-mark answers, **always label the four blocks with the layer name in uppercase** (SENSE, PERCEIVE, PLAN, ACT). Examiners award bonus marks for clear, layered block diagrams.

## 4.3 Figure 3 — ADAS Sub-System Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[ADAS FAMILY]

    root --> P[PASSIVE ADAS]
    root --> A[ACTIVE ADAS]

    P --> P1[FORWARD COLLISION WARNING FCW]
    P --> P2[LANE DEPARTURE WARNING LDW]
    P --> P3[BLIND SPOT DETECTION BSD]
    P --> P4[DROWSY DRIVER ALERT DDA]

    A --> A1[ADAPTIVE CRUISE CONTROL ACC]
    A --> A2[AUTOMATIC EMERGENCY BRAKING AEB]
    A --> A3[LANE KEEP ASSIST LKA]
    A --> A4[AUTOMATED PARKING ASSIST APA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **passive vs active** distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Memorise:
> - **Passive** = warns the driver, no actuation
> - **Active** = actuates brake / steer / throttle without driver consent

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled on the **actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**: Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6 marks) and Part B (14 marks internal choice, sub-divided into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Answer in ~50 words)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
*Define ADAS and classify its sub-systems with two examples each for passive and active types.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are electronic vehicle subsystems that use sensors, controllers, and actuators to assist the driver in the dynamic driving task. They are classified as:
- **Passive ADAS**: Warns the driver without actuating controls. Examples: **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** and **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)**.
- **Active ADAS**: Actuates brake, steer, or throttle autonomously. Examples: **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** and **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation and identify the level at which ADAS transitions to full autonomy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SAE J3016 standard defines **six levels** of driving automation: **Level 0** (No Automation), **Level 1** (Driver Assistance), **Level 2** (Partial Automation), **Level 3** (Conditional Automation), **Level 4** (High Automation), and **Level 5** (Full Automation).

ADAS covers **Levels 0 to 2**, where the driver retains fallback responsibility. The transition to **full autonomy** begins at **Level 3** and culminates at **Level 5**, where the system performs the entire dynamic driving task under all conditions.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a typical ADAS**. Explain the role of each of the four layers: Sense, Perceive, Plan, and Act.

**(b) [7 marks]** A passenger car is travelling at $v_{ego} = 20 \, \text{m/s}$ and approaches a stationary obstacle at a distance $d = 60 \, \text{m}$. Assuming a maximum brake deceleration of $a_{max} = 7 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$ and a sensor-to-actuator latency of $t_{lat} = 0.3 \, \text{s}$, determine:
- (i) The **Time-to-Collision (TTC)**.
- (ii) The **braking distance** required.
- (iii) Whether AEB intervention is **mandatory** to avoid collision.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A typical ADAS follows a **four-layer Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act pipeline** (refer to Figure 1 in Section 4.1 above).

1. **Sense layer** — Radar, Lidar, camera, ultrasonic, and GNSS/IMU modules convert physical environmental signals into digital form. For example, a 77 GHz FMCW radar provides range and Doppler velocity, while a forward camera provides lane and object classification.
2. **Perceive layer** — A sensor-fusion ECU combines the multi-modal data using Kalman or Bayesian filters to build a coherent world model. Object detection CNNs (e.g., YOLO) and tracking algorithms (SORT) maintain identities of vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists.
3. **Plan layer** — A behaviour finite-state machine and a Model Predictive Controller (MPC) generate a safe trajectory. Risk-assessment modules compute Time-to-Collision (TTC) and trigger warnings.
4. **Act layer** — Drive-by-wire actuators (brake, steer, throttle) execute the decision. HMI subsystems (HUD, haptic seat) provide driver feedback.

The **functional safety overlay** (ISO 26262, ASIL-D for AEB) continuously monitors all four layers.

*(Marks breakdown: Block diagram = 3 marks; layer explanations = 4 × 1 mark = 4 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $v_{ego} = 20 \, \text{m/s}$, $v_{target} = 0 \, \text{m/s}$, $d = 60 \, \text{m}$, $a_{max} = 7 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$, $t_{lat} = 0.3 \, \text{s}$.

**(i) Time-to-Collision**

$$v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{target} = 20 - 0 = 20 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$TTC = \dfrac{d}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3.0 \, \text{s}$$

**(ii) Braking distance**

$$d_{brake} = \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 a_{max}} = \dfrac{(20)^{2}}{2 \cdot 7} = \dfrac{400}{14} = 28.57 \, \text{m}$$

**Distance covered during sensor-actuator latency**

$$d_{lat} = v_{ego} \cdot t_{lat} = 20 \cdot 0.3 = 6.0 \, \text{m}$$

**Total distance required to stop safely**

$$d_{required} = d_{lat} + d_{brake} = 6.0 + 28.57 = 34.57 \, \text{m}$$

**(iii) AEB Decision**

Since $d_{required} = 34.57 \, \text{m} < d = 60 \, \text{m}$, the driver has a **safety margin of 25.43 m** even without AEB. However, best practice is to **trigger AEB pre-charge at TTC = 2.5 s** (i.e., at $d = 50 \, \text{m}$) to ensure full hydraulic pressure build-up. **Conclusion: AEB pre-charge is recommended; full autonomous braking is not mandatory, but advisable for human-reaction-time contingencies.**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the working of an **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system**. With a labelled diagram, explain how radar, camera, and ECU cooperate to maintain a safe headway distance.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **radar maximum range equation** for a 77 GHz automotive FMCW radar. Given $P_t = 5 \, \text{W}$, $G = 30 \, \text{dBi}$, $\lambda = 3.9 \, \text{mm}$, $\sigma = 10 \, \text{m}^{2}$, $T = 290 \, \text{K}$, $B = 200 \, \text{MHz}$, $F = 4$, and $SNR_{min} = 10$, compute $R_{max}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working principle of ACC:**

Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) is a **Level 1** ADAS that maintains a driver-set cruising speed **and** automatically adjusts it to keep a safe time-gap from a slower leading vehicle. The reference architecture is shown in Figure 2 in Section 4.2.

**Subsystem cooperation:**
1. A **77 GHz FMCW radar** (mounted behind the front grille) emits linear-frequency-modulated chirps. The reflected signal from the preceding vehicle carries time-delay (range) and Doppler (relative velocity) information.
2. A **forward monocular camera** (behind the windscreen) performs **object classification** — it distinguishes a car from a truck, motorcycle, or pedestrian — using a deep CNN.
3. The **radar–camera sensor-fusion ECU** (typically an NXP S32R or TI TDA4x) feeds the fused track list to the **longitudinal controller** (PI or MPC).
4. The controller commands the **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** and the **ESC hydraulic modulator** to either accelerate (via throttle) or decelerate (via light brake).
5. The **HMI** (instrument cluster / HUD) displays the set speed, the time-gap setting (1.0 s / 1.5 s / 2.0 s), and the detected target.

The control law is typically:
