$$d_{desired} = d_{min} + v_{ego} \cdot t_{gap}$$

where $d_{min} \approx 2 \, \text{m}$ and $t_{gap}$ is user-selectable.

*(Marks breakdown: Working description = 3 marks; diagram = 2 marks; subsystem roles = 2 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** for the full derivation. The final radar range equation is:

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}}}$$

**Substituting numerical values (in SI units):**

- $P_t = 5 \, \text{W}$
- $G = 30 \, \text{dBi} = 10^{3} = 1000$
- $\lambda = 3.9 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- $\sigma = 10 \, \text{m}^{2}$
- $(4\pi)^{3} = 1984.4$
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- $T = 290 \, \text{K}$
- $B = 200 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Hz}$
- $F = 4$
- $SNR_{min} = 10$ (linear)

**Numerator:**

$$P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma = (5)(10^{6})(1.521 \times 10^{-5})(10) = 7.605 \times 10^{2}$$

**Denominator:**

$$(4\pi)^{3} k T B F \cdot SNR_{min} = (1984.4)(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(290)(2 \times 10^{8})(4)(10)$$

$$= 6.366 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{7.605 \times 10^{2}}{6.366 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.195 \times 10^{10}$$

**Fourth root:**

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{1.195 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt[4]{11.95 \times 10^{9}} \approx \sqrt[4]{11.95} \times 10^{2.25}$$

$$\sqrt[4]{11.95} \approx 1.857, \quad 10^{2.25} \approx 177.8$$

$$R_{max} \approx 1.857 \times 177.8 \approx 330 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{R_{max} \approx 330 \, \text{m}}$. This is consistent with the **long-range 77 GHz radar** used in flagship ACC systems (e.g., Bosch LRR4).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing passive and active ADAS** — a 3-mark question can become 0 if you list LKA as "passive" (it is *active*). Memorise: *passive warns, active acts.*
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of TTC** — it is in **seconds**, not metres. Examiners deduct ½ mark.
> 3. **Wrong $G$ value in radar equation** — antenna gain is **dimensionless power ratio**, not in dBi. Always convert dB → linear: $G_{linear} = 10^{G_{dBi}/10}$.
> 4. **Omitting the sensor latency term** in the stopping-distance derivation — this is the most common 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram** — for a 7-mark ADAS question, the absence of a labelled diagram forfeits at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up SAE levels** — Level 2 still requires the driver to monitor; Level 3 does not. This is a board-exam favourite trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **ADAS definition:** Sensor + computation + actuation to **assist** the driver (Levels 0–2).
- **SAE J3016 boundary:** ADAS = Levels 0–2; AV = Levels 3–5.
- **Four-layer architecture:** **Sense → Perceive → Plan → Act**, with an **ISO 26262 safety overlay**.
- **Passive vs Active:** Passive = warn only (FCW, LDW, BSD). Active = act (ACC, AEB, LKA, APA).
- **Key sensors:** 77 GHz **radar** (long-range, weather-robust), **lidar** (precise 3D), **camera** (semantic), **ultrasonic** (close-range), **GNSS+IMU** (localisation).
- **MUST-know formulas (KTU ESE hot list):**
  - $TTC = d / v_{rel}$
  - $d_{safe} = v \cdot t_{react} + v^{2} / (2 \mu g)$
  - $R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma / [(4\pi)^{3} k T B F \cdot SNR_{min}]}$
  - $t_{scan} = 2 R_{max} / c$
  - $f_{Doppler} = 2 v_{rel} / \lambda$
- **Standard constants to memorise:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$, 77 GHz → $\lambda \approx 3.9 \, \text{mm}$, $\mu_{dry} \approx 0.7$.
- **Functional safety:** ISO 26262 mandates **ASIL** (A to D); AEB is typically **ASIL-D**, the most stringent.
- **V2X future direction:** Beyond on-board sensors, **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** communication extends ADAS perception to see-around-corners — covered in higher semesters.
- **Block diagrams are non-negotiable:** Every 7-mark and 14-mark ADAS answer **must** include a labelled Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act flowchart.
- **Numerical habit:** Always solve one worked example per derivation — KTU examiners reward quantified reasoning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Definition + Block Diagram + 4-Layer Architecture + Numerical Example + SAE Level + Active/Passive Tag" — these six elements, present in every answer, will secure you 90% of the marks on any ADAS question.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Vehicles, Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Vehicles, Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Electric Vehicle (EV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose principal propulsion system is driven by one or more electric motors, utilizing energy stored in a rechargeable energy storage system (RESS) or supplied continuously from an external source (e.g., overhead lines or fuel cells), instead of being solely dependent on the combustion of fossil fuels in an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Automobile Instrumentation framework), EVs are broadly characterized by:
- **Energy Source:** Battery pack, fuel cell, ultra-capacitor, solar cell, or overhead line.
- **Propulsion:** Electric motor(s) — typically AC induction, BLDC (Brushless DC), PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), or SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor).
- **Control Unit:** Power electronics-based controller (inverter + VCU — Vehicle Control Unit).
- **Auxiliary Systems:** Battery Management System (BMS), thermal management, regenerative braking interface, and onboard charger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Note:**
> - Typical EV efficiency: **80–90%** (motor + drivetrain) vs ICE efficiency: **25–35%**.
> - Standard Battery energy density benchmark: **150–250 Wh/kg** (Li-ion pack level).
> - India-specific FAME-II classification uses **Battery Capacity (kWh)** as the primary subsidy metric.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Rechargeable Torch" Principle:**
> Think of an EV exactly like a **rechargeable LED torch** you use at home. Instead of throwing away disposable batteries (like petrol in an ICE car that is burned and lost), the torch has a **rechargeable cell** that you plug into a wall socket. When you switch it on, the cell's stored chemical energy becomes **light energy**. In an EV, the wall socket is your **charging station**, the rechargeable cell is the **battery pack**, and instead of light, the energy becomes **rotational motion** of a motor that turns the wheels.

| Component | Torch Analogy | Real EV Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Disposable/Rechargeable cell | Stores energy | **Battery Pack** |
| Switch | Controls power | **VCU / Inverter** |
| LED | Output (light) | **Electric Motor** |
| Wall socket | Energy input | **EV Charger / Station** |
| Reflector | Directs output | **Transmission & Wheels** |

### 1.3 Why Electric Vehicles? — Driving Forces

1. **Environmental Necessity:** Transportation contributes ~**25%** of global CO₂ emissions. EVs can reduce tailpipe emissions by **60–100%** depending on grid mix.
2. **Energy Security:** Reduces dependence on imported crude oil (India imports ~**85%** of its petroleum).
3. **Performance:** Instant torque delivery — a typical EV delivers **100% torque at 0 rpm** (no gear shifts).
4. **Regulatory Push:** India's target — **30% EV penetration by 2030**; Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) norms.
5. **Cost of Ownership:** Lower running cost (₹1–1.5/km vs ₹6–8/km for ICE), fewer moving parts (no oil change, spark plugs, clutch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hot Point:** Examiners frequently ask *"List the socio-economic and environmental reasons for the global shift toward EV technology."* Memorize at least **four** valid points.

---

### 1.4 Historical Evolution of EVs (Brief Timeline)

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| 1832 | First electric carriage by Robert Anderson (Scotland) |
| 1890s | EVs outsold ICE vehicles in the USA |
| 1908 | Ford Model T (ICE) — ICE dominance began |
| 1996 | GM EV1 — first mass-produced modern EV |
| 2008 | Tesla Roadster — lithium-ion era begins |
| 2017 | India: NITI Aayog releases EV policy draft |
| 2020 | FAME-II subsidy scheme in India active |
| 2024 | KTU introduces EV modules in core syllabus |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Operational Concept — How an EV Works

The fundamental operating principle of an Electric Vehicle rests on the **electromechanical energy conversion chain**:

**Step 1 — Energy Storage**
- The rechargeable battery pack (RESS) stores DC electrical energy in the form of chemical potential energy.

**Step 2 — Power Conditioning**
- A **DC-DC converter** steps the battery voltage to the level required by the traction inverter.
- A **traction inverter** converts DC → 3-phase AC using IGBT/MOSFET switches (PWM modulation).

**Step 3 — Electromechanical Conversion**
- The 3-phase AC drives the **traction motor** (BLDC, PMSM, or induction).
- Motor produces torque $\tau$ proportional to current $I$ and magnetic flux $\phi$.

**Step 4 — Mechanical Transmission**
- A single-speed reduction gearbox transfers motor torque to the wheels.
- (Multi-speed transmissions are rare in pure EVs due to the motor's wide constant-power range.)

**Step 5 — Regenerative Braking**
- During deceleration, the motor acts as a **generator**, converting kinetic energy back to electrical energy.
- This energy is fed back to the battery (subject to BMS state-of-charge limits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key 'Why' Question Answered:**
> *Why is regenerative braking possible in EVs but not in conventional ICE vehicles?*
> Because the traction motor is **reversible** — it can operate as both a motor (forward conversion) and a generator (reverse conversion). An ICE is inherently a one-way heat engine.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Electric Vehicles — The KTU Master Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Scheme officially classifies electric vehicles along TWO axes:**
> 1. **By Degree of Electrification** (how much of the drivetrain is electric)
> 2. **By Powertrain Architecture** (how the electric and ICE components are mechanically linked)

### A. Classification by Degree of Electrification

| Type | Abbrev. | Electric Drive | ICE Drive | Battery Size | External Charging |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional ICE | — | ❌ | ✅ | — | — |
| Micro Hybrid | µHEV | ❌ (Start-Stop) | ✅ | < 0.5 kWh | ❌ |
| Mild Hybrid | MHEV | ⚠️ (Assist only) | ✅ | 0.5–1 kWh | ❌ |
| Full Hybrid | HEV | ✅ | ✅ | 1–2 kWh | ❌ |
| Plug-in Hybrid | PHEV | ✅ | ✅ | 4–15 kWh | ✅ |
| Battery Electric | BEV | ✅ | ❌ | 20–100 kWh | ✅ |
| Fuel Cell Electric | FCEV | ✅ | ❌ | (small buffer) | H₂ station |

### B. Classification by Powertrain Architecture (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel)

**1. Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**
- The ICE **does NOT** drive the wheels directly. It drives a generator to charge the battery.
- The wheels are driven **only** by the electric motor.
- *Analogy:* A diesel locomotive — diesel engine runs a generator, generator powers traction motors.

**2. Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV-Parallel)**
- Both ICE and electric motor are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels (typically via a clutch).
- Either or both can propel the vehicle.
- *Analogy:* Two oxen pulling the same cart — they can work together or one at a time.

**3. Series-Parallel Hybrid (Split Hybrid)**
- Combines both architectures using a **Power Split Device** (planetary gear set).
- Example: Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS).
- Allows operation in Series mode, Parallel mode, or pure EV mode.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations — Memorize before any EV module test.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I$ | $\tau$ = torque, $k_t$ = torque const., $\phi$ = flux, $I$ = current | N·m, T, A | Motor torque generation |
| 2 | $P_m = \tau \cdot \omega$ | $P_m$ = mechanical power, $\omega$ = angular speed | W, rad/s | Output power check |
| 3 | $E_{req} = \dfrac{m \cdot v^2}{2}$ | $E$ = kinetic energy, $m$ = mass, $v$ = velocity | J, kg, m/s | Energy required to accelerate |
| 4 | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q \cdot \eta$ | $E$ = stored energy, $V$ = voltage, $Q$ = capacity, $\eta$ = efficiency | Wh | Battery range calculation |
| 5 | $R_{EV} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{100km}}$ | $R$ = range (km), $E_{100km}$ = energy per 100 km | km | EV range estimation |
| 6 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{bat}} \int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | SOC = State of Charge, $C$ = capacity | % | Battery monitoring |
| 7 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic}} \times 100$ | Regenerative efficiency | % | Braking energy recovery |
| 8 | $F_{trac} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Max tractive force | N | Wheel slip / traction limit |
| 9 | $V_{open} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | Open-circuit / terminal voltage | V | Battery model (Thevenin) |
| 10 | $P_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{\eta_{ch} \cdot t_{ch}}$ | Charging power | kW | Charger sizing |

> **Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute value notations in formulas (e.g., $\vert v \vert$) are written using `\vert` to ensure proper rendering.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Concept | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| Battery Sizing | Determines vehicle **range** and **weight distribution** |
| Motor Selection | Defines **acceleration, top speed**, and **gradeability** |
| BMS Design | Ensures **safety, longevity, and warranty** (8-yr battery law) |
| Regenerative Braking | Recovers up to **30%** of urban driving energy |
| Power Electronics | Determines **efficiency, EMI, and thermal** profile |
| VCU Algorithms | Enables **drive modes** (Eco, Normal, Sport) and **ADAS integration** |
| Fast Charging | 150 kW DC chargers add ~**200 km** in 15–20 minutes |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basic EV Range Equation

**Problem Statement:**
> Given a vehicle mass $m$, drag coefficient $C_d$, frontal area $A$, rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr}$, and battery capacity $E_{bat}$, derive the theoretical range equation of a Battery Electric Vehicle on a flat road at constant velocity $v$.

**Step 1 — Identify the resistive forces on a moving vehicle**

Three forces oppose motion:
1. Aerodynamic drag: $F_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$
2. Rolling resistance: $F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$
3. (Neglecting gradient and inertia for constant-speed derivation)

**Step 2 — Total tractive force required**

$$
F_{trac} = F_{drag} + F_{roll} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr}
$$

**Step 3 — Power required at wheels**

Power needed to overcome these forces at constant speed $v$ is:

$$
P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \cdot v = \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr} \right) \cdot v
$$

**Step 4 — Power drawn from the battery (account for drivetrain losses)**

Let the overall drivetrain efficiency be $\eta_{dt}$ (motor + inverter + gearbox combined). Then:

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{dt}} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g C_{rr} v \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Energy consumed over distance $d$**

Time to travel distance $d$ at constant speed $v$ is $t = d / v$. Hence:

$$
E_{consumed} = P_{bat} \cdot t = \dfrac{P_{bat} \cdot d}{v}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for maximum range $R$**

Set $E_{consumed} = E_{bat}$ (full battery utilized):

$$
R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot v \cdot \eta_{dt}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g C_{rr} v}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplified Range Equation**

Cancel one factor of $v$:

$$
\boxed{\, R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{dt}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr}} \,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This equation shows that **doubling speed reduces range by a factor of 4** (since drag term dominates at high $v$). This is the *exact reason* highway driving drains an EV battery faster than city driving.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery State of Charge (Coulomb Counting)

**Problem Statement:**
> A BEV has a 60 kWh battery and a measured average current draw of 50 A during city driving. Estimate the SOC drop after 1 hour of travel.

**Step 1 — Express SOC in differential form**

By definition, the change in SOC equals the charge drawn (Ah) divided by the total capacity $C_{bat}$ (Ah):

$$
\dfrac{dSOC}{dt} = \dfrac{I(t)}{C_{bat}}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over time**

For constant current $I$:

$$
SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{I \cdot t}{C_{bat}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the values**

- $I = 50$ A
- $t = 1$ hour $= 3600$ s
- $E_{bat} = 60$ kWh. Assuming nominal pack voltage $V_{bat} = 400$ V, then $C_{bat} = \dfrac{60000}{400} = 150$ Ah.

**Step 4 — Compute the SOC change**

$$
\Delta SOC = \dfrac{50 \times 1}{150} = 0.333 = 33.3\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

After 1 hour at 50 A, the SOC has dropped by 33.3% — meaning a full 60 kWh battery supports **~3 hours** of this load before depletion (ignoring auxiliary loads and inefficiencies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Numerical problems in Part A frequently ask to compute SOC change, range, or charging time. Practice the **unit conversion** (Wh ↔ Ah) meticulously — it is the most common mistake point.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HEV vs BEV vs PHEV

| Parameter | HEV | PHEV | BEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery Size | Small (1–2 kWh) | Medium (4–15 kWh) | Large (20–100 kWh) |
| External Charging | Not required | Required | Required |
| ICE Present | Yes | Yes | No |
| Pure EV Range | < 2 km | 20–80 km | 200–600 km |
| Total Range | 600–1000 km | 500–800 km | 200–600 km |
| CO₂ Reduction | ~20–30% | ~40–60% | ~80–100% |
| Cost | Moderate | High | Highest (declining) |
| Complexity | High | Very High | Moderate |
| Typical Example | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Toyota Prius Prime | Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3 |
| Best Use Case | Urban commuter with no charger access | Mixed use (home + long trip) | Daily commuter with charging |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel Hybrid

| Feature | Series (SHEV) | Parallel (PHEV) | Series-Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link to ICE | None (generator only) | Direct to wheels | Both (via PSD) |
| Drivetrain efficiency | Lower (energy conversions) | Higher (direct drive) | Highest (mode optimized) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| City driving | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Highway driving | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Component count | More (generator) | Fewer | Many (PSD + 2 motors) |
| Example | Chevrolet Volt (early gen) | Honda Insight (1st gen) | Toyota Prius (THS) |
| ICE always at optimal point? | Yes | No | Yes |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**
> **Define an Electric Vehicle. List any three advantages of EVs over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Vehicle (EV) is a vehicle propelled by one or more electric motors using energy stored in a rechargeable battery or other energy storage system, rather than relying on an internal combustion engine.

**Three advantages over ICE vehicles:**
1. **Higher energy efficiency** (80–90% for EVs vs 25–35% for ICEs), since electric drivetrains have fewer energy conversion losses.
2. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — improves urban air quality and reduces greenhouse gas footprint.
3. **Lower operating cost** — electricity cost per km is significantly lower (₹1–1.5/km) than petrol/diesel cost (₹6–8/km).
4. **Lower maintenance** — EVs have fewer moving parts (no oil, spark plug, clutch, timing belt, exhaust).
5. **Regenerative braking** — recovers kinetic energy during deceleration, extending range.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 marks]**
> **Differentiate between BEV, HEV, and PHEV with respect to battery capacity and external charging capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery Capacity | Large (20–100 kWh) | Small (1–2 kWh) | Medium (4–15 kWh) |
| External Charging | Mandatory (AC/DC) | Not required | Required (for EV mode) |
| ICE Present | No | Yes | Yes |
| Pure Electric Range | 200–600 km | < 2 km | 20–80 km |
| Tailpipe Emissions | None | Reduced | Greatly reduced |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Format Examination Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - When listing EV types, **always** state both the abbreviation (BEV/HEV/PHEV) and the full form in the first mention. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for inconsistent nomenclature.
> - In architecture diagrams, **label every block** (e.g., "ICE", "Motor", "Generator") — a sketch without labels earns **zero marks** for the diagram component.
> - Do NOT confuse "Series HEV" with "Parallel HEV". A very common mistake: stating that in a Series HEV, the ICE directly drives the wheels. It does **NOT**.
> - Always include **units** in numerical answers. A correct number without "kWh" or "km" loses **0.5 mark**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam Pattern — Module 4, 14 marks]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**. Identify the function of each major block. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A BEV has the following specifications: Battery capacity = 50 kWh, drivetrain efficiency = 0.85, rolling resistance coefficient = 0.012, drag coefficient = 0.30, frontal area = 2.2 m², vehicle mass = 1500 kg, target constant speed = 60 km/h. Air density = 1.225 kg/m³. **Calculate the theoretical range** of the vehicle at this speed, neglecting gradient and acceleration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BEV Architecture (7 marks):**
- Draw the block diagram with the following labeled blocks (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes):
  - **Grid / Charging Station** — AC supply input.
  - **Onboard Charger** — Converts AC to DC for battery charging.
  - **Battery Pack (RESS)** — Li-ion cells in series-parallel configuration; nominal 400 V.
  - **Battery Management System (BMS)** — Monitors voltage, current, temperature, SOC, SOH.
  - **Traction Inverter** — DC → 3-phase AC using PWM; controlled by VCU.
  - **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** — Master controller; receives throttle, brake, mode inputs.
  - **Traction Motor** — AC induction / PMSM / BLDC; produces torque.
  - **Transmission (Single-speed reducer)** — Steps down motor RPM and increases torque to wheels.
  - **Regenerative Braking Path** — Motor operates as generator; feeds back to battery.
  - **Auxiliary DC-DC Converter** — Steps high voltage to 12 V for auxiliaries (lights, infotainment).

**Working:** On pressing accelerator, VCU commands the inverter → supplies 3-phase AC to motor → motor produces torque → gearbox transmits to wheels. During braking, motor acts as generator → BMS controls charging current → energy stored in battery.

**(b) Range Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert units**
- $v = 60$ km/h $= \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m²
- $m = 1500$ kg, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $E_{bat} = 50$ kWh $= 50 \times 3600 \times 1000 = 1.8 \times 10^8$ J
- $\eta_{dt} = 0.85$

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance force**

$$
F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic drag force**

$$
F_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2
$$

$$
F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.89 \approx 112.36\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Total tractive force**

$$
F_{trac} = 176.58 + 112.36 = 288.94\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Power at wheels**

$$
P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \times v = 288.94 \times 16.67 \approx 4816\ \text{W} = 4.82\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Power drawn from battery**

$$
P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{4.82}{0.85} \approx 5.67\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Range**

$$
R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{bat}} = \dfrac{50\ \text{kWh}}{5.67\ \text{kW}} \approx 8.82\ \text{h}
$$

Converting to distance:

$$
R_{distance} = 8.82 \times 60 = 529.2\ \text{km}
$$

**Final Answer:** The theoretical range of the BEV is approximately **529 km** at a constant speed of 60 km/h.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *— Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam Pattern — Module 4, 14 marks]**

> **(a)** Explain the **classification of electric vehicles based on the degree of electrification**. Compare the key features of Micro-Hybrid, Mild-Hybrid, Full-Hybrid, Plug-in Hybrid, and Battery Electric Vehicles. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **Series Hybrid and Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle** powertrain architectures. State two advantages and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification by Degree of Electrification (7 marks):**
Present the classification table from Section 2.2 of these notes. Explain in order:
- **Micro-Hybrid (µHEV):** Uses a small starter-generator to implement start-stop functionality. No electric propulsion. Battery < 0.5 kWh.
- **Mild Hybrid (MHEV):** 48 V belt-driven starter-generator (BSG) assists the engine during acceleration. Cannot propel vehicle on electric power alone. Battery 0.5–1 kWh.
- **Full Hybrid (HEV):** Both ICE and electric motor can independently drive the wheels. Examples: Toyota Camry Hybrid, Honda City Hybrid. Battery 1–2 kWh, no external charging.
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery (4–15 kWh), can be externally charged, pure EV range 20–80 km. ICE acts as range extender.
- **Battery Electric (BEV):** 100% electric. No ICE. Battery 20–100 kWh, range 200–600 km. Examples: Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.

**Key features to tabulate:** Battery size, pure EV range, charging requirement, complexity, cost, emission reduction.

**(b) Series vs Parallel HEV Architecture (7 marks):**

**Series HEV — Diagram (refer to Mermaid Section 4.3):**
- ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Traction Motor → Wheels
- The ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels.

**Two Advantages of Series HEV:**
1. ICE always operates at its **optimal efficiency point** (e.g., on a constant load curve) → lower fuel consumption.
2. **Simpler mechanical design** — no complex transmission or clutch required.

**One Disadvantage of Series HEV:**
1. **Three energy conversions** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) cause cumulative losses, making it less efficient on highways.

**Parallel HEV — Diagram:**
- ICE and Motor both mechanically coupled to a common driveshaft via a clutch.
- Wheels are driven by ICE, Motor, or both.

**Two Advantages of Parallel HEV:**
1. **Higher efficiency on highways** because ICE directly drives wheels — no energy conversion loss.
2. **Smaller electric motor and battery** can be used since the motor only assists; this reduces cost and weight.

**One Disadvantage of Parallel HEV:**
1. ICE may operate at **non-optimal points** (e.g., idle or low load), reducing fuel economy in city driving.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Topic 1**

- **EV Definition:** Vehicle propelled by electric motor(s) using energy from a rechargeable storage system.
- **Efficiency Order:** BEV drivetrain (80–90%) > HEV (40–50%) > ICE (25–35%).
- **Five Main Categories:** µHEV, MHEV, HEV, PHEV, BEV (FCEV is the sixth).
- **Series HEV:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. ICE NEVER touches the wheels directly.
- **Parallel HEV:** ICE and Motor both can drive the wheels via a clutch.
- **Series-Parallel HEV:** Uses planetary gear (Power Split Device) — Toyota's THS is the textbook example.
- **Key Equation:** $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{dt}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr}}$ — range is **inversely proportional** to $v^2$.
- **SOC Formula:** $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{I \cdot t}{C_{bat}}$ — Coulomb-counting method.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Reverses motor action to recover kinetic energy; efficiency 20–30% in urban driving.
- **Standard Indian Subsidy:** FAME-II uses **battery capacity (kWh)** as the primary metric.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing "PHEV (Parallel HEV)" with "PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid EV)". They are **different terms** — PHEV in KTU exams means **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle**.
- **Unit Conversions to memorize:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 1000 Wh; 1 Ah at 1 V = 1 Wh; 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s.
- **Standard Values to memorize:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; ICE efficiency ~ 25–35%; EV efficiency ~ 80–90%.
- **Key Components to recall in any architecture question:** Battery Pack, BMS, Traction Inverter, VCU, Traction Motor, Transmission, DC-DC Converter, Charger, Regenerative Braking Path.
- **Examiner's Pet Question:** *"Why can't a pure BEV use the same regenerative braking approach as a fuel-cell vehicle?"* — Answer: Both CAN; the question tests whether the student understands the **battery buffering** role in FCEV.
- **India-specific context to mention in descriptive answers:** FAME-II policy, PLI scheme for ACC batteries, NITI Aayog's 30% EV penetration target by 2030.
- **Energy flow direction (regen):** Wheels → Motor (now Generator) → Inverter (now rectifier) → Battery (BMS-controlled charging current).
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Automotive Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Automotive Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Automotive Instrumentation** refers to the integrated suite of electronic, digital, and networked measurement, display, and driver-information systems that replace or augment traditional electromechanical gauges in contemporary vehicles. It encompasses digital instrument clusters, Head-Up Displays (HUDs), telematics, Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) sensors, On-Board Diagnostics (OBD), in-vehicle networking (CAN/LIN/FlexRay/Ethernet), and Human-Machine Interface (HMI) technologies that collectively provide real-time vehicle status, navigation, safety, and connectivity information to the driver and external systems.

The discipline sits at the intersection of **embedded systems**, **sensor fusion**, **automotive electronics**, and **ergonomics**, and is governed by standards such as **ISO 26262** (functional safety), **ISO 15005** (dialogue principles), **SAE J1939**, and **SAE J1850**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Cockpit" Analogy:** Think of a traditional car dashboard as a wall of *separate analog clocks* — speedometer, fuel gauge, temperature dial — each doing one job in isolation. Now imagine replacing that wall with a **single large tablet screen** (like an iPad) that shows *everything at once*, *anticipates what you need*, *warns you before something goes wrong*, and *talks to the outside world* (cloud, satellites, other cars). That is modern automotive instrumentation — a *cognitively aware, networked cockpit* rather than a bank of dumb gauges.

The transition can be summarized in three paradigms:

| Era | Paradigm | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-1980s** | Electromechanical | Bimetallic temperature gauge, cable-driven speedometer |
| **1980s–2000s** | Hybrid Analog-Digital | LCD odometer inside analog dial |
| **2010s–Present** | Fully Digital & Connected | Tesla-style TFT cluster, AR-HUD, 5G-V2X |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **CAN bus data rate:** up to **1 Mbit/s** (Classical CAN); **5 Mbit/s** (CAN FD)
> - **LIN bus data rate:** up to **19.2 kbit/s**
> - **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1):** **100 Mbit/s** full-duplex over a single twisted pair
> - **Standard OBD-II diagnostic connector voltage:** **0 V to 12 V** (logic levels per SAE J1979)
> - **Refresh rate of modern TFT clusters:** **60 Hz to 120 Hz**
> - **HUD virtual image distance:** typically **2 m to 2.5 m** in front of the driver
> - **Operating temperature range for automotive-grade electronics:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** (Grade 2, AEC-Q100)
> - **GPS positioning accuracy (civilian):** **±2.5 m** horizontal (with WAAS/SBAS augmentation)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Modern Instrument Cluster

The modern digital instrument cluster is built on a layered architecture:

1. **Sensor Layer (Physical Domain)**
   - Wheel-speed sensors (Hall-effect, magnetoresistive)
   - Steering angle sensors (SAE J1939-compliant)
   - Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU): 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope
   - Radar (77 GHz), Lidar (905 nm / 1550 nm), Ultrasonic (40–70 kHz)
   - Camera modules (mono / stereo / surround-view)

2. **Acquisition & Communication Layer**
   - Analog front-end (AFE) with 12-bit to 16-bit ADC resolution
   - Microcontroller / DSP front-end
   - **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet** buses

3. **Processing Layer (ECU / Domain Controller)**
   - Sensor fusion (Kalman filter, particle filter)
   - Object detection and tracking
   - HMI rendering engine (typically a multi-core SoC with GPU)

4. **Display & HMI Layer**
   - TFT-LCD or OLED panel (typically 7" to 12.3")
   - Head-Up Display (HUD) projector
   - Haptic steering wheel, capacitive touch surfaces
   - Voice-recognition HMI

5. **Connectivity Layer**
   - 4G/5G modem, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GNSS
   - V2X (C-V2X / DSRC) at **5.9 GHz**
   - Cloud telematics backend (OEM-specific)

## 2.2 Why and How Each Layer Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Why layered architecture?** Because it decouples the physics of sensing (analog, noisy) from the deterministic logic of decision-making (digital, safety-critical) and from the ergonomic rendering of information (graphical, perceptual). The *Why* is **separation of concerns**; the *How* is achieved via standardized buses and well-defined interfaces (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262 ASIL levels A–D).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes equations frequently tested for Module 4 instrumentation problems. Use `\vert` for absolute value to keep markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheel speed from ABS sensor** | $v = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{T_s}$ | $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s), $r$ = rolling radius (m), $N$ = pulse count, $T_s$ = sampling period (s) |
| 2 | **Hall-effect sensor output** | $V_H = R_H \dfrac{I B}{t}$ | $V_H$ = Hall voltage (V), $R_H$ = Hall coefficient, $I$ = current (A), $B$ = flux density (T), $t$ = thickness (m) |
| 3 | **ADC quantization** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $V_{LSB}$ = least-significant bit voltage; $n$ = resolution bits |
| 4 | **SNR of ADC (ideal)** | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | $n$ = number of bits; gives theoretical SNR in dB |
| 5 | **CAN bus bit time** | $t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$ | Each segment in **Time Quanta (TQ)**; nominal bit rate = $\dfrac{1}{t_{bit}}$ |
| 6 | **Lidar range equation** | $R = \dfrac{c\, \Delta t}{2}$ | $R$ = range (m), $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time (s) |
| 7 | **Radar range equation** | $R = \dfrac{c\, \tau}{2\, BW}$ | $R$ = range resolution (m), $\tau$ = pulse duration, $BW$ = bandwidth (Hz) |
| 8 | **Kalman gain** | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T \left( H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R \right)^{-1}$ | $P$ = error covariance, $H$ = observation matrix, $R$ = measurement noise covariance |
| 9 | **Kalman state update** | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k \left( z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} \right)$ | Combines prediction with measurement $z_k$ |
| 10 | **GPS pseudorange** | $\rho = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2} + c\, \delta t$ | $\delta t$ = clock bias (s), subscripts $s$ = satellite, $r$ = receiver |
| 11 | **Hud optical magnification** | $M = \dfrac{f_{eye}}{f_{collimator}}$ | $M$ = angular magnification of the HUD virtual image |
| 12 | **Display luminance (cd/m²)** | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{projected}}$ | $I$ = luminous intensity (cd), $A$ = projected area (m²) |
| 13 | **CAN bus arbitration** | Dominant bit = logical **0**; Recessive = logical **1** | "0 wins" — wired-AND bus |
| 14 | **OBD-II PID formula (Mode 01)** | $A \cdot \dfrac{256}{100} + B$ | Generic scaling used for many PIDs (e.g., engine coolant temperature) |
| 15 | **IMU accelerometer (specific force)** | $a_{meas} = a_{true} - g$ | Measures specific force, not true acceleration |
| 16 | **Refresh frequency from frame time** | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | $T_{frame}$ = time to render one frame (s) |
| 17 | **V2X latency budget** | $t_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} \le 100$ ms | Per 3GPP / SAE J2945/1 for safety messages |
| 18 | **Functional safety (ASIL)** | $ASIL = f(severity, exposure, controllability)$ | Per ISO 26262, levels A → D |
| 19 | **Power dissipation in CAN driver** | $P = V_{CC} \cdot I_{dominant}$ | Important for ECU thermal design |
| 20 | **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | $d_{rel}$ = relative distance, $v_{rel}$ = relative speed |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas govern everything from the bandwidth planning of an ECU's CAN stack, to the safety-integrity level (SIL/ASIL) that an ADAS feature must meet before being homologated for road use, to the photometric design of a sun-readable HUD.

## 2.4 Key Modern Subsystems Explained

### 2.4.1 Digital Instrument Cluster (TFT-LCD)
A 7"–12.3" thin-film-transistor LCD, often paired with an OLED sub-display, driven by a **multi-core SoC** (e.g., NXP i.MX 8, Renesas R-Car, Qualcomm Snapdragon 8155/8295). It renders *virtual gauges* via a real-time graphics engine, typically **OpenGL ES**-based, and is often paired with a **safety MCU** (ASIL-B) for fail-safe fallback display.

### 2.4.2 Head-Up Display (HUD)
Projects critical information (speed, navigation arrows, ADAS warnings) onto the windshield within the driver's primary field of view. Three types exist:
- **Combiner HUD** (small flip-up glass)
- **Windshield HUD** (projector behind dashboard, image appears at ~2 m)
- **Augmented-Reality HUD (AR-HUD)** — uses dual DMD/laser projectors and a wide field of view (~10° × 4°) to overlay graphics that appear to merge with the road.

### 2.4.3 On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II / OBD-III)
- **OBD-II** (mandatory in US since 1996, EU since 2001 for petrol, 2004 for diesel): standard **16-pin J1962 connector**, protocols **ISO 15765 (CAN)**, **ISO 9141-2**, **ISO 14230 (KWP2000)**, **SAE J1850 (VPW/PWM)**.
- **OBD-III**: adds *wireless automatic reporting* of emissions and safety data to authorities.

### 2.4.4 Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) — Instrumentation View
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — radar + camera fusion
- **Lane Keep Assist (LKA)** — forward-facing camera
- **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — 24 GHz radar or ultrasonic
- **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** — multi-sensor fusion, TTC-based
- **Driver Monitoring System (DMS)** — IR camera + facial landmark detection

### 2.4.5 Telematics & Connected Car
A **TCU (Telematics Control Unit)** houses a 4G/5G modem, GNSS receiver, eSIM, and an automotive-grade MCU. It supports **eCall** (EU-mandated automatic emergency call, **GLONASS+GPS** positioning, 112 dialing post-collision), **bCall** (breakdown call), **OTA firmware updates**, and **fleet management APIs**.

### 2.4.6 In-Vehicle Networking
- **CAN (Controller Area Network)** — robust, real-time, multi-master. Two wires: **CAN_H** and **CAN_L**, terminated with **120 Ω** at each end.
- **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** — single-wire, low-cost, used for body electronics (mirrors, seats).
- **FlexRay** — up to **10 Mbit/s**, time-triggered, used in safety-critical chassis systems.
- **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** — backbone for ADAS and infotainment.
- **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)** — for legacy premium-audio networks.

### 2.4.7 AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture)
A standardized software architecture with three layers: **Application**, **Runtime Environment (RTE)**, and **Basic Software (BSW)**. Two platforms exist: **Classic Platform (CP)** for deeply embedded ECUs, and **Adaptive Platform (AP)** for high-performance domain controllers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CAN Bus Bit Time and Bit Rate

The Classical CAN bit is partitioned into four non-overlapping segments whose total duration is the **bit time** $t_{bit}$. The **Time Quantum (TQ)** is the basic time unit derived from the CAN controller clock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this needed?** KTU problems frequently ask for bit rate given the prescaler and segment configuration, or vice versa. Understanding the bit structure is essential for CAN bus design.

**Step 1 — Define the Time Quantum**

$$\text{TQ} = \dfrac{2 \cdot BRP}{f_{clk}}$$

Here $BRP$ is the **Baud Rate Prescaler** (integer) and $f_{clk}$ is the CAN peripheral clock frequency. Note the factor of 2 in CAN's definition.

**Step 2 — Express each segment in TQ units**

$$t_{SyncSeg} = 1 \cdot \text{TQ}$$

$$t_{PropSeg} = \text{PropSeg} \cdot \text{TQ}$$

$$t_{PhaseSeg1} = (\text{PhaseSeg1} + 1) \cdot \text{TQ}$$

$$t_{PhaseSeg2} = \max(\text{PhaseSeg2}, \text{SJW}) \cdot \text{TQ}$$

**Step 3 — Total bit time**

$$t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$$

**Step 4 — Nominal bit rate**

$$R_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Let $f_{clk} = 8 \text{ MHz}$, $BRP = 1$, $\text{PropSeg} = 3$, $\text{PhaseSeg1} = 3$, $\text{PhaseSeg2} = 3$, $SJW = 1$.

$$\text{TQ} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1}{8 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 250 \text{ ns}$$

$$t_{bit} = (1 + 3 + 4 + 3) \cdot 250 \text{ ns} = 11 \cdot 250 \text{ ns} = 2750 \text{ ns}$$

$$R_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{2.75 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 363.6 \text{ kbit/s}$$

> This is the classic **125 kbit/s CANopen / J1939** ballpark figure — verifying the method.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheel Speed from Hall-Effect ABS Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> A **tone wheel** with $Z$ teeth passes through a Hall-effect sensor mounted on the stub axle. Each tooth-to-gap transition flips the sensor output.

**Step 1 — Frequency of pulses**

$$f_{wheel} = Z \cdot f_{rot} = Z \cdot \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity in rad/s**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi\, f_{wheel}}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Linear speed at tire contact patch**

$$v = \omega \cdot r_{dyn} = \dfrac{2\pi\, f_{wheel}\, r_{dyn}}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative form using pulse count and time**

If the ECU counts $N$ pulses in a sampling window $T_s$:

$$f_{wheel} = \dfrac{N}{T_s}$$

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi\, r_{dyn}\, N}{Z \cdot T_s}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Let $Z = 48$ teeth, $r_{dyn} = 0.31$ m, $N = 60$ pulses, $T_s = 0.1$ s.

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 0.31 \cdot 60}{48 \cdot 0.1} = \dfrac{116.83}{4.8} \approx 24.34 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{km/h} = 24.34 \times 3.6 \approx 87.6 \text{ km/h}$$

> This matches a typical highway cruising speed — the formula is verified.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-to-Collision (TTC) and AEB Trigger

> [!NOTE]
> TTC is the **time until collision** if the relative speed remains constant. AEB (Automatic Emergency Braking) is triggered when TTC drops below a calibrated threshold.

**Step 1 — Define relative kinematics**

$$d_{rel} = d_{host} - d_{target}, \quad v_{rel} = v_{host} - v_{target}$$

**Step 2 — Constant-velocity assumption (no braking)**

$$d_{rel}(t) = d_{rel,0} - v_{rel} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Set $d_{rel}(TTC) = 0$ to find the collision time**

$$0 = d_{rel,0} - v_{rel} \cdot TTC$$

$$TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel,0}}{v_{rel}}$$

**Step 4 — Include the host vehicle's braking capability (real AEB)**

Maximum host deceleration $a_{max}$ (assumed constant braking). The closing distance follows:

$$d_{rel}(t) = d_{rel,0} + v_{rel}\, t - \dfrac{1}{2} a_{max}\, t^2$$

Collision occurs when $d_{rel}(t) = 0$. Solving the quadratic:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} a_{max}\, t^2 - v_{rel}\, t - d_{rel,0} = 0$$

$$t = \dfrac{v_{rel} + \sqrt{v_{rel}^2 + 2\, a_{max}\, d_{rel,0}}}{a_{max}}$$

(positive root selected; if $a_{max}=0$, this reduces to the simple $TTC$ form).

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Host at $v_{host} = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, target stationary, $d_{rel,0} = 30$ m, $a_{max} = 7$ m/s² (typical dry-road braking).

$$v_{rel} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

$$t = \dfrac{25 + \sqrt{625 + 2 \cdot 7 \cdot 30}}{7} = \dfrac{25 + \sqrt{625 + 420}}{7} = \dfrac{25 + \sqrt{1045}}{7}$$

$$t = \dfrac{25 + 32.33}{7} = \dfrac{57.33}{7} \approx 8.19 \text{ s}$$

> AEB would have nearly 8 s of warning — a comfortable margin for staged braking.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lidar Range from Time-of-Flight

**Step 1 — Pulse travels from emitter to target and back**

Total optical path length $= 2R$, where $R$ is the one-way range.

**Step 2 — Travel time**

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for range**

$$R = \dfrac{c\, \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**

A 905 nm pulsed lidar measures $\Delta t = 666.7$ ns for a pedestrian at the roadside.

$$R = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (666.7 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = \dfrac{200.01}{2} = 100.005 \text{ m}$$

> Range of **100 m** is consistent with a typical automotive long-range lidar.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — ADC SNR and Quantization Noise

**Step 1 — For an ideal $n$-bit ADC over a full-scale sinusoidal input**

The theoretical signal-to-quantization-noise ratio is:

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02\, n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation sketch (intuitive)**

Quantization step:
$$q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

Quantization noise RMS:
$$V_{q,rms} = \dfrac{q}{\sqrt{12}}$$

Sinusoidal signal RMS:
$$V_{s,rms} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — SNR ratio**

$$SNR = \dfrac{V_{s,rms}^2}{V_{q,rms}^2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{q^2 / 12} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB**

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) + 20 n \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\, n$$

**Step 5 — Numerical**

For a **12-bit** ADC: $SNR = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 12 = 1.76 + 72.24 = 74$ dB.

For a **16-bit** ADC: $SNR = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08$ dB.

> Important: this is the **theoretical** ceiling. Real ADCs lose 1–2 dB per bit due to thermal noise, aperture jitter, and integral non-linearity.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — HUD Virtual Image Distance (Geometric Optics)

A HUD is a simple **concave mirror** optical system (or equivalent lens + fold-flat assembly).

**Step 1 — Mirror equation**

$$\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$$

Where $u$ = object distance (image source inside dashboard), $v$ = image distance (virtual, in front of windshield), $f$ = focal length.

**Step 2 — Magnification**

$$M = -\dfrac{v}{u}$$

**Step 3 — Practical example**

$f = 0.5$ m, $u = 0.3$ m (LCD just behind the dashboard).

$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} - \dfrac{1}{0.3} = 2 - 3.33 = -1.33 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$v = -0.75 \text{ m}$$

The negative sign indicates a **virtual image**, perceived 0.75 m beyond the mirror (i.e., towards the driver). This is a short-range HUD. Increasing $f$ (using a longer-focal-length mirror) extends the virtual image to the typical 2–2.5 m design point.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed to mirror the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: **Part A** 3-mark direct questions, **Part B** 14-mark questions with **internal choice**, sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. Bloom's levels: *Apply / Analyze / Evaluate*.

## PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Head-Up Display (HUD) and list its three common types. (Recall / Understand)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Head-Up Display (HUD)** is a driver-information system that projects critical vehicle data (speed, navigation, ADAS alerts) into the driver's forward field of view, reducing the time to refocus from road to dashboard.

Three types:
1. **Combiner HUD** — small pop-up glass
2. **Windshield HUD** — image projected via a virtual image at ~2 m
3. **Augmented Reality HUD (AR-HUD)** — large field-of-view overlay that *appears merged* with the road

---

### Question 2: State the ISO standard governing functional safety of automotive electrical/electronic systems. (Recall)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

> The standard is **ISO 26262** — *"Road vehicles — Functional safety."* It defines four **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)**: **A, B, C, D**, derived from severity, exposure, and controllability of a hazard. **ASIL D** is the most stringent (e.g., steering, braking); **QM** (Quality Management) is the lowest.

---

## PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Modern Digital Instrument Cluster

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
> **(a)** Describe with a block diagram the architecture of a modern digital instrument cluster. Highlight the role of the safety MCU and the graphics SoC. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare TFT-LCD vs OLED for instrument-cluster displays across five engineering parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

A modern digital cluster is a **multi-layer embedded graphics system** consisting of:

1. **Signal Input Stage** — wheel-speed, fuel level, engine RPM, coolant temperature, gear position, ADAS messages received over **CAN** / **Automotive Ethernet**.
2. **Safety Microcontroller (MCU)** — typically a **lockstep dual-core ARM Cortex-R52** running at **ASIL-B / ASIL-D** integrity. It hosts the *safe* rendering of legally required gauges (speedometer, tell-tales). It validates every frame using a CRC and can drive a black-and-yellow **"safe-state" display** if the graphics SoC fails.
3. **Graphics SoC** — a high-performance multi-core processor (e.g., NXP i.MX 8, Renesas R-Car H3, Qualcomm SA8155P) with a **GPU** (Vivante, PowerVR, Adreno) running an **AUTOSAR-Adaptive** + **Linux / Android Automotive** software stack. It handles rich 2D/3D rendering via **OpenGL ES** or **Vulkan**.
4. **Display Panel** — typically a **12.3-inch 1920×720 TFT-LCD** with LED backlight (up to **1000 cd/m²** luminance for sun-readability) or **AMOLED** for deeper contrast.
5. **Backlight Driver & Thermal Management** — closed-loop LED current control, NTC-based temperature fold-back.
6. **Touch / Haptic Layer** (if configured) — capacitive touch slider for menu navigation.
7. **Fail-Operation Modes** — degraded mode (reduced graphics), black-screen mode, last-known-value mode.

> The **safety MCU** is *the legal guarantor* of correct speed and tell-tale indication; the **graphics SoC** is *the user-experience guarantor*. The split isolates safety-critical determinism from rich OS-style variability.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution — TFT-LCD vs OLED

| Parameter | TFT-LCD | OLED (AMOLED) |
|---|---|---|
| **Contrast ratio** | 1000:1 to 3000:1 (panel-dependent) | Infinite (true black, per-pixel off) |
| **Response time** | 5–25 ms | <1 ms |
| **Luminance** | Up to 1500 cd/m² (with high-power LED) | Typically 800–1000 cd/m² |
| **Operating temperature** | −40 °C to +85 °C (proven) | −30 °C to +85 °C (lower limit) |
| **Lifespan (to 50% luminance)** | 50,000+ h | 20,000–30,000 h (blue emitter limits) |
| **Power (white-dominant UI)** | Higher (backlight always on) | Lower (depends on content) |
| **Burn-in risk** | Negligible | Possible with static icons (clusters show static tell-tales) |
| **Sunlight readability** | Excellent (with high-nit backlight) | Good (polarizer + circular design) |
| **Cost (12.3" panel)** | Moderate | Higher |
| **Viewing angle** | Wide (IPS variants) | Essentially perfect |

> **Conclusion for cluster use:** TFT-LCD remains dominant for instrument clusters because of its **sunlight readability**, **longevity** under static-icons burn-in, and **wider operating-temperature envelope**. OLED is preferred for *center-stack infotainment* and *passenger displays* where content is dynamic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): CAN Bus and On-Board Diagnostics

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **(a)** With a clear diagram, explain the CAN bus frame structure (Data Frame). Define the function of each field. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A CAN controller uses an 8 MHz oscillator, $BRP = 1$, $\text{PropSeg} = 3$, $\text{PhaseSeg1} = 3$, $\text{PhaseSeg2} = 3$, $\text{SJW} = 1$. Compute the bit time and the nominal bit rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution — CAN Data Frame

A Classical CAN 2.0A **Data Frame** contains the following fields (in order):

| Field | Length | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SOF** (Start of Frame) | 1 bit | Dominant bit synchronizing all nodes |
| **Arbitration Field** | 12 bits | 11-bit **ID** + **RTR** bit (Dominant for Data Frame) |
| **Control Field** | 6 bits | **IDE** (ID Extension) + **r0** + 4-bit **DLC** (Data Length Code, 0–8) |
| **Data Field** | 0–8 bytes | Payload of up to 64 bits |
| **CRC Field** | 16 bits | 15-bit **CRC** + 1-bit **CRC delimiter** |
| **ACK Field** | 2 bits | **ACK slot** + **ACK delimiter**; any receiving node overwrites recessive → dominant |
| **EOF** (End of Frame) | 7 bits | All recessive — marks frame end |
| **IFS** (Inter-Frame Space) | 3 bits | Bus idle time before next frame |

> **Key points:** Arbitration is **non-destructive** — the node with the **lowest ID** (most leading zeros) wins. This is the "wired-AND" property: dominant (0) overrides recessive (1).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution — Bit Time Computation

**Step 1 — Time Quantum**

$$\text{TQ} = \dfrac{2 \cdot BRP}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1}{8 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 250 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Each segment in TQ**
- $t_{SyncSeg} = 1 \cdot \text{TQ} = 250$ ns
- $t_{PropSeg} = 3 \cdot \text{TQ} = 750$ ns
- $t_{PhaseSeg1} = (3 + 1) \cdot \text{TQ} = 1000$ ns
- $t_{PhaseSeg2} = \max(3, 1) \cdot \text{TQ} = 750$ ns

**Step 3 — Total bit time**

$$t_{bit} = 250 + 750 + 1000 + 750 = 2750 \text{ ns} = 2.75 \ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Nominal bit rate**

$$R_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{2.75 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 363{,}636 \text{ bit/s} \approx 363.6 \text{ kbit/s}$$

> This result is consistent with the **J1939 / CANopen 250 kbps or 500 kbps** family; for *true* 500 kbps, double the clock to **16 MHz** or halve the segments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing TQ formula** — some students write $\text{TQ} = \text{BRP}/f_{clk}$ instead of $2\cdot\text{BRP}/f_{clk}$. KTU explicitly penalizes this: lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in PhaseSeg1** — the segment runs for (PhaseSeg1 + 1) TQ. Skipping this loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up CAN_H and CAN_L voltages** — at **dominant** state, CAN_H ≈ **3.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **1.5 V** (differential ≈ 2 V). At **recessive**, both ≈ **2.5 V** (differential ≈ 0 V). Writing the wrong polarity loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the safety-MCU rationale** — when describing a digital cluster, simply naming parts earns *partial credit*; explaining *why* a separate safety MCU exists (fail-operational rendering of legally required gauges) earns **full marks**.
> 5. **Wrong ISO number for OBD-II protocol** — the *application layer* is **SAE J1979**, the *diagnostic-services layer* is **ISO 14229 (UDS)**, the *transport layer* is **ISO 15765**, the *data link* is **ISO 11898**. Confusing these layers loses up to **3 marks**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Modern Automotive Instrumentation"**

- **Definition:** Modern instrumentation = *digital cluster + HUD + telematics + ADAS sensors + networked ECUs + HMI*, all functioning under **ISO 26262** functional safety.
- **Digital Cluster:** Multi-core SoC (graphics) + lockstep safety MCU (legally required gauges) + 12.3" TFT-LCD.
- **HUD Types:** Combiner → Windshield → AR-HUD (widest FOV, virtual image at 2–2.5 m).
- **CAN Bus:** 2-wire, dominant-recessive, **120 Ω** termination, **wired-AND arbitration**, non-destructive.
- **CAN Bit Time:** $t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$ in **TQ** units, with $\text{TQ} = 2\cdot BRP / f_{clk}$.
- **OBD-II:** 16-pin J1962 connector, mandatory since 1996 (US) / 2001 (EU petrol). Protocols: **ISO 15765 CAN**, **ISO 9141-2**, **ISO 14230 KWP2000**, **SAE J1850**. SAE J1979 PIDs.
- **ADAS:** Radar (77 GHz, 200 m), Lidar (905 nm, ToF = $c\Delta t/2$), Camera (lane/objects), Ultrasonic (parking), IMU (6-axis).
- **Sensor Fusion:** **Kalman filter** is the workhorse; updates with gain $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
- **Telematics:** TCU with 4G/5G + GNSS; mandatory **eCall** in EU (post-2018 new cars); OTA updates.
- **In-Vehicle Networks:** CAN, **LIN** (single-wire, 19.2 kbit/s), **FlexRay** (10 Mbit/s), **Automotive Ethernet** (100/1000BASE-T1).
- **Software:** **AUTOSAR Classic** (embedded ECUs) + **AUTOSAR Adaptive** (high-perf domain controllers).
- **Connectivity:** **V2X** at 5.9 GHz, latency budget **≤ 100 ms** for safety messages.
- **Safety Levels:** ASIL A → D per **ISO 26262**.
- **Display Trade-off:** **TFT-LCD** for clusters (sunlight + longevity); **OLED** for infotainment (contrast + response).
- **GPS Pseudorange:** $\rho = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + \ldots} + c\,\delta t$.
- **Key Numbers to Memorize:** CAN classic = 1 Mbit/s; CAN FD = 5 Mbit/s; LIN = 19.2 kbit/s; FlexRay = 10 Mbit/s; Auto Eth = 100 Mbit/s; OBD-II voltage = 12 V; HUD image distance = 2 m; ADC SNR = 6.02n + 1.76 dB.
- **Most-asked Bloom level in KTU:** *Apply* (numerical) and *Analyze* (architecture diagrams).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input and Output Signal Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Input and Output Signal Conversion # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Input and Output Signal Conversion? In any modern automobile, the physical world is **analog** (continuous) — engine temperature rises smoothly, throttle pedal moves continuously, wheel speed spins without digital steps — but the **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** that process this data are fundamentally **digital** (discrete). **Input and Output Signal Conversion** is the bridge engineering discipline that translates signals between these two domains so the vehicle's "nervous system" can perceive the world and act upon it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Input and Output Signal Conversion* is the process of transforming physical automotive quantities (temperature, pressure, speed, position, flow) into electrical signals via **transducers/sensors** (Input Conversion), conditioning and digitizing these signals through **Signal Conditioning Circuits** and **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)** for ECU processing, and finally reconverting the digital control decisions back into physical actions through **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)** and **actuators** (Output Conversion). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a UN Conference" Imagine you are sitting in a UN conference where delegates from many countries speak different languages. To make communication possible, you need: 1. **Ear & Microphone (Sensor/Input Stage):** Picks up the spoken language (physical phenomenon) and converts it to an electrical waveform. 2. **Signal Boosting & Filtering (Signal Conditioning):** Removes background noise and amplifies the weak whisper so it is intelligible. 3. **Language Translator (ADC):** Converts the continuous speech into typed text on paper (discrete digital words) that the chairperson's computer can understand. 4. **The Computer (ECU/MCU):** Processes the text, makes a decision, and writes a reply. 5. **Reverse Translator & Speaker (DAC + Actuator):** Reads the digital reply aloud and physically delivers the message (e.g., adjusts fuel injector timing). > [!NOTE] > In an automobile, the **"spoken language"** is the physical parameter, the **"ear"** is the sensor, the **"computer"** is the ECU, and the **"speaker"** is the actuator — signal conversion is the chain that links them all. ## 1.3 Why Signal Conversion is Critical in Automobiles | Parameter | Physical Quantity | Sensor (Input) | Output Conversion | |---|---|---|---| | Engine Coolant | Temperature | Thermistor / RTD | Dashboard gauge / warning lamp | | Manifold Vacuum | Pressure | MAP Sensor | Fuel injection pulse width | | Wheel Rotation | Angular Speed | Hall-Effect / Magnetic Pickup | Speedometer / ABS control | | Accelerator Pedal | Position | Potentiometric / TPS | Throttle valve actuator | | Oxygen in Exhaust | Gas Concentration | Zirconia Lambda Sensor | Closed-loop fuel trim | The key engineering standard that governs analog signal ranges in vehicles is **0 V to 5 V** (legacy) or **0 V to 12 V** (some commercial vehicles), with newer systems using **0–5 V** or **4–20 mA current loops** for robust noise immunity. The reference supply voltage **V_ref = 5 V** is the most common standard in automotive microcontrollers (e.g., Infineon TC27x, NXP S32K). ## 1.4 Classification of Automotive Signals > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Universal Signal Classes in Vehicle Electronics:** > 1. **Analog Signals** — Continuous in time *and* amplitude (e.g., throttle position voltage). > 2. **Digital Signals** — Discrete in time *and* amplitude (e.g., crank position pulse train). > 3. **PWM (Pulse Width Modulated) Signals** — Discrete in time, continuous in *average* amplitude (e.g., idle air control valve, dashboard dimmer). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Complete Signal Conversion Chain A typical automotive measurement channel follows this **five-stage architecture**: ``` Physical Quantity → Sensor (Transducer) → Signal Conditioning → ADC → ECU → DAC → Actuator → Physical Action ``` Each stage introduces specific errors and noise; hence, an instrumentation engineer must understand every conversion step. --- ### 2.2 Stage 1 — Sensors (Input Transducers) A **transducer** converts one form of energy into another. In automobiles, sensors primarily convert **non-electrical** physical quantities into **electrical** signals. **Classification by Output:** | Sensor Type | Output Nature | Example | Automotive Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Active Sensors** | Generate their own voltage/current | Piezoelectric knock sensor, Thermocouple | Engine knock detection, exhaust temp | | **Passive Sensors** | Require external excitation | Thermistor, RTD, Strain Gauge | Coolant temp, MAP, fuel level | **Common Sensor Transfer Equations:** * **Thermistor (NTC):** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ * **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector — Platinum PT100):** $$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta T^2\right]$$ * **Strain Gauge:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ * **Piezoelectric Sensor:** $$V_{out} = d \cdot F \quad \text{or} \quad Q = d \cdot F$$ where **d** is the piezoelectric charge constant. --- ### 2.3 Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning Raw sensor outputs are usually **low-amplitude** (mV range) and **noisy**. The conditioning block performs four critical functions: 1. **Amplification** — Using **Instrumentation Amplifiers** (e.g., INA128) to boost differential signals and reject common-mode noise (high **CMRR ≥ 80 dB**). 2. **Filtering** — Low-pass filters remove high-frequency EMI from ignition systems. 3. **Linearization** — Corrects non-linear sensor curves (especially thermistors). 4. **Isolation** — Optical or transformer isolation protects the ECU from voltage spikes. > [!NOTE] > **Wheatstone Bridge:** Used universally with strain gauges and RTDs. The output voltage is: > $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$$ --- ### 2.4 Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) An **ADC** samples an analog voltage at discrete time intervals and quantizes it into a digital binary code. This involves two operations: **Sampling** and **Quantization**. #### Key ADC Architectures Used in ECUs | ADC Type | Speed | Resolution | Cost | Automotive Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | Medium | 10–16 bit | Low | MAP, MAF, TPS sensors | | **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** | Slow | 16–24 bit | Medium | Pressure sensors, oxygen sensors | | **Flash ADC** | Very Fast | 6–8 bit | High | Crank position, wheel speed | | **Dual-Slope** | Very Slow | 16–20 bit | Low | Battery monitoring (BMS) | | **Pipelined** | Fast | 10–14 bit | Medium | Radar/LiDAR (ADAS) | #### Fundamental ADC Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ If **f_s < 2·f_max**, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency noise folds back into the measurement band, causing erratic ECU behavior. Anti-aliasing analog filters are placed before every ADC input. --- ### 2.5 Stage 4 — Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC) A **DAC** performs the inverse operation — converting a digital word **D** into an analog voltage or current. Two architectures dominate: * **R-2R Ladder DAC** — Uses only two resistor values (R and 2R); preferred for monolithic ICs. * **Weighted Resistor DAC** — Uses binary-weighted resistors; fast but bulky and inaccurate for >8 bits. The output equation for an ideal **n-bit unipolar DAC**: $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$ For a **bipolar DAC** (offset binary): $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D - 2^{n-1}}{2^{n-1}}$$ --- ### 2.6 Stage 5 — Actuators (Output Transducers) These convert the reconverted analog (or PWM) signal back into a **physical action**: * **Solenoid Injectors** — PWM controlled fuel delivery. * **Idle Air Control (IAC) Valve** — Stepper or PWM motor. * **Electronic Throttle Body** — DC motor with position feedback. * **Gauge Movements** — Cross-coil (air-core) or stepper motor driven. * **Relays & Lamps** — Binary on/off control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is the *preferred* automotive output technique because it is highly efficient (the actuator is either fully ON or fully OFF, minimizing heat dissipation in the driver transistor). The average analog value is: $$V_{avg} = V_{supply} \cdot D_{duty} = V_{supply} \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$$ --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | ADC Resolution (LSB size) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$ | **V_LSB** in Volts, **n** = bit count | | Quantization Error (max) | $Q_{max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Half-LSB uncertainty | | Ideal Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | **dB**, for full-scale sine input | | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Hz**, anti-aliasing mandatory | | DAC Unipolar Output | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | **D** is decimal equivalent of binary word | | DAC Bipolar Output | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D - 2^{n-1}}{2^{n-1}}$ | Offset-binary coding | | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_s \cdot D_{duty}$ | $D_{duty} \in [0,1]$ | | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | $\beta$ in **K**, T in **K** | | RTD (Linear Approx.) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | $\alpha_{Pt} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^{\circ}C$ | | Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_f \approx 2.0$ for constantan | | Piezoelectric Output | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C}$ | $d$ = **pC/N**, $C$ = cable capacitance | | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | Balanced when $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb for Automotive ADC Selection:** > For an n-bit ADC with V_FSR = 5 V, the LSB size is **5 / (2^n − 1)** volts. To resolve a **±0.1°C** change in a 0–100°C coolant sensor (span = 100°C), the required bit count is approximately: > $$n \geq \log_2 \left(\frac{V_{FSR}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \log_2 \left(\frac{5}{0.005}\right) \approx 10 \text{ bits}$$ > Hence, **10-bit SAR ADCs** are the workhorse of mid-range ECUs. --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility In production vehicles, signal conversion is what enables **OBD-II diagnostics**, **drive-by-wire throttles**, **adaptive cruise control**, and **battery management in EVs**. Every malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) is essentially the output stage of a signal-conversion chain that detected an out-of-range digital reading. Mastery of this topic is foundational for diagnostics, ECU calibration, and ADAS development. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — ADC Resolution, Quantization Error, and SNR **Problem Context:** A 12-bit Successive Approximation ADC is used in an ECU to digitize the analog output of a Throttle Position Sensor (TPS). The reference voltage is **V_ref = V_FSR = 5.0 V**. **Step 1 — Compute the LSB (Resolution) Voltage:** The ADC divides the full-scale range into **2^n − 1** equal intervals. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$$ Substituting V_FSR = 5.0 V and n = 12: $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0}{2^{12} - 1}$$ $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0}{4096 - 1}$$ $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0}{4095}$$ $$V_{LSB} = 1.221 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 1.221\,\text{mV}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** Each increment of the digital output code corresponds to a real-world voltage step of **1.221 mV**. This is the smallest change the ECU can "see." **Step 2 — Compute the Maximum Quantization Error:** The maximum error occurs when the true analog voltage lies exactly between two codes. $$Q_{max} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2}$$ $$Q_{max} = \pm \frac{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$ $$Q_{max} = \pm 0.6105 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = \pm 0.6105\,\text{mV}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Theoretical SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** For an ideal n-bit ADC processing a full-scale sinusoidal input: $$\text{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76\,\text{dB}$$ Substituting n = 12: $$\text{SNR} = 6.02(12) + 1.76$$ $$\text{SNR} = 72.24 + 1.76$$ $$\text{SNR} = 74.0\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:** A 12-bit ADC provides **4096 discrete codes** and a dynamic range of approximately **74 dB**. This is sufficient for TPS (0–100% throttle), MAP sensor (10–110 kPa), and most engine-management sensors, but **insufficient** for high-precision strain-gauge applications such as chassis stress monitoring, which require ≥16-bit Σ-Δ ADCs. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — DAC Output Voltage Calculation **Problem Context:** An 8-bit R-2R ladder DAC in an electronic throttle body controller receives a digital command word **D = 01101100₂** from the ECU. The reference voltage is **V_ref = 5.0 V**. Determine the analog output voltage. **Step 1 — Convert the Binary Word to Decimal:** $$D = 01101100_2$$ $$D = (0 \times 128) + (1 \times 64) + (1 \times 32) + (0 \times 16) + (1 \times 8) + (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1)$$ $$D = 0 + 64 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 0$$ $$D = 108$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Unipolar DAC Output Equation:** $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$ Substituting V_ref = 5.0 V, D = 108, and n = 8: $$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{108}{2^8}$$ $$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{108}{256}$$ $$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot 0.421875$$ $$V_{out} = 2.109\,\text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Verify with the LSB Approach:** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 19.53\,\text{mV}$$ $$V_{out} = D \cdot V_{LSB} = 108 \cdot 19.53 \times 10^{-3} = 2.109\,\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Translate to Actuator Action:** If this DAC drives the throttle valve positioning motor, then **2.109 V** corresponds to a proportional opening of approximately **42.2%** of the throttle plate's mechanical travel, assuming a linear 0–5 V position feedback. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — PWM Duty Cycle for Idle Air Control **Problem Context:** An engine ECU commands an Idle Air Control (IAC) valve through a 12 V PWM signal at **f_PWM = 100 Hz**. To maintain 850 RPM at idle, the calculated duty cycle is **D_duty = 35%**. Determine the average voltage across the valve and the ON-time per cycle. **Step 1 — Compute the PWM Period:** $$T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01\,\text{s} = 10\,\text{ms}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ON-Time:** $$T_{on} = D_{duty} \cdot T$$ $$T_{on} = 0.35 \cdot 0.01 = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 3.5\,\text{ms}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Average Voltage:** $$V_{avg} = V_s \cdot D_{duty} = 12 \cdot 0.35 = 4.2\,\text{V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Although the average voltage is only **4.2 V**, the IAC solenoid is always switched between **0 V** and **12 V**, so power dissipation in the driver MOSFET is minimal. This is why PWM is the universal technique for solenoid and DC motor speed control in vehicles. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ADC Architectures for Automotive Use | Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) | Dual-Slope | |---|---|---|---|---| | Conversion Time | ns–µs | µs–ms | ms | seconds | | Typical Resolution | 6–8 bit | 10–16 bit | 16–24 bit | 16–20 bit | | Hardware Complexity | Very High ($2^n - 1$ comparators) | Medium | Low analog + digital filter | Low | | Power Consumption | Very High | Medium | Low | Low | | Cost | High | Low–Medium | Medium | Low | | Noise Immunity | Low | Medium | Excellent (oversampling) | Excellent | | Best-fit Vehicle Application | Crank/Cam position sensing | MAP, MAF, TPS, O₂ (narrow-band) | Wide-band O₂, pressure, strain, battery monitoring | Battery state-of-charge (BMS), slowly varying signals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule (Examiner-tested):** > Choose **SAR** when you need medium speed and ≥10-bit resolution at low cost. Choose **Σ-Δ** when high resolution and noise rejection are mandatory for slowly varying precision sensors. Choose **Flash** only when sub-microsecond conversion is required (ignition timing, knock detection). --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff Frequency **Problem:** A wheel-speed sensor produces a sinusoidal signal whose maximum frequency is **f_max = 4 kHz** at 250 km/h. Determine the minimum Nyquist sampling rate and recommend an anti-aliasing low-pass filter cutoff. **Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Criterion:** $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot 4000 = 8000\,\text{Hz} = 8\,\text{kHz}$$ **Step 2 — Practical Sampling Rate (with safety margin):** In practice, f_s is set to **5× to 10× f_max** to ease anti-aliasing filter design: $$f_s = 5 \cdot f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$$ **Step 3 — Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff:** The analog low-pass filter must attenuate frequencies above **f_s / 2 = 10 kHz** by at least the ADC's SNR. A Butterworth filter with **f_c = 4 kHz** (−3 dB) is recommended, with at least −40 dB/decade roll-off after f_s/2. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Signal Conversion Architecture (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire **Input → ECU → Output** conversion pipeline of a generic automotive subsystem (e.g., Electronic Throttle Control). It traces every signal transformation from physical phenomenon to actuator response. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Physical Quantity
Throttle Pedal Position] --> S1A[Sensor Stage 1
Potentiometric TPS] S1A --> S2A[Signal Conditioner 1
Amplifier + LPF] S2A --> S3A[ADC Stage
12-bit SAR Converter] S3A --> ECU[ECU Microcontroller
Decision Algorithm] ECU --> S3B[DAC Stage
8-bit R-2R Ladder] S3B --> S2B[Driver Stage
H-Bridge PWM] S2B --> S1B[Actuator Stage
DC Motor Throttle Body] S1B --> P2[Physical Action
Throttle Plate Rotation] P3[Physical Quantity
Coolant Temperature] --> S1C[Sensor Stage 2
NTC Thermistor] S1C --> S2C[Signal Conditioner 2
Wheatstone Bridge + INA] S2C --> S3A P4[Physical Quantity
Crankshaft Speed] --> S1D[Sensor Stage 3
Variable Reluctance Pickup] S1D --> S2D[Signal Conditioner 3
Comparator + Schmitt Trigger] S2D --> ECU ECU --> S2E[Gauge Driver
Cross-Coil OR Stepper] S2E --> P5[Physical Output
Dashboard Speedometer] style P1 fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#b91c1c style P2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style P3 fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#b91c1c style P4 fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#b91c1c style P5 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style ECU fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** * **Red blocks** = physical quantities on the input side (analog world). * **Yellow block** = the digital ECU (decision-making heart). * **Green blocks** = physical quantities on the output side (re-converted analog world). * Three independent sensor paths (throttle, coolant, crankshaft) converge into the same ECU, demonstrating **multi-channel sensor fusion**, a core concept in modern drive-by-wire systems. --- ## 4.2 ADC Conversion Process Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Analog Input Signal
V_in t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter
Low-Pass LPF] B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit
S/H] C --> D[Quantizer
2^n Discrete Levels] D --> E[Binary Encoder
Output Digital Code D] E --> F[ECU Memory Register] style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style F fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 ``` **Process Walkthrough:** 1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** — Removes frequency components above f_s/2 (Nyquist). 2. **Sample and Hold (S/H)** — Freezes the analog value during conversion to avoid aperture error. 3. **Quantizer** — Compares input to 2^n reference levels (creates staircase approximation). 4. **Binary Encoder** — Converts the quantization index into n-bit binary code. 5. **ECU Register** — Stores the digital word for firmware processing. --- ## 4.3 DAC and PWM Output Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ECU Digital Command
Binary Word D] --> B[DAC R-2R Ladder
n-bit Resolution] B --> C[Reconstructed Analog
V_out t] C --> D[Power Driver Stage
MOSFET H-Bridge] D --> E[Actuator
DC Motor OR Solenoid] E --> F[Mechanical Output
Throttle OR Injector] G[ECU PWM Command
Duty D_percent] --> H[Timer Module
Variable Duty Cycle] H --> I[Gate Driver
Push-Pull Stage] I --> J[Solenoid Valve
IAC OR EGR] J --> K[Fluid Flow Output
Air OR Exhaust Gas] style A fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style B fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style G fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style I fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style J fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style K fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 ``` **Engineering Significance:** The diagram shows that automotive ECUs use **two distinct output paradigms** — analog reconstruction (DAC) for proportional control (throttle, mixture), and **direct PWM** for high-efficiency switching control (injectors, idle valves, relays). # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1.
