$$D = \frac{V}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times 2^{n} = \frac{3.4}{5.0} \times 1024$$

$$D = 0.68 \times 1024 = 696.32$$

Rounded: **$D = 696$** (1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Temperature Resolution**

Voltage per LSB:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.8828\text{ mV}$$

Temperature per LSB:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta V}{m} = \frac{4.8828 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03} \approx 0.1628^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 0.163^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

(1 Mark)

**Conclusion:** A 10-bit ADC yields a temperature resolution of about **0.16 °C**, which is more than sufficient for coolant monitoring (typical alarm thresholds are 5 °C steps).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual & Comparative Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Enumerate and explain **any seven advantages** of computer-based instrumentation over conventional analog instrumentation in automobiles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics)** in computer-based automotive instrumentation. List the major OBD-II standardized parameters and DTC categories. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Advantages (7 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Improved Accuracy** — Digital conversion eliminates pointer parallax, mechanical wear, and scale non-linearity.
2. **Multi-Parameter Monitoring** — A single MCU polls dozens of sensors using time-multiplexed ADC, replacing separate gauges.
3. **Self-Diagnostics (OBD-II)** — Continuous BIST, DTC storage, MIL illumination, freeze-frame data on faults.
4. **Reduced Wiring via Multiplexing** — CAN bus replaces point-to-point wiring; saves 20–40 kg of copper in a typical car.
5. **Flexibility/Reconfigurability** — Display units, warning thresholds, languages, and features can be changed via firmware.
6. **Real-Time DSP** — Digital filters (moving average, Kalman) clean sensor noise without bulky analog components.
7. **Driver Information Enhancement** — TFT/OLED displays present navigation, ADAS alerts, EV battery state, fuel economy data simultaneously.

*(Additional valid points: data logging, telematics/OTA updates, ADAS integration, lower long-term cost, safety interlocks.)*

**(b) OBD-II Role (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** OBD-II is a standardized self-diagnostic and reporting system mandated in vehicles since 1996 (US) and adopted in India under **BS-IV/BS-VI** emission norms. It continuously monitors the performance of emission-related and major subsystems through the instrument-cluster computer.

**Standardized Parameters (PIDs — Parameter IDs) (2 Marks):**
- **PID $01** — Monitor status since DTCs cleared
- **PID $05** — Engine coolant temperature
- **PID $0C** — Engine RPM
- **PID $0D** — Vehicle speed
- **PID $0F** — Intake air temperature
- **PID $11** — Throttle position
- **PID $2F** — Fuel level

**DTC Categories (3 Marks):**

| Code Prefix | System |
|---|---|
| **P** | Powertrain (engine, transmission) |
| **C** | Chassis (ABS, steering, suspension) |
| **B** | Body (airbags, lighting, HVAC) |
| **U** | Network/Communication (CAN bus faults) |

**Example:** `P0301` = Cylinder 1 misfire detected.

OBD-II ports (J1962 connector, usually under dashboard) allow scan tools to read live data, DTCs, freeze-frames, and readiness monitors — all generated automatically by the CBI system.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Advantages of Computer-Based Instrumentation**

- **Definition:** Microprocessor/MCU-based measurement, display, and control of vehicle parameters.
- **Core Architecture:** Sensor → Conditioning → ADC → MCU → Display + CAN Bus.
- **Key Advantages:**
  - Higher accuracy and resolution (ADC + software correction)
  - Multi-parameter monitoring (multiplexing)
  - Self-diagnostics and OBD-II compliance
  - Reduced wiring (CAN multiplexing)
  - Flexibility (firmware reconfigurable)
  - Real-time DSP for noise filtering
  - Data logging, telematics, OTA updates
  - HMI enhancement (TFT/OLED)
  - ADAS integration
  - Long-term cost efficiency
- **ADC Resolution Formula:** $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^{n}$
- **Quantization Error:** $\pm \Delta V / 2$
- **OBD-II Standard:** SAE J1962 connector, SAE J1979 PID protocol, DTCs in P/C/B/U format.
- **Key Automotive Buses:** CAN (500 kbps), CAN-FD (2 Mbps), LIN (19.2 kbps), FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet.
- **Digital Filter Equation:** $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum a_k y[n-k]$
- **Noise Reduction by $N$-point averaging:** SNR improves by $10\log_{10}(N)$ dB.
- **Examiner Traps:** Always draw a **block diagram**, include **numerical example**, and end with a **conclusion sentence** in 14-mark answers.
- **Common Misconception:** CBI does not eliminate sensors; it **digitizes and processes** sensor outputs intelligently.
- **BS-VI India Connection:** OBD-II is mandatory for all new passenger vehicles under BS-VI norms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display Devices (any one)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Display Devices (Head-Up Display — HUD) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Head-Up Display (HUD)** is a transparent electronic display technology that projects a virtual image of critical vehicle, navigation, and driver-assistance information directly into the driver's line of sight — typically on the windshield or a dedicated combiner glass — so that the driver can monitor real-time data without diverting visual attention from the road ahead. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon (sourced from PEICT744 — Automobile Instrumentation, Module 4 syllabus), the Head-Up Display is classified as a **virtual-image projection display device** belonging to the family of *Advanced Driver Information Systems (ADIS)*. The information is generated by a **Picture Generation Unit (PGU)**, collimated through an optical assembly, and presented to the eye as if it were floating at optical infinity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4** > Under the 2024 Scheme, *Display Devices (any one)* mandates the study of a single display technology used for instrumentation. The **Head-Up Display (HUD)** is the most examinable option because it uniquely combines **optics, electronics, ergonomics, and human-factors engineering** in one subsystem — a perfect fit for Automobile Instrumentation. **Standardized Definitions (for KTU answer-script precision):** | Term | Formal Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Virtual Image Distance (VID)** | The apparent distance at which the projected image is perceived by the driver; typically set between **2 m and ∞** for automotive HUDs. | | **Eye-Box (Eyebox)** | The spatial volume within which the driver's eyes must lie to see the entire HUD image; typical size **130 mm × 80 mm**. | | **Field of View (FOV)** | The angular extent of the projected image as seen from the eye-box; horizontal FOV is typically **5°–10°**, premium units reach **20°+**. | | **Luminance (L)** | Photometric brightness of the virtual image; specified in **cd/m²**; required value for daylight readability: **≥ 10 000 cd/m²**. | | **Combiner** | The partially reflective optical surface that reflects PGU light to the driver while transmitting the road scene. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving at 100 km/h on a highway. Normally, to check your speed, you glance down at the instrument cluster — your eyes must **re-focus from infinity (the road) to about 0.8 m (the dashboard)** and back. This refocusing takes roughly **0.5–0.8 seconds**, during which at 100 km/h the car travels **14–22 meters blind**. A Head-Up Display solves this by projecting the speed, navigation arrows, and warning icons onto the windshield — but in a *pre-focused* state. To the driver, the numbers appear to float about **2 m ahead**, optically close to infinity, so the eyes do **not need to re-accommodate**. It is the optical equivalent of writing your speed on the road itself. > [!NOTE] > **The Pilot's Inheritance:** The HUD was originally developed for military fighter-jet pilots in the 1940s (the **General Motors "Phantom"** and the British **Buccaneer** were among the first), where pilots could not afford to look down into the cockpit during dogfights. Automakers — beginning with the **Oldsmobile Cutlass in 1988** — adapted the same principle to passenger cars to mitigate the same focus-shift problem for everyday drivers. ## 1.3 Why HUD Is a "Display Device" Inside an Automobile In the hierarchy of automotive instrumentation, the HUD is the **topmost (Tier-3) display layer** of the *Driver Information Subsystem*: ``` Tier-1: Sensors & ECU (raw data acquisition) Tier-2: Instrument Cluster / Center-Stack Display (driver reads with eyes-off-road) Tier-3: Head-Up Display (eyes-on-road virtual projection) ``` It is the only display device in a car that is **iso-accommodative** with the forward road scene — meaning the driver's eye lens does not have to change shape to read it. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an Automotive HUD A HUD is not a single component; it is an **optical-electronic-mechatronic system** consisting of seven functional blocks: | # | Block | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Data Source** | ECU / CAN bus feeds (speed, RPM, navigation, ADAS warnings). | | 2 | **PGU (Picture Generation Unit)** | Converts digital data to a luminous 2-D image; commonly **TFT-LCD, DLP, or LCoS**. | | 3 | **Backlight / Light Source** | High-power LEDs providing uniform illumination to the PGU. | | 4 | **Collimating Optics** | Plano-convex or aspheric lens that converts diverging PGU rays into **parallel (collimated) rays**. | | 5 | **Fold Mirror(s)** | Compact the optical path inside the dashboard by folding the light path. | | 6 | **Combiner / Windshield** | Partially reflective surface that redirects collimated light to the driver. | | 7 | **Control Electronics & Sensors** | Ambient light sensor, eye-tracking camera, dimming logic, ARAS overlay. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why collimation matters:** The human eye focuses parallel rays to a single point on the retina without any accommodation effort. By making the PGU rays parallel, the HUD image appears at optical infinity — the same focal state as the road ahead. This is the *physiological core* of the entire HUD concept. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Data Acquisition** The HUD Electronic Control Unit (HECU) reads real-time vehicle data over the **CAN / CAN-FD / Automotive Ethernet** bus. Typical signals include: *vehicle speed (km/h), engine RPM, navigation turn arrows, lane-departure warnings, traffic-sign recognition, ADAS status.* **Step 2 — Image Rendering** The HECU drives a **thin-film-transistor liquid-crystal display (TFT-LCD)** (most common) or a **Digital Light Processing (DLP)** chip (used in premium AR-HUDs). At this point, the picture exists as a small, real, inverted image at a distance equal to the lens focal length behind the lens. **Step 3 — Optical Collimation** A precisely ground aspheric lens with focal length **f ≈ 60–120 mm** refracts the diverging PGU rays into **parallel bundles**, one per pixel. Each pixel maps to a specific angle within the FOV. **Step 4 — Path Folding** Because the dashboard space is only ~150 mm deep, two or three plane mirrors fold the optical path into a "Z" or "U" configuration without altering the parallelism of the rays. **Step 5 — Combiner Reflection** The combiner (either a separate wedge-shaped glass mounted on the dashboard OR the windshield's inner surface treated with an **indium-tin-oxide (ITO) / dielectric thin-film coating**) reflects ~20–35 % of the projected light toward the driver and transmits the rest so the road remains visible. **Step 6 — Virtual Image Formation** The driver's eye lens focuses the parallel rays onto the retina, exactly as it would for distant scenery. The brain therefore interprets the image as lying at optical infinity (or at a pre-set finite VID such as 2.2 m). **Step 7 — Adaptive Dimming** An **ambient-light sensor (ALS)** measures cabin and outside brightness; the HECU modulates the backlight current so that the virtual image luminance is at least **1.5× the background luminance** (Weber's contrast law). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Board-exam-ready cheat sheet.** These six formulas collectively cover ≥ 80 % of numerical / analytical questions on HUD optics. | # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Typical KTU Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **F1** | $\text{VID} \approx \dfrac{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}$ | $f$: lens focal length, $d_{\text{PGU}}$: PGU-to-lens distance, $\text{VID}$: virtual image distance | VID = 2 m – ∞ | | **F2** | $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h_{\text{PGU}}}{2 f}\right)$ | $h_{\text{PGU}}$: PGU diagonal / height | 5° – 20° | | **F3** | $E_v = \dfrac{e_y \cdot f}{f - d_{\text{lens}}}$ | $E_v$: vertical eye-box, $e_y$: PGU height, $d_{\text{lens}}$: lens-to-PGU distance | 60 – 100 mm | | **F4** | $\theta_{\text{eye}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y_{\text{offset}}}{d_{\text{eye-relief}}}\right)$ | Look-down / look-up angle from primary driving gaze | 3° – 5° | | **F5** | $M_{\text{angular}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{virtual}}}{h_{\text{PGU}}} = \dfrac{\text{VID}}{d_{\text{PGU}}}$ | Apparent magnification of virtual image | 5× – 12× | | **F6** | $L_{\text{req}} \geq 1.5 \cdot L_{\text{bg}}$ | Weber contrast law for HUD readability | ≥ 10 000 cd/m² (daylight) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity-check pitfall:** When PGU-to-lens distance $d_{\text{PGU}} = f$ exactly, VID → ∞ (the canonical "collimated" condition). KTU examiners love setting $d_{\text{PGU}}$ slightly greater than $f$ to test if the student knows that the image becomes *real and inverted* on the wrong side of the lens — a classic "where does the image form?" trap. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility HUDs are mandated or strongly recommended in several automotive safety regulations: - **Euro NCAP 2025+ Safety Assist** protocols incentivize **speed-limit and AEB warnings** on a HUD. - **UNECE Regulation No. 125** governs *Forward Field of Vision* — a HUD keeps the driver's gaze within this field. - **SAE J1757-1** standardizes HUD optical test procedures. - **ISO 15005:2017** addresses ergonomic requirements for in-vehicle visual displays, applicable to HUD luminance, FOV, and contrast. In modern premium vehicles (BMW, Mercedes, Audi, Cadillac), HUDs are now evolving into **AR-HUDs (Augmented Reality HUDs)** with FOV up to **20° × 10°** and VID of **10 m or more**, overlaying navigation arrows directly onto the road surface visible through the windshield. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Virtual Image Distance (VID) — Thin-Lens Equation We begin with the Gaussian thin-lens equation, the foundation of all HUD optics: $$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$ Where: - $u$ = object distance from the optical centre of the lens (PGU position) → **negative** by Cartesian sign convention, - $v$ = image distance from the optical centre, - $f$ = focal length of the collimating lens (positive for a converging lens). ### Step 1 — Sign Convention Setup Place the lens at the origin. The PGU is to the **left** of the lens (object side), so $u = -d_{\text{PGU}}$. Substituting $u = -d_{\text{PGU}}$ into the thin-lens equation: $$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{-d_{\text{PGU}}} = \frac{1}{f}$$ ### Step 2 — Simplify the Algebra $$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{d_{\text{PGU}}} = \frac{1}{f}$$ Rearrange to isolate $1/v$: $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{PGU}}}$$ Combine over a common denominator: $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}$$ Invert both sides: $$v = \frac{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}$$ This is the **image distance** measured from the lens. By sign convention, if $d_{\text{PGU}} > f$, then $v$ is **positive** → image forms on the right side → this is the **virtual image** perceived by the driver on the far side of the combiner. ### Step 3 — Recognition as Virtual Image Distance The Virtual Image Distance (VID) is therefore: $$\text{VID} = v = \frac{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}$$ ### Step 4 — Boundary Behaviour (Important for KTU Theory) - If $d_{\text{PGU}} \to \infty$ → $\text{VID} \to f$ (the focal plane acts as the image). - If $d_{\text{PGU}} \to f^+$ (just greater than $f$) → $\text{VID} \to \infty$ (perfect collimation, image at infinity). - If $d_{\text{PGU}} < f$ → $\text{VID}$ becomes negative → image is **real** and forms between PGU and lens → useless for HUD (driver would see a tiny inverted image floating just above the dashboard). **Numerical Example — KTU Style** A HUD uses a converging lens with $f = 80$ mm. The PGU is placed at $d_{\text{PGU}} = 82$ mm from the lens. Compute VID. $$\text{VID} = \frac{80 \times 82}{82 - 80} = \frac{6560}{2} = 3280 \text{ mm} = 3.28 \text{ m}$$ So the driver perceives the image floating **3.28 m** ahead of the lens — close enough to infinity to require minimal accommodation, yet close enough to be readable. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Field of View (FOV) The Field of View is the angular subtense of the PGU as seen from the lens centre: $$\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot \theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{h_{\text{PGU}}/2}{f}\right)$$ Where $h_{\text{PGU}}$ is the *effective height* of the PGU image (since FOV is typically specified vertically or along the smaller diagonal). **Numerical Example** A HUD has $h_{\text{PGU}} = 24$ mm (1-inch diagonal PGU) and $f = 80$ mm. $$\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{24/2}{80}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.15) = 2 \times 8.53° = 17.06°$$ This matches the **premium AR-HUD** horizontal FOV specification. ## 3.3 Derivation of Angular Magnification The angular magnification compares the angular size of the *virtual image* as seen from the driver's eye to the angular size of the PGU as seen from the lens: $$M_{\text{angular}} = \frac{\theta_{\text{virtual}}}{\theta_{\text{PGU}}} = \frac{h_{\text{virtual}} / \text{VID}}{h_{\text{PGU}} / d_{\text{PGU}}}$$ By similar triangles from the lens equation: $$M_{\text{angular}} = \frac{\text{VID}}{d_{\text{PGU}}}$$ **Numerical Example** With VID = 3.28 m and $d_{\text{PGU}} = 82$ mm: $$M_{\text{angular}} = \frac{3280}{82} = 40$$ The driver sees the image **40× larger** than the actual PGU — this is precisely why a tiny 1-inch LCD can fill a windshield-sized virtual display. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs | Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on VID | Increase → Effect on FOV | Increase → Effect on Package Size | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Lens focal length $f$ | VID ↑ | FOV ↓ | Larger optics, deeper housing | | PGU distance $d_{\text{PGU}}$ | VID ↓ (then ↓ sharply) | No direct effect | Deeper housing needed | | PGU size $h_{\text{PGU}}$ | No direct effect | FOV ↑ linearly | Larger PGU, more heat | | Combiner tilt angle | Image shifts vertically | No effect | Styling constraint | | Eye-box size $E_v$ | No effect | Slightly reduced | Larger fold mirrors required | > [!NOTE] > **KTU design takeaway:** Premium AR-HUDs achieve 20°+ FOV by using **larger PGU panels (3–5 inches)**, **shorter focal lengths (~50 mm)**, and **multiple cascaded fold mirrors** to keep package depth under 250 mm. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture of an Automotive HUD ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CAN Bus Data Sources
Vehicle Speed RPM Navi ADAS] --> B[HECU
HUD Electronic Control Unit] B --> C[PGU Driver Electronics
TFT-LCD or DLP Controller] C --> D[Picture Generation Unit
TFT-LCD or DLP Chip] E[LED Backlight
High Power White] --> D D --> F[Collimating Optics
Aspheric Plano Convex Lens] F --> G[Fold Mirror 1
Path Compaction] G --> H[Fold Mirror 2
Path Compaction] H --> I[Combiner Surface
Windshield or Wedge Glass] I --> J[Driver Eye
Retinal Image] K[Ambient Light Sensor] --> B L[Eye Tracking Camera] --> B M[Dimming Logic
Adaptive Brightness] --> B B -.controls.-> E B -.controls.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Optical Path Schematic (Folding Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Dashboard Housing PGU[PGU
TFT-LCD 1.5 inch] -->|diverging rays| LENS[Collimating Lens
f equals 80 mm] LENS -->|parallel rays| FM1[Fold Mirror 1
45 degrees] FM1 -->|parallel rays| FM2[Fold Mirror 2
45 degrees] FM2 -->|parallel rays| FM3[Fold Mirror 3
45 degrees] end FM3 -->|parallel rays| COMB[Combiner
Windshield Inner Surface] COMB -->|reflected to eye| EYE[Driver Eye
Eye Box 130 by 80 mm] COMB -.transmits.-> ROAD[Road Scene
Forward View] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Layer Architecture (Tier View) ```mermaid flowchart TB L7[Tier 7: AR Overlay Layer
Navigation Arrows Lane Guidance] --> L6 L6[Tier 6: Warning Layer
Collision Alerts Speed Limits] --> L5 L5[Tier 5: Primary Driving Data
Speed RPM Cruise Control] --> L4 L4[Tier 4: Virtual Image Optics
Collimation Eye Box FOV] --> L3 L3[Tier 3: PGU Hardware
TFT-LCD DLP LCoS] --> L2 L2[Tier 2: Backlight and Power
LED Driver Thermal Management] --> L1 L1[Tier 1: Data Acquisition
CAN Ethernet Sensor Fusion] --> L0[Vehicle Network and ECU Layer] ``` ## 4.4 State Diagram — HUD Operating Modes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> OFF OFF --> STANDBY: Ignition ON STANDBY --> DAY_MODE: Ambient Light greater than 1000 lux STANDBY --> NIGHT_MODE: Ambient Light less than 50 lux DAY_MODE --> AR_MODE: Navigation Active and Vehicle Speed greater than 30 km/h DAY_MODE --> STANDARD_MODE: No AR Content NIGHT_MODE --> DIMMED_MODE: Auto Dimming Triggered AR_MODE --> STANDARD_MODE: Navigation Complete STANDARD_MODE --> OFF: Ignition OFF DIMMED_MODE --> NIGHT_MODE: Brightness Restored OFF --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram interpretation note for KTU:** The block diagrams above correspond to what an examiner expects when the question asks *"Draw the block diagram of an automotive Head-Up Display and explain its working."* (commonly a 7-mark sub-question in Part B). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2025] **Define Head-Up Display (HUD). Mention any two advantages of HUD over conventional instrument clusters.** **Model Answer:** A Head-Up Display is a transparent display system that projects vehicle information (such as speed, navigation, and warnings) onto the windshield or a combiner glass so that the driver can view the data without shifting gaze from the forward road. The virtual image is formed by a collimating optical assembly so that the driver's eye remains in its distance-focused state. **Two advantages (any two carry full marks):** 1. **Reduced eyes-off-road time** — the driver maintains the primary forward gaze, improving reaction time and crash avoidance. 2. **Elimination of accommodation effort** — because the image is collimated to optical infinity, the eye does not need to refocus from the road (≈ ∞) to the dashboard (≈ 0.8 m), reducing visual fatigue. 3. **Augmented-reality overlays** — modern AR-HUDs can highlight pedestrians, lane boundaries, and navigation arrows directly on the road scene. 4. **Improved night-time readability** — virtual images can be made bright and crisp without causing glare to oncoming drivers. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Automobile Instrumentation] **List the major functional blocks of an automotive Head-Up Display system.** **Model Answer:** The major functional blocks are: 1. **Data Source (ECU / CAN Bus)** — provides speed, RPM, navigation, ADAS warnings. 2. **Picture Generation Unit (PGU)** — TFT-LCD, DLP, or LCoS device that renders the image. 3. **Backlight** — high-power LED array illuminating the PGU. 4. **Collimating Optics** — aspheric lens that renders the rays parallel. 5. **Fold Mirror(s)** — compact the optical path within the dashboard. 6. **Combiner / Windshield** — partially reflective surface that redirects the image to the eye while transmitting the road. 7. **Control Electronics (HECU + ambient light sensor + eye tracker)** — manages brightness, content, and overlay logic. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer **either Question A *or* Question B in full**, including both sub-parts. Below, both alternatives are provided as required by the protocol. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an automotive Head-Up Display. List any four applications of HUD in modern vehicles.* **Model Solution Outline (7 marks):** 1. **Block diagram (3 marks)** — Draw a labelled block diagram showing: *ECU/CAN Bus → HECU → PGU (TFT-LCD) → LED Backlight → Collimating Lens → Fold Mirrors → Combiner → Driver Eye*, with branches for ambient light sensor and eye-tracker feedback. 2. **Working principle (3 marks) — explain step-by-step:** - Vehicle data is read by the HECU from the CAN bus. - The PGU generates a small real image of the data. - The collimating lens makes the light rays parallel. - Fold mirrors route the parallel beam toward the combiner. - The combiner reflects the rays to the driver's eye, which focuses them onto the retina as if they originated at optical infinity. - Adaptive brightness control adjusts luminance to ambient conditions. 3. **Four applications (1 mark):** - Display of vehicle speed, engine RPM, cruise-control set speed. - Turn-by-turn navigation arrows. - ADAS warnings (forward-collision alert, lane-departure warning, blind-spot alert). - Traffic-sign recognition and speed-limit display. - Night vision / pedestrian highlighting (in premium AR-HUDs). --- #### Q.A(b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *Derive the formula for the virtual image distance (VID) of a Head-Up Display. A HUD uses an aspheric converging lens of focal length 75 mm and a TFT-LCD PGU placed 77 mm from the lens. Calculate the VID and comment on the result.* **Model Solution Outline (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** Start from the thin-lens equation $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ with $u = -d_{\text{PGU}}$. Show the algebra step-by-step (as in Section 3.1) to obtain: $\text{VID} = \dfrac{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}$ **Numerical substitution (2 marks):** $$\text{VID} = \frac{75 \times 77}{77 - 75} = \frac{5775}{2} = 2887.5 \text{ mm} \approx 2.89 \text{ m}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** The virtual image appears **2.89 m** ahead of the collimating lens — well beyond the dashboard and close to optical infinity. This matches the **KTU-recommended ergonomic VID range of 2–10 m**, ensuring minimal eye accommodation effort for the driver. Because $d_{\text{PGU}}$ is only 2 mm greater than $f$, the system is highly sensitive to PGU positioning; a 0.1 mm shift would change VID by ~140 mm, which is why HUDs require precision mounting and active optical alignment. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) #### Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Automobile Instrumentation]** *Explain the optical concept of collimation as applied to Head-Up Displays. Why is collimation critical for driver comfort? Mention the role of the combiner in a windshield-type HUD.* **Model Solution Outline (7 marks):** 1. **Definition of collimation (2 marks):** Collimation is the process of rendering light rays parallel using a converging lens with the object placed at its focal plane. The output beam is a parallel bundle that the human eye focuses on the retina without accommodation effort, producing a virtual image at optical infinity. 2. **Optical principle (2 marks):** When $d_{\text{PGU}} = f$, the lens output rays are parallel. Each pixel maps to a unique direction (angle), and the lens behaves like an angular encoder. The angular extent equals the FOV: $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan(h_{\text{PGU}}/2f)$. 3. **Why collimation is critical for driver comfort (2 marks):** - The eye lens (crystalline lens) does not have to change shape — it remains in its distance-accommodation state. - Pupillary constriction remains stable. - Binocular fusion is preserved because both eyes receive parallel rays. - Eliminates the 0.5–0.8 s refocus time required when glancing at a dashboard, critical at 80–120 km/h highway speeds. 4. **Role of the combiner (1 mark):** The combiner is a partially reflective surface (either a separate wedge glass or the inner surface of the windshield coated with a dielectric thin film such as indium tin oxide). It reflects ~20–35 % of PGU light into the driver's eye while transmitting the rest so that the road remains fully visible. It also defines the **apparent distance** and **angular position** of the virtual image. --- #### Q.B(b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Automobile Instrumentation]** *Compare Combiner-type HUD and Windshield-type HUD. Discuss the field of view, luminance requirements, and packaging constraints of each. Mention any one limitation of conventional HUDs that AR-HUDs aim to solve.* **Model Solution Outline (7 marks):** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Combiner HUD | Windshield HUD | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Reflective surface | Separate wedge glass on dashboard | Inner surface of windshield itself | | FOV | Limited (5° – 8°) | Wider (8° – 20°+) | | Luminance | Lower (5 000 – 8 000 cd/m²) | Higher (10 000 – 15 000 cd/m²) | | Cost | Lower | Higher (special windshield needed) | | Image distance | Shorter (1.5 – 2.5 m) | Longer (2 – 10 m for AR) | | Packaging depth | Smaller (≤ 150 mm) | Larger (≥ 250 mm) | | Ghost image risk | Low | High (requires wedge film to suppress secondary reflection) | **Discussion (2 marks):** - **FOV:** Windshield HUDs require a larger PGU and shorter focal length to support wide FOV, demanding deeper housing and precise mirror alignment. - **Luminance:** Daylight readability requires $\geq 10 000 \text{ cd/m}^2$, demanding high-power LEDs and active thermal management. - **Packaging:** Windshield HUDs face styling constraints because the optical path must clear the airbag deployment zone and HVAC ducts. **Limitation of conventional HUDs solved by AR-HUDs (1 mark):** Conventional HUDs project information at a single focal plane, causing the driver's eyes to refocus between the road (far) and the virtual image (intermediate). **AR-HUDs** solve this by projecting **multiple depth planes** — for example, navigation arrows at 10 m appear *on* the road, while warnings appear at 2.5 m — eliminating accommodation conflict and enabling true augmented-reality overlays. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating "virtual image" with "real image"** — A HUD produces a **virtual** image at optical infinity. Drawing the rays incorrectly (as if converging to a real image on the windshield) costs up to 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting sign convention in the thin-lens equation** — KTU examiners explicitly test $u = -d_{\text{PGU}}$ (negative). Writing $u = +d_{\text{PGU}}$ yields a negative VID and is a *fatal error*. > 3. **Mixing up "FOV" and "eye-box"** — FOV is angular (in degrees), eye-box is linear (in mm). Conflating the two is a frequently penalized slip. > 4. **Omitting ambient-light feedback** — A HUD answer without the ALS / dimming loop is considered incomplete; deduct 1 mark. > 5. **Writing "HUD uses a concave mirror"** — incorrect; HUDs use a **converging (plano-convex or aspheric) lens** plus plane fold mirrors. Concave mirrors are used in *reflective HUD prototypes only*. > 6. **Skipping the combiner** — never describe a HUD without the combiner; it is the defining element that distinguishes a HUD from a dashboard projector. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist — re-read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - **HUD = Head-Up Display** — a virtual-image projection display mounted in the dashboard. - The virtual image is formed by a **collimating lens** that renders PGU rays **parallel**. - The image is perceived at the **Virtual Image Distance (VID)**, typically **2 m – ∞**. - **Thin-lens equation:** $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ with $u = -d_{\text{PGU}}$. - **VID formula:** $\text{VID} = \dfrac{f \cdot d_{\text{PGU}}}{d_{\text{PGU}} - f}$. - **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h_{\text{PGU}}}{2f}\right)$. - **Eye-box** is the spatial volume where the driver's eyes must lie; typical **130 × 80 mm**. - **Field of View** typical values: **5°–10°** (conventional) and **20°+** (AR-HUD). - **Luminance requirement** for daylight readability: $\geq \mathbf{10 000 \text{ cd/m}^2}$ — backed by **Weber's contrast law** ($L_{\text{HUD}} \geq 1.5 \cdot L_{\text{bg}}$). - **PGU technologies:** TFT-LCD (common), DLP (premium), LCoS (compact premium). - **Combiner** is the partially reflective surface — either a separate wedge glass or the windshield inner surface (with ITO / dielectric coating). - **Fold mirrors** compact the optical path; a typical HUD uses **2–3 plane mirrors**. - **Block sequence (memorize order):** ECU → HECU → PGU driver → PGU → Backlight → Collimating Lens → Fold Mirrors → Combiner → Driver Eye. - **Advantages:** eyes-on-road, no accommodation shift, AR overlay capability, night visibility. - **Limitations:** cost, packaging depth, windshield ghost image, sun-load heating. - **Standards to mention in answers:** SAE **J1757-1**, UNECE **R125**, ISO **15005:2017**. - **AR-HUD advantage:** multiple focal planes (e.g., 10 m for road-aligned arrows vs 2.5 m for warnings) eliminate accommodation conflict. - **Modern examples:** BMW Live Cockpit Professional, Mercedes-Benz MBUX AR-HUD, Audi Virtual Cockpit Plus, Cadillac Escalade AR-HUD. - **Always include the ambient-light sensor and dimming loop** in block diagrams — examiners reward completeness. - **Numerical trap to remember:** $d_{\text{PGU}}$ slightly greater than $f$ → VID very large (image near infinity); $d_{\text{PGU}} < f$ → real, useless image.

topics.title: Fuel Quantity Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Quantity Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuel Quantity Measurement** in an automobile is the process of continuously determining the volume (or level) of liquid fuel present inside the vehicle's fuel tank and displaying this information to the driver via a dashboard-mounted **fuel gauge**. The measurement is performed by a **fuel level sender unit (fuel gauge sender)** mounted on the tank, which converts the physical fuel level into a proportional electrical signal (resistance, voltage, or frequency). This signal is transmitted to the instrument cluster, where it is conditioned and presented as a **pointer deflection** (analog) or **bar/digital readout** (electronic).

The working principle is based on a **transduction chain**:

> **Physical Level (Fuel Height) → Mechanical Displacement (Float + Lever) → Electrical Quantity (Resistance / Capacitance / Frequency) → Driver Display (Gauge / LCD).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes both the **classical electromechanical fuel gauge** (float + rheostat + bimetallic/thermal indicator) and the **modern electronic counterparts** (capacitive, ultrasonic, Hall-effect). Students must be able to draw, label, and explain the working of at least one float-based and one electronic sender circuit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the fuel tank as a **bathtub with a floating rubber duck**, and the dashboard gauge as a **remote thermometer on the wall**. The duck (float) sits on the water surface. As the water level falls, the duck drops. The duck is connected by a string to a **sliding contact (wiper)** that moves across a long strip of resistive material. When the wiper moves, the resistance changes, and just like a volume knob on a TV, more or less current flows to the gauge. The needle on the dashboard tilts in proportion, telling you "E" (empty) or "F" (full).

If we replace the duck-and-string with an **ultrasonic beam fired downward from the tank cap**, the time taken for the echo to bounce back tells us the exact distance to the fuel surface — much like a **bat using echolocation** to judge distance to a wall. Modern cars use exactly this principle in premium and CNG/LPG tanks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Tank capacity:** typically **35 L to 80 L** for passenger cars; up to **600 L** for trucks.
- **Sender operating voltage:** **12 V DC** (conventional) or regulated **5 V DC** (CAN-bus based).
- **Float specific gravity reference:** ≈ **0.74 g/cm³** for petrol, **0.85 g/cm³** for diesel.
- **Standard empty-to-full resistance swing:** **0 Ω (Full) – 90 Ω / 110 Ω / 240 Ω / 33–250 Ω** depending on manufacturer (e.g., VDO, Bosch).
- **Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (used for hydrostatic pressure type senders).

> [!NOTE]
> The **float** must be made of a closed-cell foam (typically **NBR/PP foam**) with a specific gravity *lower* than the fuel, so it floats on the surface regardless of fuel density. If the float density is wrong, the gauge reads incorrectly — a common failure mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Fuel Level Senders

Fuel quantity sensors can be broadly classified into **two families**:

### Family A — Electromechanical / Passive Senders
1. **Float + Variable Resistor (Rheostat) Type** — most common in ICE vehicles.
2. **Bimetallic / Thermal Type** — uses a heated bimetal strip and fuel cooling effect.
3. **Hydrostatic Pressure Type** — measures fuel column weight.

### Family B — Electronic / Active Senders
1. **Capacitive Level Sensor** — uses fuel as a dielectric.
2. **Ultrasonic Level Sensor** — uses time-of-flight of acoustic waves.
3. **Hall-Effect Type** — uses a magnet on a vertical float track.
4. **Optical / Refractive Type** — uses prism and total internal reflection.
5. **Conductive (Resistive Rod) Type** — used in conductive liquids only.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Float Type (Rheostat Sender)

This is the **single most important subsystem** for KTU 2024 examination purposes.

**Construction:**
- A **buoyant float** (foam/plastic, hollow sealed) is rigidly attached to one end of a **lever arm** (pivot arm).
- The other end of the lever arm carries a **sliding wiper (brush)**.
- The wiper rides over a **wire-wound or thick-film resistive track (rheostat)** printed on a card.
- The rheostat is connected in series with the **bimetallic fuel gauge** (instrument) and the **12 V battery**.

**Operation (Step-by-step Logic):**

- **Step 1 — Full Tank:** Float rises to top → lever arm rotates → wiper positioned at **"Full"** end of the rheostat → sender resistance is at its **minimum (≈ 0 Ω to 5 Ω)**.
- **Step 2 — Gauge Current is Maximum:** Per Ohm's law, *I = V / R*. Low R → high I → bimetallic strip heats up and bends more → needle deflects to **"F"** mark.
- **Step 3 — Half Tank:** Float is at middle → wiper at mid-track → resistance is approximately **half the rheostat value** → current is intermediate → needle at center.
- **Step 4 — Empty Tank:** Float at bottom → wiper at "Empty" end → resistance at **maximum (≈ 90 Ω to 250 Ω)** → current is minimum → bimetallic strip barely heats → needle at **"E"** mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a bimetallic indicator and not a regular d'Arsonval meter?** Because automotive voltage varies (10 V to 15 V with alternator load), a regular current meter would give wild needle fluctuations. The bimetallic strip has a large **thermal time constant (≈ 30–60 s)**, which naturally smooths out voltage spikes — a brilliant example of **signal averaging using mechanical inertia**.

## 2.3 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration (Calibrated Reading)

The bimetallic fuel gauge and the sender rheostat form **two arms of a Wheatstone bridge**. The other two arms are fixed precision resistors inside the gauge. The bridge is **unbalanced** by the changing sender resistance, and the resulting current through the bimetallic coil is non-linear, but **calibration** at "E" and "F" positions makes the pointer respond in proportion to the actual fuel volume.

**Bridge balance condition (for reference design):**

$$R_{1} \cdot R_{4} = R_{2} \cdot R_{3}$$

where one of the resistors (typically $R_1$ or $R_3$) is the sender.

## 2.4 Capacitive Fuel Level Sensor

Two concentric metallic tubes (or parallel plates) form a **cylindrical capacitor**, with the annular space filled with fuel and air. The capacitance varies linearly with liquid height.

$$C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}L}{\ln\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)}$$

where:
- $C$ = capacitance (in Farads, F)
- $L$ = length of the electrode submerged in fuel (m)
- $a$ = inner electrode radius, $b$ = outer electrode radius
- $\varepsilon_{0}$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space)
- $\varepsilon_{r}$ = relative permittivity (air ≈ **1.0**, petrol ≈ **2.1**, diesel ≈ **2.3**)

The capacitance is fed to an **oscillator circuit (e.g., 555 astable)** whose output frequency is **f = 1.44 / ((R₁ + 2R₂) · C)**. The frequency is then measured by the ECU and converted to fuel volume.

> [!NOTE]
> Capacitive sensors are **safer for CNG/LPG tanks** because they have **no moving parts, no sparks, and no open contacts** — critical in flammable fuel environments.

## 2.5 Ultrasonic Fuel Level Sensor

A **piezoelectric transducer** mounted on the top of the tank emits an ultrasonic pulse (typically **40 kHz to 200 kHz**). The pulse travels down, reflects off the fuel surface, and returns. The **time of flight (ToF)** is measured.

$$d = \frac{v_{\text{fuel}} \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = distance from transducer to fuel surface (m)
- $v_{\text{fuel}}$ = speed of sound in fuel (≈ **1250 m/s in petrol**, ≈ **1390 m/s in diesel**)
- $t$ = round-trip time (s)

The tank is mounted vertically, so the **fuel level $h$** is:

$$h = H_{\text{tank}} - d$$

where $H_{\text{tank}}$ is the total tank height. The ECU computes the volume $V = A(h) \cdot h$ using a **tank-shape map** (since most tanks are non-uniform in cross-section).

## 2.6 Hall-Effect Fuel Sensor

A **permanent magnet** is attached to the float arm. As the float moves, the magnet slides past a **Hall-effect IC**. The Hall IC outputs a **PWM or analog voltage** proportional to the magnetic field strength, which the ECU interprets as fuel level. These are contactless, highly accurate, and immune to fuel contamination.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Standard Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (sender circuit) | $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{s} + R_{g}}$ | A, V, Ω | Float-rheostat type sender analysis |
| 2 | Wheatstone balance | $R_{1}\cdot R_{4} = R_{2}\cdot R_{3}$ | Ω | Bridge calibration, gauge linearity |
| 3 | Rheostat resistance (linear) | $R_{s} = R_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{\text{max}}}$ | Ω | Calculating sender resistance at any float angle |
| 4 | Bimetallic deflection | $\delta = k \cdot I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$ | m | Thermal gauge needle response |
| 5 | Cylindrical capacitor (capacitive sender) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F | Capacitive fuel sensor capacitance |
| 6 | Capacitive sensor (mixed dielectric, air + fuel) | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{air}} + C_{\text{fuel}}$ | F | When fuel partially fills the tube |
| 7 | 555 Oscillator frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_{1} + 2R_{2})C}$ | Hz | Converting C to frequency |
| 8 | Ultrasonic distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m | Time-of-flight fuel level |
| 9 | Hydrostatic head pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Pressure-type fuel sender |
| 10 | Volume from height | $V = \int_{0}^{h} A(z)\,dz$ | m³ | Non-uniform tank volume calculation |

> **Note on formatting:** In the table above, fractions and absolute value bars have been replaced with `dfrac` and parenthesized units to keep the markdown table pipe-syntax safe. For inline math outside tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|`.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EVs and Hybrids:** Modern EVs do not carry liquid fuel, but **PHEVs (Plug-in Hybrid EVs)** still use float/capacitive senders for their smaller petrol tank (typically 35–45 L).
- **Aircraft & Marine:** Capacitive and ultrasonic sensors dominate because of vibration tolerance and safety.
- **Fuel Adulteration Detection:** Capacitive sensors can detect a change in $\varepsilon_{r}$ (e.g., petrol adulterated with kerosene has higher $\varepsilon_{r}$).
- **Fleet Telematics:** Float sensors feed **OBD-II / CAN bus** data to telematics units, enabling remote fuel-theft alerts and consumption analytics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No phrase like "it can be shown that" is used. Each derivation ends with a numeric verification (where applicable).

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sender Resistance as a Function of Float Angle

**Problem Setup:**
A fuel sender rheostat has total resistance $R_{\text{total}} = 90\ \Omega$ over a total sweep angle of $\theta_{\text{max}} = 90^{\circ}$. The float lever is currently at $\theta = 60^{\circ}$. Determine the instantaneous sender resistance $R_s$, assuming a **linear wound track**.

**Step 1 — State the proportional relation:**

The resistance of a wire-wound rheostat is distributed uniformly across the wiper sweep. Hence resistance is **directly proportional** to the angle traversed.

$$\frac{R_s}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{\text{max}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$R_s = R_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{\text{max}}} = 90\ \Omega \cdot \frac{60^{\circ}}{90^{\circ}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$R_s = 90\ \Omega \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 60\ \Omega$$

**Result:** $R_s = 60\ \Omega$ at the 60° float position.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gauge Current and Bimetallic Deflection

**Problem Setup:**
A bimetallic fuel gauge (coil resistance $R_g = 40\ \Omega$) is connected in series with the sender of the previous problem. The system is supplied by a nominal automotive voltage $V = 13.2\ \text{V}$ (alternator-charged battery). Find the gauge current and the relative needle deflection.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (series circuit):**

$$V = I \cdot (R_s + R_g)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for current $I$:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_s + R_g} = \frac{13.2\ \text{V}}{60\ \Omega + 40\ \Omega}$$

$$I = \frac{13.2}{100} = 0.132\ \text{A}$$

**Result:** $I = 132\ \text{mA}$.

**Step 3 — Power dissipated in the bimetallic coil (for thermal analysis):**

$$P_g = I^{2} \cdot R_g = (0.132)^{2} \cdot 40$$

$$P_g = 0.017424 \cdot 40 = 0.69696\ \text{W} \approx 0.697\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Bimetallic strip deflection (linearized):**

For a bimetallic strip, the tip deflection $\delta$ after time $t$ is given by:

$$\delta = k \cdot P_g \cdot t = k \cdot 0.697 \cdot t$$

where $k$ is the strip's thermal-mechanical constant (m/W·s). For KTU purposes, only the **proportionality** $\delta \propto P_g$ is required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Empty and Full Calibration Resistances

**Problem Setup:**
A fuel gauge shows the needle at "E" when sender resistance is maximum ($R_{E}$) and at "F" when sender resistance is minimum ($R_{F}$). For a typical VDO-type sender:
- $R_{E} = 110\ \Omega$ (Empty)
- $R_{F} = 0\ \Omega$ (Full, short-circuit case)
- $R_g = 35\ \Omega$ (gauge internal)
- $V = 12\ \text{V}$

Find: (a) Current at "E", (b) Current at "F", (c) Current ratio.

**Step 1 — Current at Empty:**

$$I_{E} = \frac{V}{R_{E} + R_g} = \frac{12}{110 + 35} = \frac{12}{145}$$

$$I_{E} = 0.08276\ \text{A} \approx 82.76\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Current at Full:**

$$I_{F} = \frac{V}{R_{F} + R_g} = \frac{12}{0 + 35} = \frac{12}{35}$$

$$I_{F} = 0.34286\ \text{A} \approx 342.86\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Current ratio:**

$$\frac{I_{F}}{I_{E}} = \frac{0.34286}{0.08276} = 4.142$$

**Interpretation:** The "Full" current is approximately **4.14 times** the "Empty" current. The bimetallic strip's $I^{2}R$ heating produces a **17.16×** power ratio, giving a sharp, readable deflection across the dial.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Capacitive Sensor — Mixed Dielectric (Air + Fuel)

**Problem Setup:**
A capacitive fuel level sensor consists of two concentric tubes of radii $a = 5\ \text{mm}$, $b = 10\ \text{mm}$, total length $L = 30\ \text{cm}$. The fuel fills up to a height $h = 18\ \text{cm}$. The relative permittivities are $\varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} = 1.0$ and $\varepsilon_{r,\text{fuel}} = 2.2$ (typical petrol). Compute the equivalent capacitance.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of fuel-filled portion (length $h$):**

$$C_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r,\text{fuel}} \cdot h}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance of air-filled portion (length $L - h$):**

$$C_{\text{air}} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} \cdot (L - h)}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 3 — Total capacitance (parallel combination, since both sections are physically in parallel along the tube):**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{air}}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ \varepsilon_{r,\text{fuel}} \cdot h + \varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} \cdot (L - h) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{10}{5}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

$$2\pi\varepsilon_{0} = 2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 5.563 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}$$

Now compute the bracket term:

$$\varepsilon_{r,\text{fuel}} \cdot h + \varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} \cdot (L - h)$$

$$= 2.2 \times 0.18 + 1.0 \times (0.30 - 0.18)$$

$$= 0.396 + 0.12 = 0.516\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Final computation:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.516}{0.6931}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = \frac{2.870 \times 10^{-11}}{0.6931}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = 4.142 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 41.42\ \text{pF}$$

**Result:** $C_{\text{total}} \approx 41.4\ \text{pF}$. This capacitance can be wired into a **555 timer** or **LC tank** to convert it into a readable frequency.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight for a Petrol Tank

**Problem Setup:**
A vertical petrol tank is $H = 40\ \text{cm}$ tall. An ultrasonic sensor mounted on the lid measures a round-trip time $t = 480\ \mu\text{s}$. Speed of sound in petrol vapour (in the ullage space) is $v \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ (air-like above fuel; sound in the fuel itself is $1250\ \text{m/s}$, but the pulse traverses the air column above the fuel, not the fuel). Find the distance to the fuel surface and the fuel level.

**Step 1 — Apply the time-of-flight equation:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 480 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$d = \frac{0.16464}{2} = 0.08232\ \text{m} \approx 8.23\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 3 — Fuel level $h$:**

$$h = H - d = 0.40 - 0.08232 = 0.31768\ \text{m} \approx 31.77\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage fill (assuming uniform cross-section $A$):**

$$\% \text{ Fill} = \frac{h}{H} \times 100 = \frac{31.77}{40} \times 100 \approx 79.4\%$$

**Result:** The tank is approximately **79% full**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $v = 343\ \text{m/s}$?** The ultrasonic pulse travels through the **air/vapour column** above the fuel. It does not pass through the liquid. If the sensor were *submerged*, $v = 1250\ \text{m/s}$ would be used.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sender Types

| S.No. | Parameter | Float-Rheostat | Bimetallic | Capacitive | Ultrasonic | Hall-Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Signal | Variable $\Omega$ | Variable $\Omega$ | Variable F | Variable Hz | Variable V |
| 2 | Moving Parts | Yes (float, wiper) | Yes (bimetal) | **No** | **No** | Yes (magnet) |
| 3 | Accuracy | ±5% | ±10% | ±1–2% | ±1% | ±2% |
| 4 | Spark Risk | **High** | **High** | **None** | **None** | **None** |
| 5 | Suitable for LPG/CNG | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | Yes |
| 6 | Cost | **Low** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| 7 | Lifespan | 5–7 yr (wiper wear) | 7–10 yr | 10+ yr | 10+ yr | 10+ yr |
| 8 | Affected by Sloshing | **Yes (damping needed)** | Yes | No | No | No |
| 9 | Calibration Drift | Moderate | Moderate | **Low** | **Low** | Low |
| 10 | ECU Compatibility | Via ADC | Via ADC | **Direct** (via osc.) | **Direct** (via ToF IC) | **Direct** (analog) |
