- **Three node states**: Error-Active, Error-Passive, Bus-Off, governed by **TEC** and **REC** counters.
- **Bit time = SYNC_SEG + PROP_SEG + PHASE_SEG1 + PHASE_SEG2** with **Sample Point between 75 % and 87.5 %**.
- **CAN FD** brings **64-byte payloads** and **higher data-phase bit rates (up to ~8 Mbps)** while keeping arbitration compatibility.
- **Real-world use:** Engine, ABS, Airbag, Transmission, Instrument Cluster, OBD-II, **AIS-140 diagnostics**, J1939 (trucks), CANopen (industry).
- **Maximum classical speed:** 1 Mbps at 40 m; **Maximum practical nodes:** 32 high-speed.
- **Never forget:** The CAN message has **no source or destination address field** — receivers filter by ID; this is what makes CAN extremely efficient for broadcast networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-sentence board recall:** *CAN is a multi-master, differential, broadcast bus where the message Identifier IS the priority — smaller ID wins — and every node maintains TEC/REC counters to stay fault-tolerant.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trip Information Computer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trip Information Computer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trip Information Computer (TIC)** — A microprocessor-/microcontroller-based onboard electronic information system in modern passenger and commercial vehicles that continuously acquires real-time data from multiple vehicle sensors (vehicle speed sensor, fuel tank level sender, engine coolant temperature sensor, ambient air temperature sensor, ignition timing reference, etc.), processes these signals using embedded algorithms, and presents the driver with derived trip parameters such as **distance-to-empty (DTE)**, **instantaneous fuel economy**, **average fuel consumption**, **average speed**, **elapsed trip time**, and **trip distance** on a graphical or alphanumeric **multi-information display (MID)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology, the Trip Information Computer is classified under the broader family of **Driver Information Systems (DIS)** and is closely related to the **Instrument Cluster**, **Body Control Module (BCM)**, and the in-vehicle **Controller Area Network (CAN bus)**. It is essentially a *Human-Machine Interface (HMI)* subsystem that converts raw sensor transductions into *decision-grade* information for the driver.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Fuel Wallet"**
> Imagine you are walking with a wallet full of cash. A smart digital assistant in your pocket continuously tracks (a) how fast you are spending money, (b) how much money is left, (c) how long you have been walking, and (d) at your current spending rate, *how many kilometres you can still walk* before going broke. It also keeps a running average of how much you have spent per kilometre over the whole trip.
> A **Trip Information Computer does exactly this** for your car — it tracks fuel as the "currency," distance as the "journey," and computes the same set of insightful metrics in real time.

The driver no longer needs to manually calculate kilometres per litre or guess whether there is enough fuel to reach the next town. The TIC automates this cognitive load and displays the answers as readable numeric or graphical readouts.

## 1.3 Why a Trip Computer is Necessary

| Engineering Need | TIC Solution |
|---|---|
| Driver cognitive overload | Consolidates multiple parameters on a single display |
| Fuel-economy awareness | Real-time MPG / L per 100 km readout |
| Range anxiety | Computes and continuously updates Distance-To-Empty |
| Trip logging for fleet operators | Records trip time, distance, and average speed |
| Service scheduling | Trip-based and time-based service reminders |
| Eco-driving feedback | Visual/audible cues encourage efficient driving |

## 1.4 Standards & Reference Quantities

- **SI base unit for distance:** metre (m), but road vehicles use **kilometre (km)**.
- **SI base unit for fuel volume:** cubic metre (m³), but vehicle use **litre (L)**.
- **Fuel economy units:** **km/L**, **L/100 km**, or **MPG** (US: miles per US gallon ≈ 3.785 L; UK: miles per Imperial gallon ≈ 4.546 L).
- **Speed unit:** **km/h** (India/Europe) or **mph** (US/UK).
- **Standardised diagnostic protocol:** **ISO 15765 (CAN-based OBD-II)** for data exchange with the TIC.
- **Operating voltage:** Vehicle **12 V** DC system (24 V for commercial/heavy vehicles); internal logic operates at regulated **5 V** or **3.3 V**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Architecture of a Trip Information Computer

A modern TIC is functionally partitioned into the following sub-blocks:

1. **Sensor Input Stage** — Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS), Fuel Level Sender (FLS), Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS), Ambient Air Temperature Sensor (AAT), Engine RPM (from ECU via CAN).
2. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Amplification, filtering, level shifting, and analog-to-digital conversion (ADC).
3. **Microcontroller / Microprocessor Core** — Hosts the embedded firmware, executes fuel-economy algorithms, manages timers, and computes averages.
4. **Non-Volatile Memory (NVS)** — EEPROM or Flash to store trip logs, odometer, calibration constants, and last fuel-fill volume.
5. **Communication Interface** — CAN, LIN (Local Interconnect Network), or K-Line (legacy) for ECU/BCM inter-module data exchange.
6. **Display Driver & Multi-Information Display (MID)** — LCD, TFT, or OLED panel that presents computed data to the driver.
7. **Human Input Interface** — Steering-mounted or stalk-mounted switches (MODE, RESET, INFO, SET) for driver interaction.
8. **Power Supply & Watchdog** — Voltage regulator (typically 7805/LDO) and a watchdog timer to reset the MCU in case of firmware lock-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite Point:** Always draw a **closed-loop signal-flow block diagram** from *sensor → signal conditioner → ADC → MCU → display driver → display*. Marks are specifically awarded for this hierarchy.

## 2.2 Core Measured & Derived Parameters

### A. Directly Measured Parameters

| Parameter | Source Sensor | Signal Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle speed $v$ | VSS (Hall/VR/Magneto-resistive) | Frequency pulse (Hz) |
| Engine speed $N$ | Crankshaft position sensor | Frequency pulse (Hz) |
| Fuel level $L_f$ | Float-based or resistive sender | Resistance / voltage |
| Engine ON time $t$ | MCU timer counter | Digital increment |
| Coolant temp $T_c$ | NTC thermistor (ECU shared) | Analog voltage |
| Ambient temp $T_a$ | Dedicated NTC sensor | Analog voltage |
| Distance covered $d$ | VSS pulses counted by MCU | Digital increment |

### B. Derived (Computed) Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous fuel consumption (IFC) | Fuel used per unit time at current load | L/h |
| Instantaneous fuel economy (IFE) | Distance per unit fuel at this instant | km/L |
| Average fuel consumption (AFC) | Total fuel used / total distance | L/100 km or km/L |
| Distance-to-Empty (DTE) / Range | Remaining fuel × Average economy | km |
| Average speed $v_{avg}$ | Total distance / total elapsed time | km/h |
| Trip distance | Integrated VSS pulses | km |
| Elapsed trip time | MCU timer count | h : m : s |
| Time-to-Empty (TTE) | Remaining fuel / fuel flow rate | h : m |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below assume a constant sampling period $T_s$ (in seconds), which is the **time interval between two successive VSS interrupts** in the MCU. The notation $N_p$ denotes the **number of VSS pulses counted** in one sampling window.

$$
v(t) = \frac{N_p \cdot C_v}{T_s}
$$

where $C_v$ = metres of vehicle travel per VSS pulse (a vehicle-specific calibration constant).

$$
\text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{t} v(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i \cdot T_s}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_s} = \frac{d_{total}}{t_{total}}
$$

$$
\text{AFE (km/L)} = \frac{d_{total}}{V_{fuel,total}}
$$

$$
\text{AFC (L/100 km)} = \frac{V_{fuel,total}}{d_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{DTE (km)} = V_{fuel,remaining} \times \text{AFE}_{recent}
$$

$$
\text{TTE (h)} = \frac{V_{fuel,remaining}}{\dot{V}_{fuel,current}}
$$

$$
f_{VSS} = \frac{v \cdot N_{teeth}}{C_{wheel}}
$$

where $N_{teeth}$ = number of teeth on the reluctor ring and $C_{wheel}$ = wheel circumference.

**Key insight for KTU:** the **fuel economy** and **DTE** are *inverse* quantities. A high km/L = low L/100 km = long DTE. Examiners love testing unit-conversion traps such as:
- km/L $\rightarrow$ L/100 km conversion
- US MPG $\rightarrow$ L/100 km conversion

$$
\text{MPG}_{US} = \frac{235.215}{\text{AFC (L/100 km)}}
$$

$$
\text{MPG}_{UK} = \frac{282.481}{\text{AFC (L/100 km)}}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fleet management** — Dispatchers download trip logs to evaluate driver behaviour, fuel-efficiency benchmarking, and route planning.
2. **Insurance telematics (UBI — Usage Based Insurance)** — The TIC's average-speed, harsh-braking, and time-of-day data feed actuarial risk models.
3. **EV/Hybrid Trip Computers** — Adapt the same architecture but replace *fuel* with *State of Charge (SoC, %)* and *kWh*; DTE becomes **km of electric range**.
4. **Emission diagnostics** — Average fuel consumption is correlated to CO₂ emissions using the molecular-balance relationship:

$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ (g/km)} \approx 23.2 \times \text{AFC (L/100 km)}
$$

5. **Predictive cruise control & eco-routing** — Modern ADAS systems use trip-computer data to plan speed profiles that minimise fuel use over hilly routes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Vehicle Speed from VSS Pulse Count

**Given:** A Hall-effect VSS produces $N_p$ pulses in a sampling window of $T_s$ seconds. Each pulse corresponds to one tooth of a tone wheel of circumference $C_v$ metres (i.e., the distance the vehicle travels between two consecutive teeth-pulses).

**To derive:** The instantaneous vehicle speed $v$ in km/h.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance in one sampling window} &= N_p \cdot C_v \;\;\;\; \text{(metres)} \\
\text{Time elapsed} &= T_s \;\;\;\; \text{(seconds)} \\
\text{Speed in m/s} &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{N_p \cdot C_v}{T_s} \\
\text{Speed in m/s} &= \frac{N_p \cdot C_v}{T_s} \\
\text{Convert m/s to km/h by multiplying by 3.6} &= \frac{N_p \cdot C_v \cdot 3.6}{T_s} \\
\therefore \quad v \left(\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\right) &= \frac{3.6 \cdot N_p \cdot C_v}{T_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**
> $N_p = 40$ pulses, $T_s = 0.5$ s, $C_v = 0.08$ m (tone-wheel pitch).
>
> $$
> v = \frac{3.6 \times 40 \times 0.08}{0.5} = \frac{11.52}{0.5} = 23.04 \text{ km/h}
> $$

## 3.2 Derivation — Average Fuel Economy from Trip Data

**Given:** Total distance covered $d_{total}$ in km, total fuel consumed $V_{fuel,total}$ in litres.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Fuel Economy (AFE)} &= \frac{\text{Distance covered}}{\text{Fuel consumed}} \;\;\;\; \text{(km/L)} \\
\text{Average Fuel Consumption (AFC)} &= \frac{\text{Fuel consumed}}{\text{Distance covered}} \times 100 \;\;\;\; \text{(L/100 km)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These two are reciprocally related:

$$
\text{AFE} \times \text{AFC} = 100
$$

> **Numerical example:**
> $d_{total} = 350$ km, $V_{fuel,total} = 25$ L
>
> $$
> \text{AFE} = \frac{350}{25} = 14 \text{ km/L}
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{AFC} = \frac{25}{350} \times 100 = 7.143 \text{ L/100 km}
> $$
>
> Sanity check: $14 \times 7.143 = 100.0$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation — Distance-To-Empty (DTE)

**Given:** Remaining fuel in tank $V_{rem}$ (L), recent (typically last 30 km) average fuel economy $AFE_{recent}$ (km/L).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DTE} &= V_{rem} \times AFE_{recent} \;\;\;\; \text{(km)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical example:**
> $V_{rem} = 8$ L, $AFE_{recent} = 12$ km/L
>
> $$
> \text{DTE} = 8 \times 12 = 96 \text{ km}
> $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students treat DTE using **instantaneous** fuel economy. In production vehicles, DTE uses a **filtered average** (often over the last 100–500 km or the last full tank) to avoid range jumps every time the driver lifts off the accelerator.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Flow

| Step | MCU Operation | Inputs | Output / Update |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initialise peripherals (Timer, ADC, CAN, GPIO, LCD) | — | System ready |
| 2 | Read VSS pulse count over $T_s$ | $N_p$, $T_s$ | $v$ (km/h) |
| 3 | Read fuel level via ADC | $V_{f-signal}$ (0–5 V) | $V_{rem}$ (L) |
| 4 | Read coolant & ambient temperature | $V_{cts}$, $V_{aats}$ | $T_c$, $T_a$ (°C) |
| 5 | Update trip odometer | $v$, $T_s$ | $d_{total} = d_{total} + v \cdot T_s / 3600$ |
| 6 | Update moving average of fuel economy | $v$, $V_{f}$ used | $AFE_{new} = \alpha \cdot AFE_{old} + (1-\alpha) \cdot AFE_{inst}$ |
| 7 | Compute DTE, AFC, average speed, TTE | All above | Updated display buffer |
| 8 | Refresh MID with currently selected page | Mode-button input | Visible readouts |
| 9 | Service-reminder check | $d_{total}$ since last service | Warning icon if threshold exceeded |
| 10 | Repeat (interrupt-driven or main loop) | — | Continuous monitoring |

The parameter $\alpha$ in Step 6 is a **digital low-pass filter coefficient** ($0 < \alpha < 1$). A typical value is $\alpha = 0.95$, meaning new instantaneous readings contribute only 5 % weight to the displayed average — this produces a *stable, non-flickering* economy readout.

$$
AFE_{new} = \alpha \cdot AFE_{old} + (1-\alpha) \cdot AFE_{inst}
$$

If $\alpha = 0.9$ and $AFE_{old} = 14.0$ km/L, $AFE_{inst} = 16.0$ km/L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFE_{new} &= 0.9 \times 14.0 + 0.1 \times 16.0 \\
&= 12.6 + 1.6 \\
&= 14.2 \text{ km/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exponential smoothing prevents erratic display behaviour and is a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

## 3.5 Unit-Conversion Worked Examples

**Example 1 — km/L to L/100 km:**

Given: 18 km/L

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AFC} &= \frac{100}{AFE} = \frac{100}{18} = 5.556 \text{ L/100 km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example 2 — L/100 km to US MPG:**

Given: 7 L/100 km

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPG}_{US} &= \frac{235.215}{7} = 33.6 \text{ mpg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example 3 — US MPG to km/L:**

Given: 30 US mpg

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{L/100 km} &= \frac{235.215}{30} = 7.84 \\
\text{km/L} &= \frac{100}{7.84} = 12.76
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid for the exam:** If km/L is **high**, L/100 km and MPG/CO₂ are **low**. Always keep this inverse relationship in mind to sanity-check your answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full TIC Architecture

The following diagram maps every functional block, signal flow, and data path inside a production-grade Trip Information Computer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Battery 12V DC] --> REG[Voltage Regulator 5V / 3.3V LDO]
    REG --> MCU[Microcontroller Core]
    REG --> SENS[PWR to Sensors]

    VSS[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS Hall Effect] --> SC1[Signal Conditioner Schmitt Trigger]
    SC1 --> MCU

    FLS[Fuel Level Sender Resistive] --> ADC1[12 bit ADC Channel 1]
    ADC1 --> MCU

    CTS[Coolant Temp Sensor NTC] --> ADC2[ADC Channel 2]
    ADC2 --> MCU

    AAT[Ambient Air Temp Sensor NTC] --> ADC3[ADC Channel 3]
    ADC3 --> MCU

    ECU[Engine ECU via CAN Bus] --> CAN[CAN Controller MCP2515]
    CAN --> MCU

    MCU --> NVS[EEPROM Non Volatile Storage]
    MCU --> RTC[Real Time Clock DS1307]

    MCU --> DRV[Display Driver IC]
    DRV --> MID[Multi Information Display LCD TFT]

    SW[Driver Switches Mode Reset Set] --> DI[Digital Input with Debounce]
    DI --> MCU

    MCU --> BZR[Audible Buzzer for Warnings]
    MCU --> LED[Warning LED Indicators]

    MCU --> WDT[Watchdog Timer]
    WDT --> MCU
```

> **Reading guide:** Every *sensor* feeds a *conditioning block* (analog to digital, pulse to logic level) before reaching the MCU. The MCU is the *single decision authority*; the display driver and beeper are its *output actuators*. The CAN bus is the *gateway to other ECUs* such as the Engine Control Unit (ECU), ABS module, and Body Control Module (BCM).

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Driver Interaction with the MID

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BootInit
    BootInit --> ClockDisplay: Power On Self Test
    ClockDisplay --> TripA: Driver Presses INFO Once
    TripA --> TripB: Press INFO Again
    TripB --> AFE: Press INFO Again
    AFE --> AFC: Press INFO Again
    AFC --> DTE: Press INFO Again
    DTE --> AvgSpeed: Press INFO Again
    AvgSpeed --> ElapsedTime: Press INFO Again
    ElapsedTime --> OutsideTemp: Press INFO Again
    OutsideTemp --> ClockDisplay: Press INFO Again

    TripA --> TripA_Reset: Long Press RESET
    TripB --> TripB_Reset: Long Press RESET
    AFE --> AFE_Reset: Long Press RESET

    TripA_Reset --> TripA
    TripB_Reset --> TripB
    AFE_Reset --> AFE
```

> **Interpretation:** A *short press* of the INFO button advances to the next readout page cyclically. A *long press* (typically > 2 s) of RESET zeroes only the currently displayed trip variable — this is the standard OEM behaviour and is a **favourite KTU question** in Part A.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — DTE Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Read Fuel Level via ADC]
    B --> C[Convert ADC Counts to Litres using Tank Map]
    C --> D{V_rem less than Reserve Threshold}
    D -- Yes --> E[Activate Low Fuel Warning]
    D -- No --> F[Read recent AFE from NVS]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute DTE = V_rem times AFE_recent]
    G --> H[Apply Saturation Clamp 0 to 9999 km]
    H --> I[Send DTE to Display Buffer]
    I --> J[Delay 1 second]
    J --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Trip Information Computer. List any four parameters displayed by it.**

**Model Answer:**
A Trip Information Computer (TIC) is a microcontroller-based onboard electronic system that acquires signals from various vehicle sensors, processes them using embedded algorithms, and displays derived trip and fuel-economy information to the driver on a multi-information display (MID).

Any four parameters:
1. Instantaneous fuel consumption (L/100 km or km/L)
2. Average fuel consumption over a trip
3. Distance-to-Empty (DTE) / Range in km
4. Average speed (km/h)
5. Trip distance (km)
6. Elapsed trip time (h : m)
7. Outside/ambient air temperature (°C)
8. Time-to-Empty (h : m)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Distance-to-Empty (DTE)? Write the formula and explain why a filtered (averaged) fuel-economy value is preferred over instantaneous fuel economy in the DTE calculation.**

**Model Answer:**
**DTE** is the estimated distance, in kilometres, that the vehicle can travel on the fuel currently remaining in the tank.

$$
\text{DTE (km)} = V_{fuel,remaining} \text{ (L)} \times AFE_{recent} \text{ (km/L)}
$$

A *filtered/averaged* fuel-economy value is used because the **instantaneous** fuel economy fluctuates violently with every change in accelerator pedal position, gear, road gradient, and wind. Using the instantaneous value would make the DTE readout jump erratically on the display, distracting and confusing the driver. A moving average (e.g., over the last 100–500 km) gives a *stable, predictable* range estimate that matches real-world driving behaviour.

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Trip Computer Questions**
> 1. Always **label every block** in your block diagram. An unlabelled block is treated as *missing content* (–2 marks).
> 2. **Show units** at every numerical step. A number without a unit is marked as incomplete (–0.5 to –1 mark per occurrence).
> 3. In DTE / fuel-economy derivations, do **not** confuse *fuel consumption* (L/100 km) with *fuel economy* (km/L) — they are **reciprocals**. Examiners set *unit traps* to test this.
> 4. Always state the **sensor source** for each measured parameter. "VSS provides speed" is worth 1 mark; vague statements like "speed is measured" earn 0.
> 5. Mention the **filtering algorithm** (exponential moving average, $\alpha$-weighting) when discussing DTE — most students skip this and lose 2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Trip Information Computer. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's VSS produces 50 pulses per second at a constant speed. The tone-wheel pitch (distance per pulse) is 0.10 m. Compute: **(7 marks)**
   (i) the vehicle speed in km/h,
   (ii) the distance covered in 5 minutes,
   (iii) the fuel economy in km/L if 3.6 L of fuel was used during this period.

**Model Solution — (a):**

The TIC architecture consists of the following interconnected blocks (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Power Supply Block** — Regulates vehicle 12 V battery to stable 5 V / 3.3 V for the MCU and logic circuits. Includes reverse-polarity and transient protection.
2. **Sensor Input Block** — VSS, FLS, CTS, AAT provide real-time vehicle and engine data.
3. **Signal Conditioning Block** — Converts raw sensor outputs to MCU-compatible logic levels. For VSS, a Schmitt trigger cleans up the noisy pulse train. For thermistors and the fuel sender, an op-amp buffer and RC low-pass filter remove EMI, then an ADC digitises the signal.
4. **Microcontroller Core** — A 32-bit MCU (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0/M3) running the embedded firmware: real-time operating scheduler, fuel-economy engine, DTE calculator, and display manager.
5. **Non-Volatile Memory** — Stores trip logs, odometer, calibration constants (tank map, $C_v$, etc.), and the last fuel-fill quantity.
6. **Communication Block** — CAN / LIN transceiver to receive engine RPM, throttle position, fuel-injection pulse width from ECU.
7. **Display Driver & MID** — Renders the computed values on a TFT/LCD multi-information display.
8. **Human Input Block** — Switch matrix (MODE, RESET, SET, INFO) with debounce circuitry.
9. **Audible / Visual Warning Block** — Buzzer and warning LEDs for low-fuel, service-due, and door-ajar alerts.
10. **Watchdog Timer** — Resets the MCU if firmware hangs.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given:** $f_{VSS} = 50$ pulses/s, $C_v = 0.10$ m/pulse, $t = 5$ min $= 300$ s, $V_{fuel} = 3.6$ L.

**(i) Vehicle speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= f_{VSS} \times C_v = 50 \times 0.10 = 5 \text{ m/s} \\
v \text{ in km/h} &= 5 \times 3.6 = 18 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Distance covered in 5 minutes:**

$$
d = v \times t = 5 \text{ m/s} \times 300 \text{ s} = 1500 \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ km}
$$

**(iii) Fuel economy:**

$$
AFE = \frac{d}{V_{fuel}} = \frac{1.5 \text{ km}}{3.6 \text{ L}} = 0.4167 \text{ km/L}
$$

In L/100 km:

$$
AFC = \frac{100}{0.4167} = 240 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

> This economy is unrealistically poor because the example speed is very low (18 km/h) — useful to highlight that AFE depends strongly on driving conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the Distance-to-Empty (DTE) computation. With a suitable flow diagram, describe how the TIC obtains the required inputs and updates the display. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car's fuel tank holds 45 L when full. After completing a 480 km trip, the driver refills 36 L. On a subsequent trip, the driver covers 220 km and the in-tank fuel sender reads 18 L remaining. Compute: **(7 marks)**
   (i) the average fuel economy (km/L and L/100 km),
   (ii) the current DTE if the recent filtered AFE is 13.5 km/L.

**Model Solution — (a):**

The **DTE computation** is a continuous background task of the TIC. The MCU repeats the following sequence every display update cycle (typically 1 s):

**Step 1 — Acquire remaining fuel volume:**
The resistive fuel-level sender provides a variable resistance $R_f$ (typically 0 Ω at full to 90 Ω at empty, or 240 Ω to 33 Ω depending on OEM). The voltage divider with a precision reference is sampled by the ADC. The raw ADC count is converted to litres through a **tank-shape map** stored in EEPROM (because tanks are not perfectly rectangular — a motorcycle tank can be 50 % height but only 30 % volume).

**Step 2 — Acquire recent fuel economy:**
The MCU maintains an exponentially filtered AFE:

$$
AFE_{new} = \alpha \cdot AFE_{old} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot AFE_{inst}
$$

where $AFE_{inst}$ is computed from the current speed and instantaneous fuel-flow rate (from ECU via CAN).

**Step 3 — Compute DTE:**

$$
DTE = V_{rem} \times AFE_{new}
$$

The result is clamped to a sensible range (0–9999 km) and rounded to the nearest integer.

**Step 4 — Display update:**
The value is sent to the display driver. If $V_{rem}$ falls below a manufacturer-defined reserve threshold (e.g., 5 L), a low-fuel warning is activated.

(Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.3 for the visual representation.)

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given:** Tank full = 45 L, refill amount = 36 L, distance covered = 480 km.

**(i) Average fuel economy:**

The fuel used during the trip = refill amount = 36 L (assuming the tank was refilled to full at the end of the trip, and no fuel was added in between).

$$
AFE = \frac{480 \text{ km}}{36 \text{ L}} = 13.33 \text{ km/L}
$$

$$
AFC = \frac{36 \text{ L}}{480 \text{ km}} \times 100 = 7.5 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

> Note: The 220 km and 18 L remaining data is for the next part.

**(ii) Current DTE:**

Given: $V_{rem} = 18$ L, $AFE_{recent} = 13.5$ km/L.

$$
DTE = 18 \text{ L} \times 13.5 \text{ km/L} = 243 \text{ km}
$$

The vehicle can travel approximately **243 km** on the remaining fuel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Trip Information Computer**

- **Definition** — TIC = MCU-based onboard system converting raw sensor data into driver-friendly trip metrics.
- **Core sensors:** VSS, FLS, CTS, AAT, ECU link (CAN) for RPM and fuel-flow.
- **Speed derivation** — $v = 3.6 \cdot N_p \cdot C_v / T_s$ — **always** convert to km/h in the final step.
- **Distance** = integrated VSS pulses; odometer is *non-resettable*, trip meter is *resettable*.
- **Fuel-economy units** — km/L and L/100 km are **reciprocals**; their product = 100. MPG uses a constant 235.215 (US) or 282.481 (UK).
- **DTE** uses a *filtered AFE* (moving average / exponential smoothing with $\alpha \approx 0.9$–$0.95$) — **never** use instantaneous economy.
- **Tank-map calibration** is essential because fuel tanks are non-linear in shape.
- **CAN bus** is the standard communication backbone; OBD-II (ISO 15765) is the diagnostic gateway.
- **Display types** — segmented LCD, dot-matrix LCD, TFT, OLED. Driver switches cycle the displayed page.
- **Service reminders** — Based on *time-since-last-service* AND *distance-since-last-service*; reset is via long-press of a dedicated button.
- **Exponential smoothing formula** — $AFE_{new} = \alpha \cdot AFE_{old} + (1-\alpha) \cdot AFE_{inst}$ — high $\alpha$ = stable display, slow response; low $\alpha$ = quick response, noisy display.
- **Examiner traps** — (1) mixing km/L with L/100 km, (2) forgetting the 3.6 conversion factor, (3) using instantaneous economy for DTE, (4) drawing unlabelled block diagrams, (5) omitting the tank-map explanation for fuel-level linearity.
- **Modern extension** — In EVs the same architecture is reused but with **SoC (%)** and **kWh** in place of litres; DTE becomes "km of electric range."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT744 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Telematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telematics** in the automotive context is defined as the **integrated discipline of telecommunications, vehicular electronics, and information processing** that enables bidirectional wireless communication between a vehicle and an external entity (remote server, fleet operator, cloud platform, or another vehicle) for the purposes of monitoring, controlling, diagnosing, and optimizing vehicle performance, safety, and operational efficiency.

The term is a **portmanteau** of *Telecommunications* and *Informatics*, formally coined during the **1969** Conference on Telematics organized by the French Government, and adapted for automotive use in the late 1990s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEICT744 Module 4):** The telematics unit must be studied in conjunction with **on-board diagnostics (OBD-II/EOBD)**, **Global Positioning System (GPS)**, **Cellular (GSM/GPRS/4G LTE/5G) and satellite communication**, and **inertial MEMS sensors**. Together they form the *Connected Vehicle Architecture*.

## 1.2 Key Constituents of an Automotive Telematics System

A complete telematics system is composed of three inter-operating layers, summarized in the table below.

| Layer | Functional Role | Representative Hardware/Software |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Vehicle Layer** | Data acquisition, aggregation, and pre-processing | ECU, OBD-II port, MEMS accelerometers & gyroscopes, GPS receiver, microphone, camera |
| **Communication Layer** | Wireless data transport (uplink + downlink) | GSM/GPRS modem, 4G LTE/5G modem, satellite transceiver, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, DSRC |
| **Service Layer (Backend)** | Storage, analytics, visualization, dispatch | Cloud servers, fleet management software, emergency call centers (e.g., eCall), insurance portals |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor-On-Call" for Your Car

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine your car has a small "doctor" permanently living inside it. This doctor constantly checks the car's "vitals" — engine temperature, heart-rate (RPM), speed, fuel level, tyre pressure, and even location. Whenever anything unusual happens (an accident, a breakdown, or just a regular check-up), the doctor immediately calls a hospital (the cloud server), transmits the vitals, and receives instructions. The hospital then dispatches a tow-truck, an ambulance, or simply logs the data. **That "doctor with a phone" is the telematics control unit (TCU)**, and the entire process is automotive telematics.

A more accessible everyday analogy:

- The **vehicle** = a delivery truck.
- The **telematics box** = the truck's *black-box + walkie-talkie*.
- The **cloud server** = the *fleet dispatcher's computer screen*.
- The **GPS signal** = the *postman delivering the address* to the box.
- The **cellular network** = the *radio channel over which the driver reports status*.

## 1.4 Important Industry Metrics and Standardized Terms

The following metrics are *de-facto* industry standards that frequently appear in KTU exam questions and must be memorized:

- **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System):** Collective term for GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China), IRNSS/NavIC (India).
- **Update Rate:** Standard vehicular GPS operates at **1 Hz (1 sample per second)**; high-precision telematics uses **10 Hz–50 Hz**.
- **Cold Start TTFF (Time To First Fix):** Typical value **30–45 s** for a cold GPS receiver; reduced to **1–5 s** with Assisted-GPS (A-GPS).
- **Accuracy:** Civilian GPS horizontal accuracy is **2.5 m CEP (Circular Error Probable)** with SA off.
- **eCall (EU Mandate 2018):** Automatic emergency call system — required in **all new M1/N1 category vehicles** sold in the EU since **31 March 2018**.
- **OBD-II Connector:** 16-pin **SAE J1962** standardized diagnostic port, mandatory in the US since **1996** and in the EU (EOBD) since **2001** for petrol cars.
- **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications):** Operates in the **5.85–5.925 GHz** band (IEEE 802.11p), latency < **10 ms**, range ~ **300 m**.
- **C-V2X (Cellular Vehicle-to-Everything):** Based on **3GPP Release 14 (LTE-V2X)** and **Release 16 (5G-V2X)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Sense → Compute → Communicate → Act" Pipeline

The telematics data flow follows a four-stage operational pipeline:

1. **SENSE (Data Acquisition Stage)**
   - MEMS accelerometers (typically ±2 g to ±16 g range) detect sudden deceleration (collision signature).
   - Gyroscopes measure yaw rate for rollover detection.
   - Wheel-speed sensors and ECU broadcast bus (CAN, LIN, FlexRay) supply real-time vehicle dynamics.
   - GPS receiver continuously outputs position, velocity (via Doppler shift), and time (PVT) at a configurable rate.

2. **COMPUTE (Edge Processing Stage)**
   - The **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** — a ruggedized automotive-grade embedded computer (often ARM Cortex-A series with 512 MB–2 GB RAM) — fuses sensor data using a **Kalman Filter**.
   - It runs the **event-detection algorithm** (e.g., if resultant acceleration > threshold *g* for > threshold time *t*, declare crash).

3. **COMMUNICATE (Wireless Transmission Stage)**
   - The TCU packages the event into a structured payload (e.g., per the **EUserg ACP 245** or **IEEE 1616** standard) and transmits it via the cellular modem over **SMS (CSD)**, **GPRS**, **LTE**, or **5G** to a backend server.
   - For low-latency safety use cases, **C-V2X** sidelink (PC5 interface) communicates directly with nearby vehicles/roadside units (RSUs) **without cellular infrastructure**.

4. **ACT (Service Response Stage)**
   - The backend server triggers the appropriate response: notifying emergency services, dispatching roadside assistance, sending an SMS to the owner, or pushing an over-the-air (OTA) software update.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Kalman Filter?** GPS alone is noisy (≈ 2.5 m error) and unavailable in tunnels; inertial sensors drift. A **Kalman filter optimally fuses both** to yield a sub-meter position estimate with bounded error covariance — this is the cornerstone of *dead-reckoning* telematics.

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-System Theory

### 2.2.1 Global Positioning System (GPS) — Working Principle

The GPS receiver computes its position by **trilateration** from at least **4 satellites** in view:

- **3 satellites** provide 2-D position (latitude, longitude) and receiver clock bias.
- **4 satellites** provide full 3-D position (latitude, longitude, altitude) and receiver clock bias.
- **5+ satellites** improve accuracy via least-squares over-determination.

Each satellite broadcasts a **navigation message** at **50 bps** on the **L1 carrier (1575.42 MHz)** containing:
- Ephemeris (precise satellite orbit)
- Almanac (coarse orbit of all satellites)
- Clock correction parameters
- Ionospheric model coefficients

The receiver measures **pseudo-range** $\rho_i$ to satellite $i$ as:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{received} - t_{transmitted}) = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} + c \cdot \Delta t_{bias} + \epsilon_i$$

where $c$ is the speed of light, $(x, y, z)$ is the unknown receiver position, $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ is the broadcast satellite position, $\Delta t_{bias}$ is the receiver clock bias, and $\epsilon_i$ represents unmodelled errors (ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath).

### 2.2.2 Vehicle Bus Integration — CAN, OBD-II, UDS

- **CAN (Controller Area Network):** ISO 11898 standard, differential signalling on **CAN-H and CAN-L**, data rate up to **1 Mbps** (Classical CAN) or **5 Mbps** (CAN-FD).
- **OBD-II PIDs:** Standardized Parameter IDs (Mode 01) defined by **SAE J1979** allow reading of live engine data (e.g., PID 0x0C = Engine RPM, PID 0x0D = Vehicle Speed, PID 0x05 = Coolant Temperature).
- **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services):** ISO 14229 standard used for flash programming, DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code) retrieval, and remote diagnostics.

### 2.2.3 Communication Protocols for Telematics Backend

| Protocol | Transport | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** | TCP, port 1883/8883 | Lightweight IoT pub/sub messaging |
| **HTTP/HTTPS REST** | TCP, port 80/443 | Firmware OTA, configuration pull |
| **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** | UDP, port 5683 | Low-power sensor telemetry |
| **SMS (Short Message Service)** | GSM signalling channels | Fallback / control plane |
| **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** | UDP multicast | High-throughput V2X messaging |

### 2.2.4 Security and Privacy Considerations

- **TLS 1.2/1.3** mandatory for backend communication.
- **FIPS 140-2** certified Hardware Security Modules (HSM) for key storage in TCU.
- **Vehicle-to-cloud authentication** using **X.509 certificates** (e.g., per IEEE 1609.2 for V2X).
- **GDPR (EU)** and **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** regulate the storage and processing of driver location data — explicit consent required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the most frequently tested in the PEICT744 Module-4 Telematics section. Master them thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | $d$ = pseudo-range (m), $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = signal travel time (s) | Basic GPS range measurement |
| 2 | $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + c\Delta t_{bias}$ | $\rho_i$ = measured pseudo-range to satellite $i$, $(x_i,y_i,z_i)$ = satellite coords | GPS trilateration equation |
| 3 | $\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left( \frac{1}{\sigma_{GPS}^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_{INS}^2} \right)^{-1}$ | $\sigma_{fused}$ = fused position std. dev. | Kalman filter variance combination |
| 4 | $v = \frac{d}{t}$ | $v$ = vehicle speed, $d$ = GPS-derived displacement, $t$ = elapsed time | Average speed over GPS samples |
| 5 | $R = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{rx} \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot L}$ | $R$ = received power, $G$ = antenna gain, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $L$ = system loss | Free-space path loss (Friis equation) for V2X link budget |
| 6 | $T_{latency} = T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{proc}$ | $T$ = time components | End-to-end communication latency |
| 7 | $T_{prop} = \frac{d_{link}}{c}$ | $d_{link}$ = link distance, $c$ = speed of light or $3\times 10^8$ m/s (vacuum) or $2\times 10^8$ m/s (fiber) | One-way propagation delay |
| 8 | $SNR = P_{rx} - N_{floor} \;\; (\text{dB})$ | $P_{rx}$ = received power (dBm), $N_{floor}$ = noise floor | Minimum SNR for demodulation |
| 9 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | $C$ = channel capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) | Shannon capacity of wireless channel |
| 10 | $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}}{1}$ | $a$ = resultant acceleration (m/s²) | Crash detection threshold logic |
| 11 | $f_D = \frac{v \cdot \cos\theta}{c} \cdot f_0$ | $f_D$ = Doppler shift, $\theta$ = angle of arrival, $f_0$ = carrier frequency | GPS velocity measurement |
| 12 | $DOP = \frac{\sigma_{pos}}{\sigma_{UERE}}$ | $DOP$ = Dilution of Precision (GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP), $\sigma_{UERE}$ = user equivalent range error | GPS geometric quality metric |
| 13 | $E_{b}/N_0 = \frac{S/N}{R/B}$ | $E_b$ = energy per bit, $N_0$ = noise spectral density, $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio, $R$ = bit rate, $B$ = bandwidth | Digital comm. link budget |
| 14 | $L_{fspl} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | $L_{fspl}$ = free-space path loss in dB, $d_{km}$ = distance in km, $f_{MHz}$ = frequency in MHz | Cellular/DSRC link budget |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Automotive telematics is the technological foundation of:

- **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI):** Insurance premium is dynamically calculated from actual driving behaviour (hard-braking, night driving, mileage).
- **Fleet Management & Logistics:** Companies like DHL, FedEx, and Amazon monitor millions of delivery vehicles in real time.
- **Stolen Vehicle Recovery:** Systems like LoJack use RF + cellular to recover > 90 % of stolen vehicles.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Cloud analytics on engine DTCs predict component failure before it occurs.
- **eCall / ERA-GLONASS:** Mandatory automatic emergency call — projected to save **> 2,500 lives/year** in the EU alone.
- **Autonomous Driving (SAE Level 3+):** V2X communication is essential for cooperative perception and platooning.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Read First):** Numerical errors in telematics problems almost always stem from (a) unit mismatches between km/h and m/s, and (b) forgetting the **receiver clock bias** in the GPS pseudo-range equation. Always convert units explicitly and state the *four unknowns* $(x, y, z, \Delta t_{bias})$ that justify why **a minimum of 4 satellites is required**.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Minimum Number of Satellites for 3-D GPS Fix

**Problem Statement:** Prove that a GPS receiver requires a minimum of 4 satellites to determine its 3-D position and time.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the unknowns.** The receiver must determine:
- 3 spatial coordinates: $(x, y, z)$ — latitude, longitude, altitude
- 1 clock bias: $\Delta t_{bias}$ — because the receiver's quartz clock is not synchronized with the atomic clocks on the satellites.

**Total unknowns = 4.**

**Step 2 — Write the pseudo-range equation for satellite $i$.**

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} + c \cdot \Delta t_{bias}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that each satellite provides one independent equation.** Therefore, $N$ satellites in view yield $N$ independent equations.

**Step 4 — Apply the rule of solvability.** A non-linear system of $N$ equations in 4 unknowns requires $N \geq 4$ for a unique solution.

$$
N_{min} = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on redundancy.** With 5+ satellites, the system is **over-determined**, and the **Least-Squares Method** is applied to minimize the residual error.

$$
\min_{x,y,z,\Delta t_{bias}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \rho_i - \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} - c\Delta t_{bias} \right]^2
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum of 4 satellites is mathematically necessary and sufficient for a 3-D position fix with time correction.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — GPS Position Fix from 4 Pseudo-Ranges (Numerical Example)

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle-mounted GPS receiver measures the following pseudo-ranges (in metres) to four satellites. Compute the receiver's position $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $\Delta t_{bias}$.

| Satellite | $x_i$ (m) | $y_i$ (m) | $z_i$ (m) | $\rho_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15,600,000 | 7,500,000 | 21,000,000 | 21,100,000 |
| 2 | −14,000,000 | 12,000,000 | 18,000,000 | 24,500,000 |
| 3 | 8,000,000 | −18,000,000 | 19,000,000 | 22,800,000 |
| 4 | 18,500,000 | −3,000,000 | 20,000,000 | 19,700,000 |

Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the four pseudo-range equations.** For each satellite $i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$:

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} + c\Delta t_{bias}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract equation 1 from equations 2, 3, 4 to eliminate the quadratic term.** Define $R_i = \rho_i - \rho_1$ and $d_i = \rho_i^2 - \rho_1^2$. After algebraic simplification:

$$
2(x_1 - x_i)x + 2(y_1 - y_i)y + 2(z_1 - z_i)z - 2c(\rho_i - \rho_1)\Delta t_{bias} = d_i
$$

**Step 3 — Form the linear system $\mathbf{A}\vec{u} = \vec{b}$.** Let $\vec{u} = (x, y, z, c\Delta t_{bias})^T$.

For our numerical data, the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (3 × 4) becomes:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1-x_2) & 2(y_1-y_2) & 2(z_1-z_2) & -2(\rho_2-\rho_1) \\ 2(x_1-x_3) & 2(y_1-y_3) & 2(z_1-z_3) & -2(\rho_3-\rho_1) \\ 2(x_1-x_4) & 2(y_1-y_4) & 2(z_1-z_4) & -2(\rho_4-\rho_1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 59,200,000 & -9,000,000 & 6,000,000 & -6,800,000 \\ 15,200,000 & 51,000,000 & 4,000,000 & -3,400,000 \\ -5,800,000 & 21,000,000 & 2,000,000 & 2,800,000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the right-hand side vector $\vec{b}$.**

$$
\vec{b} = \begin{bmatrix} d_2 \\ d_3 \\ d_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_2^2 - \rho_1^2 - (x_2^2-x_1^2) - (y_2^2-y_1^2) - (z_2^2-z_1^2) \\ \rho_3^2 - \rho_1^2 - (x_3^2-x_1^2) - (y_3^2-y_1^2) - (z_3^2-z_1^2) \\ \rho_4^2 - \rho_1^2 - (x_4^2-x_1^2) - (y_4^2-y_1^2) - (z_4^2-z_1^2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve via linearization (Newton-Raphson or iterative least-squares).** Since this is an over-determined 3 × 4 system, we solve the **normal equations**:

$$
\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} \vec{u} = \mathbf{A}^T \vec{b}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.** The converged solution yields (illustrative magnitudes):

$$
x \approx -2.7 \times 10^6 \text{ m},\quad y \approx 4.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m},\quad z \approx 4.3 \times 10^6 \text{ m},\quad c\Delta t_{bias} \approx 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ m}
$$

Converting to clock bias:

$$
\Delta t_{bias} = \frac{c\Delta t_{bias}}{c} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^4}{3 \times 10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 40 \, \mu s
$$

This 40 µs clock bias corresponds to a **12 km** ranging error — illustrating why clock correction is *non-negotiable* in GPS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Wireless Link Budget for DSRC (V2V)

**Problem Statement:** Compute the maximum reliable communication range of a DSRC transmitter under free-space conditions.

**Given Data:**
- Transmit power: $P_{tx} = 20$ dBm
- Transmit antenna gain: $G_{tx} = 3$ dBi
- Receive antenna gain: $G_{rx} = 3$ dBi
- Carrier frequency: $f = 5.9$ GHz
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_{rx,min} = -90$ dBm
- System loss: $L = 5$ dB

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Friis free-space transmission equation (in dB).**

$$
P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{fspl}(d) - L
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the maximum allowable path loss $L_{fspl,max}$.**

$$
L_{fspl,max} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - P_{rx,min} - L
$$

$$
L_{fspl,max} = 20 + 3 + 3 - (-90) - 5 = 111 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the free-space path loss formula.**

$$
L_{fspl}(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $f = 5900$ MHz.**

$$
L_{fspl}(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(5900)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.771 = 75.42 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate to $L_{fspl,max}$ and solve for $d_{km}$.**

$$
111 = 32.44 + 75.42 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 111 - 32.44 - 75.42 = 3.14
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 0.157
$$

$$
d_{km} = 10^{0.157} \approx 1.435 \text{ km}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum reliable DSRC communication range is approximately **1.43 km** under line-of-sight, free-space conditions — well beyond the **300 m** typical operational range, providing a **+12.5 dB** fade margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Crash Detection Threshold (eCall Algorithm)

**Problem Statement:** A telematics system uses a 3-axis MEMS accelerometer. Design a crash-detection threshold such that a hard-braking event (deceleration ≈ 0.6 g) is *not* misinterpreted as a crash.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the resultant acceleration.**

$$
a_{res} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the gravity-removed acceleration (in g).**

$$
a_{net} = \frac{a_{res} - g}{g}
$$

where $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 3 — Apply the eCall threshold.** Per the **EU eCall Regulation (EU) 2015/758**, the trigger condition is:

$$
a_{net} > 1.5 \, g \quad \text{AND} \quad \Delta t_{sustained} > 30 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify rejection of hard-braking.**

For hard-braking, $a_{net} = 0.6 g < 1.5 g$ → **Not triggered** ✓

For a 25 km/h frontal impact, $a_{net} \approx 4 g > 1.5 g$ sustained for 80 ms → **Triggered** ✓

**Step 5 — Conclude.** The 1.5 g threshold with 30 ms minimum duration reliably distinguishes genuine crashes from aggressive driving.

---
